actions.thought: UTG with QJo offsuit in a 6-handed 100bb deepstack tournament. This is a standard fold from early position. QJo offsuit has poor playability out of position, is dominated by stronger broadway hands like AKo/AQo/KQo/AJs+, and vulnerable to 3-bets. Limping is exploitable and weak; raising risks commitment with a marginal hand. Folding preserves stack for premium spots on Hand 1 with no reads.

T♣8♦ is a marginal hand but openable from middle with deep 100bb stacks and UTG already folded. There are four players left to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB), so I want a sizing that gives reasonable fold equity without bloating the pot. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300 total). Rationale: 3x applies pressure to the blinds and steals better than a min-raise, keeps the pot manageable with a speculative hand, and preserves stack depth to fold to a large 3‑bet or proceed postflop if called. Plan: fold to a big 3‑bet, call or continue cautiously against single callers, c-bet favorable flops, and exercise pot control multiway.

A4 suited in the cutoff facing a 3x open from MP. This is a playable hand with flush potential and an ace. I have position on the raiser. Calling is standard here - the hand plays well postflop and isn't strong enough to 3-bet for value, but too good to fold given position and suited potential.

I am on the button with 9h 3c, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has opened for 3x the big blind and Claude has called. Given the strength of my hand and the action ahead of me, there is no reason to enter this pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Facing a 3bb open and a caller from the small blind, 98o plays poorly out of position in a likely multiway pot and is vulnerable to domination/reverse implied odds. Even with decent pot odds, calling from the SB here is a long-term loser, and it’s not strong enough to justify a squeeze. I’ll fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 4d, facing a raise from Middle Position (Minni) and a call from the Cutoff (Claude). The pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a total pot of 950, roughly 4.75:1), requiring only about 21% equity. However, 94o is one of the weakest hands in Hold'em. It plays very poorly post-flop, lacks connectivity and suit coordination, and I would be playing the entire hand out of position against two opponents. The risk of reverse implied odds (losing a larger pot with a second-best hand) and the difficulty of realizing equity out of position make this a clear fold despite the price.

I opened from middle and was called by the CO, so this is heads-up on an Ace-high, paired board (Ac 5h 5d). I have T8o — no pair, no real draw (only a backdoor club/straight possibility), so I completely missed. The caller’s range (cutoff call) is heavily weighted toward Ax, middle pairs and broadway hands that will often continue against a c-bet, so my fold equity is limited and I’m frequently behind when called. With deep stacks early in the tournament I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with air. I’ll check to see how Claude reacts: call small bets to float or fold to large bets, and only invest more if I pick up equity on a safe turn or can credibly represent an Ace on later streets.

I flopped top pair with a weak kicker (A4) on an Ac5h5d board. Minni raised preflop but checked to me - likely has missed hands like KQ/KJ or medium pairs. I should bet for value and protection. Around 1/2 pot bet (~375) is appropriate to get value from worse hands while not bloating the pot too much with a mediocre kicker.

I checked the flop for pot control with T♣8♦ and Claude bet 375 into a 750-pot (pot is 1125 after his bet). Calling 375 requires ~25% equity (375 / (1125+375) = 25%). On this A♣5♥5♦ board my hand has essentially no showdown value and only remote runner-runner outs; versus Claude’s reasonable cutoff-calling range (lots of A-x, pocket 5s, some mid pairs and broadways) my equity is well under the needed 25%. Raising to 750 as a bluff is technically possible but expensive and unlikely to fold out enough of his value-heavy range — it bloats the pot early in the tournament with air. I’ll preserve my deep stack and fold this marginal hand.

I'm UTG in a 6-max game with Ah6h — a suited ace is a playable open from early position. With ~97 BB effective I want to be proactive: open-raising gives fold equity, narrows the field, and builds a pot I can play postflop with nut-flush and top-pair potential. Standard sizing here is ~3x the BB to 300 (min raise is 200 but that's too small and invites multiway action/squeezes). I will raise to 300. If I face a large 3-bet I will re-evaluate (often fold to very large 3-bets, consider calling small ones to leverage postflop playability).
K2s is too weak to call a UTG 3x raise from middle position. Even with the suited aspect, K2 has poor playability and we're dominated by many Kx hands in villain's range. Easy fold.

I am holding 7h 5c in the Cutoff. This is a very weak hand. Minni opened from UTG to 300 chips. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, and 75 offsuit has very little equity or playability against such a range. There is no reason to defend or bluff here; I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3bb UTG open, T2 suited is too weak to defend even on the button: it’s frequently dominated when we make a pair, has poor playability versus UTG’s stronger range, and doesn’t have the blocker value to justify a 3-bet bluff. With deep stacks, preserving chips and avoiding dominated spots is best here.

I am holding 8h 5s in the Small Blind. This is a statistically weak hand (trash). The action was opened by Minni from Under the Gun (UTG), which typically signifies a strong range of hands. Playing a weak hand out of position against a strong UTG range is a losing strategy. Even though the raise size is standard (3bb), my equity realization will be poor. Folding is the correct decision.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 from Minni, with 9s2d offsuit in the big blind. This is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity against a likely tight UTG range, and I'll be out of position postflop. Pot odds of 200 to call into 450 offer about 3:1, but my equity is insufficient (roughly 25-30% vs. UTG range), and implied odds are minimal with this hand. Early in the tournament (hand 2/150), better to fold and wait for better spots to preserve stack.
95 offsuit in UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and I'm in the earliest position with 5 players to act behind me. No reason to get involved here.

Hand 3, Q9o in Middle Position. In a 6-max tournament structure with 100BB stacks, Q9o is generally outside of a standard opening range from this position. While it has some high-card value, its playability post-flop is poor, especially with aggressive players like Minni remaining in the blinds. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

CO spot but Kh2o is too weak to profitably open into three players still to act (BTN/SB/BB), especially with an active-looking BB. It has poor playability when called and doesn’t realize equity well postflop. Early in the set, I’d rather pass on this marginal steal and avoid getting 3-bet or dragged into a dominated K-high pot.

I am on the Button with 6c 7s (offsuit connectors). The action has folded to me. While the Button allows for a wide opening range to steal the blinds, 67o is quite weak and near the bottom of a standard opening range. Additionally, the player in the Big Blind (Minni) has been active in the first two hands, suggesting they may defend or 3-bet aggressively. Playing a weak hand like 67o against an aggressive opponent, even with position, is not ideal deep-stacked early in the tournament. Therefore, folding is the prudent decision.

Preflop in SB with Th3s offsuit, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. All players folded to me, but raising light risks a defend from BB Minni who has been aggressive (raised first two hands). Limping is poor as it plays OOP with a weak hand postflop. Best to fold the SB 50 and preserve stack for better opportunities in early tournament with deep stacks (~100BB).

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position, which is the first to act pre-flop. Holding Queen-Six offsuit (Q6o) is extremely weak for this position in a 6-max game. This hand has very little post-flop playability and is easily dominated by the ranges of opponents behind me. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

We’re in middle position with Q5 offsuit, which is too weak to open in a 6-max lineup and plays poorly postflop (dominated by most Qx calls/3-bets). Limping is also -EV because we invite isolation and end up in tough multiway pots with a dominated hand. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with J3 offsuit, which is a statistically very weak hand. Even though two players have folded, I still have the Button and the Blinds to get through. J3o does not have enough equity or playability to justify an open raise to steal the blinds, nor does it warrant a limp. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket deuces on the button in an unopened pot after three folds. This is a standard raising spot in late position with deep stacks. We can steal the blinds frequently, and if called, we have excellent position to realize our equity. Small pairs play well postflop from the button, with set-mining potential and fold equity preflop. Raising to 2.5x the big blind (250 total) balances steal and value, building the pot while keeping it manageable.

I have Js9d in the small blind facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, I must call 200). Calling is the best play: J9o has reasonable equity and postflop playability against a wide button opening range, and the pot odds (calling 200 into 400 -> ~3:1) justify seeing a flop. A 3-bet OOP with this marginal hand is too thin and will often put me in tough spots (facing a 4-bet or being OOP against two players). I’ll call to 250 total, see the flop, and proceed cautiously OOP — continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry A/K-high boards, and avoid bloating the pot without a clear equity advantage.

83 suited is a very weak hand. Even getting 4:1 odds to close the action, this hand has poor playability - no straight potential, weak high card, and will often be dominated. Better to save the 150 chips and wait for a better spot.

Check. I have middle pair (9s) with a decent Jack kicker but I’m out of position and the board (Tc 9c 4h) has two clubs and a ten that beat me. Pot control is important — I want to realize equity vs a wide button c-bet without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. If Elon bets a normal c-bet (small/medium, ~1/3–1/2 pot ≈ 200–300) I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn (folding to large polarizing raises or overbets). If he checks back I keep the pot small and can see a free turn card. I’ll avoid bluffing or check-raising here with only middle pair and no club draw.

Preflop raise with 22 from button was standard for steal and playability. Flop 4h Tc 9c gives me an underpair, which is still ahead of much of Minni's SB calling range (Ax, broadway, suited connectors that missed). Board has club flush draw and some straight draw potential, so betting for thin value, protection against overcards and draws, and to deny equity. Minni checked, giving me initiative. Half-pot bet of 300 balances value and bluffs from our range, manageable deepstacked. Position allows control postflop.

I have Js9d on 4h Tc 9c — middle pair with a decent Jack kicker, no club draw, out of position facing a small c-bet. Elon’s bet is 300 into a 900 pot (~1/3 pot), which offers good odds to call (I need roughly ~25% equity). Button’s continuation range is wide here (Tx, clubs, overcards, bluffs), and many of those combos I beat or can outplay on later streets. Raising OOP with a marginal pair is too thin — it bloats the pot and puts me in tough spots against value hands and polarized 3-bets. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and re-evaluate on the turn: fold to a large polarized raise or overbet (which strongly indicates clubs/sets/two-pair), call small/medium turn bets on safe cards, and look to value-bet or fold on the river depending on action and runout.

Check. I have J♠9♦ — middle pair with a decent jack kicker, out of position. The turn 2♠ is a blank versus Elon's wide button-opening and small flop c-bet range (Tx, club draws, overcards, some bluffs). Betting OOP with a marginal one-pair hand is too thin: it bloats the pot, invites check-raises from clubs/Tx/two-pair, and often gets value‑mined by better hands. Checking controls the pot (current pot 1,200), lets me realize equity, and keeps weaker bluffs in his range. Plan: if Elon checks back we go to the river cheaply; if he bets the river I’ll call small/medium bets for value or fold to large/polarized overbets that strongly represent clubs or two‑pair/set.

Turn card 2s gives us trips (222) on a board 4h Tc 9c 2s, turning our pocket deuces into a monster hand. Minni's check after calling flop suggests she may have a marginal hand like an overpair (JJ-QQ, AK/AQ), club flush draw, straight draw (e.g., JT, QJ), or weaker pairs. The board has flush and straight draw possibilities, so we need to bet for value against worse made hands and draws while protecting our hand. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing her to bluff river. With position and deep stacks, a pot-sized bet of 1200 extracts maximum value from her range without overbetting, as she has shown strength by calling flop but weakness by checking turn. This size pressures draws and gets calls from overpairs/top pair.

Fold. I’m out of position with second pair (9s) and a decent jack kicker, but Elon’s line (button open, small flop c-bet, then a pot-sized turn bet of 1,200 into the 1,200 pot) is polarizing and weighted to clear value — tens, nines, sets and strong club combos — or a polarized bluff package. Calling 1,200 into the 2,400 pot requires ~33% equity. My hand has very few clean outs and is frequently behind against that perceived range. A raise is too thin and would either get called by better hands or fold out only worse hands, so it’s not +EV. Preserving ~9,350 chips and folding to this large turn bet is the correct, disciplined play.

UTG 6-handed with no antes, opening ranges should be fairly tight. K6s is too dominated by stronger Kx continues (KJ+/KQ/AK) and will realize equity poorly out of position versus multiple players. The EV of a thin open isn’t worth the variance/risk at 100bb, so I’ll pass and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in Middle Position (Hijack) at a 6-max table with Queen-Six suited. UTG has folded. While Q6s is a playable hand from late position (Cutoff/Button), it is generally considered too loose to open from MP in a standard solid strategy, especially early in a tournament structure where stack preservation is important. There are four players left to act, increasing the likelihood that someone wakes up with a dominating hand (AQ, KQ, QJs) or a strong pair. Folding is the disciplined decision here to avoid being out of position with a marginal hand.

Cutoff position with folds from UTG and MP, giving us a strong positional advantage. QTs (noting QsTh is offsuit but still QTo, a playable hand here: broadway potential, straight possibilities, queen high blocker). Standard open-raise from this spot to build pot and take initiative. Sizing to 3x BB (300 total) is standard for deep-stack tournament play, balancing value and fold equity against blinds. No limpers, blinds unopened. Will c-bet flops frequently and use position postflop.

Button with A♥3♠ vs a cutoff open to 300. This is a playable, suited ace with great positional leverage. Calling 300 keeps my range wide, lets me use position to realize equity and apply pressure post‑flop, and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal hand. A tiny 3‑bet to 500 is too weak; a larger 3‑bet (~900+) could be used only with a read that Elon folds often to 3‑bets. I’ll call 300 and play the flop in position.

75s in SB facing a raise and a call. Getting 3:1 pot odds (250 to win 750), but I'll be out of position for the entire hand. 75s is a marginal speculative hand that doesn't play well OOP multiway. The suited nature gives some flush potential but the hand is weak overall. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 5s, which is a very weak starting hand. Elon raised from the Cutoff to 300 and Minni called from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 into a pot of 750), 85o plays very poorly multi-way and out of position. I have already seen Elon and Minni being fairly active in previous hands. Conserving my stack and waiting for better cards or a more favorable position is the better long-term strategy in this high roller tournament.

Flop Td 6h As hits me with middle pair (Tens) and Queen kicker, plus backdoor spade flush draw. As preflop raiser from cutoff vs button caller, I have strong range advantage on this Ace-high board, which connects well with my CO opening range (Ax, broadways, pairs). Minni's button calling range includes Ax (worse kickers possible), floats, weaker pairs, straight/straight draws (e.g., KJ, 87). No strong draws completed (straight draws open-ended like KJ gutshot, wheel incomplete). C-bet for thin value/protection, deny equity to draws/floats, build pot with showdown value. Half-pot sizing (400 into 750) is standard, offers good fold equity while getting value from worse.

Heads-up on the button with A♥3♠ on Td-6h-As. This is top pair but a very weak kicker. Elon opened cutoff preflop and now fires a relatively small 400 c-bet — consistent with a wide/probing c-bet range that includes worse A-x, tens, air, and some better Axs (AK/AQ). With position and a hand that wants to control the pot against better Aces, I’ll call the 400. Calling keeps worse Aces and pocket Tens in, preserves my positional advantage, and lets me reevaluate on the turn — I can extract value or fold to significant turn/river aggression. A raise here risks building a bigger pot where my weak kicker is frequently dominated and could induce costly 3-bets.

On the turn, the 9d completes possible straights (e.g., 78) and adds diamond flush draw equity to the board (Td 9d with 6h As rainbow-ish but now two diamonds). My QT holds middle pair (Tens) with Queen kicker, solid showdown value against her BTN calling range (weaker Ax, worse Tx, missed draws, floats). However, as the preflop raiser acting OOP heads-up (first to act postflop since blinds folded pre), pot control is key with medium strength vulnerable to Ax, sets, two-pair combos (T9, A9, A6, T6), and improved draws. Flop c-bet was for thin value/protection/denial; now board worsens without improving me, so checking realizes equity, induces bluffs from missed draws (e.g., diamond flush draws without flush, gutshots), and avoids bloating pot OOP where rivers could be scary (river A, flush, straight). If she bets small/medium, callable with decent kicker blocker to QJ/KQ; large bet, can fold. Deep stacks (98BB eff), tournament stage early (hand 5/150), prioritize not spewing OOP. No need to bet thin as her range calls flop with too many Ax/Tx for value and defends wide.

I have top pair (A♣3♠) but a very weak kicker. Elon’s line — a small flop c‑bet then checking the turn — looks like weakness or pot‑control rather than a strong locked Ace, and the 9 on the turn doesn’t create many new draws. Betting here would bloat the pot and commit me too often versus better A‑x or sets that dominate my kicker; checking keeps worse A‑x and pocket Tens in, controls pot size, and lets me reevaluate on the river with position. I’ll check and see the river, extracting value only when appropriate or folding to clear, large aggression.

River 7h completes straight draws (e.g., 78, 87 for T9876) but misses heart and diamond flushes (board has two hearts, two diamonds). I hold middle pair Tens with Queen kicker (blocks QJ, KQ overs), which has showdown value against her BTN calling range including weaker Ax (AJ-AQ), worse Tx, missed draws, and floats. As OOP preflop raiser who c-bet flop then checked turn for pot control, my range is polarized: strong hands (sets, two-pair, Ax) or bluffs/draws. Checking river realizes equity safely, induces folds from pure air while getting value from worse pairs/draws via showdown. Value betting thin is risky as better Ax/two-pair/straights call/raise, and pot odds poor deep-stacked (98BB eff, early tournament hand 5/150). Bluffing inefficient OOP vs her capped range after calling flop c-bet. Pot control prioritizes not bloating pot with medium strength vulnerable to Ax, T9suited, A9, etc. Check behind maximizes EV long-term.

Check. I have top pair (A) but a very weak 3‑kicker on Td‑6h‑As‑9d‑7h. Elon’s line — cutoff open, small flop c‑bet, then check on turn and river — looks like a lot of marginal A‑x, missed draws, and pot‑control hands rather than a locked two‑pair/set. There are many hands in his range that beat me (better A‑x, two‑pair/sets like TT/66/99, and occasional 8x straights), and a thin value bet would only get called by a very small subset of worse hands. Betting would bloat the pot and risk getting raised into a losing spot with a weak kicker. Pot control and realizing equity is the safer, +EV line here — check to showdown.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Deuce offsuit (Tc 2s), which is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. Given the poor position (acting first pre-flop with 5 players behind) and the lack of playability or value in the hand, the only correct strategic move is to fold immediately. I should preserve my chips for better spots.

Preflop in middle position with 4s3d offsuit, a weak uncoordinated hand with poor playability and low equity against typical ranges. Standard fold to avoid difficult postflop spots with deep stacks in a tournament. No reads or dynamics justify entering the pot.

Cutoff open is the right plan. T♠3♠ is marginal but suited and playable in position — in a 6-max game I want a fairly wide stealing range from cutoff. I don’t want to limp and invite multiway callers, so I’ll open to a standard 3xBB sizing to seize the initiative, gain fold equity against the button and blinds, and keep the pot manageable with ~102 BB effective stacks. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold or re-evaluate; if called I’ll use position to see flops and apply pressure on favorable textures.

J9o on the button facing Minni's raise to 300. I have position and a playable hand. Minni has been active (raised in hands 1, 2, and now 6). With position, I can call and see a flop. J9o isn't strong enough to 3-bet here but plays well postflop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine of diamonds, a strong suited hand. The Cutoff (Minni) has been very active in previous hands, suggesting a wide opening range. The Button (Claude) has called the raise. While a 3-bet squeeze is a viable aggressive play here to take control of the pot, calling the 250-chip raise is a solid, lower-variance play that allows me to see a flop with a hand that plays well in multi-way pots. Being out of position for the remainder of the hand is a disadvantage, but the pot odds (calling 250 to win a pot of 750) are favorable for a suited Ace.

We’re getting an excellent price (200 to win a 1000 pot) and QTs plays well multiway with strong straight/flush potential. A squeeze 3-bet would need to be large (around 1400–1600) and risks getting called in multiple spots, leaving us out of position in a bloated pot with a hand that’s strong but not premium. Calling keeps the pot manageable while realizing equity well on many flops.

On this flop of Qc-6c-2d, my Ace-high hand has limited value, though the Ace of diamonds gives me a backdoor nut flush draw. In a four-way pot, it is standard to check to the pre-flop raiser (Minni) to see how the action unfolds. I am out of position against most of the field, so playing cautiously here is the most prudent strategy. I will check and evaluate based on subsequent bets.

Multiway pot and we flopped top pair (Q) with a medium kicker on a somewhat dynamic board (club draws). Checking gives too many free cards and lets CO/Button realize equity cheaply. A small lead accomplishes (1) thin value vs worse Qx/6x and club draws, (2) some protection vs random overcards with a club, while (3) keeping the pot manageable OOP and not forcing ourselves into a big pot when better Qx is possible. If we face a big raise multiway, we can comfortably tighten up and fold; versus a call we can re-evaluate turns (especially clubs/overcards).

I opened from the cutoff with T♠3♠ and completely missed the Q♣6♣2♦ flop. FiveTwo's small bet (400 into a 1600 pot) gives me ~20% break-even equity to continue and is cheap to call with position. I have no pair and only backdoor spade outs, so a raise would be thin and likely to create multiway trouble; folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call 400 to float and use position to re-evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve or can credibly take it away.

I have J9o with just the 9c on a Qc6c2d board. I completely missed - no pair, no draw except a very weak backdoor flush draw with one club. FiveTwo bet 400 and Minni called, showing strength. I'm getting 6:1 but my hand has almost no equity here. Easy fold.

I have Ace-Nine of diamonds on a Queen-Six-Deuce board with two clubs. I checked the flop and now face a 400-chip bet from FiveTwo (Big Blind) and a call from the pre-flop raiser (Minni). My hand is currently just Ace-high with a very weak backdoor flush draw. In a multi-way pot, this hand has almost no showdown value and poor equity against a range that is betting and calling on this texture. I am out of position and would likely need to hit an Ace or specific runner-runner diamonds to win, which is not worth the 400-chip investment into a 2000-chip pot given the strength shown by my opponents. I will fold.

Turn pairs the queen, giving us trips (QQQ) with a decent kicker. Versus Minni’s flop calling range (Qx, 6x, pocket pairs, club draws), we should keep betting for value and to charge draws, while not bloating the pot so much that we only get action from better Qx/full houses. A medium sizing accomplishes this and sets up a river decision cleanly if called; if we face a big raise, we can heavily discount bluffs on this texture and proceed cautiously.

Board is Qc 6c 2d Qh and my hand is Ts 3s — I have not connected. With two spades in my hand and zero on board a flush is impossible; I only have 6 outs (three tens + three threes) to pair on the river, ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity. The pot is 3100 and the bet is 1100, so the break-even equity to call is 1100/(3100+1100) ≈ 26%. My raw equity is well below break-even. FiveTwo’s line (small flop raise then a turn bet after the board pairs) strongly suggests a queen, a set, or another strong holding — this is not a spot to chase. A bluff-raise is high variance and unlikely to fold trips/sets; calling is a negative‑EV float given the pot odds and my lack of redraws. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 84o, an offsuit, unconnected trash hand. Standard poker fundamentals dictate folding such a weak holding from early position to avoid playing out of position postflop with poor equity and playability. No reads from history suggest otherwise for this spot. Deep stacks mean discipline is key to maximize EV over 150 hands.

Fold. 5♦2♠ is the worst kind of starting hand: offsuit, unconnected and easily dominated. I’m in middle/early position with players to act behind (button, SB, BB), so calling 100 into a 150 pot would put me out of position in likely multiway action for poor equity and weak pot odds. A min-raise here is a poor bluff — I have no blockers and risk a 3-bet. With a healthy ~95bb stack I can wait for a much better spot. Fold and preserve chips for a playable hand.

I have 5h8h in cutoff with two folds ahead. Suited one-gapper has some playability. With position and fold equity against the blinds and button, this is a decent spot for a standard open raise to steal. Limping would be weak and folding is too tight from this position.

I am holding Jack-Six offsuit on the button. While I have position on the pre-flop raiser (Claude), J6o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop against a raise from the cutoff. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Claude is opening from the cutoff and we’re in the small blind with 96o—an offsuit, low-connectivity hand that’s frequently dominated and plays poorly out of position. Even with a reasonable price (200 to win 400), calling sets us up to realize equity badly OOP, and we also risk the big blind squeezing, forcing us into awkward spots. We have no relevant blockers to justify a light 3-bet bluff here. Best is to conserve chips and wait for a better defend candidate.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit (KJo), facing a standard 2.5x open raise from the Cutoff (Claude). The Small Blind has folded. The pot odds are compelling: I need to call 150 chips to win a total pot of 550, requiring only about 27% equity. KJo is a strong broadway hand that plays well against a typically wide Cutoff opening range. While a 3-bet is an option to assert dominance, it builds a large pot out of position with a hand that can be easily dominated by 4-betting ranges (AK, QQ+). Flat calling is the most standard and EV-positive play to realize equity while keeping the pot manageable. I will call the 150 difference.

I am out of position on an A-A-4 rainbow flop with King-high (KJo). This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's (Claude) range, as they hold all the strong Ax and pocket pairs. Leading out (donk betting) accomplishes little here. My strategy is to check to the aggressor. If they check back, I realize equity; if they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, though folding is likely the prudent response to significant aggression.

Board is 4d Ah Ac - extremely dry and paired. As the preflop raiser, I have significant range advantage here. Pro checked from BB, unlikely to have an ace (would often 3-bet strong aces). This is a textbook c-bet spot - I can represent the ace credibly and take this down often. Small sizing works on this dry texture.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit on a board of 4d-Ah-Ac. The Cutoff c-bets 175 into 550 (approx 1/3 pot). While the price is attractive (needing ~19% equity), this board texture heavily favors the aggressor's range, which includes all strong Aces. My hand is currently just King-high with no backdoor flush draws (rainbow board) or straight draws. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize equity even if I am currently ahead of a bluff. Floating without backdoors is generally -EV in this spot. I will fold to preserve my stack.

UTG with Jh3d is a very weak, unsuited, unconnected hand with poor playability. In early position you should be tight — this hand will be dominated or forced out-of-position postflop much of the time. Calling 100 into a 150 pot to play multiway OOP is negative EV, and an open-raise bluff from UTG is too risky here. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot.
AKo is a premium hand in middle position. With Minni already folded, I should open with a standard raise of 3x BB to build the pot and take initiative. This is a mandatory raise spot.

I am in the cutoff position holding Td 4c. This is a very weak hand to play against an open raise from middle position (Claude). Calling or raising with such a marginal holding would likely lead to losing chips in the long run. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand.

Claude’s 3x open from middle is likely fairly wide. On the button with position, T9o is a playable flat: we realize equity well, can float/attack many flops, and we avoid inflating the pot with an offsuit hand that doesn’t want to face a 4-bet. A 3-bet here is possible, but T9o (offsuit) is a bit thin as a bluff candidate versus a 3x open, and calling keeps his weaker holdings in while we keep positional advantage.

I hold 3c 2s (32 offsuit) in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from Middle Position and a call from the Button, I am out of position against two players with stronger ranges. Even with 4:1 pot odds, the equity realization of this hand from the Small Blind is extremely poor. There is no strategic justification for entering this pot. Folding is the only correct play to preserve chips.

Ten-Four offsuit is a speculative trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high cards. Facing a middle position raise from Claude (who has been active) and a button call from FiveTwo, going multiway OOP with this hand is -EV. Pot odds are about 3:1 (200 to call into 950 post-call), but equity realization is poor postflop without position. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this early tournament stage.

I have AKo on a 6h4d4h board - dry and paired. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should c-bet to continue my story. This board doesn't favor caller's range much, and my AK high is likely best. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot puts pressure while not over-committing with just overcards.

Claude’s small 1/4-pot c-bet on a paired, low board is often a range/protection bet with lots of unpaired overcards and some small pairs. With Th9d we have two overcards to the middle of his range, backdoor straight potential, and strong positional leverage to take the pot away on many turns if he checks. Getting ~5:1 immediate odds means we don’t need much raw equity, and calling keeps his range wide while avoiding a thin bluff-raise that can get floated/3-bet by better.

Plan: call now; if he checks many turns we can stab, and if we improve with a T/9 we can often get to showdown or value-bet thinly depending on runout/action.

Turn brings 5d completing some straight draws (23, 78). I have AK high with no pair on this 6-4-4-5 board. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet - could have a 4, pocket pair, or floated. My hand has showdown value but betting again risks only getting called by better. Check to control pot size and reassess on river.

Claude’s turn check after a small flop c-bet is often a give-up with overcards / weak pairs. The 5d also gives us a real semi-bluff candidate: we picked up a 1-card diamond flush draw (10 outs) plus some extra ways to win by improving to a pair. In position, betting pressures his A-high/K-high hands and denies equity, while still having solid outs when called. I’ll use a medium size that’s big enough to generate folds but not so big that a rare check-raise costs too much. Plan: barrel most diamond rivers; slow down on pairing/very bad rivers if called.

I have AK high on a 6h4d4h5d board - no pair. When I checked the turn showing weakness, FiveTwo bet 700 (56% pot). Their range for calling flop and betting turn includes: pocket pairs (55 hit trips, 66 hit full house, 77+ beat me), any 4x, suited connectors like 78 made a straight. My AK is just a bluff-catcher with ~13% to improve. Getting 2.8:1 isn't enough to continue with such a marginal holding out of position. Disciplined fold.

KQo is a strong hand from UTG. Standard open raise to 250-300 is appropriate. This hand plays well and can make strong top pairs. Opening to 250 (2.5x) to get value while not bloating pot too much from early position.

I am in a middle position with King-Four offsuit (K4o). Claude has opened with a raise from UTG (Under The Gun) to 2.5 times the big blind. K4o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by Claude's UTG opening range, which likely consists of strong pairs and broadway cards. Calling or raising with such a marginal hand is negative expected value in the long run, especially against an early position raiser. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

Facing an UTG open to 2.5bb, J3s is too weak and dominated to defend profitably even in position. Calling needs solid playability and implied odds; here we’ll often flop weak pairs or dominated jacks and lose bigger pots. A 3-bet bluff also isn’t ideal versus a strong UTG range with this blocker profile. Best decision is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold J2o on the Button, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise to 250 from the UTG player (Claude), who represents a strong range opening from early position, I cannot profitably call or raise. The hand has poor playability and is easily dominated. Despite having the position, the card strength is insufficient to contest the pot. The disciplined and standard play is to fold.

Facing a raise to 250 from UTG Claude, who acts early and likely has a strong range. My K♦5♦ is suited but a weak hand with poor postflop playability out of position in the SB. Pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 into 400), but equity against a tight UTG range is insufficient, and multiway pots OOP are unprofitable. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this deepstack tournament.

I’m in the big blind with 6♥4♦ facing an UTG open to 250. I’ve already posted 100 and would need to call 150 into a 400 pot (≈3.7:1), but this is an early-position opener so his range is relatively strong. 6-4 offsuit is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position — it has little post‑flop playability and I’ll often be forced to fold to C‑bets or get into marginal, losing spots. A min‑3bet bluff here is thin against an UTG opener and needlessly inflates the pot with a weak holding. With healthy stacks and many hands left, preserve chips and avoid marginal OOP calls — fold.

I am UTG with a pocket pair of fives. In a 6-handed game, this is a marginal but playable hand to open with. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300) allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or set up a set-mining opportunity if called. Folding is an alternative, but early in the tournament, I prefer to test the table and establish an active image.

Flash opens UTG to 3bb in a 6-max game, which should be a relatively strong range. 8-3 offsuit from middle position plays poorly (dominated, low connectivity, bad reverse implied odds) and isn’t a profitable defend versus this sizing and position. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold King-Jack offsuit in the Cutoff. While this is a reasonable hand to open with, facing a 3x raise from an Under-The-Gun (UTG) player indicates a strong range (likely AK, AQ, TT+, etc.) that dominates KJo. Additionally, there are active players left to act behind me (Button, SB, BB). Calling risks entering a dominated pot or getting squeezed. Folding is the disciplined and standard play against an UTG open in this position.

T2o is a trash hand with poor playability, offsuit and low connectivity. Facing an UTG raise to 3bb from Flash, who has folded frequently in prior hands but represents strength from early position. Button position offers some flexibility, but blinds (Minni and Claude) yet to act could squeeze or call wide. Deep stacks (nearly 100bb), early in tournament (hand 10/150), no need to defend this weak holding. Folding preserves stack for premium spots.

Facing an UTG open to 300 with K♦6♦ in the small blind — a marginal suited king. Calling 250 (300 total) leaves me out of position versus the opener (and the big blind) with a hand that’s often dominated and has poor postflop playability. A 3-bet bluff is too thin here and invites 4-bets or difficult decisions. Fold and preserve the stack for a better spot.

AQo in the BB vs UTG open of 300. This is a premium hand that plays well as a 3-bet for value. Flash opened from UTG which typically represents a tighter range, but AQo is still strong enough to 3-bet. Standard 3-bet sizing would be around 3x the open, so 900-1000. This builds the pot with a strong hand and puts pressure on Flash.

I have pocket fives and opened from UTG. Claude, in the Big Blind, has 3-bet to 900. I need to call 600 more into a pot that will be 1850. I have position on Claude for the remainder of the hand. Given the stack depths (approx 90bb effective), I have sufficient implied odds to call and 'set-mine'. If I hit a five on the flop, I am in a strong position to win a large pot. If I miss, I can evaluate based on Claude's post-flop aggression and the board texture, utilizing my positional advantage.

I flopped TPTK (top pair top kicker) with AQ on A-9-3 with two diamonds. This is an excellent flop for my 3-bet range. I should c-bet for value and to charge the diamond flush draw. Betting around 60% pot (~1100) is standard here to get value from worse aces, pairs, and draws.

I currently hold a pair of fives on an Ace-high flop after calling a pre-flop 3-bet. Claude's lead of 1100 into a 1850 pot (now 2950) shows significant strength, especially given he 3-bet from the Big Blind. My hand is extremely vulnerable to any Ace, Nine, or higher pocket pair, all of which are firmly in Claude's 3-betting range. I have no significant draws and only two outs to improve to a set. Continuing here would be a low-equity play against a range that likely has me crushed, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

6-handed from UTG I want a fairly tight opening range because I’ll be out of position versus multiple players. Q4s is too weak and too often dominated when I make a queen, and it doesn’t realize equity well without initiative. With plenty of playable hands later and no strong exploit suggesting I should widen UTG, folding preserves stack EV.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit in middle position, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With players yet to act behind me and no chips invested in the pot other than the ante (if any, though none listed here, just blinds), the only correct strategic play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

Preflop in cutoff position after UTG and MP folds. Holding Ah3h, a suited ace with good position on the button (Minni), SB (Claude), and BB (Flash). This hand has strong playability: nut flush potential, ace high blocker, and position for postflop control. Standard play is to raise for initiative and fold equity against blinds. Raising to 3x BB (300) is sized appropriately based on history of opens (250-300), builds pot while maintaining fold equity. Deep stacks (96bb effective), so value in isolating or stealing the 150 dead money.

On the button with K♥5♥ facing a standard cutoff open to 300 (pot 450). K5s is playable in position: it has a suit, king-blocker value and good postflop playability against a wide CO opening range. A small raise to 500 is a marginal isolation attempt — it’s unlikely to fold out the blinds reliably and could get me into a bloated pot out of position or face a 3-bet. With ~93bb effective I prefer to use position and see a flop, then apply pressure on favorable boards or fold to heavy resistance. Call 300 to keep the pot controllable and leverage position postflop.
3s4h is a weak offsuit hand. I'm out of position against two players who have shown interest. Even with 3:1 pot odds, this hand plays poorly multiway OOP. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Six of Spades. Elon (Cutoff) raised to 300 and Minni (Button) called. I need to put in 200 more to see a flop. While the pot odds are roughly 3.75 to 1, T6 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially when out of position against two opponents. It is easily dominated by many hands in the ranges of a Cutoff raiser and a Button caller. Saving the 200 chips is the more prudent play here.

Heads-up on the flop after my preflop raise from CO, which Minni called from the button. Board is 8d-3d-5d, extremely draw-heavy with flush and straight draws. My Ah3h gives me bottom pair with top kicker and the nut flush blocker (Ah), providing decent showdown value and some protection against diamond flush draws. However, OOP with a vulnerable holding on this texture, I prefer to check for pot control and to realize equity by inducing bluffs from Minni's wide calling range (suited connectors, broadways, Ax, pairs). Betting risks folding out better hands or getting raised off equity by draws. Checking balances my range as preflop raiser and allows me to see Minni's action; I can call small bets with decent equity or fold to aggression while preserving stack deep (93bb effective). Long-term EV positive in tournament by avoiding spewy plays.

I have K♥5♥ on 8♦-3♦-5♦: I’ve hit a middle/third pair (pair of 5s) with a good kicker, but the flop is very wet — three diamonds. Elon opened from the cutoff and his range contains a lot of diamond combos that make a flush here, plus higher pairs/sets that beat me. With ~90bb effective and a 750 pot, betting for protection/value is unattractive: it won’t fold many diamond combos and will bloat the pot against hands that currently beat me. Priority is pot control and information in position. I’ll check and see how he reacts — call small probe bets for pot odds and to keep weaker hands/air in, but fold to sizeable aggression. If checked through, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (bet non-diamond turn cards for value/protection).

Heads-up on the turn after mutual flop checks on a diamond-heavy flop (8d3d5d). My Ah3h gives bottom pair (3s) with nut flush blocker (Ah), decent showdown value but vulnerable now with Qd completing obvious flushes (any diamond in opponent's range beats me, board nut flush is Q-high). Minni called my CO raise OOP on flop as BTN (wide range: suited broadways, connectors, Adx, pairs, diamond draws suited gappers). Her flop check-behind suggests no strong made hand (trips/set unlikely to check, strong draws might c-bet) but could be weak pair, missed draw (non-diamond flush/straight draw), or trap. Turn Qd is a flush-completer and range card (Qx, AKQ suited); betting risks getting called by flushes/better or raised off equity. OOP, prefer pot control: check to realize equity cheaply, induce bluffs from missed draws (e.g., JdTd, KdJd improved to flush? Wait no, she checks suggest not holding strong diamond pre). Call medium bets with blocker equity (blocks nuts), fold to big aggression. Deep stacks (93bb eff), no need to bloat pot with marginal hand; long-term +EV by avoiding spew on scary texture while balancing check-raise range (sets unlikely). If she checks behind, cheap river showdown; happy to showdown pair vs bluffs/air.

Check. I have K♥5♥ — third pair (pair of 5s) with a decent kicker, but the turn Q♦ makes the board 8♦-3♦-5♦-Q♦ (four diamonds). That drastically reduces the value of a protection bet: many hands in Elon’s CO opening/continuation range contain diamonds (A♦, K♦, Q♦, J♦, etc.) or paired hands that already beat me, so a bet will rarely fold enough equity and will simply bloat the pot when I’m behind. I have showdown value, so I want to control the pot and gather information in position. Plan: check and call small probe bets (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ 250) since those give reasonable pot odds and keep worse hands in; fold to sizeable aggression that represents completed flushes/top pairs/sets. If checked through, re-evaluate on the river and target betting small for thin value on non-diamond blank rivers or folding to big action on diamond/high cards.

River 6h is a relatively blank card for most ranges, pairing no board cards and not completing obvious draws beyond what's already there (flush already live on turn). Heads-up OOP vs Minni (BTN caller of my CO raise), pot 750, my Ah3h gives bottom pair + nut flush blocker (blocks Adx nuts), solid showdown value against her wide range that check-backed flop (8d3d5d: unlikely strong made hands like sets/overpairs check behind as preflop caller; more weak pairs, busted non-diamond draws, floaty broadways). Turn Qd flush-completer checked through both ways, capping her value somewhat (strong Qx or early flush likely to bet one street). Betting risks polarizing to value (rare, as better hands bet turn/river) or bluff (but OOP check on river spews if called by flushes/pairs); better to check for pot control, realize equity cheaply vs her air/bluffs/weak showdown (e.g., AQo missed, 77, diamond backdoor misses). If she bets small-medium, hero-call with blocker + pair equity vs polarized range; fold to huge overbets representing nuts. Deep stacks (~93bb eff), no need to bloat; +EV long-term by showdowning marginal pair on scary diamond board while balancing check range. Early tournament, preserve stack for better spots.

River (8♦-3♦-5♦-Q♦-6♥). I hold K♥5♥ — third pair (pair of 5s) with a good kicker. The turn completed a lot of diamond flushes, so my hand is vulnerable to any diamond in the CO opener’s range, but the river 6♥ is a non-diamond blank that can induce calls from worse hands (Kx, A-high, small pairs like 3x, missed draws). Pot is 750; betting a small amount (~1/3 pot) extracts thin value from these worse hands while keeping risk limited. If Elon has a flush/top pair he can still check-raise, in which case I can fold — I’m not committed. Given stack size and the line so far (passive checks), I’ll make a small value bet to fold pure air and get called by marginal hands. Bet 250 chips.

River 6h is a blank that doesn't change much, pairing nothing and not completing new draws. Pot 750, checked to Minni who bets small 250 (1/3 pot) as BTN vs my OOP check. My Ah3h holds bottom pair with nut flush blocker on scary diamond board (Q-high flush possible). Her range after check-checking flop (8d3d5d) and turn (Qd): unlikely nuts (strong flushes/sets would bet earlier streets for value/protection); more thin value (weak Qx, 8x, pairs + SDV), bluffs (busted draws like JdTd, KJs missed, AQo float), or weak showdown (77, 99, ATs). Small bet size suggests thin value or bluff catcher call station play. Excellent pot odds (250 into 1000 = ~20% needed); my pair + blocker has strong equity vs polarized range (beats all bluffs/air/weak pairs, loses to better made hands but those likely bigger bets). Hero-call preserves balance (I check-call marginals), realizes equity cheaply deep-stacked (~93bb eff). Folding spews value vs frequent bluffs from her capping range; raising too thin without fold equity. Standard +EV call in tournament spot exploiting wide check-back range.

I am in UTG position with Kh 2h. While suited Kings have some potential for flushes, the Deuce kicker is extremely weak. Opening from early position with such a weak kicker is generally a losing play in the long run because I will often be dominated if called, and I am out of position against the rest of the table. Standard strategy dictates folding K2s from UTG.

Preflop, UTG (Pro) folded, I'm in middle position with KTo offsuit in a 6-handed game at 50/100 blinds. KTo is a playable hand from this spot, with decent high-card strength and potential for postflop playability. No prior action except blinds, so standard open-raise to 3x BB (300 total) to build the pot, take initiative, and narrow the field. My stack is deep (91BB), opponents deep, early in tournament (hand 12/150), so aggression is key to maximize stack growth. Consistent with my previous opening sizes (250-300). Limping or folding too passive.

Fold. K♦5♥ is a very weak, easily dominated hand vs an early/middle opener to 300. Calling 300 commits a large chunk of equity with poor playability and a bad kicker; a light 3‑bet is also unattractive with players behind who can call or squeeze. With ~100BB effective stacks I’d rather preserve chips for better late‑position steals or stronger hands, so I fold and wait for a clearer spot.

3s4s is a weak suited connector. While I have position on the button, calling a middle position raise with this hand is marginal. The suited nature gives some playability, but 34s doesn't make strong straights and when I hit a pair I'll often be outkicked. With blinds still to act behind me who could squeeze, folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. Elon has opened with a 3x raise to 300 from middle position. While JTo is a decent hand, it is quite weak to play out of position, especially against an open from a middle position player who has shown consistent aggression in previous hands. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and I would still have the Big Blind (FiveTwo) left to act behind me, who could potentially squeeze. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid being caught in a marginal situation out of position.

Facing an MP open to 3bb, 6♠3♦ offsuit is too weak to defend profitably out of position despite the price. It has poor playability, dominated pair potential, and no useful blockers for a 3-bet bluff, so we avoid negative-EV peel spots and keep our stack intact.

Pocket nines is a premium hand from UTG in 6-handed play, roughly top 10% range. Standard strategy is to raise for value to build the pot and isolate against weaker calling ranges from later positions and blinds. Common raise size is 3x the big blind (300 total). This disguises the strength, takes initiative, and performs well post-flop. Folding or limping is too passive with this holding, given deep stacks (~90BB effective). History shows frequent 250-300 raises pre-flop, aligning with this.

Elon opened UTG to 300. I hold A♦3♣ — a weak offsuit Ace with a very poor kicker that will frequently be dominated by an early-position raiser. Calling puts me out of position against a strong UTG range and risks multiway action; a 3-bet bluff has poor equity and little reason to target this opponent here. I have ~100BB so there’s no need to gamble — preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Q8o is a marginal hand facing a UTG raise. Elon has been active but UTG raises typically indicate stronger holdings. Q8o plays poorly against this range - easily dominated by AQ, KQ. With players still to act behind me, I should fold this weak offsuit hand.

I am on the button with Ac 6c. Elon raised from UTG, which usually indicates a strong range, but he has been quite active. Ac 6c is a strong suited hand that plays well in position. Calling allows me to see a flop with positional advantage without over-committing to a potentially dominant UTG range. A 3-bet is an option, but calling is a more standard way to realize the equity of this hand.

Facing an UTG open and a flat from the Button, 3♠2♠ is far too weak to defend from the small blind. We’ll be out of position against at least one strong range, with poor playability and significant reverse implied odds when we make small pairs/weak straights. The big blind can also squeeze, worsening our price and position. Best EV is to release and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 300 from Elon (UTG) and a call from Flash (Button). The pot is currently 750 (300+300+50+100). I need to call 200 more. This gives me pot odds of 200/950, or approximately 21%. Elon has been opening frequently in recent hands (Hands 4, 5, 11, 12, 13), suggesting a wider than average range. 8c7s is a lower-strength off-suit connector, but it retains some playability and equity (approx 20-25% vs wide ranges). Getting nearly 5:1 odds, and closing the action, it is profitable to defend despite being out of position. I will call and evaluate the flop, planning to fold if I don't connect strongly.

I hold 8c7s (8-high) on a paired board of 9c 4d 4s. I missed the flop completely, having no pair and no immediate draw (only very remote runner-runner straight possibilities). I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Elon) and a caller (Flash). There is no strategic merit in leading out (donk betting) into two players with this holding. My plan is to check and fold to any bet, preserving my stack of ~92 BBs for better spots.

Pocket 9s flops a monster full house (9s full of 4s) on a 9c-4d-4s board, which is nearly the nuts—only beaten by unlikely quads (44 or 99) or a bigger full house. The board is dry but paired, deterring some draws while inviting calls from overpairs (TT+, AQ), top pair (A9, K9), two pair (94s), or weaker sets/trips. Multi-way pot with Flash (loose Button call pre) and Pro (limp-call BB) behind; Pro checked, so betting extracts maximum value while protecting against any diamond/spade draws. Half-pot bet (500-600) balances value from worse hands and thin value from floats, induces calls/shoves from villainous ranges, and keeps pot manageable for further extraction on later streets. Deep stacks (~90BB) support aggressive sizing without scaring off callers. History shows pots build with 300-1200 bets post-flop; this aligns. Raising larger risks folding out equity; check risks free cards to draws or villain leads.

On a board of 9c-4d-4s, I currently hold Ace-high (Ac 6c). Elon, who raised from UTG, has made a continuation bet of 600 into a 950 pot. This is a substantial bet size (roughly 63% of the pot) on a very dry, paired board. Elon's UTG range is typically strong, consisting of overpairs (AA-TT) or high overcards (AK, AQ). While I have position, calling 600 with only Ace-high and very few ways to improve (only three Aces and a thin backdoor flush draw) is not profitable, especially since the Big Blind (Pro) is still left to act and could potentially check-raise. Given the board texture and Elon's demonstrated aggression in previous hands, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold 8-high on a 9-4-4 board, which has completely missed my hand. I am facing a continuation bet of 600 chips (roughly 63% of the pot) from the UTG opener after the Button folded. Even if the opponent is bluffing with overcards, I am currently behind, and drawing very thin against any made hand. I have no draws to chase and am out of position. The pot odds do not justify a call with essentially 0% showdown value and very little playability on future streets. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

UTG with 5♦8♣ in a 6-max game and ~100BB effective. This is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability out of position — it’s outside any reasonable UTG open or calling range. Calling 100 to see a flop will likely put me OOP against multiple players and dominated by typical ranges; raising with this hand would be a fancy bluff that’s unnecessary this early. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I have pocket 7s in middle position - a solid hand. Minni folded UTG, so I'm first to act among remaining players. Standard play is to raise to build the pot and thin the field. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB), which is consistent with my previous opening raises.

Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak starting hand, well below the threshold for calling a raise from middle position. Continuing with this hand would put me at a significant disadvantage post-flop, so folding is the only standard play.

Claude is opening from middle position and we’re on the button with a dominated offsuit hand (J4o). Calling puts us in many reverse-implied-odds spots, and 3-betting is a poor bluff candidate because the hand plays terribly when called and we still have blinds behind. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for stronger/button steal spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c5c, facing a 2.5x raise from Claude in Middle Position. While 6c5c is a suited connector with potential, playing it out of position against an aggressive opponent who has shown willingness to bet and raise in previous hands is difficult. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, but the positional disadvantage and the threat of the Big Blind squeezing behind me make calling marginal to negative EV. A 3-bet is an option to balance my range, but at this stack depth and stage, preserving chips and avoiding tricky spots out of position is preferable. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

Q2s is a marginal suited hand at best, but facing a raise from middle position Claude, who has been aggressive and shown strength in previous hands (e.g., Hand 7 c-bet trips, Hand 10 3-bet and c-bet). His opening range is strong: likely 22+, ATs+, KJs+, AJo+, KQo+. Q2s has poor equity (~25-30%) against this range and limited playability (gapped, low cards). Pot odds are 150 to call into 550 (~27% needed), but out of position postflop heads-up, I'll struggle to realize equity. Deep stacks mean I can't rely on fold equity. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.
Pocket 3s from UTG. With 109 BB effective, I have good set-mining depth. Standard open raise to 2.5x. If 3-bet, I'll fold. Looking to hit a set on the flop.

I am in middle position with Q2o, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has raised from UTG. Given the strength of my hand relative to an early position raise, this is an easy fold. I will wait for a better hand to commit chips.

Claude is opening from UTG into a 6‑handed table, so his range is relatively strong and well-connected. J7 offsuit plays poorly versus that range (domination issues, weak realization even in position) and doesn’t have good blocker properties for a profitable 3-bet bluff. With no antes and plenty of better spots in the cutoff, the highest-EV play is to fold and preserve stack.

I hold 6c5c (suited connectors) on the Button, facing a standard 2.5x open raise from Claude in UTG. I have a deep stack (92bb) and the advantage of position. Suited connectors play well in deep-stacked scenarios due to their implied odds against the typically strong range of an UTG opener. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, invite the blinds to potentially enter (improving pot odds), and use my position to navigate post-flop. A 3-bet is possible but risky against an UTG range, and folding is too tight given the positional advantage and playability of the hand.

54s is a marginal speculative hand with straight and flush potential, but facing an UTG raise from Claude (tight/aggressive range) and a button call from Pro (potentially wide), defending from SB OOP is -EV. Pot odds are decent (200 into 650), but postflop playability suffers OOP against strong ranges multiway, especially with BB to act who can squeeze or isolate. Stacks are deep (97.5BB), so folding preserves stack for premium spots early in tournament (hand 15/150). Standard fold to avoid difficult OOP situations.

Claude opened UTG to 250 and the button called, so this will be at least a 3‑way pot if I just defend. J♦9♣ is marginal OOP vs an UTG open — not a hand I want to 3‑bet/squeeze with out of position against two players and deep stacks. The price is attractive: 150 more into a 650 pot (~19% required equity), so calling to see a flop and realize equity is the best option. Plan postflop: play cautiously out of position, fold to heavy action on dry high‑card boards without improvement, continue or build the pot only if I pair a J/9 or pick up good draws.

Board is 6h-7c-2s and I hold Jd-9c: no pair, only weak backdoor equity (one club backdoor and a runner-runner straight). This is a 3‑way pot with an UTG open and BTN call, so ranges are relatively strong and I'm out of position. Pot is 800 and there is no bet to me — the cheapest, lowest-variance line is to check and keep the pot small. I will fold to meaningful C-bets and only look to build the pot if I pair a J/9 or pick up a clear draw on turn. No bluffing or aggressive action OOP with this hand.

I have pocket 3s on a 6-7-2 rainbow board. I flopped an underpair to two cards on board. This is a dry, low board that could hit my opponents' calling ranges (suited connectors, small pairs). My 33 is vulnerable to overcards and likely behind if they have any pair. However, I should make a small c-bet to take it down vs AK/AQ type hands and to maintain aggression. A small sizing around 1/3 pot is appropriate given my marginal hand strength.

I hold middle pair (6s) with a weak kicker, along with backdoor flush and straight possibilities (holding 5,6 on a 6,7,2 board). The UTG opener has made a small continuation bet of 275 into a pot of 800 (~1/3 pot). Given my position on the button, the small bet size, and the fact that I am ahead of missed broadway cards (AK, AQ), folding is too tight. Raising would likely only get action from better hands (7x, 88+, sets) and fold out worse hands. Calling allows me to keep the pot controlled, re-evaluate on the turn, and utilize my position.

I checked the flop with J♦9♣ on 6♥-7♣-2♠ — no pair and only weak backdoor equity, but Claude opened UTG and this is a 3‑way pot so his c‑bet is usually value-heavy. His bet is small (275 into a 1,350 pot, ~20% required), so the cheapest, lowest-variance play is to call and realize equity rather than fold a lot of marginal spots. Plan: call the flop to see a turn, but fold to a meaningful raise or large turn barrel unless I pair a J/9 or pick up a clear draw (club, 8/10 runner). If I improve on the turn I can start building the pot; otherwise keep pot control and avoid getting involved OOP with a bluff.

Turn summary: board 6♥-7♣-2♠-3♦, I hold J♦9♣ — I have no pair and essentially no drawing equity to win a big pot (no single-card straight or flush available; only clean outs are three Jacks and three Nines = 6 outs, ≈12–13% equity). This is a 3‑way pot with an UTG open (Claude) and a caller on the button; Claude c‑bet the flop (small), so his range is weighted to pair/overs/pair+ draws. I'm out of position and the pot is already 1,625 with both opponents deep — there's no reason to build the pot OOP with a naked J9. My flop plan was pot control and to fold to heavy action without improvement; I stick to that. Action: check and go to the river for free. If I miss the river I'll fold to sizable bets and only call small, suspicious bets that offer correct pot odds; if I pair a J or 9 on the river I'll consider thin value betting or calling depending on sizing and opponent tendencies.

I just hit bottom set with my pocket 3s on the turn! The board is 6h 7c 2s 3d - I now have a very strong hand. Minni checked, and I need to bet for value. There are some straight draws (45 makes a straight), and opponents could have pairs (77, 66, overpairs) or draws they'll call with. I'll bet around 65% pot to extract value while pricing in draws.

On the turn, the board reads 6h 7c 2s 3d. I hold 6c 5c, giving me a pair of sixes and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). The pot is 1625, and Claude (UTG) bets 1050, which is roughly 65% of the pot. This is a significant show of strength, especially betting into two opponents (Minni and myself). Claude's range likely consists of overpairs (88+), sets, or possibly a made straight (45s) or strong draws. Against this range, my pair of sixes is likely behind, and my draw (4 outs) provides only about 9% equity for the straight. Even adding potential outs for two pair or trips, my total equity is likely below the ~28% pot odds required to call. Additionally, Minni is still left to act behind me. Preserving my stack is preferable to chasing a weak draw against strong action.

I fold. On 6♥-7♣-2♠-3♦ holding J♦9♣ I have no pair and only six clean outs (three Jacks + three Nines) ≈12–13% equity. Claude (UTG) has opened and c‑bet the flop; his large turn bet (1,050 into a ~2,675 pot with Pro folding) is strongly value‑oriented in this spot. Calling 1,050 requires roughly 28% equity (1,050 / (2,675+1,050) ≈ 28%), well above my hand’s equity, and I’m out of position with no good blockers to bluff. A raise would be an unprofitable bluff here, so the +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position in a 6-max game. Queen-Nine suited (Q9h) is a marginal hand from this position, typically sitting just outside the standard opening range. Given that Claude (Big Blind) has been playing aggressively and several other players have shown a willingness to contest pots, opening with a hand that is easily dominated and out of position against most of the table is risky. To preserve my stack for better opportunities, I will fold this hand.

Ah7h is a solid suited ace to open from middle in 6-max. Limping invites multiway play and gets dominated often; raising lets us win the blinds, isolates weaker ranges behind, and gives us initiative with a hand that plays well postflop (nut-flush potential, top-pair value). Using the table’s common sizing, I’ll raise to 300 to apply pressure and deny cheap realization.

I hold Kh 2s in the Cutoff facing a raise to 300 from FiveTwo in Middle Position. King-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and is dominated by the opener's likely range (broadways, pairs, suited aces). Given the early stage of the tournament (Hand 16/150) and the lack of equity realization with this holding, the only correct play is to fold.

Preflop on the button facing a 3x raise from middle position (FiveTwo) with T3o, an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no high card strength, and minimal equity against a standard opening range. Pot odds are roughly 1.5:1 for a call (300 into 750), but my equity is negligible (<20%) against typical MP ranges, and even on the button, this hand doesn't warrant calling or 3-betting for fold equity or value. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament (hand 16/150). FiveTwo has shown some aggression but no exploitable looseness here to justify playing this trash.

Facing a 3BB open to 300 from middle, I have A♠4♦ in the small blind. This hand is worth defending but not worth a bloated out-of-position 3-bet — I’m dominated by stronger Aces and will face tough postflop decisions OOP. Calling for the 250 more (300 total committed including my SB) is a small price to see a flop with an Ace, good fold equity on some boards, and decent implied odds deep (stack ~95BB). If the big blind squeezes big I can fold; if we get heads-up or a fair multiway pot I can play for top-pair value or pot-control. I’ll call and proceed cautiously on the flop.

5d3d is a weak hand, but it's suited with some connectivity. Getting 3.5:1 pot odds (200 to win 700) in a multiway pot from the BB. Suited hands play better multiway, and I can hit a flush draw, straight draw, or catch a disguised two pair/trips. The implied odds are decent if I hit big. Calling is marginally +EV here given the discount and multiway action.

I called preflop with A♠4♦ intending to proceed cautiously. On a K♣–J♥–6♠ rainbow flop I have only ace‑high and no realistic draws, and this is a three‑way pot where a continuation bet from the small blind is thin and likely to get called. Checking controls the pot, keeps weaker holdings in, and lets me see a cheap turn to reevaluate. Plan: check and call a small c‑bet to realize equity, fold to a large raise/overbet, and only start building the pot if I improve (pair an Ace) or get a clear read/ fold equity on later streets.

I completely whiffed this flop with 5d3d. Board is Kc Jh 6s - no diamonds for a flush draw, no straight draws, no pair. I have zero equity here. Minni checked, and I should check behind to see a free turn card. No reason to bluff into a multiway pot with no equity or blockers.

We have position and the preflop initiative in a 3-way pot. This K-J-6 rainbow board connects reasonably well with our perceived raising range (broadways/strong kings), while the blinds still have a lot of hands that miss or are marginal (small pairs, weak floats). With Ah7h we have little showdown value but do have backdoor nut-flush potential and good turn cards to barrel (A, Q, T, any heart). A small c-bet pressures their wide check ranges at a good price and keeps our risk controlled; if we face a check-raise we can comfortably fold.

I hold A♠4♦ on a J♥6♠K♣ rainbow flop in a three‑way pot. FiveTwo (preflop opener) fires a small bet of 300 into 1200. This bet is cheap — I only need ~20% equity to justify calling — and my Ace‑high has decent showdown value vs a wide continuation range and blocks some strong A‑combos. Raising OOP here with A4o is a mistake; I want to keep the pot manageable and see a turn to either pair my Ace or fold to real aggression. Plan: call the 300 to realize equity, control the pot, and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to a large raise/overbet or continue if I pair up or get favorable action).

I have 5d3d on Kc Jh 6s - complete air. No flush draw (no diamonds), no real straight draw, no pair. Even getting 5:1 odds (300 to win 1500), my equity is near zero. I have at best a backdoor gutshot if a 4 comes. In a multiway pot where FiveTwo c-bet and Minni called, at least one of them likely has a piece of this board. Folding is the only reasonable play.

Check. I’m heads-up after calling a small flop c‑bet with A♠4♦ and I only have ace‑high — no meaningful draw. The 9 on the turn is a relatively blank card versus my range and betting here would be a thin bluff that will be called by many Kx/Jx/paired hands and pocket pairs, so I want to control the pot OOP. Checking lets me realize my showdown equity cheaply, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, and preserves stack depth to exploit a river improvement (or to fold to large aggression). Plan: call a small bet on the river if it’s reasonable, fold to big overbets, and extract value only if I pair my Ace or get a clear read.

We c-bet small on the flop as a range/position play, but once the SB calls, her range is much more condensed into Kx/Jx, some pocket pairs, and key draws. The turn 9 is a bad barrel card: it improves her (J9/K9/99) and, importantly, completes QTs/QTo into a straight, while we have essentially no real equity (no flush draw, no meaningful straight draw—mostly just 3 aces to make top pair). Firing again risks getting called by better or check-raised off our hand with very poor outs. Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our small equity, and lets us take a stab on favorable rivers (A/Q/T) if she checks again.

I missed — A♠4♦ is just ace‑high on J♥–6♠–K♣–9♦–2♦. FiveTwo was the preflop opener and c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, and the river is a blank for my hand. Betting as a bluff here is thin: many Kx/Jx/9x and pocket pairs will call, so a river stab is a negative‑EV shove for my holding. I’ll control the pot and take the showdown cheaply — check and show my Ace if he checks back. If he leads a small river bet I’ll consider a call for pot‑odds depending on sizing, but I won’t bluff into likely calling ranges.

River checks to us in a heads-up pot where we have essentially zero showdown value (A-high on K-J-6-9-2). After SB check/calls flop then checks turn and river, her range is heavily weighted toward marginal made hands (weak Jx, 6x, pocket pairs) that didn’t want to build the pot, plus a smaller portion of Kx/straights. Our turn check keeps our range wide and still credibly contains plenty of Kx that pot-controlled turn and now value-bets river. With Ah we also block some of her strongest Ax-based continues (AK/AJ), slightly improving bluff prospects. We need a meaningful size to pressure Jx/pairs; a ~2/3 pot bet should fold a large chunk of her range often enough to be profitable.

Fold. I defended preflop and called the small flop c-bet intending to call small river bets, but this river lead is too large for A♠4♦. After his 1,000 river bet the pot is 2,500, so a call requires ~28.6% equity (1,000 / (2,500+1,000)). His line — open, small c-bet, check on the turn, then a river shove/lead — is much more consistent with value hands (Kx, Jx, medium pairs, some 9x) than with frequent bluffs on this J‑6‑K‑9‑2 board. My Ace‑high has poor equity vs that value‑weighted range and I don’t block enough bluffs to make a profitable call or a credible bluff‑raise OOP. Preserve the stack and fold.

We’re 6-handed UTG with AdAc, the best starting hand. Limping invites multiway action and reduces our ability to win a big pot; we want to build the pot while keeping dominated hands (AQ/AJ, suited broadways, pairs) in. A standard open size here has been 250–300, so I’ll raise to 300 to charge the blinds and isolate while still getting calls from worse.

I hold 7h 4c in Middle Position facing a standard opening raise of 300 from the UTG player (FiveTwo). 7-4 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em, being disconnected, unsuited, and low-ranked. Against a UTG opening range, which is typically strong, this hand has very low equity. Additionally, there are four players left to act behind me. Calling or raising would be significantly negative expected value (-EV). The correct strategic decision is to fold immediately.

A5o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit with poor post-flop playability. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has shown competence in previous hands (e.g., Hand 16 value betting rivers, Hand 8 trapping then raising), their range is strong: pairs 66+, broadway, strong aces. Equity vs. this range is around 30-35% heads-up, but multiway potential with 4 players behind reduces it further. Position helps, but weak kicker on ace-hits, bad vs. sets/overpairs, and poor implied odds. Pot odds require ~29% equity for a call, borderline but post-flop dynamics (domination risk, reverse implied odds) favor folding. No strong reason to 3-bet bluff or value raise. Early in tournament (Hand 17/150), deep stacks (~100BB), preserve stack for better spots.

UTG opened to 300 from a full ring early position — that range is relatively strong. Jc2h is a weak, ill-connected offsuit hand that will be dominated by most of the opener’s range and plays poorly multiway or postflop. Calling 300 invites the blinds to squeeze or creates a marginal, low-CEV spot out of position; 3‑betting as a bluff here with J2o is too thin. With a healthy stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot on the button (suited connectors, broadways, or pocket pairs). Fold.

Q8o in the SB facing a UTG raise. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against an early position raise. UTG ranges are typically strong, and Q8o doesn't have great playability - it's dominated by AQ, KQ, and struggles against pocket pairs. With the BB still to act behind me, folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 2d. FiveTwo (UTG) raised to 300. 5-2 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially against an early position raise. Calling even with the discount is a negative expected value play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 6-handed game with Ace-Five offsuit. This is a structurally weak hand to open from early position because it is dominated by many calling ranges (better Aces) and lacks playability (unsuited, low straight potential). With a healthy stack of over 80 big blinds, there is no pressure to make loose steals from bad positions. The standard and prudent play is to fold.

Preflop in middle position with 7h2c, a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor connectivity. UTG has folded, but this is still too weak to open or call the big blind. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Long-term tournament strategy prioritizes position and hand strength over speculative plays here.

Cutoff with Ace of Clubs + Jack of Hearts (Ac Jh) — a strong opening hand with position on most opponents. Pot is 150 and it's 100 to call; with a ~89BB stack I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the button and blinds. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB sizing (to 300 total): large enough to pick off weak blinds and isolate heads-up, but small enough to preserve postflop maneuverability.
