Plan: c-bet most favorable flops; call a modest 3-bet from the blinds and play postflop; fold or seriously tighten vs a large button 3-bet/4-bet without a premium.

Q9o on the button facing a cutoff raise to 300. This is a decent hand to defend with position. Minni has been fairly active. Not strong enough to 3-bet for value, but definitely playable as a call to see a flop in position with a comfortable stack of 11700.

I am holding 4c 3s in the Small Blind. This is a very weak starting hand. Minni raised from the Cutoff and Claude called on the Button. Even with the pot odds offered, playing such a weak hand out of position against two opponents is a losing strategy in the long run. I will fold.

We’re getting a great price (200 to win 750) and can realize equity with a suited, connected hand that flops straights/flushes and good semi-bluffs. A squeeze here with 9h6h is too thin out of position versus a CO open + BTN flat at these stack depths; calling keeps the pot manageable while retaining implied odds multiway.

Multiway and out of position with 9h6h on Qd-2s-4s, we have no made hand and only weak backdoor equity. Leading into the preflop raiser + button caller on a board that connects reasonably well with their ranges (Qx, overpairs, spade draws) won’t generate enough folds to justify building the pot. Best is to check, keep ranges wide, and look to continue only on favorable turns (hearts, 5/7/8/T, or action that gives a good bluffing opportunity).

I raised preflop with AcJh and the flop (Qd 2s 4s) is very dry and disconnected — it misses a lot of calling ranges. As the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet to take down the pot or get value from weaker Ax/Kx/Jx and small pocket pairs. There are two spades on board, but I hold no spade; I also block some A-high combos which makes a c-bet more attractive. Size for fold equity and pot control: about half-pot. If I get called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to significant resistance (check/raise or big bets) since I currently have no pair or strong redraws.

I flopped top pair with Q9 on Qd 2s 4s. Minni's c-bet of 475 into a pot of ~950 is standard. I have position and a decent top pair. The board has a spade flush draw. Raising could work to protect, but calling keeps in worse hands and lets me use position. With a medium-strength top pair, pot control via a call is optimal - I can reassess on the turn.

We have 9h6h on Qd-2s-4s: no pair, no spade, and only very weak backdoor equity (backdoor hearts + distant straight possibilities). Facing a c-bet into two players and then a BTN call, their ranges are heavily weighted toward Qx/overpairs/strong spade draws, so our fold equity if we bluff-raise is poor and calling 475 needs ~25% immediate equity we simply don’t have. Best play is to release and preserve stack.

I c‑bet the dry Q♦ 2♠ 4♠ flop and Claude called. On the turn an 8♦ arrives and nothing meaningful improves my holding — I have A♣ J♥: no pair and no spade/diamond draw. With a single caller who elected to flat the flop (likely Qx, a small pair, or a spade float/draw), betting the turn offers thin fold equity and turns marginal hands into bloated pots. I want pot control and to keep options open given my blocker to some A‑high combos. Check here to see how Claude reacts: if he bets small I’ll often fold to large pressure but may call small probes with position and my Ace blocker; if he checks back I can take a free river to decide on a delayed bluff or a safe showdown with Ace‑high.

Minni checked the turn after c-betting the flop - this often indicates weakness or pot control. I have top pair with a decent kicker on Qd 2s 4s 8d. The 8d is a blank that doesn't complete draws. I should bet for value and protection against the spade flush draw. A bet around half pot extracts value from worse queens, pairs, and keeps draws paying.

I opened and c‑bet the dry Q‑2‑4 flop and Claude called. On the turn I checked to control the pot and he made a small probe (900 into the ~1900 pot; pot is now 2800). That sizing polarizes to a wide range — Qx, small pairs (2s/4s), floats/spade draws and some bluffs. With A♣J♥ I have no pair but I block many A‑high combos and have decent showdown equity versus floats/bluffs. Calling 900 (~24% immediate pot odds) is a cheap way to realize equity and keep bluffs in; raising to 1800 would bloat the pot with air and create awkward decisions on the river, while folding concedes too much. Plan: call to see the river, fold to large river aggression, or extract/value‑raise if an Ace/Jack or a favorable card hits.

The river (Q♦ 2♠ 4♠ 8♦ 9♦) does not improve my A♣J♥ — I only have Ace-high. Claude’s turn raise to 900 polarized his range into value (Qx, some medium pairs) and bluffs/floaters. Betting now would be thin: I’m unlikely to get worse to fold (Qx and small pairs will often call a river bet) and I don’t have value hands to get called by worse. I also block many A‑high bluff-catchers, so checking realizes my showdown equity cheaply and keeps bluffs in play. Plan: check to showdown and fold to large river aggression if Claude fires; call only a small river probe if it looks like a bluff.

The river 9d gave me two pair (Queens and Nines). Minni has checked again after check-calling my turn bet. Their line suggests a medium-strength hand like a weaker Queen, or possibly a missed flush draw. With top two pair, I should value bet. Betting around 1/2 pot (1800) should get called by worse queens and maybe some 8x hands that picked up a pair.

I fold. Summary: I have A♣J♥ — Ace‑high only. Claude’s line (call preflop, call my flop c‑bet, bet turn 900 and now bet 1800 on the river) is polarized toward value: Qx, turned two‑pair (Q8, Q9), sets (22/44), and now on the 9♦ river also any two‑diamond combo that just made a flush. Calling 1,800 into a 5,500 pot requires ~32.7% equity (1,800/5,500). My actual showdown equity against Claude’s betting range is much lower — I beat mainly pure bluffs/missed floats, which are unlikely to be a large enough share given his line. Raising would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity and would risk a big portion of my stack with no pair. Preserving chips for better spots and avoiding a marginal hero‑call is the correct play here.

UTG in 6-handed play with K7o, an offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by better kings. Deep stacks, but this is outside my opening range preflop to avoid difficult postflop spots. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better opportunities.

Holding K♥J♣ in middle position (6-handed) with a healthy 7,250 stack (~72 BB). This is a clear open-raise: KJ offsuit plays well heads-up and has good postflop playability. The min raise is 200 but the table’s common open size has been ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to build value, gain fold equity, and discourage multiway limps. Plan: proceed aggressively on favorable flops; call small 3-bets in position to realize equity but fold to large/shove-sized 3-bets out of position. Raising to 300 balances protection and extraction with this hand.
T2o is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from Minni with players still to act behind me, this is a clear fold. No reason to get involved with such poor holdings.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from middle position. While I have an Ace, the kicker is very weak, and I am likely to be dominated by Minni's opening range (which usually includes better Aces and pairs). Additionally, there are two active players in the blinds (FiveTwo and Pro) who could squeeze or call. Calling with A3o is generally a losing play in the long run against a middle-position open. Folding is the most disciplined action to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Minni’s 2.5bb open is likely wide, but T5o is too weak to defend profitably from the small blind: we’d be out of position, still have the big blind behind, and this hand has poor playability and reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs. It’s also not a great 3-bet bluff candidate (no blockers, not suited). Best is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit (KJo), facing a standard 3x raise from Minni in Middle Position. The Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 chips into a pot of 450, offering me odds of roughly 2.25 to 1 (requiring ~30% equity). KJo is a strong broadway hand well within the standard defending range against a middle position open, dominating weaker broadways and flipping against lower pairs. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the price is too good to fold, and 3-betting might isolate me against a stronger range. I will call to see a flop.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit on a very dry, rainbow flop of 9-4-2. I defended pre-flop against a middle position open. The board texture does not favor my hand significantly, leaving me with just two overcards. Leading out (donk betting) is generally not optimal here as it allows the opponent to play perfectly against me. Checking is the standard line to the pre-flop aggressor. If the opponent checks back, I get to realize my equity for free. If they bet, I can evaluate based on the sizing, though this is often a fold or a float depending on frequency. Currently, checking is the only logical first step.

I was the preflop raiser to 300 and we’re heads-up on a very dry, rainbow flop 9s-2d-4c with K♥J♣ — two overcards and no pair or draw. My range as the opener contains lots of Broadway overcards and value hands; this board is ideal for a continuation bet to take the pot down or fold out small pairs and floats. I’ll make a standard c-bet around half the pot to apply pressure but keep the size controllable if I face resistance. Bet 300 chips (pot = 650) — if called I’ll reassess on the turn (barrel favorable cards, or check back vs aggression); if I face a big raise I’ll fold without a strong read.

I am out of position with King-Jack offsuit on a dry 9-4-2 rainbow board. I have two overcards but no immediate straight or flush draws. The opponent c-bets 300 into a pot of 650 (roughly 46% pot). While the pot odds of ~24% are enticing, my realizable equity is low because I am out of position and likely behind the opponent's c-betting range (which includes pocket pairs, top pair, and better Ace-highs). Floating with just King-high without a backdoor flush draw or specific reads is a losing play in the long run. I fold to preserve my stack.

UTG in a 6-max game with 2♦5♦ — a very weak, low suited hand that plays poorly out of position. Opening or calling here invites multiway pots and difficult post‑flop decisions against several aggressive opponents (Elon/Claude have shown frequent raises), and the hand is often dominated. The cost to call (100) isn’t justified by the hand’s equity and the benefit of conserving a ~76BB stack for better spots is greater. I will fold and wait for a more favorable position/hand.
4c 7d is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. In middle position with several players left to act, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such a marginal holding.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Seven offsuit. The action has folded to me. While the Cutoff is a good position to steal blinds, T7o is too weak of a hand to open with, as it has poor playability post-flop and doesn't dominate many hands that will call. I will wait for a better spot.

Folds to me on the button, so this is a prime steal spot. Even though 95o is a weak holding, we have position and strong immediate fold equity versus the blinds (Pro has been fairly passive/tight; Elon opens a lot but should still defend selectively). Raising is higher EV than limping because it wins the pot uncontested often and keeps my range uncapped. Use a small standard open size to risk little while pressuring blinds; if faced with a 3-bet, this hand is an easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ks Ts, a strong suited broadway hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. FiveTwo has been active and aggressive, so their opening range is likely wide. KTs performs well against a wide Button range, having about 58% equity against the top 50% of hands. I am getting good pot odds to call (putting in 200 to win a pot that will be at least 600 if BB folds). While a 3-bet is an option to isolate, calling is a lower-variance play that keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to play a manageable pot post-flop with a hand that flops well. I will call and evaluate the flop out of position.

3d8s is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a button raise from FiveTwo (who has shown aggression in past hands) and a call from Pro in SB, the range is too strong for me to profitably continue. Pot odds are ~4:1 (150 into 750), but equity realization postflop is poor due to reverse implied odds and domination risks. BB defense charts suggest folding this; preserve stack for better spots.

I am out of position with King-high (Ks Ts) on a low, two-tone board (3h 8c 5c). I missed the flop and have no immediate draws, though I do have two overcards. The standard play against the pre-flop aggressor is to check and evaluate their action. Leading out (donk betting) here is not advisable as this board texture connects reasonably well with a button opening range (sets, straight draws, flush draws) and I have very little equity to protect or bluff with at this stage.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser in position after SB checks. This flop is somewhat connected (clubs, 8x/5x/3x available), but I still have range advantage and my hand has decent showdown value that benefits from protection. A small c-bet folds out a lot of Pro’s overcards (Ax/Kx/Qx) and denies equity to random hands with two overs or one-club floats, while not overinflating the pot with a vulnerable bottom pair. If I get raised, I can comfortably fold; if called, I’ll reassess turns (often checking back on scary cards).

I hold Ks Ts on a 3h 8c 5c board. I have missed the flop completely, having only King-high with no flush draw (my spades are useless) and no real straight draws. While I have two overcards, the board texture connects well with a Button raising range (sets, two pair, straight draws, flush draws, and pairs). The opponent bets 200 into 600 (1/3 pot). Although I am getting good pot odds (4:1) requiring only ~20% equity, realizing that equity out of position is difficult. If I call and miss the turn, I likely have to fold to a second barrel. Floating with King-high OOP on a coordination-heavy board is generally a losing play. I will fold and preserve my stack.
A8 suited is a playable hand from UTG. It has decent equity with the suited ace giving flush potential. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x) is appropriate here.

I am holding Q3o in a middle position. Claude has opened from UTG with a raise to 250. Q3o is a very weak hand and does not play well against an UTG opening range, which usually consists of stronger cards. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop out of position or against a strong range, and raising is a low-frequency bluff that isn't justified here. Therefore, the standard and most profitable play is to fold.

Facing an UTG open to 2.5bb, 6-2 offsuit is far too weak and dominated to continue, even in position. It has poor playability, makes weak pairs/straights, and can easily lose bigger pots when it connects. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I hold 6d 9d on the Button. The action began with Claude raising to 250 from UTG. While Claude has been active and raising frequently, opening from UTG typically represents a stronger range. 9d 6d is a suited two-gapper, which is a speculative hand that is generally too weak to call a UTG raise, even in position, as it suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated flushes, pairs) and the risk of being squeezed by the blinds. Although we are relatively deep-stacked (80+ BB), the disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

Claude's UTG raise indicates a strong range. My hand, 2sTd, is weak, offsuit, low, and poorly connected with minimal playability OOP in SB. Deep stacks make calling unprofitable due to poor postflop prospects and being crushed by typical calling/raising ranges. Standard fold to preserve stack.

Claude opened UTG to 250 (pot 400); I’m in the BB with K♠4♥ and must call 150. K4o is heavily dominated by a standard UTG open and has very poor post‑flop playability out of position (only weak backdoors). 3‑betting is out of line here and the break‑even call frequency (~27%) is higher than my hand’s likely equity vs Claude’s range, especially given his frequent aggressive lines in prior hands. Preserve the 7.5k stack for better spots — fold.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with 6d 7s, which is a very weak starting hand. In a 6-handed game, the UTG opening range should be relatively tight, and 76 offsuit is far outside that range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to avoid getting involved in a pot with a marginal hand out of position.

We’re 6-handed with action folded to us in middle position. QTo is strong enough to open-raise here (plays well vs blind defense ranges and can win preflop often). Limping invites multiway pots and realizes equity poorly with an offsuit broadway; folding is a bit too tight given position and stacks. I’ll use a standard ~2.5bb open to pressure the blinds and keep my range consistent with prior sizing at the table.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd), the second-best starting hand. The player in Middle Position (FiveTwo) has opened to 250. To maximize value and isolate the opponent, I should 3-bet rather than just call. Calling risks allowing the players behind to enter cheaply, creating a multi-way pot where my equity drops. A 3-bet builds the pot immediately with a premium hand. A standard in-position 3-bet size is around 3x to 3.5x the open. I will raise to 800.

Preflop on the button with K5o facing a raise from FiveTwo (middle position) and a 3-bet to 800 from Pro (cutoff). Pot is 1200, calling 800 into it offers poor implied odds for such a weak offsuit hand with poor postflop playability. K5o is dominated by likely calling ranges and has minimal fold equity if raising. FiveTwo and Pro have shown aggression but tight folding in spots; Pro's 3-bet suggests strength (QQ+, AK range). Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament (hand 22/150) for better opportunities.

I have Ah5h in the small blind facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo and a 3-bet to 800 from Pro (cutoff). Pot is 1,200 and it costs me 750 more (800 total) to call. A5s has decent postflop playability (nut-flush potential and ability to hit top pair) and calling is relatively cheap (~10% of my stack, ~74 BB deep). I’m out of position, so 4-betting light to 1,350+ would be thin — I’d be isolating against a likely stronger 3-bet range and risking a bigger confrontation. I’ll call to see a flop and play cautiously: fold to extreme aggression on dry, unfavorable boards; continue/raise for value if I hit top pair or strong draw.

Q7o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet from Pro (800) and a cold call from Minni. I'm out of position in a multi-way pot against likely strong ranges. Even with ~2.8:1 pot odds, Q7o plays poorly here - it's dominated by stronger queens and has minimal implied odds. Clear fold.

We opened QTo and now face a 3-bet from Pro plus a cold call from Minni in the small blind. In a multiway 3-bet pot, QTo offsuit plays very poorly: it’s frequently dominated by Pro’s value 3-bets (AQ/KQ/QJ/AT+) and even Minni’s cold-calling range can contain a lot of Qx/Tx that crush us. Calling 550 to win 1950 gives decent immediate price, but our realization is bad being sandwiched between SB and the 3-bettor, and we’ll be forced into many tough postflop spots. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive with a cold caller already in, increasing the chance we get jammed and reducing fold equity. Best play is to cut losses and fold.

I called the cutoff's 3-bet preflop and flopped a pair of 5s with the Ace kicker on Jc‑8c‑5s. I'm heads-up and out of position against a 3-bettor whose range contains a lot of overcards, jacks, pocket pairs and club draws (board has two clubs). I want to get value from worse aces/small pairs and charge/deter club draws, so I’ll make a protection/value bet of about half the pot. This sizing extracts from hands that will continue (one‑card flush draws, worse aces/pocket pairs) while leaving room to fold to a large raise — if he raises strongly I’ll assume I'm often behind and release; if he calls I'll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd) on a board of [5s Jc 8c]. I raised pre-flop and Minni cold-called from the Small Blind, then led out (donk bet) 975 into a 1950 pot on the flop. Minni's cold-calling range likely includes pairs (88-QQ), strong suited broadways (AQs, AJs, KQs), and possibly trapped premiums. On this texture, her lead could represent a set (JJ, 88, 55), Top Pair (AJ), an overpair (QQ), or a strong flush draw. I am ahead of QQ, AJ, and draws, but behind sets. Raising here would likely isolate me against sets and flush draws while folding out the hands I beat (like AJ). Calling keeps her range wide, allows me to evaluate the turn, and prevents bloating the pot unnecessarily against a potential set. I also hold the Kc, which blocks the second nut flush draw and gives me redraw equity.

Turn brings the 7♣ and completes a three‑club board (5♠ J♣ 8♣ 7♣). I hold A♥5♥ — a vulnerable one‑pair hand with the nut‑ace kicker but no club. Pro’s line (preflop 3‑bet, check‑call my flop bet) contains many club draws and strong broadway/paired hands; a large portion of his flop calls now become made flushes that beat me. Betting myself here just builds the pot with a hand that has poor equity versus that range and risks a check‑raise or a pot‑sized call by a flush. I want to control the pot size and preserve fold equity on the river, so I check. Plan: if Pro bets a small amount on the river (cheap enough relative to pot—roughly ≤1/3 pot) I will call to see showdown; if he bets larger I will fold most of the time. If he checks back we go to a showdown or choose a small value action on a harmless river.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd) on a board of [5s Jc 8c 7c]. The turn card completes the club flush draw. My opponent (Minni), who cold-called my 3-bet pre-flop and donk-bet the flop, has now checked. This check likely indicates the flush card is scary for her range (sets, two pair, top pair), or she has a flush and is trapping. Since I hold the Kc, I have the second-nut flush draw and a strong overpair. Betting here is thin for value because worse hands (like AJ without a club) may fold, while better hands (flushes, sets) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity (drawing to a flush or holding up), and avoid being check-raised. I will take the free card.

I check. I have a vulnerable one-pair (5s) with the nut-A kicker on a now three‑club board (Jc 8c 7c Th). Pro’s line (3‑bet pre, check‑call my ~975 flop bet, then check the turn) is consistent with a lot of club draws and also many hands that currently beat me (Jx/8x, sets, or a slow‑played flush). Betting the river is thin: a value bet will be called mostly by hands that already beat me, and a small bluff is likely to be called by worse hands or check‑raised by the few strong hands. With the pot at 3,900 and my stack 5,725, pot control and going to showdown (or folding to a larger river bet) preserves equity and stack for future hands. If Pro fires a small thin bet after my check I can call as a bluffcatcher; I’ll fold to a sizable bet.

I hold a King-high flush with the King of Clubs in my hand on a board of [5s Jc 8c 7c Th]. My opponent, Minni, cold-called a 3-bet pre-flop, led the flop, but has now checked both the turn (when the flush arrived) and the river. This passive line on the later streets strongly suggests she does not hold the Ace-high flush, as she would likely value bet the river after I checked back the turn. Her range likely consists of sets (JJ, 88), two pair, or top pair hands that are now in bluff-catch mode against the flush. I am only beaten by the nut flush (AcX), which is unlikely given the action. I will bet for value to extract chips from her strong made hands that are worse than mine. A bet of around 1600 (approx. 40% pot) is small enough to get called by sets or two pair but large enough to gain significant value.

I fold. On the river I hold A♥5♥ — a vulnerable one‑pair (5s) with the nut Ace kicker. Pro’s line (3‑bet preflop, check‑call my ~975 flop bet, check the turn, now a 1,600 river shove/bet) is highly consistent with made hands: completed two‑club flushes, Jx/8x top‑pair hands, or pocket pairs/sets. The pot was 3,900 before his bet and shows 5,500 after it; calling 1,600 to potentially win 7,100 requires ≈22.5% equity. I have no club blocker and my realistic equity against that range is well below the threshold (rough estimate ~15–20%), so a call is +EV negative. A raise (min to 3,200) is too thin — it commits a large portion of my stack, lacks blocker coverage to credibly represent the flush, and will be called or shove‑reraised by most of the hands that already beat me. Preserving chips and waiting for a clearer spot is the best play here, so I fold to the river bet.

UTG 6-max we need a relatively tight opening range. Q7o is dominated by most continuing hands (better queens, broadways, strong aces) and plays poorly out of position postflop. Limping is also unattractive with several aggressive players behind who can isolate, and raising would be too loose from this seat. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in Middle Position with 6h 9h. The UTG player folded. This is a speculative suited one-gapper hand. With 100BB effective stacks, opening this hand from MP is generally too loose, especially with aggressive players like Claude in the blinds and active players behind. The hand does not play well against 3-bets or cold calls from position. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here.

Preflop in cutoff with Q9o after two folds (UTG and MP). This is a standard open-raising spot in late position with 6-handed play. Q9o has decent queen-high blocker, straight potential, and playability post-flop. Blinds are relatively tight based on history (Claude aggressive but Minni/Flash call light sometimes). Raising to 275 (2.75x BB) is a good size to steal the 150 pot while keeping fold equity and maintaining initiative. Deep stacks (96bb effective) allow for post-flop flexibility. Folding too tight early in tournament; limping poor strategy.

On the button with J♦8♣ facing a 275 cutoff open, this is a marginal but playable hand — calling preserves position and keeps the pot manageable. A 3‑bet here is too loose (J8o has limited fold‑equity vs likely cold callers/3‑betters like Claude or Flash) and could get ugly if reraised. I’ll call 275 to see a flop in position, plan to proceed selectively (bet/raise with top pair or strong draws, check/fold to heavy aggression). If I face a sizable preflop 3‑bet I will relinquish the hand.

8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand. I'm out of position in the small blind facing a raise and a call. Even with ~3:1 pot odds, this hand plays poorly postflop - not suited, weak kicker potential, and limited straight draws. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 4d. Elon (Cutoff) raised to 275 and Minni (Button) called. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 175 to call. While the pot odds are theoretically 4:1, 5-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability, especially when playing out of position against two opponents. Defending with such low equity often leads to difficult post-flop situations. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Top pair with Queen kicker on a coordinated flop (9s8sJh) featuring spade flush draw and open-ended straight draws (T7, 10x). As preflop raiser from CO, I have range advantage over Minni's button calling range, which includes floats, weaker pairs (like Jx, Tx, Ax), suited connectors, and draws. C-betting for thin value and protection against draws; her range will continue with too many worse hands and some draws. Sizing to ~65% pot (450) balances value extraction from worse pairs/floats while maintaining fold equity against strong draws. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or losing initiative. Deep stacks allow flexibility post-flop.

I flopped two pair (Jacks and Eights) — a very strong hand heads‑up. Elon was the preflop raiser and his 450 c‑bet is small on a fairly coordinated 9s–8s–Jh board, so his range is wide and contains many single‑pair hands and spade/straight draws. I want to extract value from worse Jx/9x/8x hands and charge the spade/straight draws, so I’ll raise. The minimum legal raise is to 900; that size accomplishes value and protection without overcommitting. If he 3‑bets big or jams I’ll re‑evaluate, but versus a single c‑bet this is the clear value/protection play.

Facing a flop raise to 900 (450 to call) on a highly coordinated board 9s-8s-Jh with second pair (9s) and Q kicker. As preflop raiser from CO, my c-bet range is polarized, but Minni's BTN call range includes Jx suited, suited connectors, spades, and broadway. Her raise is strong: favors top pair Jx (esp suited), two-pair (J9s/J8s), sets (JJ/99/88), straights (KT), or semi-bluffs with strong draws (spades like AsKs/QsTs, open-enders T7s). My Q9o is second pair, vulnerable to any J, straight on turn (T7), or flush. Pot 2050 offers ~4.5:1 odds, but reverse implied odds are poor—beating draws ok but crushed by value. Raising commits too much (~1350 min) against better hands with low fold equity. Calling bloats pot OOP on scary board. History shows Minni mixes calls/folds but raises value here. Deep stacks (88bb), early tournament (hand 23/150), folding preserves stack for premium spots. Clear fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Three offsuit (T3o), which is an extremely weak hand. With 5 players left to act behind me, the probability of someone waking up with a better hand is high, and this hand plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I have a healthy stack of roughly 102 BBs, so there is no desperation to play junk hands. The only reasonable move is to fold immediately.

Pocket eights is a premium hand in middle position with an unopened pot (Pro folded UTG). Standard play is to raise for value, build the pot, and take initiative. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is appropriate given deep stacks and opponent tendencies (Minni calls wide from cutoff, Claude aggressive on button, blinds defend variably). Limping or calling is too passive; folding is too tight. This maximizes equity realization and fold equity.

Cutoff with K♥7♥ facing a MP raise to 300 (3 BB) and 66 BB effective. The raise is small so calling 300 is a cheap way to realize equity with a suited King that has flush and straight potential and good implied odds deep-stacked. I don't want to 3-bet light here (Elon looks capable of continuing or isolating and I don't want to bloat a pot out of position), and folding is too tight for the price. Plan: call 300, see the flop, fold to heavy action on dry boards, and continue only with pair/strong draws or good positional opportunities.

4c7s is a weak offsuit hand. Even with position on the button, calling 300 into a raised pot with two players already in is not profitable. This hand has poor playability and will often be dominated. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The action opened with a raise from Elon in middle position to 300 and a call from Minni in the cutoff. J7o is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against two active opponents. While the price to call is only 250 chips into a 750-chip pot, the likelihood of being dominated or facing further pressure on later streets makes this a clear fold.

AcQc is a strong suited Broadway hand and we’re getting a great squeeze spot: Elon opened and Minni cold-called, so their ranges are capped/medium-heavy and will overfold to pressure. From the big blind we want to deny equity, isolate to 1 opponent, and build a pot for value when we’re ahead of their Ax/KQ/QJ-type continues. A 3-bet also prevents us from playing a multiway pot out of position with a hand that can be dominated less often when heads-up.

Pocket eights is an extremely strong hand preflop, crushing typical BB squeeze ranges which are polarized between value (QQ+, AK) and bluffs (suited connectors, Ax). FiveTwo's raise to 1300 is a large squeeze (4.3x over my 300 open), but BB position allows wide ranges. Folding is far too tight; calling is passive multiway with Minni still involved (her calling range is wide but she folds to aggression often per history). 4-betting to ~3x isolates, applies fold equity (FiveTwo folded to 3bets before, e.g., Hand 22), gets value from worse pairs/Ax, and takes initiative. 3900 commits ~45% of stack but leaves room postflop; pot odds post-4bet are compelling for hero calls.

I called the 300 open originally to see a flop cheaply with K♥7♥, but the action escalated — FiveTwo 3-bet to 1300 and Elon 4-bet to 3900. I would need to call 3600 more into the current ~5550 pot (making the pot ~9150 after my call), so I’d need roughly 39% equity to break even. K7s has poor multiway equity here and is frequently dominated by 3‑ and 4‑betting ranges; I’m out of position to both opponents and have little fold equity. Shoving all‑in isn’t attractive either (Elon’s line looks value‑heavy and both players can call with many better hands). Preserve my ~63 BB stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Elon’s 4-bet to 3900 is very large relative to his ~50bb stack and leaves him only ~1075 behind, so this is effectively a commit-or-fold spot for him. With AcQc we have strong blockers to AA/AK, solid equity when called, and (importantly) there’s meaningful dead money in the pot from Minni’s cold call plus blinds. If we just call, we let Elon realize a flop and potentially save his last 1075 with bluffs/marginal hands; by 5-betting we maximize fold equity immediately and deny that option, while still being able to win a big pot when he continues wider than only QQ+/AK.

Facing a 5-bet shove from FiveTwo in the BB after my open-raise and 4-bet, with 88. FiveTwo's range is extremely strong and polarized: QQ+, AK, AQs, possibly JJ/TT as bluffs but unlikely given sizing and history of aggression (e.g., Hand 6, 16, 22). 88 has poor equity against this (~25-35% vs top range) and no fold equity left. Pot odds are ~4:1 (2600 to call into 13k pot), but postflop OOP (I act first as MP) with only ~2.4k behind (25bb effective), it's a nightmare spot to realize equity. Stacks are ~50bb deep, ICM/tournament considerations favor preserving stack for better spots over hero-calling medium pairs. History shows FiveTwo capable of value-heavy lines; folding 88 is standard GTO play here to avoid cooler variance.

UTG position in 6-handed play with 6d9d, a suited connector. While speculative and playable in late position, it is too weak to open from early position. Multiway pots are likely given opponents' tendencies to limp/call, and our hand performs poorly OOP postflop without hits. Effective stack ~50BB, preserve chips for premium spots to maximize long-term stack growth. Standard fold.

As♠Qs♠ in MP after UTG folded — a strong, standard opening hand. I want to take the initiative, build value and avoid giving cheap odds to the blinds and button. With a 63BB stack I can comfortably navigate postflop. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3xBB (300) to thin the field, define opponents’ ranges, and often play heads-up. If called I’ll continuation-bet on favorable boards; if I face a sizeable 3-bet I’ll evaluate based on sizing and opponent — call against standard 3-bets, fold to extremely large 4-bets/shoves from very tight players.

Ad5d in the cutoff facing a middle position open to 300. Suited ace has good playability with nut flush potential. I have position on the raiser and a healthy stack. Calling is standard here to see a flop - not strong enough to 3-bet for value but too good to fold with position.

I am on the button with Pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand. Minni raised to 3x the big blind and Claude called. With one raiser and one caller, I need to put in a significant 3-bet to build the pot for value and to avoid a multi-way pot where the equity of my kings would decrease. A raise to 1100 (approx 3.5x the initial raise plus the caller) is a standard and effective sizing in this position.

We’re in the small blind with 78o facing an open to 300, a cold-call, and then a sizable button 3-bet to 1100. Out of position versus a range that is now much stronger and more condensed, 78o performs poorly: it has no high-card playability, weak reverse-implied odds when we make marginal pairs, and it’s not a good 4-bet bluff candidate (no blockers to A/K/Q). Calling also invites the BB to come along or squeeze, worsening our realization. Best stack-preserving play is to fold and give up the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o facing a raise to 300 from Minni, a call from Claude, and a 3-bet squeeze to 1100 from Flash on the Button. The pot is 1850 and it costs me 1000 to call. My hand is very weak relative to a 3-bet range, and I have the original raiser and a caller left to act behind me, who could re-raise or call. Calling here would put me out of position in a large pot with a dominated hand that rarely flops well. The equity required (approx 26%) is not there against a strong range. The prudent play is to fold.

AQs is a top-tier hand and I was the original raiser, so this is a good spot to 4-bet for value and to isolate the button rather than playing a bloated three-way pot out of position. The button’s 3-bet to 1,100 is sizable but not huge — sizing a 4-bet to ~3x the 3-bet (~3,300 total) gives fold equity vs weaker Ax and broadway hands and will often fold out Claude (the cutoff caller), leaving a cleaner heads-up pot. Stack depth (6,000) supports a 3,300 sizing: it leaves enough behind to maneuver postflop (~2.7k) while applying pressure. Calling 800 more to play multiway OOP is doable but much less profitable long-term here. I’ll 4-bet to 3,300 to isolate and take the initiative (or get a clear action from the button).

Facing a 4-bet from Minni after Flash 3-bet. Minni's 4-bet to 3300 represents a very strong range (typically AA, KK, QQ, AK). My A5s is crushed by this range - dominated by AK/AQ and way behind big pairs. The suited ace loses most of its playability when I'm likely dominated. Plus Flash is still behind me. Easy fold - not getting the right odds or equity to continue.

I hold Pocket Kings, the second-strongest starting hand. After my initial 3-bet to 1100, Minni has 4-bet to 3300. With only 3000 chips remaining in her stack and 6850 in mine, this is a clear situation to go all-in. Pocket Kings are almost always a 'get-it-in' hand pre-flop in a 6-max game. Shoving ensures I maximize value against hands like QQ, JJ, or AK and prevents me from being outplayed on an Ace-high flop. If Minni has Aces, it is a standard cooler, but statistically, I am far ahead of her overall 4-betting range.

I opened to 300 and 4-bet to 3,300 with AsQs to isolate the button. Flash has now shoved to 7,950 leaving me 3,000 to call. If I call I will commit my remaining 3,000, bringing my total in the pot to 6,300 (I’m effectively all‑in). The current pot is 11,700, so after my call the pot will be ~14,700; I need ~20.4% equity to break even on a call (3,000 / 14,700). AQs suited is a strong hand — it is behind only the very top of a shove range (AA/KK) but has reasonable equity versus a polarized 5‑bet shove that contains AK, AQs/AKo, and some bluffs. I estimate AQs has roughly mid‑20s% equity vs a realistic shove range, which clears the ~20% pot‑odds threshold. Even if Flash is somewhat tight, the pot odds + my hand quality + the fact I’m already committed make a call correct here. I will call and put my remaining chips in the pot (all‑in).
J5o is a weak hand from UTG position. In early position, I need stronger holdings to open. This hand plays poorly postflop and has limited potential. Easy fold.

I have been dealt the best possible starting hand, Pocket Aces (Ac, As). I am in UTG+1. The previous hand was a massive pot where I likely doubled up or took a significant chunk from Minni (who is now out or significantly short-stacked, as she is not listed in the active 5 players). My stack is healthy at 14,700. My goal is to build a pot while not immediately scaring everyone away, though with Aces, I'm happy to get it all in pre-flop if someone wants to dance. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to define the range and extract value.

5-handed, ranges are wider and we’re on the button getting a very playable price: 300 to win 750 (need ~29% equity). A6o isn’t a great 3-bet candidate against an aggressive opener who can 4-bet, and building a big pot with a dominated offsuit ace is unnecessary. Flatting keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity in position, and we can comfortably fold to heavy pressure on A-high runouts where kicker issues arise.

I hold 2d 6c in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from UTG+1 (Flash) and a call from the Button (FiveTwo), I am out of position against two players with ranges likely dominating mine. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.6 to 1, the poor playability of the hand and positional disadvantage make it impossible to realize equity profitably. Folding is the only standard play here to preserve my stack.

Preflop in the big blind with 9d2h, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing a raise from early position (Flash UTG+1) and a button call from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in prior hands. Pot odds to call 200 into 750 (~3.75:1) are insufficient given the dominated range and multiway pot risk. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. My stack is 4875 (~49BB), no need to speculate here.

I hold Pocket Aces on a Jack-high paired board (Jc, 6d, Jd). While the paired Jacks are a slight concern as they are within FiveTwo's flatting range from the button, I still hold the best possible overpair. I need to bet for value against flush draws, smaller pocket pairs, or a six. A standard continuation bet of slightly over half the pot is appropriate here to maintain initiative and extract value.

We’re heads-up in position and this board heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range, so a small c-bet is expected. Our hand (JJ66A) is a solid bluff-catcher/value hand versus his entire range of overcards and pocket pairs, but it’s not strong enough to raise because most hands that continue aggressively (Jx, rare 66) have us in bad shape while we fold out the bluffs we want to keep in. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity, and lets us re-evaluate on the turn with position.

I hold Pocket Aces on a board of Jc-6d-Jd-3c. The board is paired with Jacks, which is a potential threat, but I still have the best possible overpair. FiveTwo called pre-flop and on the flop, which could indicate a Jack, but it's more likely to represent a mid-pair (77-TT) or a diamond flush draw. I need to bet here to extract value from those hands and to charge the flush draws for the river. A bet of around 60% of the pot is appropriate to maintain pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I'm actually behind a Jack.

We have Jacks and sixes with an ace kicker on a paired board (J-J-6-3), which is a solid bluff-catcher in position but not strong enough to raise for value. If we raise, we mostly fold out the hands we beat (AK/AQ/KQ, random floats) and get action from hands that crush us (any Jx, occasional 66/33). Flash’s turn sizing (900 into 1550) can still be a normal barrel with overpairs and overcards, and we’re getting a good price: 900 to win 3350 (~27% needed). Calling keeps his bluffs in, controls the pot, and lets us make a better river decision based on sizing/runout.

I hold Pocket Aces. The board is Jc-6d-Jd-3c-Qc. I have been the aggressor throughout the hand. FiveTwo has called my pre-flop raise and my bets on the flop and turn. The presence of two Jacks on the board is the primary concern, but FiveTwo's passive calling line often represents a medium pocket pair (77-TT) or a draw rather than trips. The Queen on the river is an interesting card; it might have improved a hand like KQ or QTs, which I still beat, or it could be a scare card. I will place a value bet of 1200 (approx 35% of the pot). This sizing is designed to extract value from hands like 88-TT or a Queen that might feel compelled to call, while not losing too much if FiveTwo has been slow-playing a Jack.

River: Jc 6d Jd 3c Qc. With Ad6s we have J-J-6-6-A (two pair) — a strong bluff-catcher but not a hand that wants to raise.

Flash’s line (smallish 3-barrel: 400 / 900 / 1200) is consistent with (1) value hands like Qx that just improved, some Jx, occasional slow-played boats, and some club draws; and (2) bluffs that keep firing because the Q is a good pressure card for our capped “one-pair/underpair” region.

Despite the Q improving part of his range, our hand is still near the top of what we arrive here with after calling twice (we beat all pure overcards like AK/AT, many medium pairs that choose to thin value/bluff, and missed non-club draws), and we’re getting an excellent price (1200 to win a large pot; we don’t need to be good very often). Raising would only get action from hands that have us crushed (Jx, Qx, flushes/boats), so we maximize EV by bluff-catching.

Call.

Td 6d is a weak hand to open from Under the Gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game. Although suited, the gap is significant and it doesn't play well against the likely calling ranges of the remaining players. Given the current chip lead, there's no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings in early position.

5-handed after UTG folds, pocket 3s is strong enough to open from UTG+1. Raising rather than limping gives us initiative, can win the blinds pre, and keeps our range uncapped. We’ll use a standard small open (2.5bb) to risk little while still applying pressure, and we can comfortably fold if we face a large 3-bet/jam from the shorter SB stack.

I hold Qh 2d on the Button. This is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in early/middle position, I am well behind their opening range. The hand has poor playability and equity. There are no strategic reasons to float or 3-bet bluff with this specific holding. The correct play is to fold.

Pre-flop in the small blind facing an early position raise to 250 (2.5x) from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands. My hand is K3o, offsuit with a weak kicker, offering poor equity against an EP raising range (likely 10-15% hands like pairs, suited connectors, broadways). Calling 200 OOP builds a pot with a hand that plays poorly post-flop and risks domination by better kings. 3-betting is unprofitable without fold equity or blockers. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a multi-handed tournament, aligning with GTO principles for maximizing EV over 150 hands.

Q5 suited in the BB facing a min-raise of 250 from FiveTwo. I need 150 to call into a pot of 400, giving me ~2.7:1 odds. This is a reasonable BB defense with suited cards - I can hit a queen, flush draw, or improve in multiple ways. Folding would be too tight here.

I flopped bottom pair with Q5d on a AKQ board with two clubs. This is a dangerous board for my marginal hand - any A, K, JT (straight), or club draws have me in bad shape. As the BB facing a preflop raiser, I should check and see what FiveTwo does. My pair is weak and I'm mostly looking to get to showdown cheaply or fold to aggression.

Claude’s BB flat range is wide and contains lots of medium/low pairs and suited junk that misses this A-K-Q texture. As the preflop raiser I have a big range advantage here (lots of Ax/Kx/Qx, strong broadways), so a small continuation bet should generate plenty of folds without risking much. With 33 and no meaningful backdoors, I’m betting purely for fold equity and will comfortably give up versus a raise.

I have bottom pair (queens) with a weak kicker on AKQ two-club board. FiveTwo's 1/3 pot bet of 175 gives me excellent pot odds - 175 to win 725 (over 4:1). While my hand is marginal and I'm behind to any A or K, the small bet size suggests either a draw, a weak made hand, or a probing bet. I can call cheaply and re-evaluate on the turn. Folding would be too tight given the odds.

The turn brought Kc, giving the board three clubs and a paired King. My pair of Queens with a 5 kicker is now very weak. Anyone with a K has trips, any club completes a flush, and the board is extremely dangerous. I have showdown value but should check - betting would only get called by hands that beat me (trips, flushes, full houses, two pair). I'll check and hope to see a cheap showdown.

With 33 on A-K-Q-K (two clubs) we have essentially zero showdown value: if we check back we’re almost never winning at river. Our best path is to keep leveraging our preflop range advantage and the very credible story of strong Kx (AK/KQ/KK) or boats after the king pairs. Claude’s flop check-call range contains a lot of one-pair/two-pair type hands (Qx, weaker Ax) and some floats that are now under heavy pressure on this paired board. A medium-large turn barrel should fold out a meaningful chunk of that range while not risking an overbet-sized amount with pure air. Plan: bet now; if called, continue selectively on scary rivers (A/K/Q/club/J/T) and be ready to give up on some total bricks versus strong continued resistance.

Board is Kh Ac Qc Kc - extremely dangerous. I have a pair of Queens with weak kicker. FiveTwo betting 525 into 850 (about 62% pot) on this scary turn. The board now has: paired Kings, three clubs (flush possible), AKQ broadway. My weak pair of Queens loses to any King (trips), any flush, any Ace (two pair), JT (straight). Even with ~2.7:1 pot odds, my hand is too weak and the board too dangerous. FiveTwo's continued aggression suggests strength. Folding is correct here.

5-handed UTG still needs a reasonably strong, playable opening range because we’ll often be out of position versus multiple callers and face 3-bets from the button/blinds. T5s is too close to the bottom: it’s frequently dominated when we make a pair (T-x, 5-x) and doesn’t realize equity well OOP in deeper stacks. With no dead money beyond blinds, folding avoids marginal -EV spots and keeps my UTG opens stronger.

I am in UTG+1 holding Qs 4h, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. With three players yet to act behind me, opening the pot or limping would be a negative EV play. The standard and optimal strategy for this hand in this position is to fold immediately.

A-Ten offsuit is a strong hand on the button, especially after two folds from UTG and UTG+1. Position is excellent for postflop play. Raising to steal the blinds or build the pot with initiative. Standard 3x raise to 300 total. Opponents behind have deep stacks but history shows frequent preflop folds. High equity if called.

Holding 4c6c in the small blind facing a button raise to 300. While suited connectors have some playability, 46s is on the weaker end and I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. The pot odds (~1.8:1) aren't compelling enough for this marginal hand. Better to save chips and wait for a stronger spot.

I am in the Big Blind with K3o. Elon (Button) has raised to 300. The pot is 450 and it costs 200 more to call. Given the pot odds (2.25 to 1) and the fact that Elon's button raising range is wide, calling is a marginal but acceptable defense. However, K3o plays poorly post-flop, and Elon's remaining stack is quite short (4525 total), which limits our implied odds. Looking at the tournament status, I have a healthy lead in chips (17,550). I will choose to fold this hand to avoid playing a weak king out of position against a short stack who might be looking to commit chips on many boards.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with King-Seven offsuit (Kh 7d). This is a statistically weak hand that falls well outside a standard opening range from early position. Even in a 5-handed game, UTG ranges should be tighter, typically composed of pairs, broadway cards, and suited connectors. K7o is easily dominated by opponents' calling ranges (KQ, KJ, KT, A7+) and has poor playability post-flop. With a healthy stack of roughly 100 big blinds, there is no pressure to make loose steals from this position. The correct and disciplined play is to fold.

Preflop, UTG+1 position with Qc8h offsuit is a very weak hand. Early position requires strong ranges to play profitably, especially with 5 active players and deep stacks. Folding avoids difficult postflop spots and preserves stack for better opportunities. No reads from this hand yet, but history shows aggressive play from others warranting tight preflop play.

On the button with 96 suited hearts after UTG and UTG+1 folded. This is a great stealing spot - only the blinds left. 96s has decent playability with flush potential and some straight possibilities. Standard button raise to steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. Claude has opened from the Button for 250. While Claude has been active in late positions, T5o is far too weak to defend out of position. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the lead, and 3-betting is too risky with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We’re facing a 2.5bb button open and are getting a decent price (150 to win a pot that will be 550 if we call), but 83o is a very low-quality defend: it’s frequently dominated by Claude’s opening range (better 8x, all broadways), has poor playability OOP, and tends to realize equity badly without strong connectivity or suitedness. With no compelling read that Claude is opening extremely wide and over-folding postflop, this is a small spot to conserve chips rather than take a thin, hard-to-realize defend.

UTG position with 2s3d offsuit is an extremely weak hand with no high card value, straight potential, or suitedness for flush draws. Standard play is to fold preflop to preserve stack and wait for premium hands, especially in a tournament with 5 active players and deep stacks. Raising or calling would be -EV and risks unnecessary chips early in the hand sequence.
T2 suited is a very weak hand, especially from early position (UTG+1). This hand has poor playability and limited potential even when suited. Standard play is to fold here and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Even with the action folded to me, this hand does not possess enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call against the remaining players in the blinds, especially with deep stacks behind me. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folded to me in the small blind versus a single big blind defender. 6♥4♣ is too weak to build a pot out of position, especially if Pro defends reasonably and we end up in tough postflop spots. Completing is cheap (only 50 more) and likely gets to a flop with Pro checking a lot; we can then take selective stabs on favorable low/connected boards and otherwise keep losses minimal. If Pro isolates/raises, we can comfortably fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Q9s facing a limp from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). We are deep-stacked (100BB+). Q9s is a decent suited hand that plays well post-flop, but it is not a premium hand that mandates building a large pot pre-flop. By checking, I exercise my option to see a free flop in position, keeping the pot small and controlling variance. Raising would be a valid aggressive option to punish the limp, but given the deep stacks and the decent playability of my hand, checking is a standard and safe play to realize equity.

We completed SB and are now out of position in a limped pot. On J♣7♠6♣ we have a small pair (6h) with weak kicker and no strong draw. Leading doesn’t accomplish much: better hands (Jx, 7x, overpairs) won’t fold, and worse hands often have overcards that can float or raise. Checking keeps the pot small, lets Pro stab with air frequently in a BB-checkback range, and we can decide based on sizing/turn texture whether to call once or fold.

I hold Queen-Nine of Clubs on a 7s 6c Jc board, giving me a strong flush draw and two overcards. The pot is small (200 chips) after a pre-flop limp from the Small Blind. Being in position, checking back is a prudent play. It guarantees I see the turn card for free, realizing my equity without the risk of facing a check-raise on this wet board texture. Additionally, checking balances my range and can disguise the strength of my hand if the flush completes on the turn, potentially allowing me to extract more value later.

Turn pairs the jack, and with 6h we now have two pair (jacks and sixes). After Pro checked back the flop in a limped pot, his range is heavy on overcards, random floats, 7x/6x, and club draws; there are fewer strong Jx hands than if he’d c-bet flop. Leading here gets value from worse pairs (7x/6x), charges club draws/overcards, and denies a free river, while keeping the pot small enough that we can comfortably fold if we face a big raise representing trips/full houses.

I am holding Queen-Nine of Clubs on a board of 7s 6c Jc Jd. I have a Queen-high flush draw and two overcards. The opponent (SB) limped pre-flop, checked the flop, and is now leading for 125 into a pot of 200 on the turn. The pot odds are 125 to call into a total pot of 450 (approx 28%). I have 9 flush outs (approx 20% equity) plus potential outs with my overcards, putting my total equity close to the required odds. Given the implied odds of hitting the flush in position against a deep stack, and the possibility that the opponent is stabbing with a weak pair or draw, calling is the most +EV decision. Raising risks isolating myself against trips (Jx), while folding relinquishes too much equity.

River is a total brick (2h) after we led small on the turn and got called. With 6h4c on J♣7♠6♣ J♦ 2h we have two pair (Jacks and Sixes) with a 7 kicker. Pro’s line (check flop, call small turn bet) heavily weights one-pair hands like 7x/6x, small pocket pairs, and some A-high floats; there are some slowplayed Jx combos, but not many compared to if he’d bet flop. Checking risks him checking back a lot of worse hands, while a small value bet should get crying calls from 7x/6x/88–TT and occasionally induce mistakes from A-high. I’ll use a thin, blocker-sized value bet and comfortably fold to a raise since value-raises will be trips/full houses.

I hold Queen-Nine of Clubs on a board of 7s 6c Jc Jd 2h. My flush draw missed on the river, leaving me with just Queen-high. The opponent (Small Blind) limped pre-flop, checked the flop, led the turn when the board paired, and is now betting small (200 into 450) on the river. This line and sizing are often indicative of a thin value bet with a marginal made hand like a pair of 6s, 7s, or a weak Jack, rather than a polarized bluff. Crucially, holding the Qc and 9c blocks the most natural bluffs (missed club draws), making it more likely the opponent holds a value hand or at least Ace-high/King-high which beats me. Getting ~23% pot odds is tempting, but Queen-high rarely wins at showdown against a double-barreling opponent in this spot. The risk-reward for a bluff raise is poor in a small limped pot. I will fold.
Q7 offsuit in UTG is a weak hand to open. From early position, I need stronger holdings. This hand is easily dominated and plays poorly multi-way. Standard fold.

I hold King of Diamonds and Ace of Hearts (AKo), which is a premier starting hand. In a 5-handed game, this is extremely strong. Claude has already folded from UTG. I should raise to build the pot and extract value from weaker hands that might call, or simply take down the blinds. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate.

Flash is opening to 3bb from early position with an aggressive profile. On the button, 7♠6♠ has good playability and implied odds in position versus a likely wide range, and calling keeps the pot manageable against a player who can 4-bet frequently if we 3-bet bluff. With relatively shorter stacks in the blinds, a flat also lets us comfortably fold if we face a squeeze/shove without having committed extra chips.

I hold T8o in the Small Blind facing a 3BB open from UTG+1 (Flash) and a call from the Button (FiveTwo). Although the pot odds are roughly 3:1 to call, T8o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot where I am likely dominated by hands like AT, KT, or pairs. The risk of reverse implied odds and the difficulty of realizing equity from the worst position make folding the prudent decision.

Jd9d is a strong speculative hand with suited connector potential for straights and flushes, excellent for multi-way pots post-flop due to implied odds. Facing a raise from Flash (early/mid position, likely decent range) and a button call from FiveTwo (wide calling range), pot odds are favorable: calling 200 more into a 750 pot (pot will be 950, getting ~3.75:1). My stack is 4875 (~49BB), so affordable. Folding is too tight from BB; raising is unnecessary and risky OOP with a hand that plays better post-flop. Calling keeps the pot multi-way, realizes equity, and positions well for set-mining or drawing hands.

Top two pair (JJ99) with a jack-high board that has some straight draw possibilities (e.g., 67, 78, T7, T8). This is an extremely strong hand heads-up or multiway, nearly the nuts against most ranges. Flash raised pre-flop from early position (likely strong range: pairs, broadways), FiveTwo called from late position (wider range). Pot is 950, I act first OOP in a multiway pot. Checking risks giving free cards to draws and missing value; betting extracts value from worse pairs, overpairs, and draws while protecting against straight/fill draws. FiveTwo and Flash have shown aggressive tendencies in prior hands (e.g., Flash frequent raiser, FiveTwo calls stations but also bets draws). Size to ~2/3 pot (650) balances value and protection without overbetting OOP. My stack (4675, ~47BB) supports this; implied odds were good pre-flop, now realize equity with monster.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which was a strong hand pre-flop. However, the flop of Js 5h 9c does not connect with my hand. Elon, who was the big blind, has led out with a bet of 650 into a pot of 950. This 'donk bet' often signifies a pair of Jacks or at least a nine, or perhaps a straight draw. With another player (FiveTwo) still to act behind me, calling is risky as I only have Ace-high and two overcards. I would need to hit an Ace or a King to likely have the best hand, and even then, I could be behind a set or two pair. Given the board texture and the action, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to use my stack advantage.

We’re facing a fairly big donk/lead from Elon (650 into ~1.0–1.6k) after he defended preflop and Flash has already folded, so his range is weighted toward made hands (Jx/9x/5x, occasionally slowplayed sets) and some protection/semi-bluffs. With 7♠6♠ on J♠5♥9♣ we have no pair and only a gutshot to the 8 (4 outs) plus a backdoor spade draw—our direct equity and pot odds don’t justify a call. Raising is also unattractive because Elon is short and will often respond by jamming or calling with most pairs, giving us poor fold equity and forcing us to burn chips. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am holding Ace-Five suited (Ad 5d) in the UTG position in a 5-handed game. This is a strong starting hand with both nut-flush and straight potential, making it a standard opening raise. Given the current table dynamics and stack sizes, I will open for 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and take control of the hand as the chip leader.

Flash opened UTG to 3bb in a 5-handed game, which is typically a fairly strong/tight range. T5o has very poor playability out of position, is dominated by a large portion of that range (Tx, pairs, broadways), and offers no useful blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff. With players still behind (Button and blinds), the multiway/3-bet risk is high and implied odds are bad. Best decision is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold Ten of Spades and Seven of Diamonds (T7o) on the Button. The action began with Flash raising to 300 (3BB) from UTG. Given that T7o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity, it falls well outside the range of hands I should defend with, especially against an early position open from the chip leader. Even with the positional advantage, calling or 3-betting is not profitable here. The standard and correct play is to fold.

K2o is a very weak offsuit hand from the small blind facing an UTG raise from Flash, who likely has a strong range (pairs 88+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways). My hand has poor postflop playability, dominated equity (e.g., vs KQ, AK), and low blocker value. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 (~36% equity needed, but I have <30% vs UTG range). No position after flop, BB can squeeze. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. History shows Flash aggressive but profitable folds here maximize EV.

Holding 3s5s in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. This is a very weak hand - low suited connectors. UTG raises typically represent strong ranges. While I'm getting decent odds (200 to win 450), 35s plays poorly postflop against a strong range and will often make weak pairs/weak flushes when it connects. Folding is the disciplined play here.

5-handed but still UTG with 9-6 offsuit is too weak: it’s dominated by a lot of continuing ranges, plays poorly multiway, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position. With deep stacks there’s no need to force marginal opens; I’ll preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in UTG+1 (Cutoff effectively in 5-handed play) with Ace-Jack suited, a strong starting hand. With 5 players left, this is well within my opening range. I want to build the pot and take the initiative rather than limping, which would invite others to see a cheap flop. A standard raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) balances value extraction with pot control.

KQs is a premium suited broadway hand on the button, with excellent playability, equity vs calling ranges, and fold equity. Pro's UTG+1 raise indicates a strong range, but button 3-betting KQs is standard for value, isolation, and denying equity to calling hands. Calling risks a squeeze from blinds and plays OOP postflop. 3bet sizing to 900 (about 3.6x the open) balances value and fold equity given pot of 400. Stack depths support this aggression to build pot with strong hand.

63 suited is far too weak to call a 3-bet. Pro opened to 250 and Elon 3-bet to 900. I'd need to call 850 with a very marginal hand that plays poorly multiway and is dominated by almost any range that 3-bets. Clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten of Spades and Five of Diamonds, which is a very weak hand. There has been an open raise from Pro and a 3-bet from Elon. Calling would require committing 800 chips into a multi-way pot out of position with a hand that has almost no post-flop value or equity against a range that includes strong hands like big pairs or high Broadway cards. Folding is the only sensible play here.

I am holding Ace-Jack suited (AJs) in the Cutoff (UTG+1) and facing a 3-bet from the Button (Elon) to 900. My initial raise was 250. Elon's 3-bet is large (3.6x), representing strength or a polarized bluff range, and he has a stack of roughly 47 BBs left. AJs is a strong hand that dominates many broadway hands Elon might 3-bet light with, but it is dominated by his value range of AK, AQ, JJ+. Folding is too tight given the positional dynamics (Cutoff vs Button) and my hand strength. 4-betting would likely isolate me against a range that crushes me or force a fold from hands I dominate. Calling allows me to see a flop with a disguised hand, although I will be out of position. The pot odds are favorable (calling 650 to win a pot that will be 1950), and the stack-to-pot ratio will be just under 2, making post-flop decisions relatively straightforward. I will call to realize my equity.

I am out of position on a dry, paired board (8c 2s 2c) against the pre-flop 3-bettor. This board texture preserves the 3-bettor's range advantage (typically overpairs like AA-99), while my range is capped as the caller. My Ace-Jack high has some showdown value against bluffs but is currently behind his value range and even his strong overcards (AK/AQ). Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands and fold out worse ones. The standard play is to check to the aggressor and evaluate their action. Checking allows me to potentially realize equity for free or decide on a call based on bet sizing.

Heads-up on the flop after my button 3bet and Pro's UTG+1 call. Board is 8c 2s 2c, extremely dry and paired, favoring my strong 3betting range (overpairs like QQ+, AK, AJs, etc.) over Pro's capped calling range. KQs is ace-high with backdoor equity but primarily air; however, as preflop aggressor in position, a continuation bet extracts value from his weaker pairs, Ax that missed, and bluffs, while denying equity to any live draws or floats. Pro's check suggests weakness or a trap with a 2x/set, but his UTG open-call range hits this board poorly (few 82, 2x combos). Half-pot or 2/3 sizing balances thin value and fold equity without overbetting into nuts. Betting 1300 (about 2/3 pot) builds the pot OOP with equity advantage, maintains initiative, and is standard on this board texture.

The opponent, Elon, 3-bet pre-flop and has now fired a large continuation bet of 1300 into a pot of 1950 (approx. 67% pot) on a dry, paired board of 8c 2s 2c. This sizing is polarizing and typically indicates a strong value hand (Overpairs like 99+, or AK/AQ) or a semi-bluff with clubs. I hold Ace-Jack suited, which is essentially a bluff-catcher here with just Ace-high. I am out of position, and my hand is dominated by a significant portion of his 3-betting value range (AK, AQ, AA-99). The effective stack size means a call here would set up an SPR of less than 1 on the turn, effectively committing me to the hand. Without a pair or a strong draw, and facing such aggression, floating out of position is negative EV. The disciplined play is to fold.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with Jc 6c. This is a weak suited hand (Jack-high, 3-gapper) that falls well outside of a standard opening range from early position. Even with 5 players, opening this hand invites domination and puts me out of position for the rest of the hand against competent deep-stacked opponents. With a healthy stack of ~89BB, there is no pressure to make loose speculative plays. Folding is the only standard option.

J3o (3d Js) is a speculative offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity. In UTG+1 position with 5 players left, including deep-stacked opponents behind (Claude 129BB, Flash 172BB, FiveTwo 141BB), opening this would be a mistake as it plays poorly postflop out of position against likely calling/raising ranges. My stack is 66BB, healthy enough to wait for premium hands. History shows aggressive play from others, but no specific read justifies entering with trash. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this tournament.

I have 2c 5d on the button - one of the weakest starting hands. While I'm in a good stealing position with only blinds left, this hand is too weak to profitably play even as a steal attempt. With a comfortable stack of 12,950, no need to risk chips with such poor holdings.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a very strong hand, especially when the action has folded to me. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the Big Blind, which is a standard opening size for me in this tournament.

Flash is opening from the small blind after folds, which is typically a very wide range even with the 3x sizing. K4o is not strong enough to 3-bet for value and plays poorly if we bloat the pot and get 4-bet/continued against a tighter range, but it has enough equity and a king blocker to defend profitably versus a wide SB open. We’ll take the good pot odds and realize equity in position postflop (as the BB) by calling.

I hold Ace-high on a King-high board after raising pre-flop. The board (Kc 2s 7s) is generally favorable for my perceived range as the initial raiser. A continuation bet here is a standard play to represent the King and fold out the opponent's weaker hands or draws. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain pressure while keeping the pot manageable in case I am called.

We flopped top pair on a dry-ish K-high board where SB’s range is very wide after opening small blind. The c-bet is only 1/3 pot, which will include a lot of auto-bets and underpairs/gutshots/backdoors. Raising K4 here is thin (we get called by better Kx and strong draws, while folding out a lot of his bluffs), so the best line is to keep his range wide and realize our equity in position by calling, then re-evaluate turn sizing/cards.

On the turn, the 5c doesn't significantly change the board texture. Having bet the flop and been called by the Big Blind, my Ace-Queen now has some showdown value but is likely behind a King or a 7. Continuing to bet (double barreling) into a range that called the flop is risky, as many of the hands that called the flop (Kx, 7x, flush draws) are unlikely to fold to a second bet. By checking, I control the size of the pot, realize my showdown value against missed draws, and potentially induce a bluff on the river or see a free showdown if the opponent checks back.

Flash’s SB open range is very wide and his small flop c-bet followed by a turn check contains a lot of give-ups, weak Kx that want pot control, and spade/straight draws. With top pair (but a weak kicker), I don’t want to bloat the pot with a big sizing, but I do want to deny equity to flush draws/overcards and get thin value from 7x/5x/2x and some worse kings. A small-to-medium turn stab accomplishes that and also picks up the pot immediately versus his air. If I face a big check-raise on this texture, I can comfortably fold K4.

On the turn, after I checked, FiveTwo bet 500 into a pot of 1200. This bet size is relatively small (about 41% of the pot), which could indicate a 'stab' at the pot now that I have shown weakness by checking. While I am behind any King or 7, my Ace-Queen still has some showdown value against his potential bluffs or semi-bluffs (like spade draws or straight draws like 86s). The pot odds are favorable—I need to be right about 23% of the time to justify a call. Given my stack size and the price, I will call to see a river and potentially catch a bluff or hit one of my overcards (Ace or Queen).

The river 9h is a brick that doesn't complete any likely draws (the spade and club draws both missed). My Ace-Queen has showdown value against those missed draws, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, as only better hands are likely to call. By checking, I can reach showdown cheaply if my opponent checks back, or I can evaluate their bet size to determine if they are bluffing with those missed draws.

River goes check to us in a pot of 2200 after he bet/called flop and check/called our turn stab. With Kh4s we have top pair but a very weak kicker, and Flash’s line contains a lot of Kx that continue on flop and can comfortably call turn (KQ/KJ/KT/K9/K8 etc.), all of which beat us. The hands we target for thin value (7x/5x/2x/pocket pairs/missed spades) either often won’t pay off on the river or we already beat them at showdown, while betting risks getting called by better and/or raised by slowplayed two-pair/sets. Best EV is to take the free showdown and avoid turning a bluff-catcher into a thin/losing value bet.

9s7s is a speculative suited connector with a gap, playable in late position or multiway pots for implied odds, but from UTG in a 5-handed tournament (66bb deep), it's too weak to open. Vulnerable to 3-bets from strong ranges behind, and doesn't play well postflop out of position if called. Better to wait for stronger hands to build stack without high risk early in the tournament. Standard fold.
AJo is a strong hand in UTG+1. With Elon already folded, I should open raise to 250-300 to build the pot and narrow the field. This is a standard opening hand from early-middle position.

I am holding Ten of Hearts and Four of Spades on the button. Claude has raised from UTG+1 to 250. My hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially against an early-position opening range. Given the strength of the raise and my poor holding, there is no strategic reason to call or 3-bet. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision.

Facing an UTG+1 open from Claude while we’re in the small blind with Q2o, we’ll be out of position the entire hand and risk getting squeezed by the big blind. Although the pot odds to complete are tempting, Q2o has poor playability and reverse implied odds versus an early-position range, making a defend (call or 3-bet bluff) lower EV than simply preserving stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh Ts facing a 2.5x open raise from Claude in the Cutoff (UTG+1). The pot is 400 chips and I need to call 150, giving me pot odds of roughly 27% (150/550). QTo is a reasonable defending hand against a Cutoff opening range, although playing out of position is a disadvantage. Given the good price and acceptable equity against a standard CO range, calling is the standard play to realize equity. Folding is too tight, and 3-betting turns my hand into a bluff that is uncomfortable if called. I will call and evaluate the flop.

I hold QhTs on a low, wet flop of 4c 5d 7d. I have completely missed the board, holding only two overcards. As the preflop caller in the Big Blind playing out of position against the preflop aggressor (Claude), the standard play is to check to the raiser. Leading out (donk betting) is not advisable as I have little equity and do not want to build a pot with Queen-high. I will check and evaluate Claude's action; generally, I will fold to a standard continuation bet given the lack of draws or pair.

I missed this low connected flop completely with AJo - just two overcards. However, Pro checked to me and I was the preflop aggressor. A continuation bet here makes sense to take down the pot or get value from draws. Betting around 60% pot (~330) is reasonable.

I hold Queen-high (Qh Ts) on a low, wet flop of 4c 5d 7d. I have missed the board completely, having no pair and no draw. Claude, the preflop aggressor, has bet 330 into a pot of 550 (approx. 60% pot). This board texture connects well with many hands, but not mine. Playing out of position with just overcards and no backdoor draws against a sizable continuation bet is not profitable. I do not have the required equity (approx. 27%) to call, nor is this a good spot to bluff raise. The correct play is to fold.
9d3d is a very weak hand to open from UTG position. Even suited, this is not playable from early position. Folding is the clear play here.

I am in UTG+1 with Queen-Eight offsuit. In a five-handed game, this hand is below the threshold for an opening range from early position.
