While I have a healthy stack, there is no reason to play a marginal hand like this out of position against aggressive opponents like FiveTwo and Elon. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my tournament stack for higher-equity opportunities.

Button vs blinds after two folds is a prime steal spot. Even though K2o is weak, we have position, a king blocker reduces the strongest defending/3-betting combos, and both blinds have medium stacks that will often defend/fold straightforwardly. Raising is higher EV than limping (which invites multiway and reduces fold equity) and better than folding given the dead money in the pot.

I hold Ace-King suited (As Ks) in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Given that button opening ranges are typically wide and I am out of position, the standard optimal play is to 3-bet for value. In Hand #22, I successfully 3-bet FiveTwo's 250 open to 800 and he folded, suggesting he respects re-raises. I will size my 3-bet to 900 (approx 3.6x the open) to build the pot, isolate the opener, and deny equity to the Big Blind.

Preflop in BB with 56o, a weak offsuit connector. Facing a button raise to 250 from FiveTwo and a 3-bet to 900 from Pro in SB. Pot is 1250, calling 800 commits over 12% of my stack for poor pot odds and equity against likely strong ranges from both. 56o plays terribly OOP postflop with little showdown value or playability. Opponents have shown aggression but no need to speculate here early in tournament. Clear fold to preserve stack.

We opened very wide on the button, but Pro’s small-blind 3-bet to 900 creates a low SPR pot where K2o performs poorly: it’s dominated by most of his value and many of his light 3-bets (Kx/Qx), and it can’t profitably continue versus frequent c-bets. A 4-bet bluff would need very high immediate fold equity (roughly ~60%+) and risks getting jammed on by an aggressive SB with a shortish 78bb stack. Best EV is to abandon the steal and preserve our stack for better spots.

In a 5-handed game, UTG is essentially the Hijack position. Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) is at the lower end of a standard opening range from this position, but as the table chip leader, I want to maintain an aggressive image and apply pressure. My previous opens have consistently been to 300 chips (3x the big blind), so I will maintain that sizing to avoid giving away information about my hand strength.

5-handed and Flash has opened UTG to 3bb, which is likely fairly wide. AsQs plays very well versus that range, dominates a lot of his continues (worse Ax, Qx, suited broadways), and benefits from taking initiative and thinning the field with players still behind (BTN/SB/BB). I’ll 3-bet to a sizing that pressures calls but still allows postflop maneuvering at this depth.

I hold 9h 5d on the Button, which is a very weak hand. The action in front of me is heavy: Flash opened from UTG to 300, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 900 from UTG+1. These early position raises typically indicate strong ranges (e.g., high pairs, AK, AQ). Calling a 3-bet with 95 offsuit is not profitable due to the low equity of my hand against their ranges and the likelihood of being dominated. The cost to call is significant relative to my hand strength. Therefore, the only correct play is to fold.

2d8s is a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Facing an UTG open-raise to 300 followed by a 3-bet to 900 from UTG+1, villain ranges are extremely strong (QQ+, AK likely). I'm OOP in SB with poor pot odds (calling 850 into 1350 pot, ~2.6:1, but massive postflop disadvantages). No reads from history suggest loosening up here; preserve stack for better spots in tournament.

I have pocket Aces - the best starting hand in poker. Flash opened to 300 and FiveTwo 3-bet to 900. This is a premium spot to 4-bet for value. I want to build the pot with AA while still getting action. A 4-bet to around 2400-2600 (roughly 2.7-3x the 3-bet) is standard here.

I opened with A8o from UTG, which is a marginal hand. The action behind me is very strong: FiveTwo 3-bet to 900, and Claude followed with a cold 4-bet to 2500 from the Big Blind. A cold 4-bet typically indicates a premium range (QQ+, AK). My hand is significantly behind both opponents' likely ranges. There is no reason to risk a large portion of my stack with a weak Ace in this spot.

Claude’s cold 4-bet from the BB is strong, but in this configuration (UTG open + my 3-bet + dead money) he can still have some squeeze/bluff combos, not just pure value. With AsQs I block AA/QQ and some strong Ax continues, and I’m getting solid immediate odds (1600 to win 3750 ≈ 30%). Folding is too tight, and a 5-bet (especially committing) mostly gets called by the top of his range and folds out the hands I want to keep in. Calling in position keeps his bluffs in and lets me realize equity; with the low SPR I’ll generally continue aggressively on A/Q-high and favorable draw boards, and be comfortable folding on miss vs heavy pressure.

I have AA on a 4h7h6s board. FiveTwo called my 4-bet preflop, likely holding a big pair (QQ-JJ) or AK. This is a coordinated board with hearts flush draw and straight possibilities. I need to bet for value and protection. A c-bet of around 60-65% pot (~3300) should extract value from worse pairs while charging draws.

We’re in a cold 4-bet pot versus the BB, so Claude’s range is heavily weighted to AA/KK/QQ/AK with only a small portion of bluffs. On 7h6s4h our AsQs has just two overcards and no heart draw; versus overpairs we’re in rough shape, and versus AK our “outs” are mostly just Q’s (A’s can be dominated). His 3300 bet into a low-SPR pot also sets up a very natural turn jam, meaning a flop call often turns into burning 3300 and then folding most turns. With limited equity realization/implied odds and little fold equity if we raise, the best stack-maximizing play is to cut losses now.

5-handed (effectively short-handed), UTG ranges widen and A8o is strong enough to open, especially with an ace blocker that reduces opponents’ strong continue/3-bet combos. I’ll use a standard small open to apply pressure and keep my risk controlled at 125bb deep; versus a large 3-bet I can comfortably fold this offsuit ace.

I hold 6c5c, a low suited connector, in UTG+1 (Cutoff). The player in UTG (FiveTwo) has opened with a raise to 2.5BB. While I have position on the opener, there are three players left to act behind me, including two large stacks in the blinds who could easily squeeze. Additionally, my recent history against FiveTwo involves me 3-betting them with premium hands (KK, AK), so they may be more inclined to defend or 4-bet if I re-raise. Calling is risky due to the players behind, and the hand is not strong enough to profitably call an UTG open consistently without closing the action. Folding is the disciplined low-variance play.

Preflop on the button with T4s, a weak suited connector. FiveTwo raised to 250 from UTG, which is a standard 2.5x raise and deserves respect given their position. Pro folded already. While position is good and stacks are deep (~65BB effective), T4s has poor equity against an UTG opening range (~25-30% heads-up equity at best) and plays poorly multi-way if blinds defend. Pot odds are ~2.6:1 (pot 650 after call), requiring ~28% equity, but implied odds don't justify calling with this speculative hand against a strong range. Folding preserves stack for better spots; speculative calls are better OOP or multi-way with stronger connectors. Long-term EV positive to fold.

56 offsuit in the small blind facing a UTG raise. This is a weak offsuit connector that doesn't play well out of position. While the raise is only 2.5x, playing this hand OOP against an early position raiser is -EV. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 8c. FiveTwo raised to 250 from UTG. Everyone else folded. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400, giving me nearly 3:1 odds. J8 suited is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a standard opening raise at this price, even if I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Clubs and Eight of Clubs on a board of 6h-7h-3d. The pot is 550 and it is checked to me. I have missed the flop entirely, although I have two overcards to the 6 and 7. FiveTwo opened pre-flop from UTG. This board texture (6-7-3) actually favors my defending range more than a standard UTG opening range, but J8 specifically has very little equity here and no real draws. Since I am first to act and FiveTwo has position on me, checking is the standard and most prudent play to see how they react to the board.

BB’s defend range connects well with 6h7h3d (pairs, 7x/6x, straight/flush draws), so a small c-bet will face a lot of continues and some check-raises. With Ac8s I have only ace-high + a thin gutshot to the 5, no heart blocker/backdoor flush, but I do have some showdown value. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity, and avoids getting blown off my hand by a check-raise on a dynamic texture.

The turn Deuce of Clubs is a blank that doesn't improve my hand. I currently hold Jack-high with no draws. FiveTwo checked back the flop, which might indicate they missed or are playing a marginal hand cautiously. However, I have no equity to justify a bluff here, and checking remains the most prudent play. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet, hoping to see a free river card that could potentially give me a winning pair.

After checking back the flop OOP, my range is somewhat capped but I can still credibly represent overpairs/7x that chose pot-control on 6h7h3d. The turn 2c doesn’t help my actual hand (just A-high, no heart blocker), but it’s a good spot for a small delayed c-bet to (1) fold out BB’s pure overcards that have 6 outs vs me, and (2) deny equity to hands that would happily take a free river (random hearts/straight-draw fragments). I’m not trying to build a big pot—if I get raised I can just fold, and if called I can often check back many rivers unless I improve to an ace.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high (Jc 8c). The board is 6h-7h-3d-2c. I checked the flop and the turn. FiveTwo, who opened UTG and checked back the flop, has now bet 250 into a 550 pot. While the bet is small, I have zero equity and no real draw. I am currently losing to every pocket pair, every Ace-high, and even most King-high hands in FiveTwo's range. Given I have no showdown value and no outs that I can confidently rely on, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with JTo. While 5-handed play is looser than full ring, JTo is still a marginal hand to open from early position. It is easily dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, KT, and plays poorly out of position if called. Furthermore, the table has shown significant aggression recently (e.g., Claude's large squeeze in Hand #37), making it dangerous to open light. The standard and disciplined play here is to fold.

K9o is a weak offsuit hand from early position (UTG+1) in a 5-handed game. Standard play is to fold preflop to preserve chips for better opportunities. No reads or history justify playing it here. Pot is small (150), but entering with a weak hand risks tough postflop spots against deep stacks.

Pocket 8s on the button with two folds in front - this is a premium spot to raise. Medium pair plays well in position and I should raise to build the pot and potentially isolate the blinds. Standard button raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine suited, a strong hand that plays well post-flop. The Button (Claude) has opened for 2.5BB. While 3-betting is a viable option to take the initiative, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good playability and avoids bloating the pot out of position against a capable opponent. I will call the additional 200 chips to see a flop.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action (150 to win 600) and T9o plays well multiway with strong straight potential and decent top-pair/2nd-pair realizations on many boards. A squeeze here would be high-variance and less attractive with an offsuit, non-premium hand against a button open plus an SB cold call (ranges that can continue reasonably often). Defend and realize equity in position disadvantage but with great pot odds.

On a monotone flop of 4c 2c Jc, I have King-high with no club in my hand. This board is very dangerous as any club makes a flush, and I have zero equity to continue if faced with significant pressure. Being first to act against two opponents, one of whom was the pre-flop aggressor, the most standard and prudent play is to check and see how the other players react to the board texture.

We’ve flopped a made flush (T-high) on a monotone board in a multiway pot, but it’s not a hand that wants to bloat the pot OOP: plenty of higher one-club hands (A♣/K♣/Q♣/etc.) already beat us, and if we donk we often get called/raised by better while folding out the hands we’d like to keep in (one-pair/no-club hands that may stab later). Checking keeps ranges wide, lets the Button c-bet bluffs and value hands, and allows us to realize our equity while controlling pot size; we can continue versus reasonable bets and re-evaluate if faced with heavy pressure.

Monotone club board (4c-2c-Jc) and I have pocket 8s with no club. My hand is an overpair to the low cards but under the Jack. Both opponents checked to me. I should bet small for protection/value - deny equity to single club holdings and get value from worse pairs. If I get check-raised, I can fold easily. Small sizing around 250-300 is appropriate.

On this monotone flop of clubs, I have King-high with no club in my hand, which means I have no flush draw and no made hand. The Button has placed a continuation bet of 275 into a pot of 750 (total 1025). Given the texture of the board and the fact that I am out of position against two opponents, one of whom is the pre-flop aggressor, I have very little equity to continue. Any club in my opponents' hands puts me in a very difficult spot, and even if I hit a King or a Nine, the board remains extremely dangerous. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here.

We only hold one club (Tc), so this is a 9-out flush draw rather than a made hand. Claude’s sizing is very small (275 into 1025), giving us excellent immediate pot odds (~21%) to continue, and we have enough equity versus his range even without needing fold equity. Because our draw is not the nut flush (A♣/K♣/Q♣ hands can dominate us when a club hits), I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a check-raise and risk getting played back at by better one-club holdings. Calling keeps his bluffs and worse hands in, controls variance, and lets us realize our equity and reassess on the turn based on card and sizing.

Turn brings the 6♣, putting 4 clubs on board. With T♣ we now have a made flush, but it’s only a J-high flush (J♣-T♣-6♣-4♣-2♣) and is dominated by any A♣/K♣/Q♣ type holdings that are very plausible from the Button’s preflop range and flop c-bet. Betting ourselves tends to isolate against better flushes (and folds out most no-club hands that we already beat), while also exposing us to a raise in a spot where our hand can’t comfortably continue versus big pressure. Checking keeps his range widest, allows him to bet his bluffs/no-club hands sometimes, and lets us respond based on sizing (call smaller bets, consider folding to large, river-facing bets).

Four clubs on board now and I have no club. My pocket 8s are essentially worthless - any single club in opponent's hand beats me. FiveTwo checked, so I should check behind to see a free river. Betting accomplishes nothing as I only get called by hands that beat me.

River is 4♣ 2♣ J♣ 6♣ 4h and we hold T♣, so we have a made J-high flush. It’s a decent hand but clearly non-nut: any A♣/K♣/Q♣ beats us, and those are plausible from Button’s opening range. After Claude’s very small flop c-bet then turn check-back on the 4th club, he can still have some worse one-club hands that will happily check behind if we check, so we should try to realize thin value while also “blocking” him from firing a large bet that would put us in a tough spot. A small lead targets calls from worse clubs (and occasionally stubborn Jx with a club) and lets us comfortably fold if he raises (raises here skew heavily to better flushes).

Four clubs on board and I hold 8s8d with no club. Any single club beats me. FiveTwo called my flop bet on a monotone club board, strongly suggesting they have at least one club. Their small river bet (400 into 1300) looks like a value bet with a club. Getting 4.25:1 but my hand is basically worthless here - folding is correct.

Q8o is a weak offsuit hand from UTG position in a 5-handed game. Standard play is to fold trash hands early position to avoid difficult postflop situations and preserve stack for premium hands. My stack is healthy at ~65BB, no need to open wide here.

4c8c is a weak suited gapper in early position (UTG+1). While suited, the gap makes it hard to make straights and it plays poorly against calling ranges. With 4 players left to act behind me, folding is the disciplined play.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven of spades, a decent suited semi-connector in a 5-handed game. With both players before me folding, this is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the small or big blind. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to exert pressure and define my hand range.

Button open to 3bb is likely wide 5-handed. A9o is too strong to fold and calling from the SB plays poorly (out of position, invites BB to come along, and realizes equity badly). A small 3-bet pressures Flash’s wide range, often wins pre, and helps isolate to heads-up with initiative. If Flash 4-bets large we can comfortably fold; if called we can c-bet favorable textures and control pot on bad runouts.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 3s, a very weak speculative hand. The action involves a Button open to 3bb followed by a Small Blind 3-bet to 10bb. This indicates significant strength from the Small Blind. Cold calling a 3-bet with 63s is extremely unprofitable due to the high cost (10% of my stack), the likelihood of being dominated, and the risk that the original raiser (Button) may 4-bet. Despite being suited, the gap and low rank make this an easy fold.

I opened for 300 from the button with Ten-Seven of spades, and FiveTwo in the small blind has 3-bet to 1000. While I have the benefit of position, Ten-Seven suited is a speculative hand that is likely significantly behind FiveTwo's 3-betting range. The 3-bet size (3.3x my open) is healthy, and FiveTwo has shown a willingness to play strong ranges in previous hands. Given the pot odds and the likelihood of being dominated, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better spots.
A2s from UTG in a 5-handed game. Suited ace with nut flush potential is playable. I'll open to 2.5x with my healthy stack of 155BB. Standard opening raise.

I am in UTG+1 with 4c 5c, a speculative hand. Claude has opened from UTG for 2.5x. While 4c 5c has some potential, calling here is risky as it puts me in a sandwich position with three players yet to act (including an aggressive FiveTwo on the button). Claude has shown strength in previous hands (like #37), and I would be playing a marginal hand out of position. Standard poker strategy dictates a fold here to preserve chips for better spots.

K6o is too weak versus an UTG open even 5-handed: it’s frequently dominated when we make a king, has poor playability offsuit, and calling invites squeezes from the blinds. A 3-bet bluff is also marginal here against Claude’s willingness to continue/4-bet. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 6h, an unsuited connector. The UTG player (Claude) opened for 250 (2.5bb), indicating a potentially strong range. Playing 56o out of position against a UTG open is generally a losing strategy due to the lack of playability and poor equity realization. The pot odds of calling (200 to win 400) are not sufficient to justify the disadvantage of position and hand strength. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

Td3o is a trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high cards. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has shown strength in early position raises throughout the tournament history (e.g., Hands 7,9,14,15,21,29,35,39), his range is strong (premium pairs, broadway, suited connectors). Pot odds: calling 150 to win 400 (pot odds ~2.67:1, requiring ~27% equity), but Td3o has <25% equity vs. tight UTG range and poor postflop playability. No position advantage, deep stacks (63BB), better spots to defend BB with playable hands. Clear fold to preserve stack.

In a 5-handed game, 7s 8s is a strong suited connector that functions well as an opening hand from early position. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take initiative and build a pot with a hand that has significant straight and flush potential, while also putting pressure on the blinds and the short-stacked Elon.

A♠5♠ is a strong 3-bet candidate here: we have an ace blocker to reduce the chance Flash has premiums, good playability (wheel/FD potential), and we’re in position on the UTG opener. Flash has shown a willingness to fold to 3-bets in prior hands, so applying pressure preflop should be profitable and also denies the button/blinds a cheap entry. If Flash 4-bets big, we can comfortably fold; if called, we can leverage position with high-frequency c-bets on many textures and continue on boards where we pick up equity.

I am holding Q8o on the Button. The action started with a UTG raise to 300 from Flash, followed by a 3-bet to 950 from FiveTwo in UTG+1. This action sequence indicates very strong ranges from both opponents, particularly FiveTwo who is re-raising an early position opener. My hand is significantly dominated by their likely ranges (AQ, KQ, pocket pairs). There is no strategic merit in calling or 4-betting with this holding in this spot. I must fold to preserve my stack.

Preflop in the small blind with K2o, facing an UTG raise from Flash to 300 followed by a 3-bet from FiveTwo to 950. Both are early position opens/3-bets, indicating strong ranges (likely QQ+, AK, maybe some suited broadways). K2o has poor playability, dominated equity against their ranges (~20-25% vs tight 3-bet range), no position postflop, and unfavorable pot odds (calling 900 into 1400 pot gives ~2:1 implied but high risk of domination). Folding preserves stack for better spots; no reason to speculate with trash here in a tournament ICM-aware long-term.

A3o in the BB facing a 3-bet to 950. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against a 3-betting range. I'm getting ~1.65:1 odds needing about 37% equity, but A3o likely has only 30-35% against a typical 3-bet range. Not worth calling OOP with a dominated ace and weak kicker.

FiveTwo's 3-bet against my UTG open represents a strong range in this 5-handed dynamic. While 7s 8s is a strong suited connector, calling a 3-bet out of position (OOP) is generally a losing play against an aggressive opponent. My positional disadvantage makes it difficult to realize the hand's equity post-flop, and I would be forced to fold on most flops that don't directly improve my hand. Given that we are on hand 42 of 150, I prefer to fold and wait for a more profitable spot rather than committing more chips to a pot where I am likely dominated and out of position.

5-handed UTG is effectively a late-position spot; we want to keep applying pressure and stealing blinds. K6o is not premium, but it has a king blocker, can make top pair, and plays fine as a small open if we’re prepared to fold to heavy 3-bet action out of position. Use a standard open size to deny cheap realization and keep our range consistent.

I am in UTG+1 (MP) holding As2s, facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG. FiveTwo is a deep-stacked player opening from early position, which typically indicates a strong range (pairs, strong broadways, strong Aces). Although A2s has nut flush potential, it is easily dominated by UTG's Ax range (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) and plays poorly against pairs. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me (Button, SB, BB), increasing the risk of a squeeze if I flat call. A 3-bet bluff is risky against a UTG open. The most disciplined and standard play here is to fold and wait for a better spot or position.

K2 suited on the button is a marginal hand at best, but facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo who has been aggressive in early position in past hands (e.g., Hands 6, 16, 20, 27, 34). FiveTwo's UTG range is likely strong, including pairs, broadway, suited aces, etc. K2s has poor postflop playability, is often dominated by better kings, and doesn't make strong hands often. Calling 250 into 400 gives pot odds of about 1.6:1 after rake/stack considerations, but implied odds are limited due to reverse domination risk. Raising would be a bluff with a bad hand out of position postflop. Best play is to fold and wait for better spots, preserving stack for tournament survival and value opportunities in position.

Q8o in the SB facing an UTG raise of 250. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play well out of position against an early position raise. Q8o lacks the connectivity and suited potential to profitably defend here. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the big blind with Qh6h. FiveTwo raised to 250 from UTG. This is a small raise, and I only need to call 150 more to see a flop with a suited queen in a pot that will be 550. While 6-high is a weak kicker, the pot odds (150 to win 400) and the playability of suited hearts make this a reasonable defend in position post-flop, though I must play cautiously against an UTG range.

I have an open-ended straight draw (any Nine or Ace completes my straight) on a very coordinated board. The board (Tc-Ks-Jd) heavily favors FiveTwo's UTG raising range, which likely includes strong broadway cards, sets, and two-pair combinations. As the big blind, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate their bet sizing, intending to call most reasonable bets to realize my equity.

Heads-up SRP UTG vs BB on KJT is dynamic: BB has lots of pairs/draws (Jx/Tx/Q9/98/AQ), but we still retain strong Kx/overpairs/AK/KQ/KJ. With top pair (K6) we want thin value and protection versus the many turn cards that change equities (A/Q/9/club), while keeping the pot controllable with our weak kicker. A small c-bet accomplishes that and lets us fold to large check-raise pressure more comfortably.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with 8 outs to the nuts or near-nuts (any Ace or Nine). The pot odds are very favorable: I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 750, which requires roughly 21% equity. With 8 outs, I have about a 17% chance to hit on the turn and a 32% chance by the river. Given the strong implied odds if I hit my straight against an UTG range that likely includes many top pairs, two pairs, or sets on this broadway-heavy board, calling is the most standard and profitable play. Raising is unnecessary and risky as it would likely only be called by hands that have me crushed or are drawing to the same straight with better kickers.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) on a very coordinated board. Any Ace or Nine will complete a straight for me. However, this board (Tc-Ks-Jd-8c) is also very favorable for an UTG range, which likely contains many sets, two pairs, and potentially existing straights (like AQ or Q9). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to them and evaluate their action. I have sufficient equity to call most standard bet sizes to try and realize my draw, but there is no merit in leading out (donk betting) into a range that hits this board so well.

Turn 8c is a very dynamic/wet card on KJT: it completes AQ and Q9, adds more two-pair/straight-combo density (98, Q8, etc.), and makes our one-pair K6 a thin and vulnerable value hand. After our small flop c-bet gets called, Flash’s range contains plenty of hands that either improved or picked up strong equity, and betting again risks getting check-raised into a tough spot while also bloating the pot with a weak kicker. In position, the highest-EV line is to pot-control, take the free river, and be ready to bluff-catch on safe runouts versus reasonable sizing (and comfortably fold to big polarized river bets on scary cards).

I missed my open-ended straight draw (OESD) as the river board paired the Ten (Tc-Ks-Jd-8c-Td). My hand is effectively Queen-high, utilizing the pair of Tens on the board. This board is extremely well-coordinated and hits an UTG raising range very hard, containing many straights (AQ, Q9), sets (which are now full houses), and two-pair combinations. FiveTwo's check on the turn suggests a hand with some showdown value (like a King or a Jack) that was playing it safe, or a draw that missed. Since I have zero showdown value, I must decide whether to bluff. However, on such a coordinated board where nearly any piece of the board has showdown value, a bluff is unlikely to be successful against an UTG range. I will check and concede the pot, as my hand cannot win a showdown and a bluff is too low-frequency to be profitable here.

River checks through to us after we checked turn on a very dynamic KJT8 runout that now pairs the T. Our hand improved to two pair (Ks with TT on board), which is ahead of a lot of Flash’s check/call flop range that will arrive here as bluff-catchers (Jx, 8x, QQ/99, some weaker Tx that plays passively) and we also get looked up by Kx for a chop. However, bigger sizing starts isolating us versus straights (Q9/98) and boats (KT/TT/JJ/T8) and risks a tough raise spot. A small block/value bet targets the marginal made hands, keeps our loss small when we’re behind, and we can comfortably bet/fold versus any raise.

On the river, the board has paired the Ten (Tc-Ks-Jd-8c-Td). My hand is effectively Queen-high (utilizing the Tens on the board). Although the pot odds are attractive (calling 300 to win a total pot of 1550 requires about 19% equity), I have no showdown value against FiveTwo's UTG range. An UTG raising range consists of strong broadway cards and pairs, almost all of which beat my Queen-high even if they missed their own draws (as Ace-high or small pocket pairs beat me). FiveTwo's line (bet flop, check turn, small bet river) looks like thin value from a Jack or a King, or perhaps a missed draw like Ace-high. Since I cannot beat even a bluff from an Ace-high hand, and a bluff-raise is unlikely to succeed on such a coordinated board against an opponent who has shown interest in the pot, folding is the only disciplined option.

I am in UTG position with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o), which is a very weak hand. Given my position, I have four opponents left to act who could easily wake up with a better hand. K2o plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position, and is dominated by almost any calling range. The standard play here is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

T5o is a trash hand from UTG+1 position in a multi-way pot potential. No connectivity, low cards, offsuit. Standard fold preflop to preserve stack and avoid difficult spots OOP. Early position requires tight range.

On the button with J3 suited, two players have already folded. This is a good spot to attempt a steal with position. J3s isn't premium but it's playable from the button as a raise, especially with only the blinds left to act. Standard 2.5x open.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. This is a very weak hand to defend out of position against an open raise, even in a short-handed game. I should fold and wait for a better spot to protect my stack.

Button opens to 2.5bb and it’s heads-up with great pot odds (150 to win 400 ≈ 27%). J8o isn’t strong enough to 3-bet bluff from the BB very often, but it has plenty of equity versus a wide BTN opening range and plays fine as a defend in a single-raised pot. I’ll flat and realize equity rather than inflate the pot out of position.

Single-raised pot BB vs BTN on A97 is generally better for the preflop raiser (ace-heavy range), so I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a weak/marginal holding. J8 has only a gutshot to the T (no backdoor flush), so the best line is to check my range, realize equity when priced in, and respond based on sizing (potentially peel small bets, comfortably fold to larger pressure).

Flop came 9d 7s Ac. I have Jc3c - no pair, just backdoor club flush draw. However, as the preflop raiser with position, this Ace-high board favors my range. FiveTwo checked, so I should c-bet here. Standard 1/3 pot sizing works well on this dry board.

Claude’s c-bet is very small (185 into 735 ≈ 25%), so I’m getting great immediate price: 185 to win 920 ≈ 20%. With Jh8s on A97r I have a gutshot to the T (4 outs) plus some thin overcard/runout equity and potential to realize implied odds when a T comes (often making a disguised straight). This hand isn’t a great check-raise bluff candidate (no backdoor flush, weak showdown), so the best EV line is to peel vs this sizing and continue only on favorable turns (T/8/J or if he checks and I can take it away).

OOP BB vs BTN after we peel a tiny flop c-bet, this K turn is generally better for the preflop raiser (adds strong Kx/AK/KQ and keeps his Ax advantage). With Jh8s we still only have a gutshot to the T (4 outs) and no backdoor flush, so leading doesn’t generate much fold equity versus his natural continues and just risks inflating a pot we’re rarely winning at showdown. Best is to check, keep his range wide, and respond based on sizing (peel small again, fold to real pressure).

Turn brought Kh - excellent barrel card for my range as preflop raiser. A-K board texture heavily favors my perceived range over FiveTwo's flop calling range (likely weak aces, middle pairs, or draws). I have no showdown value but strong position and a credible story. Firing a second barrel around 45% pot to continue applying pressure.

Turn bet is 420 into 920 (≈46% pot). Calling 420 to win 1760 requires ~24% equity, but with Jh8s on A-9-7-K we still only have a bare gutshot to the T (4 outs ≈ 8–9% to the river) and no backdoor flush. Implied odds are limited because the T makes a non-nut straight (villain can have QJ) and many Ax/Kx hands won’t pay off heavily. Check-raising doesn’t apply enough pressure here (we don’t block Ax/Kx and his value region continues comfortably). Best EV is to fold and preserve stack.

Preflop, UTG in 5-handed play with 6s7s suited connectors. This hand has good playability, straight and flush potential, and performs well multiway or heads-up if I hit. Game history shows loose passive tendencies with frequent limps and calls, but as UTG I want to thin the field or take initiative. Standard open-raise size of 3x BB (300) into 150 pot is profitable given deep stacks and villain calling stations. Folding too tight early in tournament; limping invites multiway trouble OOP. Raising builds pot with position potential postflop if called.
92o is a trash hand. Facing an UTG raise with players behind me, this is an easy fold.

I am on the button with Qh 4c, a very weak offsuit hand. Elon has raised to 300 from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. Given my hand's poor equity and playability post-flop against an early position opener, there is no reason to call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

Facing a 3bb UTG open 5-handed from the small blind, K8o is dominated too often and plays poorly out of position with low realization, especially with the big blind still to act. The pot odds don’t justify defending a hand that will frequently make second-best top pair, and it’s not a good 3-bet bluff candidate versus an UTG range. Preserve stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with A4o facing a 3x open from UTG (Elon). The pot is 450 and I need to call 200, offering me pot odds of roughly 3.25:1. While A4o is a marginal hand and I will be out of position, the price is attractive enough to defend, especially as the hand contains an Ace blocker and has some straight potential (wheel). Folding is a bit too tight given the price, and 3-betting against an UTG range is risky. I will call to see a flop.

I am out of position in the Big Blind against an UTG opener (Elon) on a K-8-5 rainbow board. My hand is Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw (wheel), which is very weak. The K-high texture favors the UTG opener's range (which includes AA, KK, AK, KQ). Standard strategy in this spot is to check my entire range to the aggressor. I have no reason to lead out (donk bet) as I cannot get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. I will check and likely fold to a continuation bet unless it is very small.

Flop board 5c Kc 8d gives me an open-ended straight draw (4 or 9 for the nuts straight) with 8 clean outs, plus backdoor spade flush draw. No made hand but strong semi-bluff potential IP as preflop aggressor against Pro's BB calling range, which is likely wide and weak given the check. Two clubs on board make it draw-heavy; betting charges villain's draws/pairs and protects my equity while building pot OOP for him. Half-pot bet sizes well for fold equity and SPR control in tournament with 60bb effective stacks. Checking risks giving free card to overcards/draws or letting him realize equity cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with A4o on a Kc-8d-5c board. I checked to the pre-flop raiser (UTG) who bet 350 into a pot of 650 (approx 54% pot). The UTG range connects well with the King, containing many Kx hands (AK, KQ, KJ) and pocket pairs (AA-99). My hand is merely Ace-high with very limited backdoor potential (a backdoor wheel draw). While I beat pure bluffs, the UTG range is value-heavy here, and I am out of position with very low equity against any pair. The price of calling (26% pot equity required) is not justified given my weak holding and poor playability on future streets. The disciplined play is to fold.
AQs is a premium hand, especially from UTG. Standard open raise to 2.5x. This hand plays well postflop with nut flush potential and strong high card value.

I am in UTG+1 with Qc Jh. Claude, the table chip leader, has opened from UTG for 2.5bb. In a 5-handed game, UTG is roughly equivalent to the Hijack position. QJ offsuit is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by Claude's opening range (AQ, KQ, AJ, etc.) and does not play well multi-way if the Button or blinds call. Additionally, calling here allows the Button (FiveTwo) to potentially squeeze or play the hand with position on me. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are many hands remaining, I will choose the conservative and fundamentally sound play of folding this marginal hand against the chip leader's opening raise.

5-handed, Claude’s UTG open is likely fairly wide. On the button with KTo, I prefer a 3-bet rather than a flat: we leverage position, often win the pot preflop, and take initiative for profitable c-bets on many runouts. K blocks strong continues (AK/KQ) and we can comfortably fold if Claude or the blinds come back with a 4-bet/squeeze. Size to discourage the blinds from peeling and to isolate Claude.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd 8d, facing an UTG raise to 250 and a Button 3-bet to 800. While J8s is a playable speculative hand in late position or passive pots, cold-calling a 3-bet from the Small Blind puts me out of position against two strong ranges. The original raiser (Claude) is still left to act and could 4-bet. The risk is too high and the equity is insufficient to continue.

9-5 offsuit is a complete trash hand with poor connectivity, no suitedness, and terrible post-flop playability. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (tight range) and a button 3-bet from FiveTwo (likely strong), this is a clear fold from the big blind. The pot odds (calling 700 into 1200) are not sufficient to justify playing this hand out of position against two strong ranges. Preserve the stack for better opportunities in the tournament.

AQs is a premium hand that plays well as a 4-bet here. FiveTwo's 3-bet from button could be wide given position. I'll 4-bet to around 2200 for value and protection. If they 5-bet shove, I can fold, but most likely they'll fold or call and I can play postflop with a strong range advantage.

Claude’s 4-bet from UTG is heavily weighted to strong value (QQ+/AK-type hands) even given his aggression. KTo is dominated by his continuing range, performs poorly in a 4-bet pot, and won’t realize its raw equity well even in position. A 5-bet shove has insufficient fold equity vs this range. Best is to preserve stack and move on.

I am UTG with Jack-Trey offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand and does not warrant an open from early position, especially with aggressive players like Claude and FiveTwo yet to act. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

5-handed from UTG+1, 74o is well below a profitable opening/limping range. It plays poorly postflop, is dominated when we make a pair, and we’re out of position versus the blinds. With ample stack depth, preserving EV and avoiding marginal spots is best here.

I am on the Button with 6c 5d (unsuited connectors). This is a weak hand (bottom ~25% of hands). Although being on the button allows for a wider opening range to steal the blinds, 65o is generally too weak to open profitably, especially with a very deep-stacked and aggressive player (Claude) in the Big Blind who can easily defend or 3-bet. I have 85 BBs and don't need to take marginal spots. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

Preflop in SB vs BB after all others folded. Holding As8s, a strong suited Ace with good playability, blocker to AA, and flush potential. Excellent spot to raise for value and fold equity against BB defense range. Limping is too passive; folding wastes SB equity. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is standard size: builds pot, takes initiative, and effective given ~65bb stacks. Claude has deep stack but we've seen him call/raise wide historically. High EV play to maximize stack long-term.

AJo in the BB facing a 3x raise from Elon in the SB. This is a strong hand that plays well as a 3-bet for value. Elon has only 6275 chips, so a 3-bet to ~900 puts good pressure on him. AJo dominates many hands he could be raising with from SB position.

Facing a 3bet to 900 (3x my 300 open) from Claude in BB after all others folded. Pot 1200, calling 600 more for total pot ~1800, leaving SPR ~3:1 with 5675 behind. As8s is an excellent calling hand here: ace blocker reduces villain's AA/AKs combos, suited for flush/straight potential, strong playability OOP vs BB 3bet range (JJ+, AKo/s, AQo/s, suited broadways/connectors for bluffs). Equity ~40-45% vs polarized range. Folding too tight (-EV given pot odds and equity); 4betting risks overcommitting with marginal value/bluff (Claude aggressive, may 5bet), better to call, control pot, realize equity postflop where suited ace excels (hit A/top pair good kicker, flush draw leverage). Consistent with history: Claude 3bets wide (e.g., Hand 46 over 800), we've seen him bluff/value mix. Standard play to induce folds on blank flops or get value from worse.

Flop board 7h6s4s is extremely favorable for As8s: nut spade flush draw (9 outs), gutshot straight draw to the 5 (4 outs, ~12-13 total outs, roughly 45-50% equity to improve by showdown). Two spades on board reduce villain's flush draw combos, and Ace blocker reduces his strong Ax hands. OOP first to act vs Claude's BB 3bet range (JJ+, AK, some bluffs/broadways). Checking risks giving a free card to villain's Ax, overpairs, or missed draws, allowing him to realize equity cheaply or check back. Donk betting semi-bluffs here is high EV: gets value from villain's medium strength (pairs like 77/66/44, 76s/54s two pair/worse draws, 87s str draw), protects our equity, denies villain's equity, and builds the pot when ahead in equity. Pot 1800, bet 1200 (2/3 pot) is standard size - charges draws, gets called by worse, leaves SPR ~2:1 post-bet for committed stacks (my 5675 behind). If villain raises, strong blocker and equity support a jam/call. Claude's history shows aggression but folds to postflop pressure (e.g., Hand 1 fold to bet, Hand 18 river fold). Exploits his wide 3bet range by taking initiative on a draw-heavy connected board. Maximizes stack growth long-term.

I have AJo on a 7h6s4s board - completely whiffed. Elon donk-bet 1200 into the pot after calling my 3-bet. This low connected board with flush draw potential favors his range much more than mine. He could have sets, two pair, overpairs, or strong draws. With only ace-high and no backdoor draws, continuing here is burning chips. Elon is short-stacked (4475 behind) and showing strength - he's unlikely to fold. Better to cut losses here.

5-handed, A7o is strong enough to open from UTG (effectively hijack) for initiative and to deny equity to the blinds. Limping invites multiway pots where A7o is dominated too often; folding is too tight given short-handed dynamics. Use a standard open size that matches the table’s tendencies (many opens 250–300) and keeps ranges wide while still applying pressure to stacks like Elon/Pro.

The game is 5-handed, so ranges are wider than a full ring game. FiveTwo opens from UTG (effectively the Hijack/Cutoff position) to 2.5 BB. I hold KcQd in the Cutoff. KQo is a strong broadway hand that performs well against a typical 5-handed opening range, dominating weaker Kings and Queens (like KJ, KT, QJ) and flipping with pairs. While 3-betting is a viable option to isolate, my previous 3-bets against FiveTwo were with premium hands (AK, KK), so flat calling here protects my calling range and keeps the pot manageable in position. I am aware of aggressive players behind who might squeeze, but folding KQo to a single raise 5-handed is too conservative. I will call to play post-flop in position.

On the button with QsJs, a premium suited broadway hand with excellent playability. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo (active raiser from early position in history, often with medium strength or speculative hands) and a flat from Pro (tends to call wide). Position is ideal for realizing equity multi-way or isolating. Direct calling risks SB/BB involvement and multi-way pot OOP for them, but 3-betting gains initiative, has fold equity against their ranges (FiveTwo folds ~40-50% to 3bets historically), and sets up postflop advantages. Equity realization strong vs calling ranges (~40%+). Sizing to 900: ~3.6x the open, standard button squeeze size accounting for the caller, builds pot for value while leaving maneuverability (my stack 74BB deep). Avoids min-raise trap, pressures without overcommitting.
6c5s is a very weak offsuit hand facing a 3-bet to 900. I'd need to call 850 into a pot of 1550 with terrible position and a speculative hand that plays poorly against 3-bet ranges. Clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d Th. The action has been a raise from FiveTwo (UTG), a call from Pro, and a 3-bet to 900 from Elon on the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (800 to win a total pot of 2350), 9-10 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against a 3-betting range. Elon's 3-bet likely indicates a strong hand that dominates mine, such as high pairs or strong broadway cards. Calling would likely lead to a multi-way pot where I have very little equity and poor position. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving play here.

We opened A7o short-handed and now face a squeeze to 900 with a cold-caller (Pro) in between. Flat-calling is the worst option: we’ll be out of position versus the 3-bettor, often multiway, and A7o is too easily dominated when we make one-pair. Instead, we use the ace-blocker to 4-bet bluff and try to win the dead money (blinds + Pro’s call) while also denying equity. Size needs to be large enough to discourage calls but not so big that we torch chips when forced to fold to a 5-bet shove; ~2.6x the 3-bet accomplishes that. Plan: fold to any 5-bet jam.

FiveTwo's 4-bet to 2400 from UTG, after an initial open and facing a squeeze from the button, represents an extremely strong range (likely AA, KK, QQ, AK). My hand, KcQd, is heavily dominated by this range (drawing nearly dead against AA/KK/QQ and dominated by AK). The pot odds are approximately 37% (calling 2150 to win a total pot of around 5850), but my equity against a standard 4-bet range is likely 15-20% at best. Preserving my healthy stack of ~82BB is prioritized over getting involved in a high-variance, negative EV spot.

Facing a 4-bet from FiveTwo after my button 3-bet squeeze over their UTG open and Pro's flat. QsJs is a strong squeezing hand with great playability, but vs. an UTG 4-bet range (likely QQ+, AK, maybe JJ), my equity is marginal (~25-35% depending on exact range) and requires significant postflop skill OOP on button (UTG acts first). Pot odds are ~3.2:1 (1500 to call into 5200), needing ~23% equity, but fold equity is gone, and calling commits 1500 from 6575 stack (~23% of stack) into a bloated pot OOP against a strong range. FiveTwo has shown aggression but also folds to 3-bets; here they 4-bet large, representing premium. Raising all-in would be overplay vs their range and stack (11290). Best to fold, preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 102 hands left. My image remains aggressive for future squeezes.

I am in UTG position with 7d 3h, which is an extremely weak hand (unsuited, disconnected, low cards). Opening from early position requires a strong range, and this hand is at the very bottom of any starting hand chart. With 83 BBs, there is no reason to get involved with such poor holdings. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

A3o is a marginal ace offsuit with a terrible kicker and poor playability postflop. In UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game, this is a standard fold to preserve our stack for better opportunities. No reads from opponents justify limping or raising, and calling the BB OOP with this hand is -EV. Pot is small, but folding maximizes long-term EV in the tournament.

On the button with 2d8c - a weak hand, but with position and only the blinds left to act. Folded to me, so this is a decent spot to attempt a steal. My stack is healthy at 16k. A standard 2.5x raise should take down the blinds often enough to be profitable.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Claude has raised from the Button to 250. While Ace-rag hands have some value, playing them out of position against an aggressive button open is generally a losing proposition in the long run. Calling often invites a squeeze from the Big Blind or leaves me in a difficult spot post-flop where I am easily out-kicked. 3-betting is an option due to the Ace blocker, but Claude has shown significant aggression in recent hands. I will choose the conservative path and fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Claude is opening the Button to 2.5x and we’re getting a very good price in the BB: 150 more to win a pot that will be 550, so we only need ~27% equity. K6o is comfortably inside a standard BB defend versus a Button range, and folding would be too tight. I don’t want to 3-bet this offsuit, dominated Kx too often and it plays poorly in an inflated pot out of position; calling keeps the pot manageable while still defending our blind appropriately.

Single-raised pot BB vs Button on Jc-2h-4d is generally better for the preflop raiser; my K6o has no pair, no real straight equity, and only a thin backdoor club draw via Kc. Leading here doesn’t generate many folds from Claude’s range (he has plenty of Jx, overpairs, and floats), and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a low-equity hand. Best line is to check and realize whatever equity I have, then decide versus his c-bet sizing/frequency.

I flopped bottom pair on Jc 2h 4d. As the preflop aggressor with position, this is a standard c-bet spot. The board is dry and FiveTwo will miss often. A small bet of ~1/3 pot extracts value from worse hands and can get folds from overcards that missed.

Claude’s ~1/3 pot c-bet is likely very high frequency in this BTN vs BB spot. Getting 185 to win 920 after we call means we only need ~20% equity, and K6 has at least some immediate equity via an overcard to the J plus backdoor club potential with Kc. Raising would bloat the pot with a hand that doesn’t block Jx and doesn’t have strong equity when called; better to defend and realize equity, then continue selectively (improve on K/6/club turns, or apply pressure if he checks back later).

Turn Qc gives us a strong K-high flush draw (10 clean club outs) plus some overcard runouts, but this specific card also improves the BTN c-bettor’s range (lots of Qx/QQ/AQ/KQ). In BB vs BTN I don’t want to donk into a card that’s generally better for his range; checking keeps his bluffs/value bets in, lets him continue barreling at a high frequency, and we can either call profitably versus his typical small sizing or spring a check-raise on certain bet sizes to leverage our draw and fold equity. If he checks back, we realize our equity for free and can often take a stab on many rivers (clubs/K/6, or even some scare cards).

Turn brought Qc - an overcard that connects with many hands FiveTwo would call flop with (QJ, QT, Qx suited). My bottom pair with 8 kicker is very weak now. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet which narrows their range to hands that have something. Given the scary turn card and my marginal holding, I should check to control pot size and potentially get to showdown cheaply.

River Ts leaves us with K-high and essentially no showdown value (we lose to any A-high and all pairs), so checking is unattractive because Claude can often just check back better or bet and force us to fold. Claude’s turn check-back after c-betting flop caps him heavily toward A-highs, small pairs, and some weak Jx that wanted pot control; he has fewer strong Qx/sets/straights than if he continued barreling. The Ts is also a good pressure card: it connects with a very credible BB value range (notably K9 for the straight, plus some Qx that checked turn and now value-bets), and our K blocks some of his strongest Kx continues. 

I’ll fire a near-pot, polarized bet to maximize fold equity versus his A-highs and marginal pairs/Jx that hate this runout and the line, while accepting we get called by Qx/Tx/straights. If raised, we simply fold.

I have bottom pair (deuces) with an 8 kicker on a Jc2h4dQcTs board. FiveTwo bet 900 into 920 - nearly pot-sized. The river completed broadway (any K9 has a straight, AK has broadway). FiveTwo called my flop c-bet and now leads river big - this looks like value. My hand is far too weak to call as a bluff catcher. Easy fold.

UTG position with 7d Js offsuit is a clear fold in a 5-handed tournament. This hand has poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and no position advantage. Standard tight UTG range excludes speculative offsuit hands like this. Preserve stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV.
T5o is a weak hand, especially in early position (UTG+1) with 4 players behind. This is a clear fold - not worth opening or calling with.

I am on the Button with Ten of Hearts and Five of Hearts. While this is a suited hand, it is quite weak and disconnected. The player in the Small Blind (FiveTwo) has shown significant aggression recently, frequently 3-betting or making large bets. Given my stack size and the relative strength of my hand, it is not worth the risk of being re-raised by an aggressive opponent or having to play a post-flop pot out of position against the blinds with a marginal holding. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

Folded to us in the small blind heads-up versus the big blind. KJh suited is well ahead of a BB random holding and plays strongly with nutty straight/flush potential. Raising applies immediate pressure to win the 150-chip pot, gives us initiative, and still keeps the pot manageable out of position. I’ll use a standard SB open size rather than just completing.

I am in the Big Blind with K3s facing a 2.5x open from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). Blind vs Blind, the SB opening range is typically quite wide, often 40-50% or more, especially from an active player like FiveTwo. I need to call 150 chips to win a total pot of 500, requiring 30% equity. K3s has significantly more equity than 30% against a standard SB opening range (around 42-46%). Additionally, I will have position on the post-flop betting rounds. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here to realize equity in position.

Heads-up SB vs BB with initiative. Flop 9-4-2 rainbow is fairly dry and favors the preflop raiser’s range advantage (overpairs, strong broadways), while BB has a lot of unpaired overcards and low floats that will fold to a small c-bet. With KhJh we have two overcards plus backdoor heart potential, so a low-frequency, small sizing c-bet is ideal: it applies pressure cheaply, can win immediately, and retains flexibility if called (we can barrel good turns like K/J/Q/T/heart, and often shut down on bad runouts).

I am in position (BB) against an active Small Blind (FiveTwo) on a dry flop of 9c-4d-2s. My opponent has c-bet 175 into 500 (35% pot). I hold Kd3d, giving me King-high, a backdoor flush draw, and a backdoor straight draw. In a blind-vs-blind scenario, ranges are wide, and King-high frequently has enough equity to continue, especially against a small bet size. I need ~20.5% equity to call, which I comfortably have against a wide c-betting range that includes many unconnected overcards and air. Floating here allows me to re-evaluate on the turn, potentially betting if checked to or realizing equity if the turn improves my hand.

Turn 5d is a relatively blank/low card that doesn’t improve our hand, but we still retain a range advantage as the preflop raiser (overpairs, strong 9x, some 55). Pro’s flop call contains a meaningful amount of A-high/overcard floats and weak underpairs that hate facing a second small barrel. A small turn bet pressures that portion, denies equity, and keeps our risk controlled with no real draw; if we get called we can often shut down on most rivers unless we improve to K/J.

On the turn, the board is 9c-4d-2s-5d. I hold Kd3d. The 5d significantly improves my hand, giving me a flush draw (to the King) and an open-ended straight draw (A-2-3-4-5 or 2-3-4-5-6). I have approximately 15 very clean outs (9 diamonds + 3 non-diamond Aces + 3 non-diamond Sixes), plus 3 King outs for top pair, totaling up to 18 outs. My equity is likely over 35%. The opponent bets 300 into a pot of 850, offering me odds of roughly 4.8:1 (requiring ~20.7% equity). This is a trivial call based on pot odds and implied odds. Raising is an option to exert pressure, but calling guarantees I see the river in position cheaply against a potentially wide blind-vs-blind range.

River Qh is a strong range card for the preflop raiser after we’ve bet flop+turn: we credibly represent Qx (AQ/KQ/QJ), overpairs, and occasional slowplayed strong hands, while Pro’s call-call range contains a lot of underpairs (66–88), 4x/5x that now hate the overcard, and some A-high floats. Our actual hand (KJ high) has essentially no showdown value here (we lose to any pair and to A-high), so checking surrenders too often and relies on Pro bluffing. A medium river bluff pressures exactly the hands we’re targeting and only needs to work ~30% of the time to be profitable. If raised, we can comfortably fold.

I hold Kd3d on a board of 9c 4d 2s 5d Qh. I missed my flush draw and straight draw, leaving me with only King-high. My opponent (FiveTwo) has fired three barrels: pre-flop raise, flop c-bet, turn barrel, and now a 700 chip bet into a 1450 pot on the river. While the Small Blind's range is wide, a triple barrel usually signifies strength (top pair or better) or a committed bluff. Although I hold a 3 which blocks the straight combinations (A3, 63), I also hold the Kd, which blocks missed flush draw bluffs that I could beat. With only King-high, I lose to any pair, including random pairs the opponent might have picked up (like a pair of Queens on the river) or was valuing thinly. The pot odds of roughly 25% are not compelling enough to make a hero call with K-high in this spot, especially when I have better hands in my range to defend with (like 9x or pocket pairs). Preserving my stack of ~7500 chips is the prudent play.
5s6h is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. This is not playable from early position - easy fold.

I am holding 7 of hearts and 4 of diamonds in UTG+1 position. This is a very weak, unsuited hand that does not play well in a 5-handed game. Since the first player (Claude) has already folded and there are still three players behind me including the blinds, there is no strategic value in entering the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folded to us on the button vs two blinds, so this is a prime steal spot. Even though Td2o is near the bottom of holdings and plays poorly when called, our positional advantage plus the immediate 150 in the pot makes a small open profitable if the blinds fold at a reasonable frequency. I’ll use a reduced sizing to lower the cost of getting played back at and to keep SPR manageable; if faced with a 3-bet, we comfortably fold. If called, we’ll proceed cautiously and mostly lean on position with selective c-bets on favorable textures.

I hold 4h 5c in the Small Blind facing a standard raise from the Button (FiveTwo). This is a very weak off-suit connector that performs poorly post-flop, especially when out of position. Despite the potentially wide range of the Button, my hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff. I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 225 after two folds, with a pot of 375 and needing to call 125 more. My hand J5o is very weak offsuit with poor connectivity, dominated by better jacks or suited connectors in villain's button opening range, which is wide but still contains many stronger hands. Equity against a typical button range is around 30-35%, but post-flop playability is terrible with reverse implied odds. Pot odds are roughly 3:1 (25% needed), but folding preserves my 65BB stack for better spots in a tournament setting. FiveTwo has been aggressive historically, often stealing and value betting, so no need to defend this trash hand.

I am dealt Nine-Six offsuit in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak hand and does not meet the requirements for an opening range, especially from early position with four players left to act behind me. There is no reason to commit chips to the pot with this hand.

5-handed and first-in after Flash folds, 6h5h is a reasonable open: it has strong playability (can flop straights/flushes), and raising lets us win the blinds often and keep initiative. Limping invites isolation from the button and puts us in tougher spots. I’ll use a modest 2.5x size to risk less while still applying pressure. Plan: generally fold to bigger 3-bets (especially from Pro on the button), continue versus flats from the blinds and play postflop with equity.

I hold Ten-Five offsuit (T5o) on the Button. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a standard opening range. FiveTwo raised to 250 from early position (UTG+1). Despite having position, calling or 3-betting with this specific hand is not profitable. The standard and most +EV play is to fold.

Preflop in SB facing a 2.5x raise from UTG+1 (FiveTwo, who is aggressive and raises frequently from early position based on history). Hand is 75dd, a suited one-gapper with good playability, flush and straight potential. Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 more into a pot that will be 600 gives about 3:1. Equity vs a typical early position range (~12-15%) is around 35-40% due to suited connector dynamics. OOP but deep stacks (64BB), so speculative value in multiway potential or BB folding. Folding weak hands preserves stack for better spots, but this is playable defense vs small raise. Raising not justified without stronger hand. Call to realize equity postflop.

I have 4h9c in the BB. FiveTwo raised to 250, Elon called. I need 150 to call into a 600 pot, getting 4:1 odds. While 94o is weak, the pot odds are favorable and I close the action. I can see a cheap flop and potentially hit something.

Flop of 2s2cQh completely misses my 75dd speculative hand—no pair, no straight draw (75 is too gapped for 234), no flush draw active. Pot is 750 multiway (FiveTwo's UTG+1 raise range strong, Claude's BB defense wide). OOP first to act with air vs two opponents, checking to induce bluffs or realize equity if checked through is suboptimal; pure fold equity absent and reverse implied odds if hit later. Check-fold preserves stack for better spots; no bluff value on paired board protecting strong hands, queen likely in villain ranges. Prioritize discipline over thin value.

I have 9-high on a 2s2cQh board - essentially nothing. Elon checked, and I'm last to act. No reason to bluff here with two opponents still in the hand and zero equity. Taking a free card to see if I can improve on the turn is the correct play.

Paired Q-high flop after I opened and got two blind callers. This texture heavily favors the preflop raiser (I have all the strong Qx/overpairs and some 2x, while the blinds have lots of whiffs), and both opponents checking gives me a good chance to take it down with a small c-bet. With 6h5h I also have some backdoor equity (hearts + straight potential), so betting small is preferable to a big stab multiway. Plan: use a ~1/3 pot sizing, fold to a check-raise, and continue selectively if called (barrel good turns like hearts/A/K or give up on bad runouts).

Flop of 2s2cQh completely misses 75dd: no pair, no flush draw (board has no diamonds), no straight draw (gapped hand disconnected from board). Facing a bet of 250 into 1000 pot from aggressive UTG+1 FiveTwo after two checks, in multiway pot OOP. FiveTwo's UTG+1 range favors Qx, overpairs, sets on this paired queen-high board, giving my air hand ~5-10% equity at best. Pot odds 4:1 but reverse implied odds huge if called and villain has showdown value. Preserve stack (6225, ~62BB) for premium spots; discipline over spew. Check-fold optimal, realizing zero equity postflop.

I have 9-high on a 2s2cQh board with no draws. FiveTwo c-bet 250 after Elon folded. I need 250 to call into 1000 (4:1 odds), but with 9-high I have almost no equity. Even if I hit a 9 or 4, FiveTwo likely has me dominated with any Q, pair, or overcards. No reason to continue with this weak holding.

5-handed and first to act, AJs (suited) is well within an UTG opening range and plays strongly versus the blinds and the two shorter stacks behind (Pro 7.5k, Elon 6.2k). Raising builds a pot with a hand that dominates many calls, applies pressure/I CM-like leverage on the shorter stacks, and avoids giving a cheap multiway flop. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x open that matches the table’s sizing tendencies.

I am holding Queen-Six offsuit in UTG+1 facing a raise from the UTG player (FiveTwo). Q6o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by the opener's range (which includes AQ, KQ, QJs, etc.) and plays poorly post-flop. With 75 big blinds, there is no pressure to play garbage hands. Folding is the only standard option.

95o is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity against an UTG raiser's strong range from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive historically. Facing a 2.5BB raise preflop, pot odds are insufficient (250 to call into 400, ~1.6:1), and postflop prospects are grim without hits. Folding preserves my 62BB stack for premium spots in the tournament.

K9o in the SB facing a UTG raise of 250. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, getting 2:1 odds. K9o is marginal but playable with these pot odds. FiveTwo has been active with raises. Folding is disciplined but calling to see a flop is reasonable given the price. I'll call and see if I can connect with the board.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Eight offsuit. FiveTwo opened for 250 and Claude called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 600, and it only costs me 150 more to call. This gives me 4:1 pot odds to see a flop. King-high hands have decent raw equity in a multi-way pot, especially when defending the blind at this price. I will call to see the flop and close the pre-flop action.

I completely missed this flop with Ks9c on 5c6d8d. I have K-high with no pair, no draws (need diamonds or a 7 for a gutshot). The board is coordinated with possible straight and flush draws. First to act with Flash and FiveTwo behind me. Standard check here - no reason to bluff into two opponents on a wet board with nothing.

I have top pair (Kings) with an Eight on a board of 5-6-8. This is a very strong holding in a multi-way pot, especially since I hold the Eight of clubs. The board is quite connected with straight draws (7-9, 4-7, etc.) and a flush draw. Claude has checked to me. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, is yet to act. Given the coordinated nature of the board, I want to protect my hand and extract value from draws and smaller pairs. However, since the pre-flop raiser is behind me, checking to see if he continues with a bet is a standard play to gather information. If FiveTwo bets, I will most likely call or raise depending on the sizing. For now, I will check to see how the action develops.

Three-way on a low, connected, two-tone flop (5c6d8d), this board interacts well with the blinds’ calling ranges (pairs, 76/87, 98, diamond draws). With AsJs we’re currently just two overcards with a backdoor spade flush; we don’t benefit much from bloating the pot and we’re not thrilled to face a check-raise. Checking keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity, and allows us to respond appropriately on favorable turns (A/J, spade turns for backdoors, or certain high cards to barrel if checked to).

Turn 3h doesn't help my K-high. Everyone checked the flop showing weakness. I could try to bluff here, but with two opponents and no equity, it's risky. K-high might actually have showdown value if it checks through. Check and see a free river.

I hold top pair (Eights) with a King kicker on a board of 5-6-8-3. The flop checked around, which suggests that neither the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) nor the Small Blind (Claude) has a monster hand like a set or an overpair. While my hand is likely the best at the moment, the board is highly coordinated with various straight and flush draws. By checking again, I am controlling the size of the pot and avoiding a situation where I bet and get raised by a semi-bluff or a slow-played hand. I am prepared to call a reasonable bet from FiveTwo to see the river, as his range contains many missed overcards.

Both blinds have checked twice on a board that should connect with their preflop calling ranges (pairs like 6x/8x/55, straighty hands like 76/87/98, and diamond draws). That caps them somewhat toward medium-strength hands and draws rather than strong made hands that want to build a pot. With AsJs we have little showdown value (ace-high will usually lose to any pair), so our best path is to apply pressure while we still have fold equity and while we can credibly represent overpairs/strong one-pair hands from an UTG open. A small turn stab also denies a free river to diamond/straight draws and sets up profitable river barrels on scare cards (A/J, some high cards) when checked to again.

FiveTwo bet 400 into 750 after everyone checked the flop. I have K-high with zero equity - no pair, no draws. The board is very connected (5-6-8-3) with diamond draws possible. FiveTwo's delayed bet suggests either a made hand, a draw, or sensing weakness. With complete air and no way to improve, calling to bluff river is too speculative. Clear fold.

On the turn, the 3h is largely a brick, although it does complete some unlikely gutshot draws like 4-2. FiveTwo has bet 400 into a pot of 750 after the flop checked around. My top pair with a King kicker remains a very strong hand in this sequence, especially since FiveTwo's check on the flop often indicates overcards or a marginal made hand rather than a monster. I have the pot odds (nearly 3:1) and the hand strength to continue. Raising here might bloat the pot unnecessarily against a range that could contain better pairs or straights, so I will call to keep his bluffs in and see the river.

On the river, the board is 5c-6d-8d-3h-2c. I hold top pair (Eights) with a King kicker. The 2c on the river is effectively a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. On the turn, FiveTwo bet 400 into 750 after checking the flop, which could represent a delayed continuation bet with overcards, a medium pair, or a draw. By checking here, I allow FiveTwo to continue bluffing with missed draws or to check back with marginal hands that might not call a bet. Given the action, my hand is likely the best, but checking is the most effective way to reach showdown or induce a mistake from an aggressive opponent like FiveTwo.

River is a brick (2c) that doesn’t complete the diamond draw and doesn’t meaningfully change the texture. After BB check/calls the turn, his range is a mix of one-pair hands (8x/6x/5x/77/99) and a decent chunk of missed diamond draws that took the cheap turn peel. With AsJs we actually have showdown value versus those busted diamonds (we beat KdQd/QdJd/JdTd, etc.), so turning our hand into a bluff is unattractive—most pairs we target will call often enough, and we’d just light chips on fire when called. Checking realizes our equity and wins the pot whenever he has the missed draws he’s incentivized to check behind with here.

I hold 3d 4s in the UTG position. This is 4-3 offsuit, one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Despite the table being short-handed (5 players), opening from early position with such low unsuited connectors is a losing play in the long run. My stack is healthy at 75 BBs, so there is no pressure to make desperate moves. I will fold immediately.

89o is a weak offsuit connector with poor playability from early position (UTG+1). Standard fold preflop to preserve stack for better opportunities. Deep stacks don't change this; no dead money or limpers to justify playing.

72 suited is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even on the button with position, this is too weak to open. The blinds have healthy stacks and I don't need to take unnecessary risks with such a marginal hand. Folding is the disciplined play here.

It's folded to me in the Small Blind. I have King-Six offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to play heads-up against the Big Blind. FiveTwo (Big Blind) has been playing aggressively recently, so I want to take the initiative rather than just limping. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either win the pot immediately or define the range of the opponent.

Heads-up SB vs BB, Flash’s raise to 3x is likely fairly wide and we’re getting a great price (200 more to win 400 now, and play a 600 pot). 8c7c is a strong defend: suited, connected, can make robust draws and strong hidden hands. I’d rather flat than 3-bet here—3-betting builds a bigger pot out of position with a hand that plays well as a call and doesn’t love getting 4-bet.

The flop of Queen-Jack-Nine is highly coordinated and connects well with the range of hands the Big Blind (FiveTwo) would use to call a pre-flop raise. Although I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw to a Ten, I am out of position against an aggressive opponent. Betting here is unlikely to generate many folds from hands that have any piece of the board. I will check to see a turn card for free if possible, or evaluate based on the size of a potential bet from FiveTwo.

Flash (preflop raiser) checking this very dynamic Q-J-9 flop is often a sign of range weakness or pot-control with marginal made hands. In position, I can profitably stab and pick up the pot a high % of the time, and when called I still have equity via the gutshot to the T plus backdoor clubs and some pair/improvement potential. I’ll use a small-to-medium sizing to maximize fold equity while keeping my risk low and comfortably folding to a check-raise.

The flop of Queen-Jack-Nine is very coordinated and connects heavily with the range of hands the Big Blind (FiveTwo) would defend with. Although I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), I am out of position against an aggressive opponent. The pot odds are decent for a call, but my hand's equity is low on this specific texture, and I likely have very little fold equity if I were to raise. Given the board connectivity, it is highly probable the opponent has at least a pair or a strong draw. I will fold to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

K2 suited is a marginal speculative hand with low kicker and poor playability from UTG in a 5-handed game. Facing multiple players to act behind, including deep stacks like Claude and Flash who have shown aggression in past hands. Folding preserves stack for better spots, as raising OOP with this hand leads to difficult post-flop decisions and calling risks domination by better kings. Standard play is to fold weak suited kings from early position to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

K9o in UTG+1 is a marginal hand. With 150BB stack and decent card strength, I'll make a standard open raise to 250. K9o plays reasonably well and I can take down blinds or continue with position on some opponents if called.

J7o is a very weak hand to defend against an open raise from Claude in early-middle position. Even with the advantage of being on the button, this hand is easily dominated by Claude's opening range and does not play well post-flop. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

AQo is a strong, linear 3-bet from the small blind versus a likely fairly wide 5-handed UTG+1 open. Flatting invites the BB in and leaves us out of position without initiative. By 3-betting we isolate Claude, take the betting lead, and deny equity to hands like KJ/QJ/JT/suited connectors. The A/Q blockers slightly reduce the chance we run into AA/QQ/AQ. If Claude responds with a large 4-bet, we can comfortably fold AQo given typical 4-bet ranges and our OOP disadvantage.

I hold 7c 2d, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am facing a raise from Claude and a significant 3-bet to 1000 from FiveTwo in the Small Blind. Facing this much aggression with the worst possible hand makes this an automatic and trivial fold. I must preserve my stack of 74 BBs for better opportunities.

K9o is a marginal open from UTG+1, and facing a 3-bet to 1000 from FiveTwo, this is an easy fold. K9o plays poorly out of position against a 3-bet range. FiveTwo has shown they can 3-bet light but also value bet, and I don't have the equity or playability to call 750 more here. Cutting losses is the right play.
7c Th from UTG is a weak offsuit hand. T7o is not in a standard UTG opening range - it's too loose from early position where I have 4 players left to act behind me. Easy fold.

I am holding 9d 3d in UTG+1. In a 5-handed game, while ranges are wider than in full-ring, 93 suited is still far too weak to open or call with from this position.
