The hand has poor high-card value and minimal connectivity. Given the stack sizes and the activity levels of players like FiveTwo and Claude, I should stick to a tighter range and fold this hand.

Button vs blinds with two folds in front is a prime steal spot. Even though 8♠5♥ is weak, position plus fold equity makes a small open profitable, especially against shorter/medium blind stacks that can’t defend everything. I’ll use a standard 2.5x sizing to risk little while still pressuring the blinds; if either blind 3-bets, I’m comfortably folding this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s Tc, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo). This is a very weak hand (Ten-Deuce offsuit) that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. The pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400) are roughly 2:1 immediate, but reverse implied odds and playability issues make this a clear fold. There is no strategic merit in defending this hand against a button raise.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 250 from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in late position throughout the history, often raising and showing down medium strength hands or bluffs. Pot is 400, calling 150 more (total 250) gives excellent implied odds with position postflop out of position but my Kc7d has a king blocker to their value range (KQ+, AK), decent high card strength, and playability for floats or hitting top pair. Equity vs a wide button opening range (~30-40%) is around 40-45%, and pot odds are favorable (27% needed). Folding is too tight in BB defense strategy; raising is overplay with offsuit mediocre hand and deep stacks. Calling keeps pot small, realizes equity, and allows postflop flexibility to fold if missed or play if hit. Tournament phase mid-early (hand 56/150), stack 61BB effective vs villain's larger stack, no need to speculate aggressively.

Flop board Jh 2h Qc is somewhat coordinated with queen-high, a gutshot possibility, and a weak heart flush draw. My Kc7d is king-high with no pair, no straight draw (though K blocks KQ/AK), and the club doesn't help flush-wise here. Equity against FiveTwo's button c-betting range (wide due to aggression history: ~40-50% including broadways, suited aces, connectors, Ax suited) is roughly 30-35%, as they often bet any two or draws OOP opponent. OOP as BB caller, check-folding weak showdown hands like this is standard to realize fold equity and avoid difficult turn spots. Donk betting is suboptimal with no strong equity or protection value; it polarizes my wide BB calling range awkwardly. Checking allows villain to realize their range advantage by betting wide, to which I can fold profitably given pot odds (~2:1 if they bet half-pot). Tournament mid-stack (60BB), no need to bluff-catch thinly here when we can find better spots. Consistent with preflop plan: realize equity postflop only when improved.

Heads-up BTN vs BB after a steal, this flop favors my preflop range (lots of AQ/KQ/QJ, big broadways) and Elon’s check range contains plenty of hands that will fold to a small c-bet (A-high, small pairs, random floats that don’t want to continue vs pressure). My hand has essentially no showdown value, so betting is better than checking, and a small size keeps my risk low while leveraging fold equity. If I get raised, I’m done with it.

Facing a 200 bet into 750 on a Qh 2h Jh board (wait, Jh 2h Qc) as OOP with Kc7d king-high no draw. Board hits villain's wide button range hard with Qx, Jx, sets, heart draws (many suited broadways), straight draws (KT, T9s). FiveTwo aggressive history, c-bets wide ~60-70% on this texture to deny equity/charge draws. My equity <25% vs polarized range (value crushes, bluffs <10% like pure air since board favors them). Pot odds 3.75:1 require ~21% equity, but realized equity low OOP facing aggression (turn barrel likely 50%+ time). Preflop call was speculative for multiway/implied, but heads-up missed flop, no fold equity check-raise (weak blocker). Check-fold exploits their wide betting, preserves stack for better spots mid-tourney (60BB deep, hand 56/150). Calling builds tough river spots or forces fold equity-less check; raising too bluff-heavy OOP.

In a 5-handed game, A7 suited is a strong enough hand to open from under-the-gun. It has good post-flop playability and nut-flush potential. I will stick to a standard opening size of 300, which has been consistent throughout the tournament, to maintain pressure and build the pot.

Flash opens UTG to 300. With QTo offsuit in UTG+1, we’re in a tough seat: if we flat, we’ll often be dominated by Flash’s value-heavy opens (AQ/KQ/QJ/TT+) and we’ll have poor equity realization being out of position to the Button. A 3-bet is also unattractive here—QTo has weak blockers, doesn’t play well when called, and we don’t have a strong read that Flash is opening/folding wide enough UTG to make a bluff 3-bet clearly profitable. Best is to pass and preserve chips for better spots.

I hold 8c4h on the Button. This is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. The player in UTG (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB), which typically indicates a strong range coming from early position. Despite having position, my hand has very poor equity against an UTG opening range and poor playability post-flop. There is no strategic merit in calling or bluff-raising with this specific holding. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Facing a raise to 300 from UTG (Flash) in a 5-handed game, with Jd6s offsuit from the small blind. This is a marginal hand with poor postflop playability OOP against a strong UTG opening range. Equity is low, and calling 250 into a 450 pot commits too much without position or hand strength. Folding preserves my 59BB stack for better spots.

K3o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Flash opening from UTG typically has a strong range, and K3o is dominated by most of his value hands (AK, KQ, KJ, etc.). Even getting ~2.25:1 odds, this hand plays poorly postflop out of position. Clear fold.

5-handed UTG I need to open fairly tight. 2h4s is an offsuit, disconnected trash hand with very poor equity realization and reverse implied odds when called/3-bet, especially out of position versus the field. No good reason to limp at 50/100 with these stacks. Best play is to simply fold and preserve stack.

I hold Pocket Queens, a premium starting hand, in the Cutoff position (UTG+1). My goal is to build the pot for value and deny equity to free-rollers. A standard open-raise is the correct play here. Based on my previous play (Hand #33 where I opened to 250) and the table standard (often 250 or 300), I will raise to 250. This amount balances my range and encourages calls from inferior hands while charging a price to see the flop.

Preflop on the button facing an UTG+1 raise to 250 into a 400 pot from Pro (7100 stack). Qh8d is offsuit, mediocre hand with poor postflop playability against an early position opening range, which likely includes strong hands like pairs, broadways, suited connectors. While position allows some speculative calls or 3bets, this hand has low equity, gets dominated often (e.g., by Q9+, KQ, A8+), and doesn't block strong hands well for bluffs. Pot odds to call (250 to win 650, ~27.8% needed) don't justify entering with a hand this weak given implied odds risks and Pro's history of occasional strength. Folding preserves stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

J5o is a weak hand, especially out of position against an UTG+1 open. Pro's raising range from early position should be reasonably strong. This is a clear fold - the hand doesn't have enough equity or playability to profitably continue.

I have Ace-four suited in the Big Blind, which is a strong starting hand in a 5-handed game. Pro raised to 250 from UTG+1. A4s is an excellent hand to defend the Big Blind with due to its nut-flush potential and straight possibilities. While it is a candidate for a 3-bet bluff because it blocks AA and AK, calling is a standard and lower-variance play that allows me to see a flop and realize my equity. Pro has a stack of 7,100, and I am getting good pot odds to call the additional 150.

On this Th-7s-Js flop, I have one overcard (Ace) and a backdoor nut flush draw with the Ah. This board texture is quite connected and likely connects well with a standard raising range from UTG+1 (Pro). As the defender in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. I don't have enough equity to lead out, and checking allows me to realize my equity or potentially see a free turn if Pro checks back.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qc Qs) on a coordinated flop of Ts 7s Jh. I have an overpair, which is a strong hand, but the board is 'wet' with straight draws (89 makes a straight, various gutshots) and a flush draw (two spades). My opponent (Flash) is in the Big Blind and likely has a wide range that connects well with this board (pairs, straight draws, flush draws). Checking back is risky because approximately 40% of the turn cards (A, K, 8, 9, spade) could either scare me, improve the opponent, or kill my action. Therefore, I should bet for value (targeting Jx, Tx, and draws) and protection. A bet size of roughly 55-60% of the pot (300 into 550) is appropriate to charge draws while not overcommitting against potential two-pair or made straights immediately.

On this Jack-Ten-Seven flop, the board is extremely coordinated and connects very well with Pro's UTG+1 raising range, which likely includes hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, JTs, and sets. While I have one overcard (the Ace) and a backdoor nut flush draw with the Ace of Hearts, the board texture is too dangerous to continue. An Ace turn could be dominated by AJ or AT, and I have no spade coverage on a two-spade board. Pro's bet of 300 into 550 is a strong continuation bet, and I lack the equity or favorable position to justify a call or a bluff. I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

I am holding 2s Tc in the UTG position with a stack of 76.5 BB. This is an extremely weak hand (off-suit, unconnected low cards) with very poor playability. Playing trash hands from early position is a losing strategy in the long run, especially against active opponents with deep stacks behind me. The correct and only decision here is to fold immediately.

9s3d is a complete trash hand from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. No playability, low equity, no suited or connected value. Standard fold to preserve stack and avoid tough spots OOP with a weak range. Position and stack depth don't change this.

On the button with Q6o after two folds. This is a good spot to attempt a steal with position. Q6o isn't premium but decent enough for a button steal attempt against the blinds. Raising to 2.5x to apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Q♥ 4♠. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button. Q4o is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind, especially when I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Against a button open, I should play a tighter range. This hand is well below the threshold for calling or 3-betting. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Claude is opening the button wide, but 5♠2♦ offsuit is at the very bottom of any defend range and plays terribly out of position with heavy reverse implied odds. Even getting ~27% immediate pot odds (150 to win 400), we’ll realize equity poorly and face tough postflop decisions. A 3-bet bluff with this hand is unnecessarily spewy given stack depths and Claude’s ability to continue. Best stack-preserving decision is to fold.

UTG position in 5-handed game with 68o, a weak offsuit non-connected hand with poor playability post-flop. No position advantage or stealing incentive here against deep-stacked opponents behind. Standard fold to avoid difficult spots and preserve medium stack (59BB). Prioritizing tight-aggressive play to maximize long-term stack growth.

3s9s is a weak suited hand with poor connectivity. In early position (UTG+1), this is a clear fold. The hand lacks the strength to open-raise profitably and calling would be even worse. Standard fold here.

I am on the button with 7-2 offsuit, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While this is a common position to attempt a steal, the hand is extremely difficult to play post-flop if either of the blinds calls. Given my healthy stack and the early/middle stage of the tournament, there is no strategic reason to take unnecessary risks with such a poor hand. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Folds to us in the small blind, so this is a very profitable steal spot against the big blind. Even with a weak holding (6h3d), raising applies pressure and often wins the pot immediately. Completing invites us to play a weak hand out of position, and the BB’s shorter stack can put us in tough spots postflop. I’ll open to a standard small-blind size that still allows us to comfortably fold if the BB shoves.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kh Kc) in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has raised to 300. FiveTwo is an active player who has shown aggression but also folded to my 3-bet when I held Kings previously (Hand #22). In a Blind vs Blind scenario, ranges are wide. By flat calling, I disguise the strength of my hand, keep their wide opening range involved, and use my position to extract value post-flop against their likely c-bets or bluffs, rather than forcing a fold pre-flop from their weaker hands.

We opened very wide from the SB and got a BB defend. On J♠ Q♠ 2♦ our range can credibly represent strong one-pair+ and big spade combos, but this specific hand (6♥3♦) is near the bottom: no spade, no real backdoor equity, and very poor turn-barrel prospects when called. A small c-bet would get through sometimes, but versus a BB calling range that contains lots of Qx/Jx, Kx/Ax with a spade, and straight/flush draws, we’ll be forced to shut down too often. Checking keeps the pot small and lets Pro bluff into us; we can simply fold to most bets and continue only if checked through and we pick up rare equity.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kh Kc) on a board of [Js Qs 2d]. The opponent (FiveTwo) checked to me. The board is wet with flush and straight draws. My overpair is likely ahead, but vulnerable to free cards (Spades, Aces, Tens, Nines). Checking back would be a mistake as it allows draws to realize equity for free and misses a street of value from weaker pairs like Qx or Jx which are well within the Small Blind's wide opening range. I need to bet for value and protection. A bet size of roughly 60% of the pot (350 into 600) extracts value from top pair and draws while denying profitable odds for flush draws.

We checked flop with the bottom of range (6♥3♦) on J♠ Q♠ 2♦: no spade, no pair, and only a very thin runner-runner straight possibility. Pro’s small stab is going to get called/raised only by hands with real equity (Qx/Jx, spade draws, some KTx/ATx type hands), and we have essentially no profitable continues or barrel cards. Best play is to give up and preserve stack rather than float or turn this into a low-equity bluff versus a BB range that connects with this board a lot.
K3s from UTG is too weak to open. While suited, K3 lacks the playability needed from early position. Better to fold and wait for a stronger hand.

I am in UTG+1 with Ten-Eight offsuit. This is a weak hand in an early position, even in a five-handed game. My stack is healthy at 14,550, and there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better opening opportunity.

Folds to me on the button 5-handed. 98o is comfortably in my BTN opening range: good equity vs blind defend ranges, decent playability in position, and we pick up the dead 150 very often. Limping gives up initiative and lets the blinds realize equity too easily. I’ll use a small/standard steal size to risk little while still applying pressure to the shorter BB stack.

The opponent on the Button (FiveTwo) has been opening a wide range of hands, currently raising to 250 (2.5 BB). I am in the Small Blind with 6c4c, a suited one-gapper. While this hand has some playability, playing it out of position against an aggressive opponent is difficult. The price to call is 200 chips into a pot of 400, offering 2:1 direct odds, but my realized equity will be significantly lower due to being out of position and the threat of the Big Blind squeezing or overcalling. FiveTwo has shown a tendency to barrel post-flop, making it expensive to chase draws. 64s is slightly too weak to defend consistently from the SB against a 2.5x open. Preserving my stack of 79 BB for better opportunities is the prudent long-term play.

Facing a button raise to 250 (2.5x) from FiveTwo, who tends to open wide from late position based on hand history (frequent raises and some bluffs shown). My T7c suited is a strong defensive hand in the BB: suited connectors with straight/flush potential, good playability, and decent equity (~35-40% vs. wide button range including Ax suited, broadway, pairs, suited gappers). Pot odds are excellent: 150 to call into 400 pot (getting ~2.67:1, needing ~27% equity). Implied odds strong given stacks (mine 58BB, villain deeper). Raising risks too much with speculative hand; folding too tight vs. button. Calling to realize equity and see flop multiway potential if SB defends, but SB folded so heads-up.

Facing a highly coordinated flop (5h Qh Th) with a completed flush draw on hearts, straight draws (e.g., 89, J9), and overcards possible. My Tc7c gives top pair (tens) with a weak kicker, blocking some Tx value from villain's button range, but vulnerable to Qx, better Tx, sets, or draws. Pre-flop, villain's button raise is wide (suited Ax, broadways, pairs, connectors), and my call realized equity well—we're heads-up OOP with ~56BB effective. Pot 550, acting first: leading risks folding out worse (e.g., Ax suited hearts he might float), while checking induces bluffs/draws from his wide c-bet range (~60-70% freq on this board) and allows pot control. Plan: check-call small/medium bets with decent equity (~40% vs polarized range), check-fold to large overbets or check-raise monsters. Protects against reverse floats; deep stacks support multi-street play. Avoids bloating pot OOP with marginal top pair on scary board.

This is a very wet, high-connectivity flop (Qh-Th-5h) that smashes a BB defend range: lots of suited hearts, Qx/Tx, and combo draws that won’t fold to a small c-bet. With 9s8d I have no heart blocker, no pair, and only a weak gutshot to the J, so betting risks getting floated/raised and puts me in tough barrel decisions with low equity. In position, the higher-EV line is to take the free card, realize my gutshot equity, and look to stab favorable turns (J, A/K, or some bricks) when his range stays capped.

Turn board pairs the 5s, making the board even scarier: 5h Qh Th 5s now possible trips, full houses (Q5, T5, 55), while killing some straight draws but keeping flush draws live if hearts. My top pair tens with 7 kicker improved relatively (blocks some T5s), but still vulnerable to Qx, Ax Q-high, sets, two-pair combos, or the paired board hands. Villain's flop check behind suggests not a strong c-betting hand (missed top pair/draw/value), likely weaker range: missed Ax suited (non-heart flush draws), broadway blockers, lower pairs, air. Preflop button raise wide, but checking this flop caps high-value range somewhat. OOP first to act, pot 550 (~9BB), effective stack 57BB deep. Betting risks: thin value called by better (Qx), folds out worse Ax/draws, or raises from monsters. Check best: pot control with marginal top pair weak kicker on paired scary board; induces bluffs/value bets from villain's polarized checking range (weaker than c-bet hands); ~45%+ equity vs. that range (per previous flop analysis adjusted for board pairing). Plan: check-call small/medium bets (up to ~1/2 pot), check-fold overbets/shoves, check-raise only quads+. Maximizes EV long-term in tournament by realizing equity OOP without bloating pot.

After checking back a very dynamic flop, BB has now checked twice on a turn that pairs the low card. That line often caps him at a lot of ace-high/king-high/no-pair hands, some weak Tx, and some draws. My 9♠8♦ has little showdown value and only a thin gutshot, so the best way to realize equity is to take the pot down with fold equity. I’ll use a medium stab that pressures his weak holdings without risking too much; if he check-raises, I just fold.

Facing a 350 bet into 550 pot (~2/3 pot) on this turn board (5h Qh Th 5s), which pairs the 5 and keeps flush draws live while improving trips/straights possible. Preflop button raise wide from FiveTwo (aggressive per history: frequent steals, postflop bets even with marginal shows). Flop check-behind caps villain's range somewhat (missed high-card draws, Ax suited non-hearts, lower pairs, air rather than Qx/Tx/sets/draws he'd c-bet). Turn bet likely polarized: thin value (Qx, T5s/Q5s suited hearts, trips) or bluffs (missed flush/straight draws now turning into semi-bluffs, or pure air inducing folds). My Tc7c: top pair tens weak kicker, blocks some Tx, decent equity (~35-45% vs polarized range via equity calcs adjusted for history). Pot odds excellent: 350 into 900 (pot becomes 1250, ~2.57:1, need 28%). OOP call realizes equity without bloating pot; folding too tight vs aggressive villain who bets wide; raising too thin (weak kicker vulnerable to better top pair, check-raises jammed by monsters). Plan: call, evaluate river OOP—fold to big bets if blank (protects vs value), call/raise rivers if improves (e.g., club flush unlikely but runner-runner). Deep effective stacks (my 57BB) support multi-street play; preserves stack for tournament survival while exploiting villain's aggression long-term.

River Ace of diamonds is a relatively neutral card: bricks most remaining flush/straight draws (hearts flush missed unless holding it, no new diamonds flush), but improves villain's Ax suited/high (e.g., AQs/AJh hearts from button open range) to top pair or better, possible AJ for straight (but unlikely given flop check). Board scary with paired 5s, possible trips (55, Q5s, T5s), two-pair QxTx. Preflop button raise wide (suited broadway, Ax, pairs, connectors per history); flop check caps strong value somewhat (Qx/Tx/sets more likely c-bet, especially hearts draw), turn bet 350 into 550 (~2/3 pot) polarized: thin value (Qx suited, two-pair) vs. bluffs (missed draws like J9s, 89 hearts, air). My Tc7c top pair weak kicker blocks some Tx, solid equity (~40%+ vs. polarized range post-turn call with great odds). Called turn OOP realizing equity deep (53BB eff.). Now river first OOP, pot 1250 (~12.5BB): Betting thin value risks folds from worse Ax/draws or calls/raises from better Qx/Ax/trips; no strong blockers to villain's value. Check best: pot control marginal top pair weak kicker; induces bluffs/value bets from villain's aggressive history (frequent postflop aggression even marginal); check-call small/medium bets (~1/2 pot, pot odds >2:1), check-fold overbets/shoves/monsters, happy to showdown if checks behind. Maximizes EV exploiting wide button range long-term in tournament without risking stack OOP.

River brings the Ad after I stabbed turn and got called. With 9-high I have essentially no showdown value, so checking back is losing versus any pair/Qx and even many missed-heart hands that still have overcard high. The Ace is a strong card for my perceived range (BTN open contains plenty of Ax that can check back flop on a very wet texture, then bet turn for value/protection), while it’s uncomfortable for Elon’s turn check-call range (many missed heart draws, Tx, small pairs, and some Qx now downgraded). I’ll apply maximum pressure on his capped/medium-strength holdings with a slightly over-pot polar bet; if he check-raises, I abandon the bluff.

River Ad is a strong improvement card for villain's wide button range that checked flop (Ax suited/high non-nut draws, broadway blockers, lower pairs, air). Pairs the board with 55/Q5/T5 trips possible, enables AJT straight/nut flush blocks unnecessary since hearts missed. My T7c is top pair weak kicker, blocks some Tx but crushed by Ax (now top pair aces), Qx, trips, straights (e.g., AJ makes broadway). Villain's line: pre button raise (wide ~30-40% per history aggression), flop check (caps nutted value, polarized weaker/missed), turn bet 350 (~2/3 pot) after 5s pairs board (value trips/two-pair or semi-bluff missed draws), river pot-sized 1500 bet strong value polarization (Ax hits, monsters). Pot odds 1500 into 2750+1500=4250 (~2.83:1, need ~26%) but equity <25% vs value-heavy range (Ax 50%+, Qx/Trips/sets 100% loss); vs bluffs win but history shows FiveTwo value-bets rivers aggressively when ahead (e.g., Hand 16,43,50). OOP no fold equity on call, stack 5325 calling to 3825 commits ~28% stack risking vs superior hands. Previous plan: check-call small/medium, fold overbets/large (this qualifies ~55% pot). Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament, exploits villain thin value/overbetting long-term by not paying off.

I am dealt 9c 4h in the UTG position. This is a very weak starting hand, especially for an early position opening. With a healthy stack of 14,550 and plenty of hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to commit chips with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

5-handed and first-in from the cutoff, we can open fairly wide, but J6o is near the bottom of an optimal opening range and plays poorly postflop (dominated Jx, weak kicker, limited equity realization). With an active big blind (Claude) and a capable small blind (Elon) who can 3-bet, this hand will often have to fold to pressure or get into reverse-implied-odds spots when we make a jack. Preserving chips and waiting for better open candidates (suited connectors, better offsuit broadways/aces) is higher EV.

I am on the Button with a pocket pair of Deuces (22). The action has folded to me. This is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play the pot in position. 22 is a vulnerable hand, but playing it from the Button gives me a significant advantage. If called, I will look to hit a set or evaluate the flop texture. A raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) is a standard opening size.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 250 (200 to call into 400 pot). Hand is Ad4c, offsuit ace with poor kicker, weak overall. Position is poor (SB OOP postflop). Button raiser (Pro) likely has wide range, but A4o doesn't play well postflop, especially OOP. Pot odds ~3:1 but equity realization low due to dominated when hitting ace and trouble when missing. Deep stacks (53bb), no need to gamble with marginal hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

K8 suited in the BB facing a button open to 250. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). K8s is a comfortable defend against a button steal - has decent high card value and flush potential. Calling is standard here.

Missed this flop completely - I have Kd8d on 9s Ac 4s. No pair, no draw (wrong suit). Checking is standard here. I have showdown value with K-high if it checks through, but betting into the preflop aggressor on an Ace-high board with no equity would be turning my hand into a bluff unnecessarily.

I hold pocket deuces (22) on an Ace-high board (9s, Ac, 4s). As the pre-flop aggressor on the Button, this board texture favors my range (containing many Ax hands), but my specific hand is very weak relative to the board. The Big Blind (Claude) checks to me. While a continuation bet could fold out overcards or weak pairs, Claude has shown a willingness to check-raise on Ace-high boards in previous hands (e.g., Hand #1). If I bet and get called or raised, I have very little equity to improve (only 2 outs). Checking back allows me to pot control, realize my minimal equity, and potentially use my hand as a bluff-catcher on later streets or fold cheaply if faced with aggression. Therefore, checking is the best move to minimize loss and avoid difficult spots.

Turn brought 2h, a complete brick. I still have K-high with no draws. Pro checked behind on the flop which could indicate weakness, but they could also be trapping with an Ace. My K-high has some showdown value if it checks through. Betting as a bluff here is risky since Pro might have checked to trap. Best to check and try to get to showdown cheaply.

I have improved to a set of Deuces on the Turn with the [2h]. The board is [9s, Ac, 4s, 2h]. Claude checks to me again. Now that I have a very strong hand on a somewhat wet board (flush draw, possible straight draws with 3-5), I must start building the pot. Checking back the flop disguised my hand strength, so a bet now looks like a delayed c-bet or a stab. I want to extract value from Ace-x hands, flush draws, and pairs like 9x. Checking again would be a mistake as it gives a free card to draws and misses a street of value. I will bet approximately 60% of the pot (325) to charge draws and get calls from top pair.

Pro checked flop then bet 325 on turn. I have K-high with no draws on 9s Ac 4s 2h. Getting ~2.7:1 but my K-high is rarely good here against a delayed c-bet. Pro likely has some showdown value they're protecting (weak ace, pocket pair). No reason to continue with this hand - folding is clearly correct.

5-handed from UTG, 5c3d offsuit is well below a profitable opening/calling threshold. It has poor playability, makes dominated weak pairs/straights, and performs badly versus 3-bets. With a short-stacked button who can profitably shove over opens, opening this hand increases variance for little EV. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff position (UTG+1 in 5-handed play) with Jack-Seven suited (J7s). This is a marginal hand. While the Cutoff allows for wider opening ranges to steal the blinds, J7s is at the very bottom or outside of a standard opening range, especially with active deep-stacked opponents in the blinds (Claude and Flash) and the Button (Elon) still to act. Raising risks domination by better Jacks or being 3-bet. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack of 82 big blinds.

Preflop on the button with T2s suited after two early folds. Excellent position with initiative in an unopened pot. T2s has speculative value (straight/flush potential) and is playable postflop in position against blinds. Standard play is to raise for value/steal the dead money (150 in pot). Raising 3x BB to 300 provides good fold equity against likely calling ranges from blinds and builds pot if called. My stack of 52BB supports this aggression. Opponents deep but history shows they fold pre often.
8c4s is a very weak hand - offsuit, no connectivity, and I'll be out of position against Elon's button raise. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit (J4o). Elon has raised from the Button to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), J4o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against a standard button opening range. Defending here would likely lead to losing more chips on future streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Spades and Eight of Hearts (T8o). With 5 players at the table, UTG is still an early position. T8o is a weak off-suit hand that does not fall into a standard opening range from this position, especially with 82 big blinds deep. There is no strategic merit in trying to steal the blinds from UTG with this holding, nor is there reason to limp. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips for better spots.

3c6s is a complete trash hand, uncoordinated, low cards, offsuit. From UTG+1 (early position), with only Pro folded before me, this is an automatic fold. No value, no playability post-flop, and raising would be spew. Preserve my 54BB stack for premium hands in a deep-stack tournament. Pot is small (150), but no need to commit chips here.

On the button with J5o, folded to me. This is a good spot to steal the blinds. J5o isn't premium but it's playable from the button when it folds to me. Standard 2.5x raise to pick up the blinds or play in position postflop.

King-Jack offsuit is a premium hand when facing a standard button open from Claude. Being in the small blind, I am at a positional disadvantage for the rest of the hand. Calling would invite the big blind (FiveTwo), who is currently the chip leader and very active, to squeeze or see a cheap flop. To prevent this and take initiative, I will 3-bet to 850. This amount (3.4x the open) puts pressure on the button's wider range and should ideally win the pot pre-flop or allow me to lead on most flops. Claude has shown a willingness to fold to 3-bets in previous hands (e.g., Hand #55), which increases the effectiveness of this move.

We’re in the big blind facing an open from Claude and a 3-bet from Flash. With 8♠4♥ offsuit we have poor equity versus both players’ likely ranges, no relevant blockers, and very weak postflop playability—especially out of position in a bloated pot (and potentially multiway if Claude continues). A call is dominated and a 4-bet bluff is too ambitious with this hand profile and these stack depths. Best decision is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash with J5o. My steal attempt got challenged. J5o is far too weak to continue against a 3-bet range - I have very poor equity and can easily find myself dominated. Even with position, calling here would be lighting money on fire. Easy fold.

UTG position with 5h9d offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity, and no strong potential. Standard pre-flop strategy dictates folding trash hands from early position to avoid difficult post-flop spots out of position against a full table. Pot is small (150), to call 100 is not worth risking with this hand. Preserving stack for better opportunities in later position or with premium hands aligns with maximizing long-term EV over 150 hands.
Pocket 99 in UTG+1 is a strong hand. Standard play is to raise for value. I'll open to 2.5x BB (250) to build the pot while not overcommitting with a medium pair.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven suited, which is a strong hand in a 5-handed game. Claude has opened for 250 from UTG+1. In the previous hand (Hand #64), Claude opened for the same amount and folded when I 3-bet to 850. While repeating this action might appear aggressive, A7s is an excellent candidate for a 3-bet because it contains an Ace blocker and has good post-flop playability. By 3-betting, I put pressure on the blinds and force Claude to either define his hand or fold. If he calls, I have the advantage of position throughout the rest of the hand. I will size my raise to 750 to maintain a consistent 3-betting strategy.

K2o is far too weak to continue versus an UTG+1 open and a button 3-bet, especially from the small blind where we’ll be out of position and likely invite the big blind in getting a good price. Calling creates a dominated, low-equity multiway 3-bet pot; 4-bet bluffing here risks too much versus ranges that can continue/jam, and we have no playability when called. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I hold T4o in the Big Blind facing a raise from Claude (UTG+1) and a 3-bet from Flash (Button). This is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and equity against strong ranges. I am getting roughly 2.7:1 odds, but with the original raiser still to act and being out of position against a 3-bettor, calling is not profitable. This is a trivial fold to preserve chips.

Flash 3-bet to 750 from button. With pocket 99, I'm facing a common set-mining spot. Pot is 1150, I need 500 to call - getting about 2.3:1. Flash could be 3-betting light from button position. 99 is too strong to fold but not strong enough to 4-bet for value. Calling to set mine with decent implied odds makes sense - if I hit a set I can stack a big hand.

Terrible flop for my 99. Both an Ace and Queen hit - two major overcards. Flash's 3-bet range from the button hits this board hard with Ax, KQ, QJ, AK type hands. My pocket 9s are likely behind now. I should check and likely fold to a bet - no point in putting more money in when I'm crushed on this board texture.

On a flop of Ac-Qc-4d, I have flopped top pair with an Ace. As the pre-flop 3-better, I have a significant range advantage on this board texture, as my range contains more AA, QQ, and AK than Claude's calling range. While my kicker (7) is relatively weak, I still have a value hand against his likely calling range of mid-pairs (88-JJ) or suited broadways involving a Queen. Betting small here (roughly 1/3 pot) allows me to maintain initiative, extract value from Queens or draws, and simplify my decision-making on later streets while keeping the pot manageable if I am currently out-kicked by AJ or AQ.

Flash 3-bet preflop and c-bets into A-Q high board. My 99 is crushed here - Flash's 3-bet range (AK, AQ, AJ, KQ, QQ, AA, KK) smashes this board. I have only 2 outs to improve and terrible reverse implied odds. Clear fold.
4c7c is a weak suited connector. From UTG (earliest position), I need to play tight. This hand doesn't have enough equity to open raise profitably from this position against 4 players yet to act.

In a 5-handed game, QJ offsuit is a solid hand to open from middle position (UTG+1). Claude has already folded, leaving only three players behind me. I have a healthy stack and have been successfully applying pressure in recent hands. A standard 3x open (300 chips) is appropriate here to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position against the blinds.

Flash opened to 3bb and we’re on the button with J4o. This hand is heavily dominated by Flash’s opening range (Jx, broadways, better suited connectors) and has poor playability postflop even in position. Calling burns EV, and a 3-bet bluff is unattractive given Flash has shown willingness to continue versus aggression. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold 5s 2h in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unsuited, disconnected, low ranks). Flash has raised to 300 from UTG+1, indicating a potentially strong range. Being out of position with a trash hand against an early position raise offers poor playability and negative expected value. Even with pot odds, the hand is too weak to defend. Folding is the only reasonable option.

Preflop in BB facing an UTG+1 raise to 300 from Flash after folds from Claude, FiveTwo, and Pro (SB). Pot 450, calling 200 more (total 300 from me). KcTc is a strong suited connector with good playability OOP. Flash's EP raise range is strong but KTs has good equity against it (~40-45% vs typical EP range), blocks AA/KK, dominates broadways like QJs/KQo. Excellent pot odds (3:1), position heads-up postflop allows realizing equity on favorable boards. Folding too tight; 3-betting polarized range not ideal with this hand. Defend wide in BB vs single raise. Plan to play postflop aggressively on good boards, control pot on misses.

Flop board 4d Qc 7d is somewhat coordinated with diamond flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., 56, 85). My KcTc completely misses: King-high overcard, backdoor club flush draw (three clubs total), and minimal straight potential. Heads-up OOP vs Flash's strong UTG+1 range (high pairs, broadway, suited aces), which connects well with Qx, 77, 76s+, diamond draws. Equity ~25-35% vs polarized range, but OOP realization is poor without a draw. Pot 650, no bet yet, first to act. Betting risks folding out equity or getting raised; check allows pot control, induces bluffs from missed draws/A-high, takes free card if checked behind. Plan: check-call small/medium if draw-heavy board evolves favorably or check-fold to aggression. Stack 5125 effective, preserve for better spots.

On a flop of 4d-Qc-7d, I have top pair with a Jack kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot against the Big Blind. Elon checked to me, so I should place a continuation bet to get value from weaker hands like middle pairs, draws (especially diamond draws), or even some Ace-high hands. A bet of around half the pot (350 chips) is standard here to protect my hand and build the pot.

Facing a half-pot c-bet from Flash's strong UTG+1 range on a Qc-7d-4d flop that connects well with pairs, Qx, sets, and flush draws. KcTc is complete air: king-high no pair/draw, only backdoor club potential. Equity ~25% vs polarized c-bet range but poor OOP playability/realization on draw-heavy board. Pot odds ~3.8:1 require ~21% equity, but calling risks stacking off to better hands or folding to turn aggression while burning equity. Folding preserves stack for better spots; consistent with preflop BB defense plan to check-fold misses. Flash's history shows aggression, no reason to float here.

I'm in UTG in a 5-handed game with 6h 7h. While it's the earliest position, the table is short-handed enough that opening suited connectors like 76s is standard to maintain an aggressive image and realize equity post-flop. A 3x raise (300) is consistent with my previous play.

Facing a 3x open from Flash UTG, J3o is too dominated and plays very poorly multiway/in position against an UTG range. Calling invites reverse implied odds (making weak jacks), and it’s not a good 3-bet bluff candidate given the lack of meaningful blockers and terrible postflop playability. Preserve stack and wait for better spots to attack opens with stronger blockers/connectivity.

I am holding Queen-Deuce offsuit on the Button. The player in UTG (Flash) has opened for a raise to 300. Q2o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity against an opening range, even a loose one. Given the raise in front of me, I have no reason to get involved in this pot. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Facing an UTG raise from Flash to 300 (3x BB), which indicates a strong range. My hand is 89o, offsuit connectors with mediocre playability. In the small blind, I'm out of position post-flop, and my stack is 5075 (about 50BB), not deep enough to speculate profitably against an early position raiser. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 are decent (~1.8:1), but BB still to act and likely to defend or raise, worsening odds. Equity realization OOP with this hand vs UTG range is poor; better to fold and preserve stack for better spots. No history suggests Flash is overly loose UTG.

I have 9c7c in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. Suited connectors have decent playability, and I'm getting about 2.25:1 on the call (200 to win 450). While I'm out of position, the implied odds with a suited connector make this a reasonable defend. 3-betting would be too aggressive against UTG, and folding is too tight given pot odds.

Board is KKK - three kings. I have 9c7c, giving me trip kings with 9-7 kickers. Flash raised UTG which typically indicates a strong range (big pairs, AK, AQ, broadway). Against that range: if they have any King they have quads, any pocket pair gives them a full house, and any unpaired hand likely has higher kickers than my 9-7. I'm in bad shape against most of their range. Best to check and see what they do - no value in betting and I'd be forced to fold to aggression.

On a flop of K-K-K, the board is extremely dry and favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes more pocket pairs and high Ace-high hands. Although my 7-high is currently just Three of a Kind with a weak kicker, Claude's check allows me to place a continuation bet to represent a stronger hand (like an Ace or a pocket pair). A small bet here is often enough to force a fold from many of the hands Claude would defend the big blind with that missed the board, allowing me to take down the pot without a showdown.

Board is KKK. I have trip kings with 9-7 kickers. Flash opened UTG (strong range) and bet 250 into 650. Against UTG range: any K = quads, any pocket pair = full house, any A/Q/J/T = better kicker than my 9. I beat almost nothing in their range. Even with 3.6:1 pot odds, my equity is too low. Fold.

5-handed UTG, AQs is a clear open for value and initiative. Limping lets the blinds realize equity too cheaply and invites multiway pots where our top-pair hands are harder to play. I’ll use a standard 2.5x open that pressures the blinds and still allows me to respond flexibly versus 3-bets (continue vs reasonable 3-bets, and be prepared to 4-bet selectively depending on size/opponent).

I am holding Jh 6s in UTG+1 facing a raise from FiveTwo in UTG. J6 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and is dominated by the opener's range. There are still three players left to act behind me. The only reasonable play is to fold.

Qc3s is a very weak offsuit hand, not suited or connected, dominated by most UTG raising ranges. FiveTwo's UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB) indicates strength, even if they are aggressive historically. On the button with position, but pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 400 for ~1.6:1), and my stack is 5075 (~50BB). Defending wide is unnecessary; standard fold preserves stack for better spots in this tournament phase (hand 68/150). No squeeze value with this holding against deep stacks.
6d9s is a weak hand out of position against a UTG raise. Poor connectivity and no suited advantage. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Eight suited. FiveTwo, who has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands, raised from UTG to 250. I only need to call 150 more to see a flop, getting nearly 3:1 on my money. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the strength of King-Eight suited and the favorable pot odds make this a standard defend in the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with a King-high flush draw on a 2d-5c-6c board. I am facing the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo, who raised from UTG. Since I am out of position, checking to the raiser is the standard play. My hand has significant equity with the flush draw and one overcard (the King). If FiveTwo bets, I will likely call to see the turn, or potentially check-raise if I want to play the draw aggressively. For now, checking is the best way to start the action on this street.

Heads-up as preflop raiser on 2d-5c-6c, this board connects much better with BB’s defending range (pairs, 65/54/76, lots of club combos) than with our UTG range. With AsQh we have two overcards but no club backdoor and little ability to continue comfortably versus a check-raise. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and lets us value-bet turns when we improve (A/Q) while avoiding getting put in tough spots on a dynamic texture.

I have turned the King-high flush with the Kc8c on a board of 2d-5c-6c-Ac. Since the Ace of Clubs is on the board, my flush is the highest possible flush at the moment (the only hand beating me is exactly 3c4c for a straight flush, which is highly unlikely for an UTG raiser). On the flop, both of us checked. The Ace is a significant card that likely improves FiveTwo's range if he has high cards, or it provides a good scare card for him to bluff. By checking again, I am playing my monster hand deceptively, giving him the opportunity to bet his Aces or attempt a bluff. If he bets, I will likely just call to keep his range wide and maximize value on the river, unless his bet size suggests he is committed to the hand.

Turn brings the Ac giving us top pair (A) with a strong kicker, and BB checks again. After we checked back flop, BB’s range contains lots of 5x/6x, pocket pairs, and some weaker Ax that can call a small value bet. At the same time, the 3-club texture and some straight combos (notably 34) mean we don’t want to build a huge pot or get forced to stack off versus a check-raise. I’ll take a controlled, thin value/protection stab that targets worse made hands while keeping my range protected and allowing an easy fold if raised big.

I have turned a King-high flush (Kc8c) on a board of 2d-5c-6c-Ac. With the Ace of Clubs on the board, the only hand that beats me is exactly 3c-4c for a straight flush, which is highly improbable given FiveTwo's UTG raise. After checking the flop and checking the turn, FiveTwo has bet 225 into 550. While calling is an option to keep his bluffs in, FiveTwo has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands (Hands #24, #48). I want to build the pot now to extract maximum value from an Ace, a lower flush, or a set. A raise also protects against a fourth club on the river which might kill the action or make my hand less certain (though I hold the King). I will raise to 725 to induce a call or a potential reraise from an aggressive opponent.

We’re getting a very good price (500 to win a pot that’s already sizable) and our hand is underrepped after checking back the flop. Flash’s check-raise on the Ac is often value (made flushes/straights/two pair), but the sizing is not huge and he can also do this with some worse Ax (protection) and occasional club/semi-bluff holdings. With top pair/top kicker we can’t profitably 3-bet (we’d isolate ourselves vs stronger hands and risk getting stacked on a 3-club board), but folding immediately is too tight given the pot odds and position. I’ll call and re-evaluate river: continue mainly on blanks versus reasonable sizing, and be prepared to fold on club/straightening rivers or heavy river pressure.

I have the King-high flush (Kc8c) on a board of 2d-5c-6c-Ac-3h. With the Ace of Clubs on the board, I hold the second-best possible hand, losing only to the extremely unlikely 3c-4c straight flush. On the turn, I check-raised to 725 and FiveTwo called, bringing the pot to 2000. The 3h on the river is a total blank. Since I took the lead on the turn, I should continue with a value bet to extract maximum profit from FiveTwo's likely holdings, such as an Ace, a set, or a lower flush. A bet of 1300 (65% of the pot) is a standard value sizing that aims to get called by his range while maximizing my return.

We’re getting 1300 to win 4600 (~28% needed), but Flash’s line is extremely value-dense: check flop (keeps in all suited clubs/connected hands), then check-raise turn on a 3-club board (very consistent with made flushes, sets, and strong draws), and now barrels 65% pot on a river (3h) that importantly completes the straight for any 4x (A4/74/84/etc), in addition to all the flushes already available. With AsQh we have a naked one-pair hand, no club blocker, and the river is a bad card for our relative hand strength. It’s hard to find enough natural bluffs that take this exact check-raise turn → bet river line at the frequency we need. Raising is out (only gets action from better), so the best EV is to let it go.

I am in UTG position (first to act) with Ace-Deuce offsuit (Ad 2s). In a 5-handed game, A2o is a weak hand to open from early position. It is easily dominated by better Aces and plays poorly post-flop out of position. With a healthy stack of roughly 80 big blinds, there is no pressure to make loose opens or steal attempts from this position. The standard and disciplined play is to fold.

5c3s is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low card strength, and no suited potential for flush draws. In UTG+1 position with 5 active players, this hand has negative equity against typical calling ranges and is unplayable multiway or heads-up out of position. Standard play is to fold trash preflop to preserve stack and wait for premium hands, especially with a tournament goal of maximizing stack over many hands. No history indicates loose limpers behind, but folding avoids speculative plays OOP.

On the button with K6o after two folds. This is a standard button steal opportunity. K-high has some playability and I'm in position against the blinds. Raising to 250 to attempt to take down the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 5h, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised from the button, which is a standard position to steal blinds, but my hand is not strong enough to defend out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Claude is opening from the button, so his range is wide, and the price (150 more) is tempting. But J2o is at the very bottom of a reasonable big blind defense range: it’s frequently dominated by his Jx holdings, has poor playability out of position, and will realize equity badly across postflop streets. With no good 3-bet bluff qualities and little ability to continue versus c-bets, folding is the highest-EV, lowest-variance choice.

UTG in 5-handed play with T4s, a weak suited gapper. Poor post-flop playability out of position, especially multiway. My stack is ~51BB, others deeper and aggressive based on history (frequent raises and post-flop action). Folding preserves stack for better spots; limping or raising is -EV here as first in with marginal hand.

K8 suited in UTG+1 is a marginal hand but playable with flush potential. With a healthy stack of 133bb and standard opens working well for me throughout the tournament, I'll make a standard 2.5x open raise to 250.

Claude has opened for 2.5bb from the Cutoff. I am on the Button with Six-Seven offsuit. While I have the benefit of position, this hand is significantly below the standard defending range against an early/middle position raise. It lacks the playability of suited connectors and has very little showdown value. Given the stack sizes and Claude's frequent opening, there are better opportunities to contest pots than with a marginal offsuit connector.

We’re in the small blind facing a 2.5bb open from Claude (UTG+1 5-handed). 4♠3♣ is near the bottom of the deck: poor raw equity, dominated when we make a pair, and plays terribly out of position. Completing also invites the big blind to come along, worsening our position and realization. A bluff 3-bet isn’t attractive here versus an early-position open with decent stack depths. Best EV is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o facing a 2.5x raise from Claude in the Cutoff (UTG+1 in 5-handed). The pot offers odds of 150 to call 400 (~27%), but being out of position with a weak offsuit King makes realizing equity difficult. K5o is typically dominated by the opener's calling or value range, leading to reverse implied odds. Standard charts suggest folding K5o vs a Cutoff open. I will fold to preserve my stack.
K9o in UTG at 5-handed. Marginal hand but playable in a short-handed game. I have a healthy stack at 135bb. I'll open to 2.5x as standard.

I have pocket deuces (2c 2h) in UTG+1. Claude has opened from UTG for 250. This is a classic set-mining situation. My stack is deep (over 170 BBs), and Claude also has a significant stack (over 130 BBs), meaning the implied odds are favorable if I hit a set. The price to call (250) is small relative to the potential payout if I can crack a big UTG starting hand like pocket Aces, Kings, or Ace-King. I will call and evaluate the flop, looking for a Deuce.

We’re on the button getting a decent price, but J5o is a dominated, low-connectivity hand that plays poorly multiway—especially after an UTG open and an UTG+1 flat, which keeps ranges relatively strong. A squeeze 3-bet doesn’t have great blocker value here and risks getting called in position by one or both deep stacks (or facing awkward blind jams/defends), creating low-EV postflop spots. Best decision is to pass and preserve stack for higher-quality button attacks.

I hold Pocket Tens (Th Ts) in the Small Blind. Claude (UTG) opened to 250, and Flash (UTG+1) called. Claude has been playing very loosely and opening wide, meaning TT is likely well ahead of his range. Flash's flat call suggests a capped range (likely excluding AA, KK, QQ, AK). Calling here is an option, but it invites the Big Blind to overcall, creating a difficult 4-way pot where I am out of position with a hand vulnerable to overcards. A 3-bet squeeze is the superior line: it extracts value from Claude's wide range, denies equity to random live cards, attempts to take down the dead money (650 chips) immediately, or isolates the action to a heads-up pot where I have the initiative. Based on previous behavior (Hand #22), I will size the raise to roughly 4x the open plus the caller's chips to discourage calls and maximize fold equity.

Facing a 3-bet from Pro in the SB to 1050 after Claude's UTG open to 250 and Flash's call, with 2s3s in BB OOP. This is a speculative low suited connector with minimal equity against strong 3-bet ranges (e.g., JJ+, AK, some broadway). Pot is 1650, calling 950 commits ~19% of my 4975 stack for poor implied odds multiway OOP. Equity likely <25% needed for pot odds, better to fold and preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 80 hands left.

Facing a 3-bet from Pro after I opened K9o from UTG. K9o is at the bottom of my opening range and plays poorly vs 3-bet ranges, especially out of position. Pro's 3-betting range from SB after a call is typically strong (TT+, AQ+). K9o has poor equity against this range and is dominated by hands like AK, KQ, KJ. Clear fold - not worth 800 more to play OOP with a dominated hand.

Pro's 3-bet from the Small Blind against an UTG open and an UTG+1 caller represents a very polarized and strong range, typically JJ+, AK, or occasionally AQs. I am holding pocket deuces, which have very low equity against this range (around 20%). While I have position, the primary value of deuces here is set-mining. For set-mining to be mathematically profitable, the implied odds generally need to be at least 10 to 15 times the call amount. I need to call 800, and Pro has only 5,850 chips remaining behind. This ratio (approx 7.3:1) is insufficient to justify a call based on implied odds alone. Additionally, with an SPR around 2.4 on the flop, I won't have much room to maneuver if I miss. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold.

In a 5-handed game, UTG is the earliest position. Ace-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand that is generally considered a fold in this position to avoid playing a weak-kicked Ace out of position against potentially stronger ranges. Given the stack depths and the aggressive play styles of FiveTwo and Claude, I will fold to maintain a tight and disciplined table image.

5-handed but still effectively early/middle position with three players left to act. Td6h is an offsuit, poorly connected hand that’s frequently dominated when we make a pair of tens and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus the blinds. No dead money beyond blinds and we don’t need to force marginal steals with this stack depth, especially with a short SB who can reshove over opens. Best is to pass and keep our opening range stronger here.

I am on the Button with Qh 2c. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, Queen-Deuce offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very poor post-flop playability. The Big Blind (Claude) has a large stack covering me significantly, which increases the risk of a defense or 3-bet. Opening here would be too loose given the hand strength. The standard play is to fold.

Preflop in SB with 4c2h offsuit, an extremely weak hand with poor playability, low connectivity, and no suited potential. Pot has 150 (my 50 SB + 100 BB), to call another 50 for ~3:1 implied odds but OOP postflop disaster if hit nothing. Raising risks being called or 3bet by BB Claude who defends aggressively from history (often calls/raises pre). No fold equity premium worth it with trash. Stack ~49BB healthy for tournament, fold to preserve chips for premium spots. GTO SB vs BB folds bottom ~35% including 42o.

5-handed UTG ranges are wider than full-ring, and QJs suited is a strong, playable hand that benefits from initiative. Limping invites multiway pots and makes realizing equity harder; opening applies pressure to the shorter BTN stack and allows us to win blinds/antes outright at a good frequency. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open size that’s consistent with the table’s prior sizing and keeps the pot manageable if we face a 3-bet.

I am holding 8c 2s in UTG+1 (MP), which is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker. I am facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG. Given the poor quality of my hand and the early position raise, I have no equity to continue. The only standard play is to fold.

J4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raising range from FiveTwo, who raises frequently but from early position this is still strong. On the button, position is an advantage, but calling 250 into a 400 pot offers insufficient implied odds for a hand this weak, as it rarely hits strong enough to realize equity postflop and is easily dominated. No reason to raise or bluff with this trash hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.
9c7h is a weak offsuit hand in the small blind facing an UTG raise. Out of position with poor playability. Standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Kd 8d, which is a strong suited king that plays well post-flop. FiveTwo (UTG) opened for 250, and it has folded around to me. I need to call 150 to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me excellent pot odds (27%). Given FiveTwo's relatively frequent opening range and the strength of my hand, a call is standard to realize my equity and see if I hit a flush draw or top pair.

I am first to act on the flop after calling from the Big Blind pre-flop. The board is Ac-6s-3h, which heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's (FiveTwo) UTG opening range, as it contains many Ace-high hands. I have King-high with no draws. Standard defensive play here is to check and see how the opponent reacts. If they bet, I will likely fold as I have very little equity against their range on this specific texture.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on A-6-3 rainbow, we retain a strong range advantage (we have all the big Ax and overpairs), while BB has a wide mix of whiffs and some Ax/6x/pairs. QJs has little immediate showdown value but benefits from folding out his KQ/KJ/QT/JT/98 type hands and denying equity. I’ll use a small, range-oriented c-bet that keeps my risk low and sets up easy decisions (fold to a check-raise; mostly give up on bad turns when called, continue selectively on spade/straight-improving cards).

FiveTwo opened from UTG and has now fired a continuation bet on an Ace-high board. As I am holding only King-high with no draws (not even a backdoor flush draw, as there is only one diamond on the board), I have very little equity against his range. While the bet size is small (around 1/3 pot), calling would be 'float' territory with very little chance to improve or take the pot away out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Current hand is K7 suited (Clubs) in the UTG position with 5 players remaining. The blinds are 50/100. I have a stack of 8550 (approx 85 BB). While 5-handed play encourages looser opening ranges, K7s is still on the weaker side for an Under-The-Gun open, especially with three large stacks behind me (Claude, Flash, FiveTwo) who can apply pressure or call in position, and a short stack (Elon) who might shove over an open. The hand is easily dominated by better Kings and Aces. Discretion is the better part of valor here; folding preserves chips for better spots closer to the button.

Pocket fives is a strong hand from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. Pro has folded UTG, leaving three players to act behind: Flash (SB deep), FiveTwo (BB deep), and Claude (button deep). My stack is 49BB, which is playable. Standard play is to raise for value and initiative, aiming to take down the blinds or build a pot heads-up/in position postflop. Raising to 3x the BB (300) is standard sizing here, as seen in prior hands where I've opened successfully with similar strength. Folding or limping is too passive; 55 has good equity vs calling ranges and set-mining potential.

K4o is a weak hand. Facing a UTG+1 raise from Elon, even with position on the button, this hand doesn't play well enough to call or 3-bet profitably. K4o is dominated by better Kings and doesn't have good suited/connected potential. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Six of Hearts. Elon has raised to 300 from an early position. T6o is a very weak hand and does not play well out of position against an opening range, especially from UTG+1. The pot odds are not sufficient to justify a call, and raising would be a low-percentage bluff against a relatively short stack (roughly 46 BB). Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

Elon opens from UTG+1 5-handed and we’re out of position with 9-4 offsuit—an extremely low-realization hand that’s dominated often and struggles to make strong, playable pairs or draws. Although the price is tempting (200 to win 650), the equity we realize OOP versus an opener’s range is poor and we risk bleeding chips in marginal spots. No need to defend this far down; better to preserve stack for higher-EV BB defenses and 3-bet spots.

UTG in 5-handed NLHE with 50BB stack. Holding 2c4s, which is one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, unconnected low cards, no speculative value. Early position requires tight range to avoid tough post-flop spots OOP. No prior action, but folding trash preserves stack for premium hands. Opponents have deep stacks, but irrelevant here as this hand plays poorly against any range.
6c5h is a weak hand in UTG+1 position. Not suited, not connected enough. With 3 players behind me including the blinds, this is a clear fold from early position.

I am on the button with 4d 2d. This is a very weak hand, but it is suited. However, with the current stack depths and the fact that I'm facing two blinds yet to act, a fold is the most prudent play. While it is tempting to steal, my stack of 16,825 is healthy and I don't need to take unnecessary risks with a bottom-tier starting hand.

Folds to me in the SB, so this is a classic steal spot vs the BB. Even with a weak hand like J3o, I can profitably open very wide here because Pro will still fold a meaningful portion and I’m risking relatively little. I’ll use a small 2x sizing to keep my risk down out of position and make it easy to fold if Pro 3-bets.

I am in the Big Blind with K9o facing a min-raise from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). This is a standard Blind vs Blind defense scenario. The pot odds are 3:1 (I need to call 100 to win a total pot of 400), requiring only 25% equity. K9o has significantly more equity than 25% against a typical wide SB opening range (often 50%+ of hands). I also have the advantage of position post-flop. Raising is unnecessary as it might fold out worse hands I dominate, while calling keeps the opponent's range wide and realizes my equity effectively.

Blind-vs-blind SRP and I’m the preflop aggressor. On KQ7r my actual hand (J3o) has essentially no immediate equity, but I still have decent fold equity versus Pro’s wide BB defend (lots of low/off-suit junk that missed). A small range c-bet is efficient here: it risks little, pressures his air, and keeps my sizing consistent with value hands. If I get raised I’ll just fold; if called, I’ll mostly shut down unless I pick up strong barrel cards (A/T or club turns).

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Nine kicker on a [Ks Qc 7d] board in a Blind vs Blind scenario. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has c-bet 125 into a pot of 400 (approx. 30%). This small sizing is standard for a wide range, which includes weaker Kings, Queens, draws (like JT), and air. My hand is well ahead of their bluffing range and value range of Qx or lower pairs. Raising would likely fold out the bluffs I want to keep in and isolate me against better Kings (KT+). Calling is the most profitable line to control the pot size, utilize my position, and allow the opponent to continue firing with inferior hands.

Pro called a very small flop c-bet on KQ7r, so his range contains a lot of Kx/Qx/7x, some pocket pairs, and some floats/A-highs. The 3s turn is mostly a brick for him but it improves us to a weak pair (3x), giving us some showdown value. Barreling again doesn’t target his strong continues (K/Q/7/pairs) and risks getting check-raised off our equity; it also turns our hand into a bluff when we can now win some showdowns. Best is to check, keep the pot manageable, and consider bluff-catching small river bets versus missed floats.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Nine kicker on a [Ks Qc 7d 3s] board. The Small Blind checked the turn after c-betting the flop. This check often indicates a medium-strength hand (like a Queen or a weaker pair) exercising pot control, a give-up with air, or occasionally a trap. My hand is strong enough to win at showdown but vulnerable to better kickers if the pot gets too large. Betting might fold out the hands I beat (bluffs) or isolate me against better Kings (KT+). Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce a bluff on the river or easily call a reasonable value bet. It is the lowest variance line that keeps the opponent's range wide.

Rivered a weak pair (3x) after c-betting small and checking turn. This hand now has some showdown value vs Pro’s missed floats/A-high, and a river bet is unlikely to get called by worse while frequently getting called by any Kx/Qx/7x and many pocket pairs (all of which beat us). With no real blockers and the runout not changing much, the best EV line is to take the free showdown and be prepared to bluff-catch only versus a small stab.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Nine kicker on a completed board of [Ks Qc 7d 3s 2s]. The action went check-check on the turn, and my opponent (FiveTwo in the Small Blind) has checked to me again on the river. The opponent's line of small c-bet flop, check turn, check river suggests a marginal made hand (like a Queen, a weak King, or mid-pair) or a missed draw. While I likely have the best hand, my kicker is weak, and the river brings a backdoor flush and completes a potential two-pair for random holdings like K2 or K3. Betting for thin value targets Qx hands, but risks value-owning myself against better Kings (KT+) or getting check-raised by a disguised strong hand. Given the small pot size and my stack depth, checking back is the lowest variance play to secure the pot against weaker pairs and air without opening the door to mistakes.
J3 suited from UTG is too weak to open. Even suited, this hand lacks the strength and playability needed to open from early position. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am holding King-Queen offsuit in UTG+1 (equivalent to the Cutoff in a 5-handed game). This is a strong starting hand that is well within my opening range. Claude has already folded. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and narrow the field. Both FiveTwo and Elon have shown some aggression, so I want to set a price for them to see a flop. Elon is relatively short-stacked with about 50 big blinds, so I need to be aware of a potential shove from the big blind.

Flash has been opening frequently to 3bb and has shown a willingness to fold to 3-bets. J5s is a bit too dominated to flat comfortably versus a larger open, especially with two blinds behind who can squeeze, but it works well as a light in-position 3-bet: we apply immediate pressure, often win the 450 pot outright, and when called we still have decent playability with position and a suited hand. Plan: fold to any 4-bet/shove, and in single-raised 3-bet pots use position to take advantage of favorable boards.

I hold 9d 2c in the Small Blind, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing an open raise from Flash and a 3-bet from FiveTwo, my hand has negligible equity and is completely dominated by their ranges. Folding is the only correct play here to preserve my stack.

Facing a raise from Flash (UTG+1) and a 3-bet from FiveTwo (Button) preflop in the BB with J9o. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability out of position. Pot odds are roughly 1300:750 (1.73:1), insufficient given the likely strong ranges (Ax, broadway, etc.) and my effective stack of ~5k. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

I opened to 300 with KQo, a standard open from the cutoff in a 5-handed game. FiveTwo responded with a 3-bet to 850 from the button. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the sizing of the 3-bet is relatively small (less than 3x my open), and KQo is too strong to fold at this stage of the tournament. FiveTwo has shown frequent aggression in previous hands, suggesting their 3-betting range might be wider than premium pairs and AK. I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

On this flop of Ac-5s-Ad, the board is extremely dry and heavily favors the pre-flop 3-better's range, which includes many Ace-X combinations like AK, AQ, and AJ. As the caller of the 3-bet, I am out of position and have completely missed the board with my King-Queen offsuit. I have no draws and very little equity against a range that contains pairs or Aces. Leading out would represent a very narrow range and is unlikely to fold out better hands. The most standard play is to check to the aggressor. If FiveTwo bets, I will likely fold as I have no real way to continue profitably on this texture.

We’re in a heads-up 3-bet pot with a big range advantage on A-A-5. Our hand (J5h) makes aces up with a decent kicker, which is ahead of all non-ace hands and can get value from pocket pairs (66–KK) and stubborn broadways, while still being clearly behind any Ax (and occasionally better 5x like A5). I want to apply pressure and deny equity to his whiffs, but keep the pot controlled so we don’t get blown off our equity by a big check-raise. A small continuation bet accomplishes that and keeps my overall range strategy consistent on paired-ace textures.

I started the hand with KQo and called a 3-bet from FiveTwo pre-flop. The flop comes Ac-5s-Ad, which is a very difficult texture for me as the caller. This board heavily favors the 3-better's range, as FiveTwo is likely to hold many Ace-X combinations (AK, AQ, AJ, ATs). My King-Queen has no equity against an Ace or any pocket pair, and I have no draws. While FiveTwo's bet of 500 is relatively small (about 1/4 of the pot), calling out of position with only King-high on this board is a losing play in the long run. I don't have enough potential to improve to the best hand, and even if I hit a King or Queen on the turn, I could still be drawing dead against an Ace. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am UTG with King-Six offsuit. This is a very weak hand to open from early position, even in a 5-handed game. My stack is currently healthy, and there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K7o is a marginal offsuit king that plays poorly postflop (dominated by better kings and struggles to realize equity), especially from UTG+1 where my opening range should still be fairly tight. With an active/defending big blind and a small blind that can 3-bet or apply pressure, the steal EV doesn’t justify putting chips in with this hand. Best is to pass and wait for a higher-quality open/3-bet spot.

I am on the Button with Q7o, a marginal hand. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds, looking at the history, the Big Blind (Claude) has defended frequently against raises (e.g., Hand 1, 6, 16). With a stack of 88 BB, I do not need to force action with weak-marginal hands against a sticky opponent. Q7o plays poorly post-flop even in position if called, as it is often dominated. Therefore, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the prudent decision.

Preflop in SB with 3d2h, one of the worst possible hands (32 offsuit). All players folded to me heads-up vs BB Claude. While SB vs BB is a profitable spot due to pot odds for limping (pot 150 to call 50), this hand has terrible playability post-flop out of position: low equity, easily dominated, minimal straight potential (wheel needs specific suited connectors which this lacks effectively). Claude defends BB aggressively based on history (frequent calls and raises). Raising light risks getting shoved on or playing post-flop OOP with trash. Folding preserves stack for premium hands in a tournament setting (49BB effective), prioritizing long-term EV over speculative SB steals with unplayable hand.

5-handed, AQ suited is a premium opening hand UTG. I want to build the pot versus dominated Ax/Qx, deny equity to the blinds, and keep initiative. A 2.5bb open is standard here and keeps SPR workable versus the deeper stacks while still pressuring the shorter stacks behind.

I hold Q8o in UTG+1 (MP) facing a raise to 2.5bb from the UTG player (FiveTwo). Q8 offsuit is a weak hand that is easily dominated by a UTG opening range (which includes AQ, KQ, QJs, 88+). Playing this hand out of position or even in position against a strong range is generally -EV due to reverse implied odds. With 88BB, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot. The correct play is to fold.

Facing a UTG raise from FiveTwo to 250 into a 400 pot, with Ac6s on the button. This is a playable hand for defense: suited ace with good playability, ace blocker against opponent's strong range, and excellent position. Pot odds are favorable (~3:1). My stack of 4925 (~49BB) allows for postflop flexibility without overcommitting. FiveTwo shows aggression historically but can be exploited postflop. Calling keeps the pot multiway potential while maintaining initiative options on later streets. Raising is unnecessary with this hand; folding is too tight for button range in 5-handed play.

K7o in the small blind facing an UTG raise and button call. This is a weak hand that plays poorly multiway and out of position. Even with 3.25:1 pot odds, K7o doesn't have the playability to justify calling here - it's dominated by better Kx hands and will be difficult to navigate post-flop OOP against two opponents. Clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8c 5d. FiveTwo raised from UTG to 250, and Elon called from the Button. The pot is currently 650, and it costs me 150 to call. While the pot odds are roughly 4.3 to 1, 85o is an extremely weak hand to play out of position against two opponents, one of whom raised from UTG. Defending with such a marginal hand usually leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am likely to be dominated or forced to fold. Given my healthy stack size, there is no need to take a marginal-to-losing risk here.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser, I want to keep initiative and deny equity on a dynamic T97 board. Even though this texture connects well with Button’s flatting range (Tx/9x, suited connectors, OESDs like QJ/KJ/J8/86), my UTG range still contains many overpairs that can credibly bet here. With AhQh specifically, I have two overcards plus backdoor heart equity, so a small c-bet is a good mix of fold equity vs his whiffs (AK/KQ/AJ type hands) while keeping my risk low versus his short-stack check-jam range. If Elon raises big/jams, I’m comfortable bet-folding this combo.

Flop Tc9h7c gives me Ace-high with the nut club blocker, blocking strong club draws and top pair aces. FiveTwo's UTG raising range (strong pairs, broadways) often whiffs this uncoordinated two-club board. His small 225 bet into 650 (~33% pot) is standard c-bet but weak sizing, possibly auto or protecting medium strength. Excellent pot odds (225 to win 875, ~3.9:1) and button position justify a float/call. My hand has good playability for turn (backdoor straight/flush potential, fold equity on bluffs).
