Plan: call, evaluate turn, potentially raise as bluff if scary card or check-raise weak turns. Folding top-of-range blocker too nitty multiway potential avoided. Deep stacks (46BB) support speculative call to realize equity.

Turn pairs the 7, which is a reasonable card for my UTG range to keep applying pressure (I still credibly have overpairs/strong Tx), while Elon’s flop call contains a lot of one-pair hands and draws. With AhQh I don’t have a real flush draw anymore, but I do have two overcards plus a gutshot to the J (and some live A/Q rivers vs 9x/draws). I want to deny equity to his straight/club draws and fold out his pure floats while keeping my risk controlled versus his shorter stack (easy bet-fold vs a jam). A small turn barrel accomplishes this and keeps my range consistent with overpairs that want protection/value.

Turn 7s pairs the board's 7c, significantly improving villain's UTG raising range which includes 77 (now full house), any 7x (now trips or two pair), and strengthens medium pairs or top pair hands from flop. My Ac6s remains ace-high with only a nut club blocker and no straight/flush draw, backdoor potential minimal. Flop float was correct with position, blockers, and pot odds, but no improvement and villain's continued aggression (27% pot bet) indicates strength. Pot odds (400 into 1900 ~4.75:1 require ~17% equity, but vs polarized range my hand has <20% and poor playability to river. Deep stacks don't justify speculative call; folding preserves stack for better spots. Bluff raise risky on paired board where villain snaps with improved hands. Disciplined fold maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

I am holding Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o) in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against four other players. With 88 big blinds, I have plenty of chips and no reason to get involved with marginal holdings from early position. The standard and correct play here is to fold.

Preflop from UTG+1 (early position in 5-handed), ATo offsuit is a strong hand warranting a raise. No limpers after Pro's UTG fold. Standard open-raise to 3x BB (300) to build the pot, take initiative, and narrow the field. My stack of 4450 (~44BB) supports this without overcommitting. Opponents are deep-stacked and aggressive based on history, but ATo plays well postflop in position or as a stealing hand.
Q2o is a weak hand even on the button. Facing a raise from Elon who has a shorter stack (4150), this hand doesn't have enough equity or playability to call or 3-bet. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. Elon has opened with a 3x raise from UTG+1. While Elon's stack is relatively short (41.5 BB), T8o is a weak hand to play out of position against a standard opening range. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with poor equity and positional disadvantage, especially with FiveTwo, an active player, still to act in the Big Blind. Folding is the most profitable long-term play here.

Elon opens UTG+1 to 3bb in a 5-handed game with ~41bb effective. Tc4s offsuit is near the bottom of my range: dominated by his broadway/Tx holdings, poor connectivity, and low playability out of position. The pot odds (200 to win 450) aren’t enough to justify defending such a weak hand against a likely strong range, and a 3-bet bluff is unattractive versus a shorter stack who can shove or continue often. Preserve stack and avoid marginal OOP spots.

J5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability from UTG position in a 5-handed tournament. It has low equity against typical calling/raising ranges, gets dominated frequently by better jacks or suited connectors, and doesn't warrant limping or raising preflop. Folding preserves my 46BB stack for better spots, aligning with GTO strategy to maximize long-term stack growth over remaining hands.
Q8o from UTG+1 is a weak holding. While Queen-high has some potential, the 8 kicker is poor and this hand plays poorly out of position against multiple opponents. Standard fold from early position.

I have pocket Kings on the button, the second strongest starting hand in poker. After two folds, I am opening the action. My standard opening size in this tournament has been 300, which builds the pot while remaining consistent with my previous ranges. I want to encourage a call or a 3-bet from the blinds (FiveTwo or Pro) to maximize value with this premium hand.

We’re 5-handed and Flash is opening the Button wide. A7o plays poorly as a flat from the small blind (dominated, out of position, and we invite the BB to come along getting a great price), so I prefer taking the initiative and trying to win preflop or get heads-up with range advantage. Flash has shown willingness to fold to pressure in prior hands, making a light 3-bet attractive. I’ll use a solid OOP sizing around 4x to reduce BB’s odds and apply pressure; if we face a 4-bet we can comfortably fold.

I am holding Pocket Sixes (6s 6d) in the Big Blind. The action started with a Button raise to 300 by Flash, followed by a substantial 3-bet to 1200 by FiveTwo in the Small Blind. To call, I would need to invest 1100 chips, which represents approximately 12.6% of my remaining stack (8725). The standard guideline for set-mining suggests investing no more than 5-10% of the effective stack to ensure sufficient implied odds. Additionally, Flash is still left to act behind me and could potentially 4-bet, forcing me to fold and lose the investment. Given the large size of the 3-bet and the risk of being squeezed or dominated by higher pairs, calling is negative EV. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

I have pocket Kings, the second best starting hand in poker. FiveTwo (SB) has 3-bet my button open to 1,200. Given FiveTwo's history of high aggression and frequent 3-betting throughout the tournament, I want to 4-bet here for value. A 4-bet helps define FiveTwo's range, protects my hand against overcards, and builds a pot against hands I dominate like QQ, JJ, or AK. I will raise to 3,000, which is a standard 4-bet size that leaves plenty of room for further action.

We 3-bet A7o as a light, initiative-taking bluff, but once Flash comes back with the 4-bet to 3000 our hand quality (offsuit, easily dominated) and position (OOP) make continuing very unattractive.

Calling 1800 to win 4300 gives decent immediate pot odds, but in a 4-bet pot with a low SPR we’ll frequently be forced into stacking off on A-high boards versus better aces and strong broadways, so our equity realization is poor. Turning it into a 5-bet shove would require a lot of fold equity (we’re risking a huge amount to win a relatively small pot), and when called we’re in rough shape versus Flash’s continuing range.

Best play is to abandon the bluff and preserve stack.
3d 5s is a very weak offsuit hand in UTG position. No reason to open with this - clear fold.

I am holding Queen-Ten of hearts (QTh), which is a strong suited connector and a premium starting hand in a 5-handed game. With Claude folding in front of me, I am the first to act. Raising is the standard play here to seize the initiative and build a pot with a hand that has great post-flop playability. I will use my standard opening size of 300 chips (3x the big blind) to put pressure on the remaining three players, including the shorter stack in the Big Blind.

Flash opens to 3x from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. On the button we can defend fairly wide, but 8♣7♥ offsuit is near the bottom of a profitable continue range versus a larger sizing: it’s easily dominated, plays poorly multiway (both blinds can peel, especially the short BB), and doesn’t perform well as a 3-bet bluff given Flash’s demonstrated willingness to 4-bet. With no ante and plenty of better button continues available, this is a disciplined fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Five of Clubs, a very weak unsuited hand (T5o). The player in UTG+1 (Flash), who is the chip leader and has been aggressive, raised to 300 (3BB). Even though the price is somewhat standard, playing T5o out of position against an early position raise is a losing play in the long run. My stack is healthy (86 BB), and there is no incentive to defend with this holding. Folding is the only correct decision.

Preflop in the big blind facing an early position raise to 300 from Flash after folds from UTG, Button, and SB. My hand Qs8h is offsuit and marginal at best, with poor playability out of position. Flash has been aggressive historically, but calling OOP risks difficult postflop spots with dominated equity against a wide raising range. Pot odds are 450:200 (~2.25:1), requiring too much equity long-term given position and hand strength. Raising is not justified without a premium hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament phase (45BB effective).

I am in UTG position with Queen of Hearts and Six of Clubs. This is a very weak starting hand to play from first position, even in a 5-handed game. My current stack is the chip lead, and there is no reason to risk chips with a sub-par hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

5-handed we can open wider, but 97o from UTG+1 is still a weak, easily dominated holding that plays poorly versus 3-bets and multiway pots. With three players left to act (including an active/aggressive BB and a short-ish SB who can jam), the EV of opening or limping is low and the downside (reverse implied odds) is high. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with JTo (Ten of Diamonds, Jack of Spades) in a 5-handed game. The action has folded to me. This is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. JTo is well within the opening range for the Button in a short-handed game. My stack is healthy (86 BBs). I will raise to 2.5x (250) to put pressure on the Small Blind (44 BBs) and Big Blind (131 BBs). If 3-bet, I can easily fold, but I expect to pick up the pot pre-flop or play profitably in position post-flop often enough.

9d4c is a trash hand offsuit with no connectivity or high cards. In small blind facing a button raise to 250 (needing 200 to call), the pot odds are not sufficient for this unplayable hand. Postflop equity is minimal against a wide button range, and multiway potential with BB makes it worse. Preserve stack for premium spots. Clear fold.

Pro min-raised from button to 250. I have 57o in BB which is weak, but I'm getting good pot odds - 150 to win 400 (about 2.7:1). Against a button min-raise, I should be defending fairly wide given the price. I can play fit-or-fold post-flop.

I completely whiffed this flop with 57o on Kh8h3c. No pair, no draw. Action checked to me with 0 to call. I should just check and see what Pro does. If he bets, I'll fold - no reason to put more money in with nothing.

I raised pre-flop from the Button with TdJs and was called by the Big Blind (Claude). The flop is Kh-8h-3c. This texture hits the Big Blind's defending range reasonably well (Kx, 8x, hearts), but is also good for my range as the pre-flop raiser. However, TdJs has very little showdown value (Jack-high) and no immediate draws other than runner-runner straight potential. While a C-bet is standard on K-high boards to fold out smaller pairs or floats, my hand has decent playability on future streets if I pick up equity (a Queen or Nine gives me an open-ended straight draw, a Ten or Jack gives me a pair). Checking back controls the pot size, avoids getting check-raised by a King or a tricky line (which Claude has shown capable of), and realizes my equity for free. I intend to see the turn and re-evaluate.

Turn brought me a pair of 5s on Kh8h3c5d. Pro checked behind on the flop showing weakness. My bottom pair has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value. Checking to control pot size and potentially show down cheaply is the best line here.

On the turn, the board is Kh-8h-3c-5d. I have JTo (Jack-high) and checked back the flop. Claude (BB) checks again. The 5d is a brick that doesn't significantly change the texture. My hand has very little equity (6 outs to a pair) and no draws. Betting here after checking the flop represents a narrow range (like turned two pair or a delayed c-bet with a King), but given Claude's passive line, he might have a weak King or middle pair he's looking to check-call. It is difficult to get a fold from better hands (small pairs, A-high) reliably enough to justify a bluff, and I have showdown value against total air. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free (hitting a Jack or Ten on the river) or bluff-catch/fold on the river depending on the action. Pot control is preferred.

River paired the 8s, giving me 5s full... wait no, I just have a pair of 5s with the board showing 88. Pro checked both flop and turn showing weakness - likely has missed broadway or a small pocket pair. My pair of 5s is weak but has some showdown value. Betting for value is risky since any K, 8, or pocket pair 66+ beats me. Better to check and potentially win at showdown against his missed hands like QJ, QT, AJ, etc.

I hold TdJs on a board of Kh-8h-3c-5d-8c. The board has paired eights, meaning my best hand is a Pair of Eights with King, Jack, Ten kickers. My opponent, Claude in the Big Blind, has checked to me on all three streets (flop, turn, river). This passive line strongly suggests he has a capped range, likely consisting of Ace-high, small pocket pairs (22-77), weak board pairs (3x, 5x), or missed draws. While I lose to Ace-high, Queen-high, and any pair, I do beat his missed lower straight draws and some missed heart flushes (e.g., T9, 76). Betting here is risky because my line (check-check) does not credibly represent a strong hand like a King or Trips, making it likely Claude would look me up with Ace-high or any pair. Since I have slight showdown value against his total air and bluffing has low credibility, checking back to minimize loss and potentially win against busted draws is the best play.

We’re 5-handed but still first to act (UTG) with 9♥3♦ offsuit, which has poor playability and is heavily dominated by the ranges that will continue (especially from Pro/Elon/Flash). With deep stacks there’s no ICM pressure forcing us to gamble, and opening or limping just creates tough multiway spots with little equity realization. Best EV is to fold and preserve our stack for better opens/steals.

I am holding King-Three offsuit in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with 84 big blinds. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and reverse implied odds. Although I have position on the blinds, I still have the Button and two blinds left to act. The risk of being dominated or 3-bet is not worth the potential reward of stealing the blinds. Standard play is to fold.

Folded to me on the button with QdTc, a strong speculative hand with good position, straight potential, and queen blocker. Standard play is to raise for value/steal against the deep-stacked blinds. Raising 3x the BB (300 total) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pots OOP. My stack of 4450 (44.5BB) supports aggression to maximize tournament life and stack growth.

8c9d is a weak offsuit hand in the small blind facing a button raise. Out of position with a marginal hand that plays poorly postflop. Not worth calling 250 to defend, and not strong enough to 3-bet. Fold is the best option here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six suited. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me roughly 2.25:1 odds. King-Six suited is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard button opening range, especially given the pot odds and the suitedness of my hand which provides post-flop playability. Although I will be out of position, folding would be too tight here. Raising is an option but calling allows me to see a flop and keep the pot manageable against Elon's 41BB stack.

I am out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop raiser (Elon). The flop of 7s-5s-2d does not connect with my hand (Kh 6h), providing no pairs or draws other than a backdoor heart possibility. The standard play in this situation is to check to the aggressor and see how they respond, rather than leading into them with no equity.

Flop is 7s5s2d, a low coordinated board with a spade flush draw. My QdTc has zero equity: no pair, no draws, queen-high. However, as the pre-flop raiser with position on the button, I have range advantage. Flash's check after calling pre-flop from BB suggests weakness – likely speculative hands or floats that missed. A continuation bet exploits this, charging draws (like 64s, 43s, 86s for straight/flush potential) and taking down the pot ~60-70% of the time against a calling station/weak range. Pot is 650; betting ~half pot (325) maintains fold equity without overcommitting my 4150 stack (41BB). If called, I have outs to fold turn or turn into a bluff on backdoors/stone misses. Checking risks giving free cards to draws/improvers and forfeits dead money. Aggression key in tournament to build stack.

I am currently holding King high (Kh 6h) on a flop of 7s-5s-2d. This board does not connect with my hand in any way, and I have no significant draws (only backdoors). Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 325 into a 650 pot. Given Elon's relatively short stack and the fact that I have zero equity on this board, there is no reason to continue. Calling would be a waste of chips, and raising as a bluff into a short-stacked player who is likely to be sticky or committed is low-ev. Folding is the most profitable and disciplined play.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Three offsuit (T3o), which is a very weak hand with poor playability. Playing trash hands from early position is a losing strategy in the long run due to the positional disadvantage and low equity. With a healthy stack of over 80 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks. The clear and standard play is to fold.

UTG+1 position with 2d8s, a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity, high card value, or suited potential. Early position requires a tight range, and this hand has zero playability post-flop. Stack of 48BB is comfortable for folding trash. Pot has dead money from blinds, but calling 100 OOP with this hand is spew, and raising is nonsense. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots with 66 hands left.

As9s on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a premium stealing spot with a suited ace. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x) to apply pressure and potentially take down the blinds or play in position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 6d. Claude opened for 250 from the Button. While I have a large stack, 5-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand and FiveTwo is still to act in the Big Blind. The standard and most disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude is opening the Button to 2.5bb and we’re getting a good price in the big blind (150 to win 400). J4 suited has enough playability (suitedness, some top-pair potential, backdoor/flush possibilities) to defend versus a Button range, and it’s too weak to profitably 3-bet as a bluff in this configuration. Calling keeps the pot manageable out of position and realizes our equity at a good price.

We defended BB vs a BTN open and flop comes Q-7-6 rainbow; our J4dd has no pair, no immediate straight draw, and only weak backdoor equity (backdoor diamond flush / runner-runner straight possibilities). Out of position, the highest-EV baseline is to check to the preflop raiser, keep our range protected with stronger hands we also check (Qx, 7x, 66/77, 76), and decide based on Claude’s c-bet sizing/frequency. Leading here as a pure bluff is unnecessary and likely overbluffs this texture versus a BTN range that connects reasonably often (many Qx, 77/66, 87/98, etc.).

I raised preflop and FiveTwo defended from BB. Flop is 7s Qh 6c - relatively dry board. I have A-high with backdoor flush draw (one spade on board). Standard c-bet spot to represent the Q or overpairs. Betting ~40% pot to put pressure on FiveTwo's wide defending range.

Claude’s c-bet is very small (225 into 775 ~29%), which forces us to defend quite wide from the BB. J♦4♦ is weak (no pair/no direct draw), but the backdoor diamond flush plus some turnability (can pick up equity on diamonds, 5/8, or even a J/4 that may be good versus his high-frequency range bet) makes it a reasonable continue at this price. Check-raising here would be high-variance with poor equity when called and isn’t necessary versus this sizing; we can realize and reassess on turns.

Turn 8h doesn’t materially improve J♦4♦: we have no pair, no flush draw (diamonds didn’t come), and only a small gutshot to the 5 (4 outs). Bluff-leading into the preflop raiser on this card is high-frequency overreach because Claude’s range still contains plenty of Qx/8x/7x, plus heart draws that won’t fold. Checking keeps the pot controlled OOP, preserves our stronger hands in the same line, and lets us respond to Claude’s sizing (folding to real pressure, continuing only versus very small bets with a plan).

Turn brought 8h, giving me an open-ended straight draw (any 5 or T makes me a straight) to go with my A-high. FiveTwo called flop and checked turn. Board is getting coordinated with straight draws possible. Checking back makes sense here - I can realize my equity for free, control pot size with A-high, and potentially bet river if I improve or if checked to again.

River brings Th, which completes the most natural draw in this line (9x makes a straight) and also improves some of Claude’s turn-check range. With J♦4♦ we have essentially zero showdown value if we check (we lose to almost all of his A/K-high and any pair), so our best way to win the pot is to bluff. Our turn check keeps our range wide and can credibly contain 9x (98/97/96/A9) that would lead river for value now, plus some two-pair/sets that took a pot-control line. A medium sizing targets his air/underpairs and some weak one-pair holdings without risking an overbet that gets snapped by all Qx and straights; we also block some Jx bluff-catchers (QJ/JT/J8).

I have A9s and the river completed my straight: 6-7-8-9-T. FiveTwo bet 450 into 1000 (small ~45% pot bet). My straight is strong but the board has three hearts (Qh, 8h, Th) so flushes are possible, and J9 makes a higher straight. Given the small sizing, this could be thin value or a bluff. With a made straight, calling is mandatory. Raising only gets called by better hands (flushes, J9). Easy call.

A6o from UTG in a 5-handed game is a weak hand for an early position open. It has poor post-flop playability with a weak kicker, dominated by better aces, and unlikely to make strong hands. With 48BB stack and other players deeper, better to fold and wait for stronger holdings to maximize EV over remaining hands. No reads from history suggest loosening up here.

7s9s is a suited connector with some playability, but UTG+1 is too early a position to open with this hand. With 4 players behind me including the blinds, I'd be playing out of position if called. Standard fold here - save chips for better spots.

I am currently the chip leader on the button with T8o. While this is a potential stealing hand in a 5-handed game, FiveTwo in the Small Blind has a very healthy stack (second in chips) and has shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands. Engaging in a pot with the second-largest stack while holding a marginal hand out of position post-flop (if he calls or 3-bets) is unnecessary risk at this stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

Folds to me in the small blind, so this is a classic steal spot. Even though Ts7h is not a premium hand, heads-up it has decent playability and benefits a lot from taking initiative and generating fold equity versus Pro’s big blind range. I’ll use a small-but-standard SB open size to pressure their wide defend range without risking too much if they 3-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 2s, facing a raise to 250 from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). The pot is 350, and I need to call 150, offering me pot odds of roughly 30%. Although I would have position on the Small Blind post-flop, 92o is one of the worst hands in poker with very poor playability and equity realization. Even against a wide blind-vs-blind steal range, this hand does not justify a defense for 2.5bb. Preserving chips is better than calling with trash.
5d 6s is a weak offsuit connector in UTG position. This hand has very poor equity and playability from early position. Easy fold.

Ten-Six suited is a marginal hand that doesn't play well from UTG+1. With several players behind, including an aggressive FiveTwo on the button and a short-stacked Elon in the big blind who might look to shove, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

On the button 5-handed we can and should steal fairly wide, but 9♢2♣ offsuit is at the very bottom of any reasonable BTN opening range. When either blind defends, this hand realizes equity extremely poorly and creates too many tough postflop spots versus check-raises and floats. With no strong read that SB/BB are massively over-folding, the clean highest-EV line is to pass and preserve stack for better steal candidates.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 2d, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em (32 offsuit). The action has folded to me. The Big Blind (Elon) is still to act. Even though it only costs 50 chips to complete the blind, playing 32o out of position is a losing proposition due to poor equity and playability. Attempting to steal with a raise is risky against an active opponent like Elon with this specific holding. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and preserve chips.

I am UTG in a 5-handed game with King-Five offsuit. This hand is far too weak to open from early position, especially with several competent players still to act. Standard professional play dictates a fold here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

5-handed we can open wider, but 3c4s offsuit is still too far down the range from UTG+1: it has poor high-card value, weak playability, and realizes equity badly versus the blinds/BTN calling ranges. With no compelling ICM pressure and plenty of playable stacks behind, there’s no need to burn 100 chips or create bigger pots with this hand.

I am on the Button with 9c 6s (96o), a very weak hand. Action has folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, 96o falls well outside a profitable opening range, even for a wide steal attempt. The Small Blind has ~48BB and the Big Blind has ~143BB, so they have plenty of chips to defend or 3-bet. Playing this hand post-flop would be difficult as it is often dominated and has poor connectivity. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

Preflop in the small blind with folds all around, this is an excellent stealing opportunity against the big blind. 8c7d is offsuit but has decent straight potential and live cards. My stack is 48BB, which is playable, and raising here applies fold equity to pick up the 150 pot dead money frequently. Claude in the BB has been aggressive but will fold weak hands to a raise OOP. A standard steal size of 3x the BB (300 total) balances protection against floats and fold equity. Calling OOP with this hand is poor due to positional disadvantage postflop, and folding wastes the steal spot.

J5o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from Elon in the SB, I'm getting about 2:1 odds but J5o plays poorly postflop. If I hit a jack I could be dominated, and 5 is a weak kicker. Folding is the disciplined play here.

5-handed from UTG this plays like a fairly late position, and AcTs is well within a profitable opening range: it has strong top-pair potential, nut-flush potential, and blocks strong Ax continues. With a short-ish stack on the Button and blinds that can defend wide, I want to take initiative and apply pressure while keeping the sizing efficient.

I’ll open to 250 (2.5x): standard steal size that builds a pot when we’re ahead of calling ranges, discourages overly wide blind calls, and keeps our risk controlled if we face a 3-bet or shove.

I hold 4s 9c (Nine-Four offsuit), which is a statistically very weak hand. I am facing a raise to 250 from the Under-the-Gun (UTG) player, FiveTwo. Given that UTG raising ranges are typically strong and I am in early position (UTG+1) with players left to act behind me, calling is not profitable. My hand has poor playability and low equity. The correct play is to fold immediately to preserve my stack.

Pre-flop on the button with J9o against an UTG raise to 250 (2.5x) from FiveTwo, who has a strong early-position range. My stack is 49.5BB, and while position is good, J9o is offsuit, not suited or connected strongly enough to speculate profitably against a tight UTG range. Pot odds are mediocre (400:250), and calling risks multiway pots or reverse domination post-flop. Folding preserves stack for better spots, especially with deep-stacked opponents behind who could 3-bet. Consistent with GTO strategy in tournaments.

Q4 suited is a marginal hand facing an UTG raise. While suited, Q4s doesn't play well out of position against an early position opener. The pot odds of calling 200 to win 400 aren't compelling enough given I'll be OOP postflop with a weak holding. Folding is the disciplined play here.

KQs is a premium hand in a 5-handed game. FiveTwo is opening from UTG, which effectively acts as the Cutoff in this seat configuration. Given FiveTwo's high frequency of opening raises and the fact that I am in the Big Blind, I want to take the initiative and exert pressure rather than just defending and playing out of position. A 3-bet here can either take down the pot immediately or allow me to lead the action on the flop. I will raise to 850 chips.

Flash’s BB 3-bet to 850 versus my 2.5x open is a common spot for him to apply pressure, and given the 5-handed dynamic his 3-bet range should be wider than a full-ring UTG vs BB range. With AcTs (offsuit) I’m not strong enough to 4-bet for value, and turning it into a 4-bet bluff is a bit spewy without better blockers/suited playability and with stacks still ~150bb.

Calling is workable because I’ll have position postflop (BB acts first), and the price is reasonable: 600 to win 1750 total gives me enough immediate equity plus positional realization to defend. Postflop plan is to proceed cautiously on A-high textures (domination risk) and lean on position to pot-control, while being willing to continue versus smaller c-bets on favorable boards and take stabs when he checks.

I have flopped top pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a King-high board. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, this board texture is very favorable for my perceived range. I want to bet for value to get called by weaker Kings, middle pairs, or spade draws. A bet of around 1/3 pot is effective on this dry board to maintain balance and extract value while keeping the pot manageable.

This K-high flop in a 3-bet pot heavily favors Flash’s range (AK/KQ/KK and plenty of Kx), and with AcTs (no flush draw, no meaningful straight/backdoor equity) I’m basically hoping to spike an Ace. The c-bet sizing is small and likely range-based, but my hand is too low in my continuation range and will struggle versus turn barrels. Raising as a bluff is also poor because he continues with a large portion of his range (all Kx, big pairs). Best EV is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with Pocket Eights (8c 8s). This is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to thin the field. In a 5-handed game, 88 is well ahead of a random hand and plays reasonably well post-flop, though I prefer to be heads-up. A standard raise size of 2.5x BB (250 chips) is appropriate here to balance my opening range and offer a reasonable price without committing too many chips out of position. I intend to fold to significant re-aggression from the deep stacks unless the price to set-mine is very favorable.

K6o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by typical UTG raising ranges (strong broadways, pairs, suited aces, etc.). I'm in early position (UTG+1), OOP post-flop against an UTG raiser with a solid 80BB stack. My 50BB stack is the shortest, so preserving chips for better spots is key in a tournament setting aiming to maximize stack over many hands. No history suggests Pro is overly loose from UTG. Folding is straightforward GTO and preserves fold equity/range advantage.

I have 2d3s on the button - one of the worst starting hands in poker. Pro raised from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. 23 offsuit has almost no playability: no high card value, not suited, and very limited straight potential. Easy fold despite having position.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce of clubs. Pro has raised to 250 from UTG. Q2s is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against a UTG opening range. Additionally, FiveTwo in the Big Blind has been active and could easily squeeze or put me in a difficult spot post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the strength of a UTG open, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing a 2.5bb UTG open with T3o from the big blind, this hand is too weak and dominated to defend profitably. Even with decent immediate pot odds, T3o realizes equity very poorly out of position versus a range that contains many better tens and broadways, creating reverse implied odds. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive against Pro’s shorter stack, since we risk getting jammed on and burning chips. Best play is to conserve stack and wait for a better spot.

UTG position in a 5-handed game with 3c7d offsuit, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Standard play is to fold weak hands early position to avoid tough spots post-flop. My stack is ~50BB, sufficient to wait for premium hands. No reads suggest otherwise; opponents have been aggressive, but no value or bluff justification here. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.
4d7s is a weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high cards. From UTG+1 (early position), this is a clear fold. Not worth opening or defending with this holding.

I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand. Since the action folded to me, I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially thin the field or win the blinds immediately.

Qc6s is a weak offsuit queen that’s frequently dominated by Flash’s wide button opening range, and we’ll be out of position the whole hand with the BB still to act (increasing the chance of going multiway). Calling 250 to chase marginal top pairs is a low-EV spot, and turning it into a 3-bet bluff from the SB at 150bb effective is unnecessary variance with poor playability when called. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Deuces (2c, 2h) facing a standard 3x raise (to 300) from the Button (Flash), who is the aggressive chip leader. I have a stack of 8,325 chips (83 BBs), and the cost to call is 200 chips. This gives me tremendous implied odds (over 40:1 ratio of effective stack to call amount) to set mine. My plan is to call to see a flop. If I hit a set of twos, I will look to get chips in against Flash's wide range. If I miss the set, I will fold to any significant aggression, as playing bottom pair out of position is not profitable. Folding is too tight given the stack depth and price.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket Deuces (2c 2h) on a flop of Ah Th 7c. I called pre-flop primarily to set mine, but I missed the set. The board texture is very unfavorable for my hand, heavily favoring the pre-flop raiser's range (containing many Aces, Tens, and broadway cards). I have 4th pair (underpair to the board) with no significant draw. My plan is to check and fold to any bet, as I have virtually no equity against a continuing range.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (AcKh) on a board of Ah-Th-7c. My opponent in the Big Blind checked to me, which is expected. I should now place a continuation bet for value, targeting weaker Aces, Tens, and various draws (hearts or gutshots). A bet of 350 into a 650 pot is a standard size that provides value while not being so large that it forces out all of my opponent's marginal calling range.

I hold pocket Deuces (2c 2h) on a flop of Ah Th 7c. I missed my set, which was the primary reason for calling pre-flop. The board texture heavily favors the opponent's range (Aces, Tens, broadway draws), and my underpair has negligible equity against any value hand and struggles even against bluffs. Facing a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650, I am getting roughly 2.8:1 odds, but my hand is essentially dead. Sticking to the plan established pre-flop and on the flop check, I will fold immediately to preserve my 81 BB stack.
JTs offsuit in UTG. Decent connected broadway hand with straight potential. Standard open raise to 2.5x from early position.

Claude opened from UTG with a standard 2.5x raise, which typically represents a solid range. My hand, Ace-Deuce offsuit, is at the bottom of the Ace hierarchy and is likely dominated by Claude's opening range. Additionally, I have three players acting after me who could wake up with a hand or squeeze. Ace-Deuce offsuit lacks post-flop playability and equity against a UTG open, so folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my chip lead.

We’re on the Button facing an UTG open to 2.5x from Claude. 9-3 offsuit has very poor playability, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well even with position. With shorter stacks in the blinds (especially Elon ~49bb), there’s also a non-trivial risk of a squeeze/jam that would force us to forfeit any raise or call investment. No need to defend this far down versus an UTG range—just conserve chips for better spots.

I hold Q8o in the Small Blind facing a raise from Claude in UTG. An UTG opening range is typically strong (top 15% of hands), dominating my Q8o. Being out of position post-flop with a weak off-suit hand against a strong range makes realizing equity difficult. Although the pot odds are okay, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a clear fold.

74o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has shown aggression but likely a strong range here (top 15-20% of hands). Pot odds require calling 150 into 400 (effective pot after call 550, ~27% equity needed), but 74o has at most ~25% equity against a typical UTG range and horrible post-flop playability in position (Claude acts first post-flop). My 48.5BB stack is playable but no need to defend this wide from BB against early position strength. Folding preserves stack for better spots; there are 59 hands left to build.

I am dealt 9-3 offsuit in the UTG position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in the game and is almost always a fold, especially from early position with four players left to act behind me. Given I am the current chip leader with over 18,000 chips, there is no reason to risk any part of my stack on a hand that has very little post-flop potential and is easily dominated.

Qs3s is too weak to open from UTG+1 even 5-handed: it’s heavily dominated by the ranges that will defend (Claude in the BB, Pro on the Button) and Elon’s short-ish SB stack increases the chance of a 3-bet/shove. Limping is also unattractive since it invites isolation and plays poorly multiway. Preserving stack and waiting for better steal spots from later position is higher EV.

I am on the Button with 8c 9s (89o). The action has folded to me. While the Button allows for a wide opening range to steal the blinds, 89o is at the very bottom of that range and is generally considered a fold, especially with effective stack sizes of 48BB (SB) and 143BB (BB). The Big Blind (Claude) has shown a tendency to defend, and playing a weak offsuit connector post-flop against a large stack is not ideal. I will fold to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Pocket tens is a premium hand, strong enough to raise for value and to steal the dead money from the blinds. With three folds already, we're likely heads-up against Claude in the BB, who defends reasonably wide based on history. Raising from the SB takes initiative, builds the pot, and puts pressure on the BB. Standard sizing of 3x the BB (300 total) is appropriate given my 48BB stack and the pot dynamics; it's not too large to get shoved on lightly but charges draws and weak hands to continue.

I have AJo in the BB facing a min-raise from Elon in the SB. AJ is a strong hand heads up, and Elon has only 4550 chips. I should 3-bet for value here - AJ plays well against his likely raising range from the SB. A 3-bet to 900 puts pressure on him while keeping the pot manageable.

Pocket Tens remains very strong heads-up preflop against Claude's BB 3-bet range after my SB raise. His range includes bluffs (suited broadways, Ax suited, weaker pairs), AQ+, KQ, TT+ likely, but we have blockers and top of our perceived range. Calling OOP would be suboptimal as we'd play postflop out of position with a vulnerable pair facing further aggression. A 4-bet shove leverages fold equity (Claude folds lighter 3-bets often per history), gets value from his premium calling hands (JJ+, AK) where we have ~40-45% equity, and denies equity to his floats/draws. With 45BB effective stack, pot-committing is standard and maximizes EV in this tournament spot late (hand 92/150). No slower play needed.

Elon 4-bet shoved to 4550 (essentially all-in with only 300 behind). Pot is 5450, I need to call 3650. Getting pot odds of 3650 to win 9100 (~40% equity needed). AJo has blockers to AA/AK and plays decently against typical 4-bet ranges. Even against a tight range, I'm close to the needed equity, and Elon could be squeezing light from SB. With these pot odds and a solid hand, I should call.

With only 300 chips remaining (~3% of the 9100 pot), post-flop first to act OOP holding TT on Jd5d2h. TT is a strong overpair to the 52 low cards (second pair overall but blocks Claude's TT/JT/AT), with decent equity vs his tight flatting range after calling my large 4-bet pre (QQ+, AK/AQ, JJ/AJs likely: ~25-35% equity, e.g., dominates AK/AQ ~70%, live vs QQ+/JJ). Board dry-ish (fd possible but missed many overcards for AK). Shoving extracts thin value, realizes equity, leverages initiative from preflop aggression. Checking invites check-raise (Jx/sets) or check-back (weak), folding equity minimal but pot odds insane (need <4% equity to break even if called). Maximizes EV in tournament spot (hand 92/150), short stack play dictates commitment.

I flopped top pair top kicker (Jacks with Ace kicker) on Jd5d2h. Elon has only 300 chips left and bet them. Pot is 9400, I need to call 300 - that's 32:1 pot odds. This is an automatic call with TPTK regardless of what he has.

I am in the UTG position (effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with T9o. While my stack of roughly 80 big blinds allows for some play, T9 offsuit is generally considered too weak to open from the first position, even in a short-handed game. It is easily dominated by hands like AT, KT, QT, JT and pairs. Given that I am the shortest stack at the table, preserving chips and waiting for stronger holdings or better positional spots is the optimal strategy. I will fold.
JTo on the button is a standard open. Good position with a connected broadway hand. I'll raise to 250 to pick up the blinds or play in position against callers.

Claude is opening from the button, which typically indicates a wide range of hands. While Ace-Six offsuit is a mediocre hand, it has significant blocker value with the Ace. Calling from the small blind would put me in a difficult position post-flop, playing out of position against an aggressive opponent. I will put in a 3-bet to 850 to assert dominance and attempt to take the pot down immediately. If Claude calls, I will have the betting lead; if he 4-bets, I can easily fold without sacrificing too much of my stack.

K2o is too weak versus a button open and a small blind 3-bet. Even with a decent immediate price, our equity is typically below what we need and we’ll be dominated often (Kx, Ax), playing out of position in a bloated pot with very poor playability. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here with only a single K blocker and no postflop potential. Best is to preserve stack and wait for a better defend/squeeze candidate.

JTo offsuit facing a 3-bet from Flash. While I have position, JTo is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against typical 3-bet ranges - dominated by AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, and behind all pairs. Getting 2:1 isn't enough to overcome the reverse implied odds and tough post-flop decisions. Fold is the disciplined play here.
A8o from UTG at a 4-handed table is a solid opening hand. With only 4 players, this position plays like cutoff in a full ring. I'll open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) as standard.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight offsuit. Claude has opened for 250 from UTG in a 4-handed game. While J8o is a marginal hand that can occasionally be played from the button, calling a raise against a reasonably active UTG opener is generally a losing play in the long run. The hand doesn't flop well and is frequently dominated by Claude's opening range (which likely includes AJ, KJ, QJ, and higher pairs). Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I have either better cards or a more favorable situation to exploit my position.

We’re 4-handed and Claude’s UTG open is likely fairly wide. Getting 200 to win 400 (good immediate price) with a connected hand that can make strong straights/two pair, I prefer defending rather than overfolding the SB. I don’t like a 3-bet with T9o here because it creates a bloated pot OOP with a hand that’s often dominated when called/4-bet, and a small “min” 3-bet would invite flats from both Claude and the BB. Flatting keeps the pot manageable and lets us proceed cautiously postflop (and we can still fold if the BB squeezes big).

I am in the Big Blind with [Qd, Kc]. Claude opened from UTG for 2.5bb (250) and FiveTwo called in the Small Blind. I am getting excellent pot odds, needing to call 150 chips into a pot that will be 750 total (including my call), which is 5:1 odds (approx 16-17% equity required). KQo is well ahead of this requirement. While a squeeze (3-bet) is an option, doing so against an UTG open is risky as it often isolates me against a stronger range or faces a 4-bet. Calling allows me to realize my equity and play a pot with a strong hand relative to the price.

Multiway (SB vs UTG raiser + BB) on Jc-7h-3h, our T9o has only a gutshot to the 8 plus a backdoor heart flush draw with Th. We don’t have enough equity/board coverage to profitably lead into two players, and checking keeps the pot controlled OOP while allowing us to realize equity when bets are small and fold when pressure is heavy.

On a flop of 3h-Jc-7h, I have missed with King-Queen high. I have two overcards but no immediate draws other than backdoor possibilities. In a multi-way pot against an UTG opener and an SB caller, my range disadvantage is significant. Leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) and evaluate based on their action.

I was the preflop aggressor with Ah8c. The flop is 3h Jc 7h - both opponents checked to me. I have ace high, backdoor nut flush draw in hearts, and two overcards. This is a standard c-bet spot to represent the Jack and apply pressure. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should be effective here.

Claude’s c-bet is very small into a multiway pot (275 into 1025 ~27%), giving us attractive immediate odds. With T9 (Th) on Jc-7h-3h we have a gutshot to the 8 plus some backdoor potential (runner-runner hearts) and occasional thin showdown value if we pair T/9 versus a wide UTG range. Raising as a bluff here is poor multiway and OOP, but folding would be too tight given the price and implied odds when we hit an 8. We call and re-evaluate turns: continue aggressively on 8/heart turns, otherwise mostly check/fold versus bigger pressure (and we can fold if Pro springs a check-raise).

I am in the Big Blind with [Qd, Kc]. The flop is [3h, Jc, 7h]. Claude (UTG) bet 275 and FiveTwo (SB) called. I am getting good pot odds (approx 17.5%), but I am in a multi-way pot out of position with only overcards. FiveTwo's call indicates he likely has a piece of the board or a draw, and Claude's c-bet into two players represents strength. My overcards (K, Q) may not be clean outs due to potential domination (AK, AQ) or the risk of drawing dead against sets or two pair. Without a flush draw or significant straight potential, continuing is negative EV.

Turn Qc is a great card for us: we pick up an open-ended straight draw (8 or K), but we still don’t have showdown value and we’re OOP. Claude, as the preflop raiser, has the range advantage on this Q-high board (many Qx/AQ/KQ), so leading risks getting raised off our equity and bloating the pot without a made hand. Checking keeps his bluffs/value bets in, gives us a chance to realize our equity for free if he checks back, and preserves the option to respond based on sizing (call small, potentially check-raise some sizes as a semi-bluff, or fold vs heavy pressure).

Turn brought Qc. I have just ace-high with no real draws anymore (heart flush needs river heart only). FiveTwo called my flop c-bet and checked to me. Continuing to barrel with A-high against someone showing interest is burning chips. I should check back to control pot size and potentially see a free showdown or evaluate river action.

We river the nut straight (8-9-T-J-Q). After Claude’s small flop c-bet and turn check-back, his range contains a lot of one-pair/two-pair hands (Jx/Qx, QJ) that will often check behind if we check, so we should value-bet ourselves. The 3-heart board means flushes exist, so I prefer a medium “block/value” sizing that gets crying calls from worse while keeping us from getting stacked when he shows up with a heart flush (and we can comfortably fold to a big raise).

The river 8h completed the heart flush draw. I have Ah8c, which means I have the nut flush with the Ah and three hearts on board (3h, 7h, 8h). FiveTwo is betting 550 into 1300 - a small bet that could be a blocker bet with a weak flush, value from two pair/straight, or trying to get me to fold. With the absolute nuts, I need to raise for value. A raise to around 1400-1500 should get called by worse flushes, straights (T9), or strong two pairs.

We have the Q-high straight (best possible straight on this runout); only a rivered heart flush beats us, and a lot of Claude’s range after small flop c-bet + turn check can include heart-heart draws that now raise for value. His raise is also small enough that he can be doing it with some worse value (two pair like QJ/J8) or occasional bluff vs our blocker-ish lead. Raising back mostly gets action from flushes (bad) and maybe chops vs T9, while folding is far too tight given pot odds. Best line is to just call and take the very favorable price, keeping the pot controlled against the portion of his range that has us beat.

I am holding Seven of Spades and Eight of Hearts (78o) in the first position (UTG/Cutoff) in a 4-handed game. While ranges widen significantly in short-handed play, 78o remains a weak hand to open with three opponents still to act, especially since they have shown a willingness to be active and 3-bet. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

4-handed on the button after UTG folds, we should open very wide to pick up the blinds. Q3 suited (Qc3c) is weak but has decent playability (can make top pair + backdoor flush/straight possibilities) and benefits a lot from position. Both blinds have shown plenty of folding to pressure in prior hands, and Pro in the SB is shorter and generally more passive, making this a good steal spot. I’ll use a small, risk-controlled open size to keep our risk down versus a 3-bet while still applying pressure to the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 3s, which is a very weak off-suit hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Given the poor playability of my hand and being out of position, defending by calling or 3-betting is not profitable. The standard play is to fold.

74o is a weak hand with poor connectivity and no high card value. Against a button raise, even with decent pot odds (150 to win 400), this hand doesn't play well postflop. Folding is the correct play.

4-handed UTG we can open fairly wide, but Q2o is still a low-quality offsuit queen that’s dominated by the continuing ranges (Qx better, Ax, broadways) and plays poorly OOP in multiway pots. Given the table’s demonstrated aggression (frequent raises/3-bets), opening risks getting 3-bet and bleeding chips, while calling is too weak. Best is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Action is folded to me on the Button. I hold King-Seven suited (K7s), which is a legitimate hand to open-raise from this position to steal the blinds or play in position. My stack is healthy enough (77 BB) to play standard poker. I will raise to 2.5bb (250) to put pressure on the blinds.

2c 6s is a very weak offsuit hand with no real playability. Against a button raise, even with the small blind discount, this hand should be folded. No straight potential, no flush potential, and any pair I make will likely be dominated.

With 9h 7c in the Big Blind, I am facing a button raise from Pro. While the pot odds are tempting (calling 150 to win a pot of 400), this hand is quite weak and performs poorly out of position. Given that I am currently the chip leader, I prefer to avoid marginal situations with weak starting hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Three offsuit (Qs 3d) in a 4-handed game. My stack is roughly 79 BBs. Despite the short-handed table, Q3o is a very weak hand with poor playability, lack of connectivity, and no flush potential. It falls well outside a reasonable opening range from the earliest position. Opening here would be too loose and expose me to domination or difficult post-flop situations out of position against 3 opponents. The correct play is to fold.

T2o is a weak hand even on the button. While I have position, this hand has very poor playability and limited equity. With a healthy stack at 169bb, I don't need to force marginal steals with trash hands. Folding and waiting for better spots.

Folded to me in the small blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. This is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. While a raise could potentially steal the big blind, FiveTwo has shown enough aggression and willingness to defend in previous hands that I prefer a disciplined fold here to maintain my stack and wait for better opportunities.
4s9d is a weak offsuit hand with no connectivity. UTG is the worst position to open from, requiring a tighter range. This hand doesn't meet the threshold for an open raise. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit. While I am in a good position to steal the blinds, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range even for a 4-handed game. With both FiveTwo and Pro still in the blinds and having shown a willingness to defend or 3-bet in previous hands, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

4-handed, folded to us in the SB. K9o is comfortably ahead of BB’s random range and plays well heads-up. Completing gives up initiative and lets BB realize equity too cheaply; raising pressures a wide portion of BB’s range and wins the dead money often. I’ll use a standard small blind open size that keeps my range wide while still generating fold equity and building a pot when called. Plan vs resistance: continue versus calls; versus a larger 3-bet, mostly fold this offsuit K9.

I hold a premium hand, Ace-King suited, in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Small Blind. In a blind-versus-blind scenario, the opener's range is typically wide. 3-betting here is the most profitable play to extract value from dominated hands (like weaker Aces, Kings, and pairs) and to build a pot in position. I will raise to 850, which is approximately 3.4x the opening raise, a standard size consistent with previous table action.

We opened SB wide, but BB’s 3-bet to 850 over a 250 open is a very large sizing (especially 4-handed), which typically skews toward a tighter/value-heavy range. K9 offsuit is dominated by the Kx/9x that continue, plays poorly out of position, and doesn’t realize its raw equity well enough even with ~35% pot odds. 4-bet bluffing this hand is also unattractive given the short effective stack (7075) and the likelihood BB continues aggressively versus a 4-bet. Best play is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

I am holding Ace-Queen of Diamonds in a 4-handed game, which is a very strong starting hand. Being UTG (Under the Gun), I want to open for a standard raise to build the pot and take the initiative. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is consistent with my previous play and allows me to see how the other three players react. This hand has high equity and plays well post-flop, especially since it is suited.

Flash opens to 3bb from UTG 4-handed (still a relatively strong range). T4o has poor playability, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and doesn’t have the blockers/playability to justify a 3-bet bluff. Calling invites tough postflop spots even in position. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better steal/3-bet candidates.

I hold 7s 4h in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand (74 offsuit). The player in UTG (Flash) has raised to 300. Given the strength of a typical UTG opening range and my positional disadvantage, playing this hand is not profitable. Even with pot odds, the reverse implied odds and poor equity realization make this a clear fold.
6h8c is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG raise. Even though I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds, 68o plays poorly against UTG's typically strong range. Easy fold.

4-handed, opening ranges are much wider than full-ring and QTo is comfortably within an UTG open. Raising is better than limping because it leverages position disadvantage by taking initiative, can win the blinds immediately, and defines ranges vs the button/blinds. I’ll use a standard small open to keep the pot manageable OOP and maintain fold equity versus the blinds. Plan: proceed vs flats; typically fold vs a sizable 3-bet from the button/blinds given poor playability OOP and domination risk.

The current game is 4-handed. FiveTwo opens from UTG (effectively Cutoff) to 250. I am on the Button with QTo (Queen-Ten offsuit). While having position is advantageous, QTo is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by an UTG opening range (which often includes AQ, KQ, AJ, AT, and pairs). Calling invites the blinds to enter or squeeze, and playing a dominated hand for a significant portion of my stack (even though deep) can lead to difficult post-flop situations where I lose a large pot with top pair weak kicker. Folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve chips for better opportunities.
K4o in the SB facing UTG min-raise. This is a weak hand that plays poorly OOP. Even with decent pot odds (200 to win 400), K4o doesn't have the playability to profitably defend here. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (93o). The UTG player (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Although the pot odds are somewhat attractive (150 to call into a 400 pot), 93o has very poor playability post-flop, especially out of position against a solid stack. As the chip leader, there is no reason to defend with such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with Qc 2d (Q2 offsuit). This is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity. With 4 players remaining and being the shortest stack (though still deep with ~81 BBs), there is no strategic justification for opening the pot with this holding from early position. The standard and correct play is to fold and wait for a better hand.

Ad Td suited on the button is a strong hand. Pro folded, and I only have the blinds behind me. This is a clear raise spot - good hand, good position. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or play in position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand, 2-6 offsuit. Claude has raised from the Button, which is a common steal position, but my hand is too poor to defend, especially out of position. Folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack.

Claude is opening the button for 2.5bb, but 8♣3♠ is near the bottom of any big-blind defense range. Even getting decent pot odds, this hand realizes equity very poorly out of position (dominated by all the 8x/3x he opens, no strong straight potential), and calling just invites tough postflop spots where we bleed chips. A bluff 3-bet is also too low-quality since we’ll often get called and be forced to play a bigger pot with terrible playability. Best EV is to conserve chips and fold.
8s7s is a suited connector but from UTG position this is too weak to open. Standard fold from early position - suited connectors play better in position and multiway.

I am on the button with King-Eight suited, which is a strong opening hand in a four-handed game. Since the UTG player (Claude) has folded, I have an opportunity to attack the blinds and take control of the pot. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to apply pressure and utilize my positional advantage.

We’re facing a 3x button open and hold 72o in the small blind. This hand is far below a reasonable defend range (dominated, poor playability, and realizes equity terribly out of position). Squeezing is also unattractive because our blockers are nonexistent and we’d be turning total trash into a bluff against a button range that can continue often, while also risking playing a bloated pot OOP. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from Flash on the Button. My hand is 7c 3c. The pot odds are calling 200 to win 450 (2.25:1), requiring ~30.7% equity. While 73s technically has around 32-33% raw equity against a wide button range, realizing this equity out of position is extremely difficult due to the hand's poor playability and lack of high card strength (reverse implied odds). Facing a 3BB open rather than a min-raise makes this a straightforward fold.

I am UTG with 7s 5c, which is a very weak hand. In a 4-handed game, opening ranges should be wider, but 75 offsuit is still bottom-tier and not profitable to open from any position. Folding is the clear best play to preserve my stack.

Three-handed with position on the button and no opener, we should be attacking the blinds very wide. K3o is weak but has reasonable high-card value and plays fine in position, and both blinds will have to defend fairly tight given stack/pot dynamics. I’ll use a small open to keep risk down with a marginal holding and fold comfortably to any substantial 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 4s, a very weak off-suit hand (junk). The player on the Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Although Button opening ranges are typically wide, J4o performs very poorly against any reasonable range and will be difficult to play out of position post-flop. The pot odds do not justify a call with this specific hand, and a bluff raise is unnecessary and risky at this stack depth. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Pocket 7s in the BB facing a button open to 250. This is a solid hand to defend with - I'm getting good odds (150 to win 400) and can set mine effectively. While 3-betting is an option, flatting keeps the pot manageable and lets me play for set value.

I have pocket 7s on a 4c-5h-9d board - an overpair to two cards. Board is dry with no flush draws. Checking makes sense here to let villain bet their range and I can check-call with my medium-strength hand. No need to bloat the pot when I'm only beating bluffs and small pairs if raised.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on a 9-5-4 rainbow board, I have a range advantage (overpairs, strong 9x, some big cards) even though BB can have plenty of 9x/5x/4x. With K3o I have little showdown value but some backdoor equity (backdoor clubs via Kc, and a few backdoor straight runouts). I want to use a small c-bet to fold out his pure air and deny equity to hands like two overs, while keeping my risk low versus check-raises on a board that connects with the BB. If raised, I’ll just fold this hand.

I have pocket 7s on a 4-5-9 rainbow board. My overpair beats small pairs and bluffs, but loses to 88, 99, and overpairs TT+. FiveTwo's small c-bet (200 into 500) is typical of a range bet from button position. With a medium-strength hand on this dry board, calling is the best play - I have showdown value but don't want to raise and face a 3-bet from stronger hands.

Turn brought a deuce, which is a blank. I have pocket 7s - an overpair to 4, 5, and 2 but the 9 beats me. The pot is 950 and action is on me first. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value - I'd only get called by better hands and fold out worse. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets me see a river. If FiveTwo bets, I can call with my decent showdown value.

Turn comes 2s after my small flop c-bet gets called. With Kc3d I still have essentially no showdown value, but I picked up good equity: a double-gutshot (A or 6) to a straight. Claude’s flop-calling range contains a lot of marginal one-pair hands (some 4x/5x/weak 9x) and floats/draws that don’t love facing a second barrel, while my range can credibly contain overpairs/sets/strong 9x that want to keep betting for protection on this connected board. I’ll apply pressure with a medium turn barrel that leverages fold equity while keeping risk controlled; if I face a check-raise, I’m done with the hand.

I have pocket 7s - an overpair to 4, 5, 2 but under the 9. FiveTwo's sizing increased from 200 to 600, which is concerning but not necessarily indicative of a monster. The 2s is a brick. Pot is 1550, need to call 600 - getting about 2.6:1 odds. My hand has showdown value against bluffs, draws (67, 36), and smaller pairs. Folding seems too tight with these odds. Raising only gets called by better. Calling to see the river is the play - I can reevaluate on the river.

River brought the 8d. My pocket 7s are now an underpair to both the 9 and 8 on board (4-5-9-2-8). I beat bluffs, busted draws (like 36, A6), and smaller pairs (66 or less), but lose to any 8, 9, or overpair. With a pot of 2150 and no bet to call, I should check. Betting doesn't make sense - I'd only get called by better hands. Checking lets me showdown cheaply if FiveTwo checks back, or I can make a decision if they bet.

River bricks my double-gutshot and I have essentially zero showdown value with K-high after firing flop and turn and getting called twice. Claude’s line (check/call, check/call, check/call, check) is very consistent with one-pair holdings (4x/5x/weak 8x/9x) that are now under pressure because the 8 completes 76 and improves a lot of two-pair/value for my perceived barreling range. This is a good card to apply a polarized final barrel and try to fold out his marginal pairs (especially 4x/5x and some weak 8x/9x). I’ll bet a strong, but not all-in, size and fold if he check-raises.

River brought 8d. My 77 is now an underpair to both the 9 and 8 on board (4-5-9-2-8). FiveTwo's sizing escalated significantly: 200 flop → 600 turn → 1600 river. This is a large bet (1600 into ~2150) suggesting strength. I need ~30% equity to call but my hand loses to any 9x, 8x, overpair, or 67 straight. The board is very dry with few missed draws - the 8 improved their range significantly. Against this sizing, FiveTwo's range is weighted toward value. Folding my underpair is the disciplined play here.

4-handed UTG plays much wider than full ring, and 5h4h is a reasonable open as a suited connector/wheel hand with good playability and some nut potential. Limping gives up initiative and invites multiway pots out of position; raising lets us steal the blinds often and take the lead when we do get action. Use a standard small open size to risk little while pressuring blinds and the button.

I am on the Button with A4o (Ace-Four offsuit). The player in UTG (FiveTwo) has opened to 250. In a 4-handed game, UTG ranges are wider, but A4o is still a marginal hand that is often dominated by better Aces or is flipping against pairs. While FiveTwo has been active and capable of folding to 3-bets, calling with A4o invites domination issues post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff is suboptimal compared to suited variants. With a comfortable stack of ~80BB, I can wait for better spots to enter the pot. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.
4s 2d is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Even getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400), I'm out of position against a raise with a hand that will rarely make anything strong. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding K8o. FiveTwo, who has been active and aggressive, raised to 250 from UTG in this 4-handed game. With the Small Blind folding, I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1. In a 4-max game, K8o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind with against a standard opening range, especially given the attractive price. While I will be out of position, the strength of a King-high hand warrants seeing a flop.

On this flop of 10-8-4, I have hit middle pair with a King kicker. I am first to act against FiveTwo, who was the pre-flop aggressor. Standard poker strategy in this spot is to check to the raiser to see how they value the board. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out hands I beat or get raised by better, while checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from a wider range. I plan to check and most likely call a standard continuation bet to see how the turn develops.

Heads-up and in position after BB checks, but 5h4h is just bottom pair on 4d-8d-Th with a weak kicker and only thin improvement paths (mainly turning trips/2p). This board connects well with BB’s preflop calling range (many Tx/8x, pocket pairs, and diamond draws), so a small c-bet will get called often by better hands/draws and can invite check-raises that put us in a tough spot for a marginal gain. Checking back realizes our equity, keeps the pot manageable, and also protects our checking range (we’ll check back some stronger hands here too).

On the turn 3c, the board remains relatively unchanged since the flop. My 8s are still a middle pair with a good kicker. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, checked back on a wet flop (4d 8d Th), which often indicates a hand with marginal showdown value or a desire to keep the pot small. By checking again, I maintain my pot control strategy with a medium-strength hand. This allows me to see how FiveTwo reacts; if they check again, I'm likely ahead going into the river. If they bet, I have a relatively easy call given the flop action and the pot odds.

Flash has checked twice on a board that hits his BB defend range reasonably well, but after he checks turn again he’s still capped at a lot of one-pair/overcard hands that will fold to pressure. With 5h4h we have weak showdown value (bottom pair) and want to avoid building a big pot, so I’ll use a small delayed c-bet to pick up the pot often and deny equity to hands like A-high/K-high/QJ, while keeping the risk low and comfortably folding if check-raised.

On the turn 3c, I still have middle pair with a King kicker. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, checked back on the flop, which often suggests they don't have a monster or are playing a marginal hand cautiously. Their small turn bet of 225 into a pot of 550 offers me excellent pot odds of approximately 3.4:1. Given my hand strength and the board texture, folding would be too tight. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (like a Ten or a slow-played set) and would force out the bluffs I currently beat. Calling allows me to reach the river cheaply and keeps FiveTwo's wider range in the pot.

The river card is the 3rd diamond, which completes the flush draw that was present on the flop. The board also paired with the 3s. My hand, a pair of 8s with a King kicker, has decent showdown value in a 4-handed game, especially considering FiveTwo checked back on the flop. However, it is not strong enough to value bet here, as I'm unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I risk being raised by a flush or a slow-played Ten. By checking, I can potentially induce a bluff from missed straight draws or lower pocket pairs, or simply get to a cheap showdown. If FiveTwo bets, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most solid play.

River pairs the 3 and we’re left with a very weak one-pair (4s with a 5 kicker). After checking flop and getting called on a small turn stab, Flash’s range is heavy on Tx/8x/pocket pairs/4x plus some missed diamonds. Betting now mostly folds out the hands we already beat (missed draws/A-high) and gets called by better one-pair hands, so it’s a poor bluff spot and not thin value. Checking back realizes our showdown equity and avoids turning a marginal hand into a losing bluff.

I am in UTG position with J7 offsuit. This is a very weak hand (trash) that does not justify opening the pot, especially from early position where I am liable to be 3-bet or called by better hands. My stack is 7,975 (approx 80 BB), so I have plenty of chips to wait for better spots. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

JQo on the button is a premium stealing hand. With Pro already folded, I'm in excellent position to raise and potentially take down the blinds or play postflop with position. Standard button open to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ten offsuit (KTs). Claude, on the Button, has opened with a standard raise to 2.5bb (250). My previous interactions with Claude (Hands #93 and #64) show that he often opens from late position but tends to fold to 3-bets. KTo is a strong enough hand to play, but calling from the Small Blind often leads to difficult post-flop situations out of position, especially if the Big Blind decides to join the pot. To maximize my fold equity and isolate the Button, I will put in a 3-bet to 850, which has been my consistent sizing for this action in previous hands.

Facing a small blind 3-bet after a button open, ranges are generally strong and we’ll be out of position against at least the 3-bettor (and sometimes the button if he comes along). 8c4c is too weak and too dominated to realize equity profitably in a 3-bet pot, and turning it into a 4-bet bluff is poor given blocker-less hand, deep stacks, and likely continue range. Take the disciplined spot and preserve stack.

JQo facing a 3-bet from the SB. I have position and decent pot odds (600 to win 1200). JQo has reasonable equity vs a 3-betting range and I'll have position postflop. 4-betting feels too aggressive, folding seems too tight given the pot odds and position. Calling to see a flop in position.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting from the Small Blind. The flop is monotone (all diamonds), and I hold the Ten of Diamonds, giving me a flush draw and one overcard to the board (King). On monotone boards, a small continuation bet of around 1/3 pot is a standard strategic play to maintain initiative and pressure hands without a diamond. If Claude has a made flush or a higher diamond draw, he will likely call or raise, but this bet helps define his range while I still have significant equity with my draw.

I flopped top pair with Jc on Jd6d5d, but the board is monotone diamonds and I have no diamond. Flash 3-bet preflop and is now c-betting small (1/3 pot). My hand is vulnerable - any diamond gives opponent a flush if they have one diamond. Raising seems risky as Flash's 3-bet range likely contains many diamond holdings. Folding top pair seems too weak. Calling to see a turn card in position is reasonable - I can reassess based on the turn card and Flash's action.

On the turn 8h, the board remains monotone with three diamonds. I currently hold the Ten of Diamonds, giving me a flush draw. I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, but after Claude called a 1/3 pot bet on the flop, his range likely contains at least one diamond or a pair of Jacks. By checking now, I can see how Claude reacts and potentially realize my equity more cheaply. If he checks back, I get a free river card. If he bets, I can decide whether to call based on the price offered, keeping the pot manageable while I am still on a draw.

Flash 3-bet preflop, c-bet flop on a monotone diamond board, then checked turn. This check signals weakness - with a made flush they'd likely continue betting. The 8h turn is a blank. I have top pair with queen kicker. I could bet for thin value/protection, but checking back controls pot size on this dangerous board. If Flash had a flush or strong draw, they might be trapping. By checking I get to showdown cheaply and can make a better decision on the river based on their action.

I have improved to top pair on the river with the King of Hearts. However, the board still shows three diamonds from the flop. While I hold the Ten of Diamonds, any higher diamond in Claude's hand would complete a flush. Claude called my 3-bet and my flop continuation bet, then checked back on the turn. This suggests he might have a marginal hand (like a Jack) or a draw. By checking the river, I avoid value-towning myself against a flush while allowing him to check back with worse hands or potentially bluff. I will evaluate his bet sizing if he decides to lead.

Flash 3-bet preflop, c-bet flop on monotone diamond board, then checked both turn and river. This passive line indicates weakness - a flush would keep betting for value. The Kh river could have helped hands like AK/KQ, but Flash checked. My top pair with Q kicker is likely good at showdown. Betting for value is risky - I only get called by better hands (flushes, Kx that just improved, better Jx). Checking back to realize my showdown value is the optimal play here.
J4o is a weak hand from UTG position. Not profitable to open - fold and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo) in a 4-handed game. Claude has folded. JTo is a solid opening hand from this position as it performs well against the range of hands the blinds are likely to defend with. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately or play a pot in position.

Flash is opening 3x on the button, but Q3o is heavily dominated by his continuing range (all better Qx, most Kx/Ax) and plays terribly out of position. Flat-calling also invites the big blind to come along or squeeze, creating a multiway pot where our hand realizes equity very poorly. With no strong blocker structure or suitedness to justify a 3-bet bluff, this is a disciplined fold.
