I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5s, facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button (Flash). The Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 chips to win a pot of 450, offering pot odds of roughly 30.7%. While Button opening ranges are typically wide, 95o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. It struggles to realize its equity and is dominated by many hands in the opener's range. Against a 3bb open, defending this hand is -EV. I will fold.

I am in the UTG position with Ace-King offsuit (AKo), which is a premium starting hand, especially in a 4-handed game. My stack is the largest at the table (20,700 chips). I want to build a pot and narrow the field or win the blinds immediately. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate here to extract value and maintain aggression.

Flash’s 4-handed UTG open is relatively wide, but 9♥4♦ offsuit is still far too weak: it’s frequently dominated when we make a pair, has poor connectivity, and plays badly in 3-bet pots. Flat-calling also invites squeezes from the blinds and puts us in marginal spots postflop for 3bb. Best EV is to conserve chips and wait for a better button attacking hand.

I hold J2o in the Small Blind. Flash raised to 300 from UTG. J2o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against an UTG opening range which tends to be stronger. The pot odds do not justify calling with this holding. I will fold to preserve my stack.

78o in the BB facing UTG raise of 300. This is an offsuit connector that plays poorly out of position against an UTG opening range. Flash has been active but UTG raises typically indicate strength. With 200 to call into a 450 pot, I'm getting decent odds but 78o will be difficult to play profitably OOP. Disciplined fold.

4-handed (effectively CO/UTG) we should be opening fairly wide, and 9d8d is a strong suited connector that plays well in position postflop, can make nutty draws, and benefits a lot from having initiative. Limping invites the blinds to realize equity cheaply and increases multiway variance. I’ll use a standard small open size consistent with the game flow to pick up the blinds/antes often while keeping my risk controlled versus a 3-bet.

I am holding 9c 3s on the Button, which is a very weak hand (9-3 offsuit). Facing a standard raise to 250 from the Under-The-Gun player (FiveTwo), who covers me, I have no reason to get involved. The hand has very low playability and equity against an UTG opening range. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here to preserve my 78BB stack for better spots.
8-4 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG raise, I'm out of position with poor cards that have little playability. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. FiveTwo has raised from UTG. While it is only 150 more to call into a pot of 400, Queen-Deuce is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by an UTG opening range. Since I have a healthy stack and there's no reason to defend with junk hands, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold 3s 5h in UTG, which is a statistically very weak hand (unsuited, low cards, one-gapper). In a 4-handed game, UTG is still a position requiring reasonable hand strength to enter the pot. With a stack of 78BB, there is no pressure to make desperate moves with trash hands. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to preserve chips for better spots.

On the button with 87o after UTG folds. This is a good steal spot with only the blinds left. 87s would be better but 87o still has playability with straight potential. Standard button open to 2.5x to attempt a steal.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit, a very weak hand. Claude has raised from the Button, which is a standard play, but my hand is too weak to defend even against a potentially wide range. Calling would put me out of position with a marginal-at-best hand, and a 3-bet bluff is not ideal with such poor blockers. I will fold and wait for a better spot, as I have a healthy stack and don't need to take unnecessary risks with junk hands.

Claude is opening the button to 2.5x in a 4-handed game, so his range should be quite wide. From the big blind we’re getting a good price (150 more to win 400 already in the pot), and K7o has enough raw equity versus a button opening range to continue. A 3-bet with K7o is doable as a light bluff sometimes, but it plays poorly when called and we don’t block Claude’s strongest continues (AK/KQ/KJ), so the higher-EV, lower-variance line is to defend and realize equity postflop.

We defended BB vs a wide BTN open and flop a marginal made hand: third pair (7s) with a decent kicker. On this 9-7-4 two-diamond texture, BTN will c-bet at a high frequency; checking keeps his range wide and lets us realize equity without inflating the pot with a hand that dislikes pressure. Versus typical small c-bets we can often continue (check-call) depending on sizing, but leading or check-raising here would mostly build a pot with a medium-strength hand and no strong draw to support aggression.

Flopped bottom pair (7s) on 4d-9h-7d board. FiveTwo checked to me. I have position and a made hand, though it's vulnerable to the diamond flush draw. Standard c-bet here to get value from overcards/draws and protect against free cards. Betting around half pot.

Claude’s BTN opening range is wide and this 1/2-pot c-bet (275 into 550) will include lots of overcards and unpaired hands, plus some diamond/straight-draws. With K7 we have third pair with a decent kicker, a diamond blocker (Kd) and some backdoor improvement, and we’re getting a great price (need ~25% equity). Check-raising would mostly isolate ourselves versus better 9x/overpairs/strong draws, while folding is too tight versus his c-bet frequency. Best is to keep the pot controlled and realize equity.

We’re OOP after defending BB vs a wide BTN open and check-calling flop with third pair. The turn Ace is a high-impact card that strengthens Claude’s perceived range (he has lots of Ax that c-bet flop) and reduces the relative strength of our 7x. Leading here doesn’t credibly represent many strong hands from our line and risks getting called/raised by better hands while bloating the pot with a marginal bluff-catcher. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and evaluate versus his sizing/frequency—continuing mainly versus smaller bets and being able to fold to heavy pressure.

Turn brought an Ace which is a scare card. I have bottom pair (7s) with 8 kicker. FiveTwo called flop and checked again. The Ace could easily have helped them if they were floating with Ax. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet three streets. Checking back for pot control makes sense - I can call a reasonable river bet or value bet if checked to again.

River is a complete brick (2c) after Claude c-bets flop and then checks back the Ace turn. With K7 we have a medium-strength bluff-catcher (pair of 7s) that wins at showdown versus his missed overcards/draws, but doesn’t want to bloat the pot or value-own itself versus his 9x/Ax that can easily be in his line. Leading doesn’t get called often by many worse hands, and our Kd blocks a chunk of his missed diamond-draw bluffs, so checking and realizing showdown value is best; we can then decide versus his sizing if he bets.

I have a pair of 7s on 4d-9h-7d-As-2c. FiveTwo checked the river after we both checked the turn. My hand has showdown value but is marginal - betting thin here risks getting check-raised or only getting called by better hands. The missed diamond draw and various overcards they could have floated with make checking back correct to realize my equity at showdown.
KQo is a strong hand in UTG position. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate here. This hand plays well against calling ranges and I want to build a pot with this premium holding.

Claude has opened for 250 from UTG in a 4-handed game, which typically indicates a reasonably strong range. Holding K6o on the button, this hand is significantly behind an average UTG opening range. While I have position, K6o plays poorly post-flop and doesn't have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff against this specific open. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

4-handed, Claude’s UTG open to 250 is likely fairly wide, and he’s shown a willingness to open/fold versus 3-bets. From the small blind, flat-calling with Q5s is tough to realize equity (OOP and can go multiway vs the BB), so I prefer turning this into a light 3-bet bluff. Qc blocks some of his stronger continues (AQ/KQ/QQ) and the hand has decent playability when called (flush potential, some top-pair boards). Sizing larger OOP to pressure folds and deny the BB a cheap entry.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 3d facing a raise from UTG and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. This indicates very strong ranges from my opponents (UTG opening range and SB 3-betting range). My hand is very weak and speculative. Although the pot odds might seem mathematically tempting if it were a single raise, calling a 3-bet cold with 9-high suited gappers is a losing play in the long run due to reverse implied odds and being dominated. The disciplined play is to fold.

KQo facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo. Getting about 1.9:1 pot odds (need to call 650 into 1250). KQo has reasonable equity against a 3-bet range and good playability. FiveTwo has been active throughout the tournament. While I'll be out of position, folding KQo to a single 3-bet is too tight. 4-betting would be aggressive but KQo doesn't play great as a 4-bet. Calling and seeing a flop is the best line here.

We’re the preflop 3-bettor and this flop (A♠ J♠ 9h) heavily favors our perceived range (lots of strong Ax, big broadways, overpairs). With Qc5c we have essentially no showdown value, but we can profitably apply pressure with a small range c-bet to fold out his underpairs (22–88), some suited connectors, and hands like KQ/QT that whiff or are marginal. Small sizing also keeps us from over-investing when he continues with many Ax/Jx and spade draws; if raised, we can comfortably fold.

Flop is Js As 9h. I have KQo - just a gutshot straight draw (need a T for broadway). FiveTwo 3-bet preflop and is c-betting 600 into 1900 (about 31% pot). This A-high board hits their 3-bet range hard - they likely have AK, AQ, AJ, or sets. My gutshot gives me only ~8% equity, and I need ~24% to call profitably. Even if I hit a K or Q, I could easily be dominated by AK/AQ. No backdoor flush draw either. This is a clear fold - I'm drawing thin against their range.

K5o is a weak hand to open from UTG (Under the Gun), even in a 4-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 200 big blinds, making me the current chip leader. There is no strategic reason to enter a pot with a sub-optimal hand from the first position when I have aggressive opponents like FiveTwo behind me. Folding is the standard professional play in this spot to preserve my stack and wait for a better opening opportunity.

4-handed on the button we should be opening quite wide, and JTo is comfortably in that range: it has good playability in position, makes strong top-pair/straight possibilities, and benefits from fold equity versus blinds that have shown a tendency to give up to pressure. Limping invites the BB to realize equity cheaply and reduces our ability to win the pot preflop. I’ll use a standard small open to attack the blinds while keeping the sizing efficient against the shorter SB stack. If facing a big 3-bet (especially from Pro), I’m generally folding; versus a smaller BB 3-bet I can consider calling in position depending on sizing.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o facing a Button raise from FiveTwo. My hand is statistically very weak and plays poorly out of position. Although the price to call is relatively cheap, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make calling a losing play in the long run. I have a healthy stack of around 76BB and can wait for better opportunities. Folding is the standard decision here.
3h6c is a weak offsuit hand. Even getting ~2.7:1 pot odds against a button raise, this hand plays poorly postflop with no connectivity or high card value. Folding is the disciplined play.

4-handed UTG is still the worst position postflop (OOP to the button and both blinds). Q8o is a dominated, low-playability hand that performs poorly versus the likely defend/3-bet ranges here—especially with Flash and Claude showing plenty of aggression historically. With deep stacks, there’s no need to take a thin, high-variance open that will often face a 3-bet or create tough multiway spots. I’ll pass and preserve stack for better opens.

Action is folded to me on the Button. I hold Queen-Nine suited (Qd 9d), which is a strong enough hand to open-raise from late position to steal the blinds or play the hand with positional advantage. The standard open size in this game appears to be 2.5x to 3x the big blind. With a stack of over 75 BBs, I can comfortably afford this raise. I will raise to 250 to apply pressure on the blinds.

KJs is a strong hand in the small blind facing a button raise. Pro opened to 250. Rather than flatting out of position, I should 3-bet for value and to take initiative. A 3-bet to around 800 puts pressure on Pro's likely wide button opening range.

I am holding Q6o in the Big Blind. Pro (Button) raised and Claude (Small Blind) 3-bet to 800. Q6o is a very weak hand to play in a 3-bet pot out of position. Facing strength from two active players, the most prudent course of action is to fold and preserve my chip lead as the tournament reaches its final stages.

I am facing a 3-bet to 800 from the Small Blind after opening to 250 from the Button. My hand, Queen-Nine suited (Qd9d), is a reasonable holding to defend with position. The price to call is 550 chips into a pot of 1150, offering me roughly 32% pot odds. My hand retains decent equity against a standard Small Blind 3-bet range and plays well post-flop, especially given my positional advantage. With a stack depth of over 70 big blinds, I have room to maneuver post-flop. Folding is a bit too tight given the positional dynamic, and 4-betting would be overplaying my hand strength. Therefore, calling is the most profitable play.

I 3-bet preflop with KJs and completely whiffed this Ac-6d-4c flop. I have no pair, no flush draw (wrong suits), and only two overcards to the middle cards. The Ace on board hits Pro's calling range hard - they'd call a 3-bet with many Ax combos, pocket pairs, and broadway hands. C-betting here as a bluff is risky since I'm unlikely to get folds from hands that beat me. Checking and giving up or seeing a cheap turn is the prudent play.

I am in position against a pre-flop 3-bettor on an Ace-high board (Ac 6d 4c). My opponent checked to me. While the check might indicate weakness (like pocket pairs KK-77 fearing the Ace), the Ace hits the 3-bettor's range very hard (AK, AQ, AJs). It is quite possible he is trapping or pot-controlling a made hand. My hand (Qd9d) has very little immediate showdown value but possesses backdoor flush and straight possibilities. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff that is unlikely to fold out an Ace, and if I get check-raised, I must fold immediately. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a turn card, and potentially re-evaluate if the opponent shows continued weakness on later streets. It avoids bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I have KJs on Ac-6d-4c-5s. Still no pair, no draws. Pro checked back the flop which suggests either a marginal hand (small pair, weak Ax) or complete air. The turn 5s doesn't change much for my hand. Betting as a bluff is risky - Pro has shown some interest by calling my 3-bet and might have an Ace or pocket pair. With K-high I have showdown value against complete bluffs but no real equity against made hands. Checking and potentially giving up is the safest line.

On the Turn, the board is Ac 6d 4c 5s. The pot is 1700. My opponent (Claude), who 3-bet pre-flop, has checked the Flop and now the Turn. This double check on an Ace-high board signals weakness (like underpairs KK-77) or pot-control with a weak Ace, though a trap is possible. My hand, Qd9d, has only Queen-high and no significant draws on this coordinated board (4-5-6 on board). Betting here would be a pure bluff. It is unlikely to fold out an Ace, and even pocket pairs might call one street. Checking back is the prudent play to realize any small equity I have for free, avoid being check-raised, and see the river card to re-evaluate.

River completes flush draw (3 clubs) and I have K-high. Pro checked back both flop and turn after calling my 3-bet, suggesting either a weak made hand or air. My K-high beats complete air but loses to any pair. Betting as a bluff is an option to represent the flush or an Ace, but Pro's passive line could indicate a marginal hand that will call. Checking keeps the pot small and I might win at showdown against missed draws.

The board is Ac 6d 4c 5s 9c. The River 9c gives me a pair of Nines (Qd9d). My opponent, Claude, who 3-bet pre-flop, has checked to me on all three streets (Flop, Turn, River). This line strongly suggests either pot-control with a made hand (like KK, QQ, JJ, TT, or a weak Ace) or a complete give-up with missed high cards. My pair of Nines likely beats his missed high cards (like KQs) and lower pocket pairs (77, 88), but loses to the higher pocket pairs and any Ace. Betting here has little utility: worse hands will fold, and better hands (like TT-KK or Ax) will likely call, leading me to value-own myself. Checking back allows me to take my showdown value against his bluffs/weaker holdings without risking more chips.

I am holding Ten of Spades and Six of Hearts (T6o) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a 4-handed table. T6o is a statistically weak hand with poor playability, especially from early position where I have three opponents left to act behind me. There is a high likelihood that at least one opponent has a better hand, and if I raise, I risk getting 3-bet or called while playing out of position post-flop. With a healthy stack of over 85 big blinds, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The standard and most profitable play with this hand is to fold immediately.

On the button with J4o. Pro folded. This is a marginal hand but I have position and can attempt to steal the blinds. A standard 2.5x open to 250 is reasonable - if I get 3-bet I can fold easily given the weak holding.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 4s, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised to 2.5bb from the Button. While I have a large stack, calling from out of position with a marginal hand against a Button raiser is generally a losing play. Folding is the most prudent action here to conserve chips for better opportunities.

Claude is opening from the button to 2.5x and will have a wide range. Getting 150 to win a 400 pot gives strong immediate odds, and 8-7 offsuit has decent playability and implied odds in positionless but deep-stacked heads-up (can make straights/two pair and realize equity reasonably vs a wide range). It’s not a great 3-bet candidate offsuit (more dominated and poorer postflop clarity), so I’ll defend by calling.

We flop top two pair (8♣7♣2♣) with no club, which is very strong but somewhat vulnerable on a monotone board (villain can already have flushes, and many hands have a single-club draw). Versus Claude’s wide button range, he should c-bet this flop frequently (often small), so checking maximizes EV by:
- inducing bluffs/value bets from worse (overpairs, 8x, 7x, A-high with a club)
- keeping his range wide (a lead can fold out lots of his air)
- setting up a profitable check-raise to charge single-club hands and build a pot with a hand that can still boat up even if he has a flush.
Plan: check with intent to raise most c-bets (especially small/medium sizes).

Flop is monotone clubs (2c 7c 8c). I have Jc giving me a flush draw - specifically the 3rd nut flush draw. FiveTwo checked showing weakness. This is a good spot to c-bet with position and equity. Betting around 60% pot to put pressure and build the pot with my draw. If called I have 9 outs, if raised I can fold.

We flopped top two pair on a monotone board (8c7c2c) with no club, so our hand is very strong vs Claude’s wide button c-betting range, but it’s also vulnerable: many of his one-club hands have substantial equity and will continue, and we don’t want to let him realize cheaply or check back turns.

A check-raise here builds the pot while we’re likely ahead and charges single-club floats/draws (AcX, KcX, pair+club), and can still get value from worse made hands (8x/7x). I’ll size it moderately so those hands can call, while keeping in mind that if Claude responds by jamming/3-betting, his range skews heavily to made flushes/sets and we can make a disciplined fold without the club in hand.

FiveTwo check-raised my c-bet from 350 to 1400 - a strong line representing real strength. I have the Jc for a flush draw, but this is only the 3rd nut flush draw. If FiveTwo has any made flush (Ac/Kc/Qc with another club), I'm in bad shape. Even if they're semi-bluffing with a higher flush draw, I'm dominated. Pot odds are ~2.2:1 needing to call 1050 into 2300, but with only ~18% equity on the turn with a flush draw (and potentially less if they already have a flush), the math doesn't work. Folding is disciplined here - I lose my c-bet but avoid a potentially costly situation.
UTG with 6h9s - this is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability from early position. No reason to open this hand from UTG where I need to play tight. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Queen-Nine suited, which is a strong opening hand in a 4-handed game. With the UTG player folding, it's a standard situation to raise for value and to put pressure on the blinds. A 3x open is a consistent sizing for my strategy here.

Facing a BTN min-open, A6o is too dominated to realize equity well by flat-calling out of position (and it invites the BB in). With an ace blocker we can profitably 3-bet as part of a polarized SB vs BTN strategy: we deny equity, often win pre, and take initiative for postflop barrels on favorable textures. If Flash 4-bets, we comfortably fold; if he calls, we can c-bet many boards and shut down on heavy resistance.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9d 4s, which is a very weak hand. The action involves a raise from the Button (Flash) to 300 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (FiveTwo) to 1100. Facing a 3-bet with 94o offers terrible equity and reverse implied odds. The pot odds are insufficient to justify a call, and the hand is too weak to consider a bluff 4-bet. The only correct play is to fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with Queen-Nine suited, a strong hand for 4-handed play. FiveTwo in the small blind 3-bet to 1100. Given the positional advantage I have for the rest of the hand and the pot odds (calling 800 to win a total pot that will be 2300), folding is too conservative. $Q9s$ plays well post-flop and can realize its equity effectively in position. I will call to see the flop, keeping the pot manageable while retaining the ability to outplay my opponent on later streets.

We’re the preflop 3-bettor in a heads-up pot and this K-Q-5 rainbow flop favors our overall range (we have more AA/KK/QQ/AK/KQ than BTN’s flat range). Even though Ad6s has very little direct equity and weak backdoors, a small range c-bet pressures his underpairs, A-highs without a king/queen, and some suited connector floats, and it keeps our risk low if he plays back. If raised, we can comfortably fold; if called, we can selectively barrel on strong turns (A, diamonds, some good broadways) and otherwise shut down.

On a flop of K-Q-5, I have middle pair with my Queen. My opponent, FiveTwo, who 3-bet from the Small Blind, has led out with a small bet of 600 into a 2,300 pot. This small sizing (about 1/4 pot) is often a continuation bet used to maintain range advantage on a board that hits a 3-betting range well. While there is a King on the board that beats me, I cannot fold middle pair to such a small bet given the pot odds (calling 600 to win nearly 3,000). I have position for the rest of the hand, which allows me to see how the turn develops. I will call to evaluate the turn.

Turn 8d doesn’t improve our hand, but it’s a good card to continue applying range pressure in a 3-bet pot: our range still contains more AK/AA/KK/QQ/KQ than BTN’s flatting range. Flash’s flop call can include a lot of one-and-done hands (JJ/TT/99, some floats, some weaker Qx) that dislike facing a second barrel. Using a small-to-medium sizing keeps our risk controlled, leverages fold equity, and we’re comfortable folding if raised since we have virtually no equity.

On the turn 8d, the board is Ks-Qd-5h-8d. FiveTwo has led again with a sizing of 1,200 into a pot of 3,500 (approx. 34% pot). I still hold middle pair (Queens) with a 9 kicker. Given the pot odds (calling 1,200 to win a total pot that will be 5,900) and the fact that I am in position, I have enough equity to continue against a range that includes bluffs, semi-bluffs, or potentially some smaller pocket pairs that FiveTwo is overplaying. While the King is a concern in a 3-bet pot, folding middle pair to such a small sizing would be too exploitable. I will call and evaluate the river action.

River A♥ is a great card for our range after 3-bet/c-bet/barrel, and it also improves us from A-high to a pair of aces. But betting here is awkward: most of the hands we beat now (Kx, Qx, JJ–99, missed diamonds) will often fold to a third barrel on this scare card, while the hands that continue (Ax with a better kicker, KQ, sets, slowplayed AK/AQ) largely have us crushed. That makes a river bet mostly turn into a thin/negative value bet.

Checking realizes our showdown value and keeps in his missed-draw bluffs (some diamonds/gutters) that might stab when we finally slow down; and if he checks back we win a lot versus his turn-calling range that’s now downgraded to second/third pair.

On the river, the board is Ks-Qd-5h-8d-Ah. My hand is middle pair (Queens) with a 9 kicker. The Ace on the river is a significant card that hits a 3-betting range (AK, AQ, AJ) which FiveTwo likely has. FiveTwo has led the flop and turn with small sizes and has now checked on the river. My hand has showdown value against missed draws or lower pocket pairs (like JJ or TT) that FiveTwo might have 3-bet pre-flop. However, it is not strong enough to bet for value, as better hands (Kings or Aces) will call and worse hands will likely fold. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and reach showdown without risking more chips in a pot where I am likely behind or at best in a marginal spot.

I am dealt Ten-Deuce offsuit in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a very weak starting hand with poor equity and post-flop playability. With a healthy stack of over 170 big blinds and only 35 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to attempt a bluff or play a sub-optimal hand from early position. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

4-handed on the button after UTG folds, we should attack the blinds very aggressively. 8h4h is weak but suited and has decent playability/realization in position, and the immediate pot (150) makes a raise profitable if we generate folds often enough. I’ll use a small standard BTN open to keep risk low versus 3-bets while still applying pressure. Plan: fold comfortably to a sizeable 3-bet; otherwise use position to c-bet/selectively bluff on favorable textures.

I hold 8s7s in the Small Blind facing a Button raise to 250 from an active player (FiveTwo). 8s7s is a premium suited connector with good playability post-flop. The raise size is relatively small (2.5x), offering me good pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of at least 500). While 3-betting is an option to isolate, calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to realize equity against a wide range, potentially inviting the Big Blind for a multi-way pot where my hand plays well. I decide to call.
K5o in BB with 150 to call into a 600 pot. Getting 4:1 odds. With a king-high hand and good pot odds, this is a standard defend. I'll call and see if I connect with the flop.

The flop comes Jd-2s-4c, which is a very dry rainbow board. I have 8s7s, giving me only 8-high with a backdoor flush draw (runner-runner spades) and very little immediate equity. In a multi-way pot out of position against the pre-flop raiser, leading out (donk betting) is ill-advised as I represent very little and likely won't fold out better hands. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If they bet, I will fold comfortably. If it checks through, I can try to realize equity on the turn.

Missed the flop completely with K5o - I have king high on a J42 board. No pair, no draws. Pro checked and I can check behind for free. No reason to bluff here with two opponents and no equity if called. Check and see a free card.

Multiway pot on a fairly dry J-4-2 rainbow board, our 8h4h is a marginal made hand (bottom pair) with limited ability to withstand pressure. A small c-bet won’t generate enough folds vs two ranges that connect with any Jx and pocket pairs, and it also exposes us to a check-raise where we’d be forced to fold and burn chips. Checking back realizes our equity, keeps the pot manageable, and lets us continue versus reasonable turn bets while picking up occasional improvement (trip 4s / two pair with an 8) or better spots to bluff on later streets.

I hold 8s7s on a board of 4d-2s-Jc-Jh. I have 8-high with no flush draw (only 3 spades total) and no straight draw. The action checked around on the flop, suggesting weakness from the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and the Big Blind (Claude). However, with zero equity and two opponents still in the hand, firing a bluff on a paired board is risky as I likely won't fold out Ace-highs or small pairs that checked the flop. My best play is to check, hoping to see a free river or folding easily if faced with a bet.

Turn paired the Jacks. I still have just K-high with no connection to this board. Pro checked, and with a paired board showing trips potential, there's no value in betting K-high. Anyone with a Jack has trips, and even mid pairs beat me. Check and hope for a free showdown or fold to aggression.

Turn goes check-check to me in a 3-way pot after everyone checked the flop, which heavily weights both blinds toward weak one-pair hands (2x/4x), pocket pairs, and ace-high, with fewer Jx combinations (many players bet Jx on the flop). With 8h4h on Jc Jh 4d 2s I have two pair (JJ44) — strong enough to value bet, but not strong enough to bloat the pot versus a range that can still contain traps like any Jx (full house) or 22/44. I’ll take a small-to-mid sizing to get called by worse (2x, 4x, 55–TT, some A-high floats) and deny a free river, while keeping the bet/fold option intact if I face a check-raise (which is heavily weighted to boats).

I hold 8s7s on a board of 4d-2s-Jc-Jh. I have 8-high with no pair and no meaningful draw. The action checked through on the flop. On the turn, the button (FiveTwo) bets 300 into a pot of 750. Although the price is good, I have effectively 0% equity against any made hand and very little bluffing equity given the multi-way nature and my passive line so far. Chasing with air is not profitable. The correct play is to fold and preserve chips.

K-high with no draws on a paired board. FiveTwo bet 300 into 750. I have zero showdown value against any reasonable betting range - any pair beats me, any jack has trips. Getting 3.5:1 but I only have ~6% to hit a king. Easy fold.

4-handed, UTG is effectively the cutoff, so opening ranges widen substantially. J9o is a reasonable open here: it has decent high-card/straight potential and benefits from taking initiative rather than limping into the blinds. With our big stack, we can apply pressure and win the blinds/antes-equivalent frequently while keeping the sizing small enough to comfortably fold to a strong 3-bet if needed. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open consistent with the table’s common sizes.

Current situation: 4 players left, I am on the Button with 82 BBs. The chip leader (FiveTwo) opens from UTG to 2.5 BBs. My hand is Q8s. Analysis: In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff, so the opener's range is somewhat wide but still contains reasonable strength. Q8s is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by better Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ, QTs) in the opener's range. While playing in position is advantageous, calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the blinds and reverse implied odds post-flop against the deep-stacked aggressive leader. 3-betting is risky as the leader has plenty of chips to call or 4-bet. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
T7o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against an UTG raise. I'm out of position with poor hand equity. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 6d. FiveTwo, the current chip leader, has opened from UTG in this 4-handed game. While the price to call is attractive (150 more to call into a pot of 400), 86o is a very weak hand that realizes its equity poorly out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are only 35 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer a disciplined fold here rather than defending with a marginal hand against the most active and well-stacked player at the table.

I am in UTG (which is also Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 9c 8h, an off-suit connector. With 82 big blinds, I am the shortest stack at the table. While 4-handed play requires wider ranges, 98o is below the threshold for a profitable open-raise from the first position, especially with aggressive chip leaders in the blinds who can easily apply pressure. The hand plays poorly post-flop when called and cannot withstand a 3-bet. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here to preserve my stack for better spots.
On the button with 9d5s - a weak hand, but I have position and only the blinds remain. This is a good spot for a steal attempt. If I get 3-bet, I can fold easily. Standard button raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 4h, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has raised from the Button. Defending such a weak hand out of position against a standard open is not profitable, especially with the chip leader yet to act in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re heads-up versus a Button open with a premium top-of-range hand (AKo). Calling invites the SB/BB dynamic is gone (SB folded) but still keeps the pot small and allows Claude to realize equity with a wide opening range. Out of position, the best way to maximize EV is to 3-bet for value, deny equity to hands like suited connectors/broadways, and start building a pot where our hand performs very well against his continuing range. Given the 2.5x open, a solid OOP 3-bet sizing is ~3.5–4x to pressure his flats and keep his weaker continues in.
I raised 9d5s as a button steal, but FiveTwo 3-bet to 900. My hand is far too weak to continue - 95o has terrible equity against any 3-betting range. Easy fold.

J7o from UTG is a clear fold. This hand is too weak to open from early position - it's easily dominated and has poor playability. With 32 hands remaining, no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings.

I am on the button with Qc 6c. After the UTG player (Claude) folded, I have a standard opening opportunity. Qc 6c is a speculative but playable suited hand, especially in position. I'll raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play a pot in position against the SB or BB.

Facing a 3x BTN open from the small blind with Q7o, this is too dominated and plays very poorly out of position, especially with the big blind’s shorter stack behind who can jam/squeeze and force us to realize equity badly. A light 3-bet bluff would need a very high fold frequency (given the dead money vs the extra risk) and we don’t have strong blockers or playability. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for better spots.

I hold King-Ten suited in the Big Blind facing a standard 3bb raise from the Button (Flash). KTs is a strong hand that plays well post-flop and is well ahead of a typical button opening range. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 450) and my stack depth (~81BB), defending by calling is the standard and most profitable play. 3-betting is an option but calling keeps the pot manageable while out of position against the chip leader.

On a dry, low board of 8d-3d-2c, I have King-high with two overcards and a backdoor spade flush draw. While the board likely missed the pre-flop raiser (Flash), it doesn't connect strongly enough with my range to justify a leading bet (donk bet). Checking is the standard line out of position to the pre-flop aggressor. If Flash bets, I will evaluate based on sizing, as K-high often has enough equity to peel one street or is sometimes ahead of pure air c-bets.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have a positional advantage on a relatively dry, low board. My hand, Qc 6c, has two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. Pro's range from the big blind could include many hands that missed this board entirely. A continuation bet here is standard to exert pressure and win the pot immediately. If called, I still have some equity to improve on later streets.

I hold KsTs on a flop of 8d-3d-2c. I checked and the opponent (Flash, Button) bet 300 into a pot of 650. I have King-high with two overcards but no flush draw or straight draw potential on this texture (my spades are dead). While my hand might be ahead of pure air, realizing equity out of position is difficult against an aggressive chip leader. I need roughly 24% equity to call, which I have raw against a range, but the playability is poor. I likely face a barrel on the turn which I cannot call. I have better hands in my range to defend (pairs, 8x, flush draws, straight draws). Folding is the prudent long-term play here.

I am holding 5-2 offsuit in the Under-The-Gun position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in the game. With a healthy stack and plenty of hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a poor hand, especially from early position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

4-handed on the button after UTG folds, we should open very wide and leverage position. K9o is comfortably ahead of the blinds’ random holdings, plays fine in position, and benefits from fold equity versus two players. I’ll use a small-but-standard raise size to risk little while still pressuring the blinds and building a pot when called.

I hold 9c 4d in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand (9-4 offsuit). Facing a standard raise from the Button (FiveTwo), even though I am getting decent pot odds, playing a trash hand out of position against the chip leader is a losing strategy. My stack is 7875 (approx 79 BB), so I am not desperate and should preserve chips for better spots. The correct play is to fold.

I have 3d4h in the BB facing a button raise to 250. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Even with decent pot odds (150 to win 400), 34o is at the bottom of my defending range. FiveTwo has been aggressive and I'd be OOP for all postflop streets. Better to fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed from UTG our opening range widens significantly, and KTs suited is a clear open: it plays well postflop (strong high-card value, good top-pair kickers, and solid straight/flush potential) and benefits from taking initiative. With a big stack, raising also applies pressure to the shorter stacks (especially Pro on the Button) and avoids giving the blinds a free realization by limping. I’ll use a standard small open size to keep my range wide and manageable against 3-bets.

Current hand is 120/150. I am on the Button with Jc8c (Jack-Eight suited). The player in UTG (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has been opening a very wide range of hands recently. While my hand has some playability in position, especially against a loose opener, it is a two-gapper that can be easily dominated by higher Jacks. Additionally, I am currently the shortest stack at the table (though still deep with ~79BB), and flat calling invites the larger stacks in the blinds to squeeze. Given the tournament structure with only 30 hands remaining, I want to preserve my stack for spots where I have a clearer equity advantage or blockers to 3-bet. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

3c4c is a weak suited connector. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, I'm out of position in the SB. While suited connectors can have implied odds, 34s is at the bottom of playable hands and UTG ranges are typically strong. Easy fold.

I have 7s 6s in the Big Blind, which is a very playable suited connector. FiveTwo raised to 250 from UTG. To call, I only need to commit an additional 150 chips into a total pot of 400, giving me roughly 2.67-to-1 pot odds. Given our stack depths and the playability of my hand, this is a standard call to see a flop.

On a monotone board of hearts (Qh Th 6h), I have bottom pair (Six of Spades) but no heart in my hand. This makes my hand extremely vulnerable to any heart, overcards, or already completed flushes. FiveTwo raised from UTG, which typically represents a strong range that often contains high hearts or big pairs. Checking to the aggressor is the most standard and safest play here to see how they react to this texture.

Monotone Q♥ T♥ 6♥ is a board where the BB’s flatting range contains a lot of suited hearts and connected hands that now have strong equity (made flushes, pair+heart, A♥/K♥ highs). With K♣T♣ we have some showdown value (2nd pair) but zero heart to continue comfortably versus a check-raise, and a c-bet doesn’t get many clean folds from his range. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and lets us bluff-catch versus reasonable turn bets while avoiding getting blown off our hand by aggression.

The turn brings a fourth heart to the board. Although I have improved to two pair with the 7s, my hand has zero equity against any flush. Since I do not hold a heart, even the lowest heart in my opponent's range has me beat. FiveTwo checked back on the flop, which could indicate they are either weak or trapping with a high heart. I will check here to see if I can reach a cheap showdown; however, I am likely to fold if met with any significant bet, as I am essentially drawing dead against a flush.

Turn brings the 4th heart, which heavily favors the BB’s preflop defending range (many one-heart combos now become flushes, and even hands like Qx/Tx with a heart improve). With KcTc we have some thin showdown value (pair of tens) but we hold no heart, so betting opens us up to a very high-EV check-raise from flushes and strong one-heart hands, and even when called we’re usually drawing very thin. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and lets us evaluate a river bet based on sizing/line (potential bluff-catch vs small bets, fold vs big polarized bets).

The board has completed a five-card flush (Qh Th 7h 6h 4h). My own cards (7s 6s) do not contain a heart. In this situation, the player with the highest heart in their hand wins the pot. If neither player holds a heart, or if both players' hole cards are hearts lower than the ones on the board (the 4h), the pot is split. Since FiveTwo checked the flop and turn, it is possible they do not have a heart or are holding a very weak one. However, any heart they have that is an Ace, King, Jack, 9, 8, or 5 would beat the board's flush. There is no value in betting here; if I bet and they have no heart, they fold and we split (or I win what was already in the middle). If they have any heart, they call or raise. Checking is the most logical action to reach a cheap showdown or a split pot.

Five-heart river massively favors BB’s defending range: any single heart is now a flush and his check can easily be a trap. With KcTc we have a weak pair and no heart blocker, so betting has poor value (worse hands often fold, better hands call/raise) and exposes us to high-EV check-raises that force us to fold. Checking back maximizes our showdown equity versus non-heart holdings and avoids lighting chips on fire.

I am in UTG (Cutoff in 4-handed) with A9o. With 79 big blinds, I am deep, but still the shortest stack at the table. A9o is on the borderline of an opening range from this position. Given the aggressive tendencies of the deep-stacked opponents in the blinds (FiveTwo and Flash), opening this hand risks facing a 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand prone to domination. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better spots.

2c4c on the button is a very weak hand even suited. While I have position, this is at the bottom of any reasonable opening range. Both blinds have larger stacks and have been active. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey suited (Tc 3c). The action has folded to me. Only the Big Blind, FiveTwo, who is the chip leader, remains. While the hand is suited, it is quite weak and playing it out of position against an aggressive chip leader is not ideal. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

5h6c offsuit in UTG position is a clear fold. This is a weak hand with poor playability from early position. With 28 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack of 106BB, I should maintain tight play from UTG and wait for better spots.

Q6o is a weak hand even for a button steal attempt. With an aggressive chip leader in the small blind (FiveTwo) and a shorter stack in the big blind (Pro), the risk of being 3-bet or shoved on is significant. Q6o doesn't play well post-flop if called, so folding is the most disciplined play in this position to preserve my stack.

Heads-up from the small blind versus a medium stack in the big blind, TJo is comfortably ahead of BB’s random holdings and plays well with initiative. Completing gives up fold equity and lets BB realize equity too easily; raising builds the pot when we’re likely best and wins the pot outright a good portion of the time. I’ll use a solid SB open size rather than min-raising to pressure their defend range and simplify postflop.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). This is a Blind vs Blind scenario where ranges are typically wide. I hold A7o, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a steal attempt, having good equity against a wide opening range. I am getting decent pot odds to call in position. A 3-bet is possible but calling controls the pot size and leverages my positional advantage post-flop. I will call the additional 200 chips to match the 300 total.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on 9s-8s-4c, TJo has a strong combo of equity and initiative: open-ended straight draw (7/Q) plus two overcards that can be good vs BB’s floats. This is a dynamic board where we benefit from denying equity to random overcards/spade-peels and getting folds from unpaired hands. I’ll continuation-bet a solid size to pressure his wide defend range while building a pot for when we improve.

I am in the Big Blind with Ac7s. The board is 9s-8s-4c, a very wet texture with flush and straight draws. My opponent (Small Blind) bets 400 into a pot of 600 (2/3 pot). While I have position, my hand is only Ace-high with a weak kicker and weak backdoor draws (7-high flush draw, runner-runner straight). The large bet size on this connected board puts significant pressure on my marginal hand. Calling would require me to likely face further aggression on later streets with very little equity improvement potential. Preserving my stack of ~7500 chips is preferable to making a loose float here.

I am in the UTG position (first to act) in a 4-handed game. I am holding Jack-Four offsuit (J4o), which is a very weak hand. Given that there are three players behind me, including the chip leader on the button, there is no reason to commit chips to the pot with such low equity. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here.

4-handed on the button with AQo after UTG folds is a clear value/open-attack spot. We have a strong high-card hand with good playability and blockers to the strongest continuing ranges (A and Q), and we cover both blinds, so we can apply pressure and deny their equity versus a limp. Standard sizing here is ~2.5x to keep their ranges wide while still building a pot when ahead.

Current hand is Q4s in the Small Blind. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and playing aggressively, opens to 2.5 BB (250). While Q4s has some playability due to being suited, I am out of position against a strong range and aggressive player. Additionally, the Big Blind is still to act behind me, creating a risk of a squeeze or playing a multi-way pot out of position with a weak hand. Given my stack depth (75 BB) and being the shortest stack, preserving chips is better than engaging in a marginal spot OOP. Folding is the standard and disciplined decision here.

KTo in the BB facing a button raise of 250. This is a standard defend - I'm getting good pot odds (150 to win 400) and KTo plays well against a wide button opening range. Calling is the clear play here.

Flop is 5s6s6d - completely missed with KTo. I have two overcards but no pair, no draw. This is a check to the preflop aggressor. If FiveTwo bets, I'll likely fold as my equity is minimal on this paired board.

Heads-up vs BB on a paired, low flop (6-6-5). As the preflop raiser I retain a meaningful range advantage (overpairs 77+ and strong broadways) while Claude has a lot of random overcards and some medium hands that don’t want to continue versus pressure. With AcQh I have two overcards but no strong draws, so I want to use a small c-bet to pick up the pot immediately at high frequency and deny equity, while keeping my sizing efficient vs potential check-raises.

Missed the flop completely with KTo on 5s6s6d. I have no pair, no meaningful draw (just backdoor spade draw with Ts). FiveTwo bet 200 into 550. Even with 3.75:1 odds, my equity is minimal - I'm only winning if I hit a K or T and FiveTwo doesn't have a 6, a 5, or a pocket pair. This paired board heavily favors the aggressor. Folding is the disciplined play.

4-handed from UTG we should be opening fairly wide, and ATo is comfortably in the value/initiative part of that range. Raising builds the pot with a hand that can make top pair/top kicker often enough, denies equity to the blinds, and gives us positional leverage postflop against the blinds when they defend too wide. I’ll use a standard small open (2.5bb) to keep my risk controlled while still pressuring the shorter stacks behind (especially the button).

Current hand is 124/150, 4-handed. I am on the Button with 6c Tc. FiveTwo (chip leader) opens to 250 from UTG. My hand is a suited three-gapper, which is generally too weak to call a raise with, even in position, especially against a UTG open range. The pot odds are not compelling enough to justify the reverse implied odds and the risk of being squeezed by the blinds. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.
J3 offsuit is a weak hand, out of position against an UTG raise with the BB still to act behind. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The player FiveTwo raised to 250 from the cutoff/UTG position in this 4-handed game. The Small Blind has folded, and I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400. This gives me pot odds of 150 to 550, or roughly 27%, which is more than enough to justify a call with a broadway hand like QTo. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, QTo plays reasonably well in a single-raised pot, and my stack is deep enough to see a flop.

On an Ace-high board like As-5h-4s, the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) has a significant range advantage. My Queen-Ten offsuit has completely missed this flop, and I have no real draws or equity to continue if faced with aggression. My standard play here is to check to the raiser and see how they respond. If they bet, I will most likely fold as I have no piece of this board and very little potential to improve.

We’re heads-up in position as the preflop raiser on A♠-5♥-4♠, a board where our UTG range has a clear ace advantage. With A♦T♠ we have top pair and a reasonable kicker, so we want value from worse Ax, 5x/4x, and spade/straight-draw continues, while also denying equity to hands like 6x/3x/2x and random floats. A small c-bet keeps dominated hands in and controls pot size vs a check-raise range that can include two-pair/sets and strong draws.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The flop of As-5h-4s heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo, who raised from under-the-gun. My hand has completely missed the board, and I have no significant draws (not even a backdoor flush draw since I don't hold a spade). Calling a continuation bet here out of position with only Queen-high and virtually no equity would be a losing play in the long run. There is no reason to float or bluff on this texture against an opponent who likely has an Ace or a strong draw in their range. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am dealt 2h 5c (5-2 offsuit) in the UTG position. This is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em due to its low rank and lack of connectivity or suit coordination. With 75 big blinds (stack of 7525), there is absolutely no incentive to play this hand, especially from early position where I would be out of position against most opponents. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to preserve chips for better opportunities.

On the button with Q6o after UTG fold. This is a steal opportunity against the blinds. Q6o is marginal but playable from the button. I'll make a standard 2.5x open raise to try to pick up the blinds. Both Flash and FiveTwo have much larger stacks, so I need to be careful, but a steal attempt is standard here.
I am in the Small Blind with 6c 2s, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised from the Button. Folding is the most prudent move here to avoid playing a weak hand out of position.

Claude is opening the button fairly wide and we’re deep enough that QJs is strong as a value/semi-bluff 3-bet from the BB. I want to deny his positional advantage, take initiative, and leverage my chip lead to pressure his ~100BB stack. If he folds we win a nice preflop pot; if he calls we play a higher-SRP with a hand that flops well (broadway equity, strong draws) and can apply postflop pressure. I’ll use a standard BB vs BTN sizing that’s big enough to cut his odds but not commit me versus a 4-bet.

Q6o was a speculative button steal attempt. FiveTwo 3-bet to 900, and I need to call 650 into a 1200 pot. Q6o plays poorly against a 3-betting range - it's dominated by better queens and lacks the suited/connected qualities to flop well. FiveTwo has been active with 3-bets throughout the tournament. Easy fold here.

K5o from UTG is a weak hand that doesn't play well out of position. In a 4-handed game, I need reasonable hands to open from UTG. K5o lacks connectivity and is dominated by better Kx hands. Folding is the correct play here.

I am on the button in a 4-handed game with Queen-Jack of Diamonds, which is a premium starting hand in this context. Claude has already folded from the UTG position. My goal is to open for value and put pressure on the blinds. Given the stack sizes, specifically Pro's shorter stack (approx. 74 BBs), a standard raise of 3x the big blind (300) is appropriate to build the pot while remaining flexible if FiveTwo or Pro decide to contest the hand.

Button is opening wide and T8s is comfortably within my small blind defend range. Calling keeps the pot manageable while I’m out of position, preserves my stack leverage as chip leader, and lets me realize equity with a hand that flops well (pairs + draws) rather than bloating a 3-bet pot with a non-premium that doesn’t block strong continues. I’ll proceed as a call and play fit/semi-bluff aggressively on favorable textures.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Jack suited (Qh Jh), facing a button raise to 300 and a call from the Small Blind. I am getting excellent pot odds of 3.5 to 1 to call (need to put in 200 to win a pot of 700). My hand has strong playability and equity in a multi-way pot. While a squeeze raise is an option, flat calling minimizes variance and keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to realize my equity against wider ranges. I will call to close the action and see the flop.

Multiway and out of position, this Q-high flop will get c-bet less often, and my hand (Tc8c) is a strong but non-nut flush draw that benefits a lot from realizing equity without inflating the pot unnecessarily. Checking keeps my range protected (I can have strong Qx/sets), lets Flash continuation-bet his wide button range, and gives me the option to check-raise as a semi-bluff if sizing/opponent actions are favorable. If it checks through, I’m happy taking the free card with excellent equity.

On a flop of 6c Qs 2c, I have top pair with a good kicker (Queens with Jack kicker). The pot is multi-way with the pre-flop raiser (Flash) on the Button and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) also in the hand. The Small Blind has checked. As the Big Blind, it is standard to check to the pre-flop raiser in this spot. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands (AQ, KQ, sets) or strong draws, while folding out weaker hands I dominate. Checking allows the pre-flop raiser to make a continuation bet with a wider range, including bluffs and worse value hands, which I can comfortably call. If it checks through, I am happy to see a safe turn card.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a board of Queen-Six-Deuce with two clubs. Both opponents have checked to me. This is a clear spot to bet for value and to protect my hand against various draws (like flush draws) or overcards. I will bet 450, which is 50% of the current pot, to extract value from weaker Queens, middle pairs, or drawing hands.

We’re getting an excellent price (450 to win 1800 immediate = 25%), and Tc8c has strong equity on Qs6c2c with a clean 9-out flush draw plus some additional implied odds when we hit (Flash can have plenty of one-pair Qx / overpairs / bluffs that will pay). Multiway and out of position, a flop check-raise would have reduced fold equity (Pro can continue more tightly but still has plenty of Qx/club continues) and risks bloating the pot with a non-nut flush draw that doesn’t block higher club draws. Calling keeps dominated draws/air in, realizes equity efficiently, and preserves the option to apply pressure on favorable turns (club turns, high cards that are good for our perceived range, or when action checks to us).

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of 6c Qs 2c. The pre-flop raiser (Flash) bet 450 into a pot of 900, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) called. The pot is now 1800, and I need to call 450, offering me 4:1 pot odds (requiring 20% equity). My hand is strong enough to continue against a Button opening range and a SB calling range, which likely includes flush draws and weaker Queens, though I am behind AQ, KQ, and sets. Raising would likely isolate me against better hands, and folding is too tight given the price and hand strength. Calling allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

Turn is essentially a brick for ranges (Qs6c2c3d) and we still hold a strong but non-nut flush draw (Tc8c). Multiway and out of position, leading doesn’t credibly represent many new value hands and risks getting raised and pushed off our equity, while also bloating the pot when we’re drawing. Checking keeps our range protected (we can still have Qx/sets), lets the preflop raiser continue barreling his wide button range (including bluffs), and if it checks through we realize our equity for free and can value/bluff appropriately on the river depending on whether we hit a club.

I hold Top Pair with a Jack kicker on a board of 6c Qs 2c 3d. The pot is multi-way with 2250 chips. After check-calling the flop c-bet from the Button (Flash), the Small Blind (FiveTwo) has checked to me on the turn. The 3d is a blank card that likely doesn't change hand values significantly. Leading out here (donk betting) is generally suboptimal as it often folds out bluffs and weaker hands while isolating myself against better Queens or sets. Checking is the standard play to the pre-flop aggressor. If checked through, I am happy to see a river; if Flash bets again, I will evaluate based on sizing, but my hand is likely strong enough to call a standard bet. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch bluffs, rather than building a large pot out of position with a one-pair hand.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a board that is relatively dry, although a club flush draw is present. After my flop bet was called by both opponents, the turn 3d is a complete blank. By checking twice, both FiveTwo and Pro are showing weakness or potentially trapped with a check-calling range. To protect my hand against the flush draw and extract value from weaker Queens (like Q9 or Q8) or middle pairs (77-JJ), I will place a value bet. A bet of 1100 (roughly half the pot) is large enough to charge draws while remaining a comfortable size for me to fold if either opponent check-raises significantly, which would likely indicate a set or a stronger Queen.

We’re getting a very good price: 1100 to win 4450 (about 24.7%). Our raw river equity with 9 club outs is ~19.6%, and while this is slightly short, the multiway dynamic adds implied odds when we hit (both Flash and Pro can have Qx/overpairs or smaller draws that pay), and we can also occasionally realize extra EV on some rivers when action checks. A check-raise semi-bluff is tempting, but with Pro still to act (short and capable of jamming made hands/draws) the variance and risk of getting blown off our equity is too high, especially with a non-nut flush draw. I’ll take the efficient realization line and call, then proceed cautiously on club rivers (value bet but fold to big raises) and mostly give up on bricks versus continued aggression.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a turn board of 6c Qs 2c 3d. The action involves a bet from the Button (Flash) of 1100 into 2250, and a call from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). I am closing the action. The pot is now 4450, and I need to call 1100, giving me approximately 4:1 pot odds (requiring ~20% equity). While the double-barrel from the Button and the call from the SB indicate strength, my hand beats all flush draws and chops with QJ. I am behind AQ, KQ, and sets, but given the wide Button opening range and the likelihood of semi-bluffs with club draws, I have sufficient equity to continue. Folding Top Pair for a single bet with these odds is too conservative. Raising would isolate me against superior hands. The best play is to call and evaluate the river.

We river a made flush (Tc8c on 6cQs2c3d7h), but it’s a non-nut flush in a multiway pot where both opponents can credibly have higher clubs (A/K/Jc combos from preflop ranges and Flash’s barrel line). Checking risks missing clear value versus one-pair Qx hands that will often check back, while also potentially facing a large bet that forces us into a tough (and higher-variance) bluff-catch spot.

A small “block/value” lead targets calls from Qx, stubborn pairs, and occasional worse flushes, while defining the hand and allowing us to comfortably fold if we face a significant raise (which will be heavily weighted to higher flushes in this multiway context).

On the river, the Small Blind leads out for 1800 (about 1/3 pot) after check-calling both the flop and turn. Leading into two opponents, one of whom is the pre-flop aggressor who double-barreled, usually signifies a polarizing range: either a busted flush draw (clubs) or a strong value hand (rivered two pair, set, or strong top pair) trying to prevent a check-back. While my Top Pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker beats the bluffs, I am crushed by the value range (AQ, KQ, Q7, sets). Most importantly, the Button (Flash) is still to act behind me. Flash has shown strength throughout the hand. If I call, I risk Flash overcalling with a better Queen or raising with a monster, leaving me in a terrible spot. My hand is a pure bluff-catcher in a multi-way pot where I do not close the action. Preserving my 56BB stack is superior to making a high-variance call.

I have Top Pair with a Jack kicker on a board of Qs-6c-2c-3d-7h. The river 7h didn't change much, though it technically makes Q7 or 77 possible. FiveTwo leading for 1800 into a 5550 pot is a relatively small sizing (about 32% of the pot). This 'donk' bet often indicates a blocker bet with a weaker Queen, a pair that just improved (like 7x), or a missed draw (like clubs) trying to steal the pot cheaply. Given that I have been the aggressor throughout the hand, FiveTwo might be leading to prevent me from checking back a hand like mine or to set a cheap price for a showdown. With pot odds of roughly 5-to-1, I only need to be right about 20% of the time to make this call profitable. My hand is too strong to fold in this spot, but not strong enough to raise for value, as a raise would likely only be called by hands that beat me.

I am holding 9d Td in the UTG position (which is effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game). This is a strong suited connector and a standard opening hand. Given my stack lead, I want to apply pressure and build the pot. A 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play style and allows me to play the hand aggressively or fold if faced with significant resistance from the shorter stacks.

We’re on the button with AsAc versus Flash’s UTG open to 300. In a 4-handed game ranges are wider, and AA is far ahead of any continuing range, so the priority is to build a pot and deny good prices to the blinds. Flat-calling invites the SB/BB in with attractive odds and makes the hand harder to play multiway. A value 3-bet also sets up comfortable stacks for later streets against Flash (deep) while putting real pressure on the shorter blinds (who may jam worse or fold).

Sizing: versus 3x (300), a standard button 3-bet is ~3–4x. I’ll go to 1100 to charge the blinds, keep dominated hands (AK/AQ, medium pairs) in, and still leave room for opponents to make mistakes (calls or spewy 4-bets).

I hold 5s 7d in the Small Blind, facing an UTG open to 300 and a Button 3-bet to 1100. My hand is extremely weak (7-high offsuit), and I am out of position against two players showing significant strength. The cost to call is nearly 20% of my stack, and the pot odds are poor. Getting involved here would be a major mistake. This is a clear and easy fold.

J4o is a very weak hand facing a 3-bet. FiveTwo has been aggressive with 3-bets throughout the tournament. Even with the pot odds (~1000 to win 1550), J4o plays poorly against a 3-betting range and will be dominated frequently. Easy fold.

I opened with a standard raise using 9d Td, and FiveTwo 3-bet me from the button. 9d Td is a very strong suited connector that performs well in deep-stack situations. Given that we both have over 20,000 chips (200bb), the implied odds of hitting a straight or flush are significant. I need to call 800 more into a pot of 1,550, which is a fair price to see a flop. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, my hand has high playability, and folding now would be too conservative against an active opponent like FiveTwo.

On the flop of Kc Jd 5d, I have a powerful multi-way draw with both a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw. As the out-of-position player against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo), checking is the standard and most flexible play. This allows me to gauge FiveTwo's strength based on their betting action and sizing. I am looking to see a turn card and have enough equity to continue against most bet sizes, but checking first is the best way to control the pot while on a draw or potentially set up a check-raise if I decide to play more aggressively.

Heads-up in a 3-bet pot, AA is still a clear value hand on K-J-5 with two diamonds. Flash’s preflop call contains plenty of Kx/Jx, QQ–TT, and diamond/AQ/QT type draws that can continue versus a c-bet. Betting also denies equity to hands like QTs/ATs/Axd and forces worse pairs to pay to realize. I’ll use a medium c-bet size that gets value while charging the flush/straight draws without bloating the pot so much that only better continues.

On a board of Kc Jd 5d, I have a powerful multi-way draw with both a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (Queen), totaling 11 outs. The pot currently stands at 3,250 and I need to call 900, which gives me pot odds of approximately 3.6 to 1. This requires about 21.7% equity to make a profitable call. Since I have a roughly 24% chance of hitting one of my outs on the turn alone, and significantly higher equity by the river, calling is a mathematically sound decision. Given our deep stacks (both around 200bb), calling also preserves the opportunity to realize implied odds if I hit my draw on the turn, while avoiding a high-variance confrontation on the flop against FiveTwo's strong 3-betting range.

On the turn, the 4 of hearts is a total brick that doesn't change the board texture. I still hold a powerful multi-way draw with 9 diamonds for a flush and 3 non-diamond Queens for a gutshot straight (12 outs total). As the out-of-position player who has been calling the aggressor's lead, the most standard and effective play is to check. This allows me to see how FiveTwo reacts to the turn. Given my equity, I am very likely calling any reasonable bet to see the river, but leading out here would likely only fold out hands I beat or get raised by better, bloating the pot while I'm still just on a draw.

Turn is a relative brick (4h) after we c-bet and got called. With AA on K-J-5 two-diamond, we’re not the nuts (any Kx is ahead), but Flash’s flop call still contains plenty of worse hands that can continue: Jx, QQ–TT, and a lot of diamond/gutshot holdings that we want to charge (we don’t block diamonds). I don’t want to polarize too hard and bloat the pot into a range with many Kx, so I’ll fire a controlled second barrel (~1/3 pot) to deny equity and get thin value from worse pairs/draws. If Flash check-raises big, we can comfortably fold this one-pair hand.

On a board of Kc Jd 5d 4h, I hold 9d Td, giving me a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (11 total outs: 8 remaining diamonds and 3 non-diamond Queens). The current pot is 5,550 and FiveTwo has bet 1,400, meaning I need to call 1,400 to win a total pot of 6,950. This gives me pot odds of approximately 20.1%. With 11 outs, my probability of hitting on the river is roughly 23.9%. Since my equity (23.9%) is higher than the pot odds (20.1%), calling is a mathematically profitable decision. Additionally, given the deep stacks and FiveTwo's aggressive line, I have significant implied odds to extract more value on the river if I complete my draw.

The river card is the King of Hearts, which pairs the King on the board. I have missed both my flush and my gutshot straight draw, leaving me with Ten-high. In this situation, I am the out-of-position player against the pre-flop 3-bettor (FiveTwo). FiveTwo has shown significant strength by 3-betting pre-flop and barreling both the flop and the turn. The pairing of the King on the river is a card that likely helps FiveTwo's range, as they could easily have hands like AK, KQ, or even KJ which have now improved to trips or a full house. Given that my hand has no showdown value and a bluff is unlikely to succeed against an opponent who has shown such consistent aggression and whose range is well-represented on this board, the most prudent action is to check. If FiveTwo bets, I will fold, as I cannot justify a call with only Ten-high.

River pairs the K, giving us KKAAJ (top two pair). Flash’s check-call line can still contain a lot of Jx and QQ–TT that will often pay off a small value bet, plus some missed diamond draws that fold. The problem hands are any Kx (now trips/full houses) which will continue and sometimes check-raise, so I don’t want to size big and value-own myself. Best play is a thin, controlled value bet that targets his bluff-catchers and keeps my loss small when he’s trapping with a king; if he check-raises, I’m folding.

On the river, the King of Hearts pairs the board. I have missed my flush draw and my gutshot straight draw, leaving me with only Ten-high. FiveTwo has been the aggressor for the entire hand, 3-betting pre-flop and betting on every street. Their small river bet of 1,700 into a pot of nearly 7,000 looks like a value bet designed to get paid off by a Jack or a middle pair. Given that I have zero showdown value and my opponent's range is heavily weighted towards strong made hands like AK, KQ, KJ, or even AJ, there is no justification for a call. Attempting a bluff-raise is also extremely risky given the board texture and the strength FiveTwo has shown. The most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my chip lead.

4-handed UTG plays like a cutoff, and AcKc is a top-tier value open that also blocks AA/KK/AK. With deep stacks vs Flash/Claude, I want to build a pot while keeping the SPR manageable and my sizing consistent with the table’s frequent 250–300 opens. A 2.5x open pressures the shorter Button stack (Pro) and still invites dominated Ax/Kx and suited broadways to continue, while leaving me well-positioned to call reasonable 3-bets or 4-bet selectively if needed.

Current stack is 56.25 BBs. The UTG player (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has been opening extremely wide (raising nearly every recent hand), which weakens their range significantly. However, 9h 6h is a speculative suited one-gapper. While having position on the Button is advantageous, cold-calling invites squeezes from the aggressive big stack in the Big Blind (Flash) or the Small Blind. Given the depth of my stack, I do not need to take marginal spots with easily dominated hands like 9-high. I can afford to wait for better opportunities to exploit the leader's width or pick up premium holdings. The risk of being squeezed or dominated post-flop outweighs the potential implied odds.
T7o in the SB facing UTG open. This is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Not worth calling or 3-betting - easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit. FiveTwo, who is the current chip leader and has been playing very aggressively, raises from UTG to 250. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting (150 to call into a pot of 400), J3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against a wide and aggressive range. Given that there are only about 22 hands left in the tournament and I have a healthy stack, there is no reason to risk chips with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with 9s 4h, which is a very weak hand (9-4 offsuit). With 4 players left and being the shortest stack (though still relatively healthy at ~56bb), there is no incentive to play this trash hand out of position. The standard play is to fold immediately and wait for a better opportunity.

Q5o on the button is a marginal hand. While I have position, this is a weak offsuit hand against two big stacks in the blinds who can easily call or 3-bet me. With 99BB effective, I don't need to take marginal spots. Folding and waiting for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with T3o. It has been folded to me. While I could attempt a steal, the Big Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has shown a high level of aggression in recent hands. T3o is far too weak to defend or play post-flop out of position. I will fold and wait for a stronger range to engage with the chip leader.
J2o is a weak offsuit hand with no playability. From UTG (earliest position), I need strong hands to open. This is an easy fold.

In a 4-handed game, the button opening range is typically wide, but Ten-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand. FiveTwo (SB) is the chip leader and has been playing very aggressively with frequent 3-bets, while Pro (BB) has a stack size of approximately 55 big blinds, which is a prime reshoving stack. Given the aggressive tendencies of the small blind and the risk of a shove from the big blind, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a more robust opening candidate to maintain my healthy stack as the tournament nears its conclusion.

Folded to us in the small blind with A♠K♠ against a short-stacked big blind (≈55bb). Completing gives away initiative and lets BB realize equity too easily. This is a clear value-raise and also sets up a low SPR pot where we can comfortably stack off on many flops if Pro shoves or continues. I’ll use a larger-than-min blind-vs-blind open to pressure the short stack and encourage mistakes (calls too wide or shove too light).

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 2s, facing a large 7x raise (700 chips) from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). While I have position post-flop, 7-2 suited is one of the weakest starting hands. The pot odds are poor (requiring ~43% equity to call), and playing a large pot with such a weak hand against the chip leader is not a winning strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack of 55 BBs for a better spot.

In a 4-handed game, UTG is effectively the Cutoff position. Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo) is a standard opening hand in this position. While FiveTwo (Button) has been playing very aggressively and holds the chip lead, folding JTo here would be overly tight. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300. This allows me to take control of the pot early. If I face a significant 3-bet from FiveTwo or a shove from the short-stacked Pro, I can evaluate based on the price, but JTo is generally a fold to a large re-raise out of position.

Flash opens UTG to 3bb, and while we have position on the Button, 54 offsuit is too weak to profitably continue versus an UTG range—especially with two blinds behind who can squeeze/jam (SB is short enough to do so). Calling creates dominated, low-realization situations and 3-bet bluffing this combo is unnecessary given stack preservation and better candidates. Best play is to fold and wait for stronger/cleaner spots.

I am in the Small Blind with [5c 8h], a very weak off-suit hand. The UTG player (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB). Facing an early position raise with a trash hand while out of position is a clear fold situation. My stack is 5,475, and defending here would be chip suicide. I have no pot odds or equity reason to continue.

J8o in BB facing UTG raise. Flash opened from UTG which represents a stronger range. J8o is too weak to defend profitably here - it plays poorly postflop and often makes second-best hands. With 20 hands left and a comfortable stack, no need to take marginal spots. Fold.

4-handed UTG still requires some discipline. 9c3d is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability (dominated top pairs, weak connectivity) and doesn’t benefit much from our stack leverage because we’ll often have to fold to 3-bets or play postflop out of position versus strong ranges. Preserving chips for better steal spots (button/SB) and stronger opens is higher EV.

I am on the Button with Ace-Three offsuit (A3o) in a 4-handed game. The player UTG has folded. This is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds.
