Although A3o is not a premium hand, having an Ace blocks opponents' strong hands, and position allows me to navigate post-flop easier if called. My stack is 5,475 (approx 55bb), which allows for this play without committing too much. I have previously opened to 250 with both strong hands (AK) and marginal hands (K7s), so using 250 again balances my range and disguises my hand strength. I plan to fold to a 3-bet given the hand's vulnerability.

KJs is a strong hand in the small blind facing a button open. Pro has a relatively short stack (5225) and this is a good spot to 3-bet for value and to isolate. I'll 3-bet to around 800 to put pressure on Pro and potentially take it down preflop or play a heads-up pot in position relative to the BB.

I am in the Big Blind with Td 6s. The Button (Pro) raised to 2.5x, and the Small Blind (Claude) 3-bet to 800. T6o is a very weak hand and does not play well against a raise and a 3-bet from players with relatively short stacks (Pro and Claude). Claude's 3-bet indicates a strong range, and calling here would put me out of position in a bloated pot with a poor hand. Folding is the only sensible play.

I opened from the Button with A3o, attempting to steal the blinds. The Small Blind (Claude) has 3-bet to 800. While I would have position post-flop, A3o is a weak hand that is often dominated by a 3-betting range (which typically includes stronger Aces and pairs). The pot odds are approximately 32%, but my equity against a reasonable 3-bet range is likely lower (around 28-30%), and the hand suffers from significant reverse implied odds if an Ace flops. As stated in my pre-flop plan, this hand is too vulnerable to defend against a re-raise. Preserving my 52BB stack for better spots is the prudent play.

I am in UTG position (4-handed) with Queen-Eight offsuit (Qd 8c). This is a weak hand that is easily dominated by many hands in opponents' calling or raising ranges (KQ, AQ, QJ, pairs). With a stack of roughly 52 big blinds, I am the shortest stack at the table but not in push-fold territory. Opening this hand out of position against deep-stacked aggressive opponents is not profitable. The standard play is to fold.

74o on the button is a very weak hand. While I have position, both blinds have large stacks and FiveTwo has shown aggression with 3-bets. The risk of getting 3-bet and having to fold outweighs the potential blind steal. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 53-offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands. The table has folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind, FiveTwo, who is the current chip leader with a substantial stack. While I could attempt a steal or complete the bet to see a flop, 53-offsuit performs very poorly out of position, and FiveTwo has shown a tendency to be aggressive. Given we are at hand 133 of 150 and I have a healthy second-place stack, there is no need to risk chips with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

T2 suited is a weak hand, especially from UTG. With 16 hands remaining and a healthy stack of ~101bb, I should maintain discipline and fold this marginal hand rather than open with a weak offsuit/barely-suited holding from early position.

I am on the button with J8o. While this is a marginal opening hand in 4-handed play, I have to consider the players behind me. FiveTwo in the small blind is the chip leader and has shown significant aggression, frequently 3-betting or raising large. Pro in the big blind has a shorter stack (approx. 51 BBs) and may be looking for opportunities to double up. Given that we are at hand 134 of 150, I want to preserve my solid second-place stack and avoid getting into an inflated pot with a weak hand against an aggressive big stack. Folding is the safest play here to wait for a better spot.

Blind-vs-blind and folded to me: I should be opening very wide from the small blind. Even with 32o, a min-raise risks only 150 more to win the 150 already in the pot, so it’s immediately profitable if Pro folds more than ~50% of the time. Given Pro’s generally tight/passive tendencies in prior hands, I expect enough folds and I also keep the raise size small to limit loss if they 3-bet/shove (which I will fold to with this hand).

I am in the Big Blind facing a minimum raise from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). The pot odds are extremely favorable: I need to call 100 chips to win a pot of 300, requiring only 25% equity. My hand, K6o, performs well enough against a typically wide Small Blind opening range to justify a call, as I likely have around 45-50% equity. Folding would be too tight given the price and position advantage post-flop. A 3-bet is unnecessary and turns a hand with showdown value into a bluff. Therefore, calling is the most profitable standard play.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on A♦-5♦-2♣, I have a big range advantage (I contain lots of Ax) and Pro’s BB defend range has a lot of whiffed broadways/overcards that will fold to a small c-bet. With bottom pair (2♠3♣) I also benefit from denying equity to hands like KQ/QJ and charging diamond floats, while keeping the pot manageable. Versus Pro’s generally tight/passive profile and shorter stack, I’ll c-bet small and comfortably fold if they raise/shove.

I am holding King-six offsuit on an Ace-high board (A-5-2). The Small Blind (FiveTwo), who is the aggressive chip leader, c-bets small (150 into 400). While the price is enticing (getting 3.6:1 odds) and I have position, the Ace on the board interacts heavily with the opponent's opening and c-betting range. My hand has very low showdown value, only a weak runner-runner flush draw, and reverse implied odds if I hit a King. With K6o being at the bottom of my range and having plenty of better hands to defend with (Ax, pairs, straight draws, flush draws), folding is the disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) is a strong starting hand, particularly from the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position which functions similarly to the Cutoff in full-ring games. My stack is healthy at nearly 195 BBs. Standard strategy suggests opening with a raise to take control of the pot and narrow the field. A 3x big blind open (300) is standard for this table dynamic.

4-handed, UTG opens are meaningfully wider than full-ring, and we have position plus a strong blocker (K) with a hand that plays reasonably postflop but can be dominated if we just flat and go multiway. As chip leader, a small 3-bet pressures Flash’s open and discourages the blinds (especially with Pro shorter) from coming along, letting us win the pot preflop often or play an in-position, initiative pot when called. If Flash 4-bets big, KTo is an easy fold; otherwise we can barrel favorable boards and realize our equity well in position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Jack offsuit (KJo). The action involves an UTG open to 300 followed by a 3-bet from the Button (FiveTwo) to 900. KJo is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated in 3-bet pots, especially against an UTG opening range and a Button 3-bet. Playing this hand out of position against two players, one of whom has a massive chip lead, is risky. Additionally, the original raiser (Flash) is still to act and could 4-bet. Calling would commit nearly 20% of my remaining stack in a very unfavorable spot. The prudent play is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K8o is a marginal hand facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo. Even with position, K8o is dominated by most 3-betting ranges (AK, KQ, KJ, better kings). Need about 37% equity to call 800 into a pot of 2150, which K8o won't have against a typical 3-bet range. Clear fold.

Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) is a reasonable hand to open 4-handed, but it performs poorly against a 3-bet when out of position. The opponent, FiveTwo, is the chip leader and has been extremely active and aggressive, often putting players in difficult situations. While the price to call 600 more into a pot of 1350 is theoretically acceptable, the lack of position and the high probability of being dominated or outplayed on future streets makes calling unattractive. A 4-bet bluff is also risky against an opponent who has shown a willingness to play back. I will fold to preserve my stack for more advantageous situations in the remaining 15 hands.

4-handed from UTG we can open fairly wide, but 3-2 offsuit is at the absolute bottom of the range: very poor playability, dominated when it pairs, and no real straight/flush equity. With three players left to act (including the BB) and no reason to spew chips near the end of the tournament, folding is the highest-EV option.

Playing 4-handed on the Button. K7o is a marginal hand, often an open-raise candidate in late position to steal the blinds. However, looking at recent history (Hand #132), the Small Blind (Claude) 3-bet my Button open, forcing me to fold. Given that I am currently the short stack (approx 50BB compared to 100BB+ opponents) and K7o plays poorly against a 3-bet or a calling range (often dominated by better Kings), and lacking the suited potential of Hand #96 where I successfully raised K7s, discretion is the better part of valor here. Tightening up slightly to avoid bleeding chips into aggressive blinds is a sound adjustment.

T2o is a very weak hand. Against Flash in the BB who has a large stack, completing would put me out of position with a dominated hand. Folding is clearly correct here - no need to invest more chips with such poor holdings.

I am in the UTG position (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with J7 offsuit. This is a very weak hand that falls well outside of a standard opening range, even in a short-handed game. With 50 BBs and being the shortest stack with only 13 hands remaining, I should preserve my chips for better opportunities rather than opening with a speculative and easily dominated hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

8s2s is a very weak hand, even on the button. While I have position, this hand has minimal playability - it's one of the weakest suited combinations. Both blinds have large stacks and FiveTwo in particular has shown aggression with 3-bets. The risk/reward of trying to steal here with such a weak hand isn't favorable. Folding and waiting for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Ten suited (Qd Td) and the action has folded to me. This is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. FiveTwo is the chip leader and has shown aggression, but I cannot play passively with a premium Broadway hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and apply pressure.

Flash is opening from the small blind into my big blind with a likely wide range. Getting 200 to win a pot that will be 600 after I call means I need ~33% equity; 5d3d has reasonable equity versus a wide SB range and plays well with deep stacks due to implied odds when we make straights/flushes. A 3-bet bluff here is possible but this hand is too low to realize fold equity reliably and prefers seeing flops cheaply. I’ll defend and keep the pot controlled out of position.

On this flop of 9-6-8, I have an open-ended straight draw (a 7 or a Jack will complete my straight) along with two overcards (Queen and Ten). This is an incredibly strong semi-bluffing opportunity. As the pre-flop aggressor, I want to maintain the initiative and put pressure on FiveTwo, who may have missed the board or is holding a weak pair. A standard continuation bet of two-thirds the pot is appropriate here to build the pot in case I hit my draw and to potentially win the hand immediately.

Heads-up SB vs BB on 9c-6h-8d, Flash’s small c-bet (400 into 1000) is likely made with a very wide range. This board is much better for my BB defend range (lots of 9x/8x/6x, two-pair, sets, and straight draws like T7/97/87), so I can credibly apply pressure. My hand (5d3d) is weak at showdown but has some backup: a gutshot to the 7 plus backdoor diamonds. Raising as a semi-bluff leverages my chip lead, targets his overcards/air, and sets up profitable barrels on good turns (7, diamond, or high cards) while allowing me to shut down on bad runouts if he continues strongly.

On a flop of 9-6-8, I have an open-ended straight draw with my Queen-Ten of diamonds, and two overcards. FiveTwo has raised my continuation bet to 1300. The current pot is 2300 and it costs me 900 to call. This gives me pot odds of approximately 28% (900/3200). With 8 outs to a straight, my equity to hit on the turn is about 17%, but my total equity by the river is roughly 32%. Given FiveTwo's aggressive nature and the deep stacks, I have significant implied odds if I hit my straight, as he is likely to continue betting or call a large bet from me. Folding would be too tight given the strength of the draw, and re-raising would likely commit me to a massive pot where I am currently behind. Calling is the most prudent play to see the turn and attempt to realize my equity.

On a board of 9-6-8-7, I have turned a Ten-high straight (6-7-8-9-T) with my Queen-Ten of diamonds. However, this is an extremely coordinated board texture. Any Jack-Ten makes a higher straight (Jack-high), and any 5 makes a lower straight (9-high). Since FiveTwo raised me on the flop, he has shown significant strength, which could include sets, two pairs, or straight draws that have now completed. Leading out here might force out his bluffs while getting me into trouble against a higher straight. By checking, I can control the size of the pot, keep his bluffs in, and evaluate his reaction to this dangerous turn card. If he bets, I plan to call to see the river, as my hand is too strong to fold but potentially second-best to the nut straight.

Turn brings the 7c, giving me a made 9-high straight (9-8-7-6-5). After I raised flop and Flash just called, his check on this dynamic turn often caps him to a lot of 9x/8x, overpairs, and pair+draw type hands that can pay off. I want to bet for value while also charging any hands drawing to a higher straight (Tx) or to a river boat with two pair/sets. That said, this is the bottom end of the straight on a 4-to-a-straight board, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with an overbet; a solid mid-size bet gets called by worse frequently and lets me comfortably fold if he comes back with a big raise that is heavily weighted to a higher straight/boat.

On this board of 9-6-8-7, I have successfully turned a Ten-high straight (6-7-8-9-T). However, the board is extremely coordinated; any Jack in FiveTwo's hand (J-T, J-9, J-8, J-Q, etc.) makes a Jack-high straight (7-8-9-T-J), which beats me. FiveTwo's raise on the flop (9-6-8) indicated a very strong hand, likely a set, two pair, or a high-equity straight draw like J-T or J-7. While I have a very strong hand, the 4-to-a-straight texture makes it vulnerable. Raising here would likely only be called or re-raised by a hand that has me beaten (a Jack) or at best another Ten for a split pot. By calling, I maintain pot control, keep his potential bluffs or lower straights/two-pairs in the hand, and can evaluate the river card. I am getting roughly 2.6:1 on a call (2000 to win a total pot of 7200), which is sufficient to continue given the strength of my hand, but I must remain cautious of the straight hierarchy.

On the river, the board is 9-6-8-7-A. I hold a Ten-high straight (6-7-8-9-T). While this is a very strong hand, the board texture is highly coordinated, and any Jack in FiveTwo's hand completes a higher Jack-high straight (7-8-9-T-J). FiveTwo's aggression on the flop and turn (raising my c-bet and then betting 2000) is consistent with a very strong range, including sets, two-pair, or the nut straight (J-T). Since I am out of position, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to evaluate FiveTwo's reaction; if he checks behind, I can safely win the pot against his sets or lower straights. If he bets, I can decide whether to call based on the sizing, but leading out myself risks a raise from the superior straight and doesn't extract much value from hands I already beat that might check behind.

River A♣ doesn’t change my hand strength (still 9-high straight), but it’s a scare card that will reduce the frequency Flash turns marginal made hands into bluffs and also makes this a 4-to-a-straight board where a raise is heavily weighted to Tx. After he check/calls flop raise + turn barrel, his range contains lots of 9x/two-pair/sets that will often check-call a reasonable value bet, while Tx is the main hand that beats me. I want to extract value from the many worse one-pair/two-pair hands without bloating the pot or inducing a big check-raise spot, so I’ll use a smaller value sizing and comfortably fold to a large raise.

On the river, the board reads 9c-6h-8d-7c-Ac. I have a Ten-high straight (6-7-8-9-T). FiveTwo has bet 2600 into a pot of 7200. I only lose to a Jack in his hand, which would make a Jack-high straight (7-8-9-T-J). While his aggression throughout the hand (raising the flop and betting the turn) suggests a very strong range, my Ten-high straight is still ahead of many value hands he might play this way, such as sets (99, 88, 77, 66) or two-pair combinations. Given the pot odds of roughly 3.7:1 (2600 to win 12400 total), I need to be right only about 21% of the time to justify a call. Folding a straight to a small bet on a board with no flush or full house possibilities would be overly tight and exploitable. Raising is too risky as it only gets called by the nut straight (a Jack). I will call to see the showdown.

3s5d offsuit from UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest starting hands - no high cards, not suited, poor connectivity. With 3 players behind me and 100BB deep, there's no value in playing this hand.

I am on the button with A5o. It has been folded to me, making this a standard opening situation. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a consistent play that allows me to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position. Pro in the big blind is relatively short compared to the rest of the table but still has nearly 50 big blinds, so I don't need to worry about an immediate desperation shove just yet.

Facing a 3x button open from the chip leader, J7o is too dominated to defend profitably from the small blind. Calling puts us out of position in an inflated pot with weak playability, and 3-bet bluffing is better done with suited/connectors or stronger blockers. With 4 players and us sitting second in chips, preserving stack and avoiding marginal high-variance spots versus Flash is best.

I am in the Big Blind with K7o facing a 3x raise (300) from the chip leader on the Button. While I have 48 BBs, I am the shortest stack at the table. K7o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position and is frequently dominated by the opener's calling or raising range (K9+, KT, KJ, KQ). Against a 3x sizing, the price is not attractive enough to defend considering the difficulty of realizing equity post-flop. I folded a similar hand (K6o) to a c-bet recently after defending. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am currently the chip leader with 25,575 chips. Hand #139 is nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 150). My current hand, 9c 4c, is a very weak starting hand to open from Under the Gun (UTG), even in a 4-handed game. Opening with such a wide range from early position can lead to unnecessary chip loss against three opponents yet to act, especially since I want to protect my lead. Folding is the most prudent play here.

4-handed on the button after UTG folds, this is a prime steal spot. A7o is comfortably ahead of the blinds’ random holdings and plays well in position with an ace blocker that reduces the chance of facing strong Ax continues. With our big stack relative to both blinds, we can apply pressure and profit from frequent folds while still being able to navigate postflop in position when called. I’ll use a standard small open size to risk little while keeping fold equity high.

I hold 8h 4s in the Small Blind, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo). 8-4 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability. Being out of position against an aggressive Button raise makes this a clear fold. I need to preserve my stack of roughly 48 big blinds for better opportunities, especially with only 11 hands remaining in the tournament.

K8o in the BB facing a button open to 250. Getting ~2.7:1 pot odds to call 150 more. K8o is marginal but playable in a heads-up pot with position closing the action. FiveTwo has been active. Calling to see a flop seems reasonable given the price.

Completely whiffed this flop with K8o - no pair, no draw. Board is 6h 2h Jd. I have king high with no real equity. Best play is to check and fold to any bet. No point bluffing into an active player who could easily have connected with this board or have overcards that are ahead of me.

We have the betting lead in position and this flop (J-6-2 with two hearts) is a reasonable range c-bet spot: Claude’s BB defending range contains plenty of unpaired overcards and weak holdings that will fold to a small bet, and we can deny equity while keeping the pot controlled with just A-high. Our hand also has some backdoor potential (runner-runner hearts, some turn cards that let us improve or barrel). I’ll use a small sizing to risk little and keep his range wide while still generating folds.

Completely missed this flop with K8o - no pair, no draws (no hearts). King high has essentially no equity against FiveTwo's continuing range. The 200 bet into 550 is small but I have no reason to continue with air and no backdoor draws. Easy fold.

4-handed UTG is wider than full ring, but 9c3c is still near the bottom of playable opens, especially when we’ll be out of position to the button and both blinds. With deep stacks against Flash and Claude, this hand realizes equity poorly (dominated 9x, bad reverse implied odds) and doesn’t need to be in our range given we’re healthy and near the end of the set. Preserve stack and avoid marginal OOP spots.

I am on the Button with Ace-Four suited (A4s) in a 4-handed game. With 48 big blinds, this is a standard spot to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. A4s has good playability post-flop and blocks opponents' Ace-holdings. My standard opening size in this tournament has been 2.5bb (250), which balances applying pressure while not committing too many chips if I face a 3-bet. Folding is too passive given the position and hand strength.

K2 suited in SB facing a button raise. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400). K2s has playability with flush potential and could hit top pair. Button raises are often wide, so defending here seems reasonable. With 10 hands left and a solid stack, I can afford to see a flop.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant margin as the tournament nears its end (hand 140 of 150). In this hand, the Button (Pro) raised and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I am in the Big Blind getting 4:1 pot odds (150 to call into a 600 pot). While Ten-Six offsuit is a weak hand, the price to close the action and see a flop is mathematically justified given my stack depth. I intend to play cautiously post-flop, likely adopting a 'fit-or-fold' strategy to protect my lead.

Flopped bottom pair (deuces) with king kicker on J92 board with two diamonds. This is a marginal made hand - I have some showdown value but it's vulnerable. Two opponents remain. Checking to control pot size makes sense here. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a tough spot. Let me see how the hand develops.

I have no connection to the Jc 2d 9d flop with my Ten-Six offsuit. As the tournament is nearly over and I have a substantial chip lead, there is no reason to bluff into two opponents in a multi-way pot. I will check to see what the original raiser (Pro) does and likely fold if there is significant betting action.

I am in position (Button) in a multi-way pot (3 players). The flop comes Jc 2d 9d. Both opponents (SB and BB) checked to me. My hand is Ah 4h (Ace-high). I have no direct draws (no flush draw, no straight draw), just an overcard and very weak backdoor potential. The board texture interacts reasonably well with defending ranges (broadways, flush draws, pairs of Jacks or Nines). C-betting into two opponents with very little equity is risky, as getting two folds is unlikely and I don't want to build a pot with a weak hand. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (hitting an Ace or seeing a cheap showdown) and keeps the pot control under management. I will check back.

Turn paired the 9s. My hand is now two pair (9s and 2s) but this is effectively bottom pair with the board pair. Anyone with a Jack has me beat with Jacks over 9s, and anyone with a 9 has trips. Given both opponents checked the flop, they might not have strong hands, but I shouldn't be betting here. Check for pot control and to see a free river if possible.

The turn 9h pairs the board (Jc 2d 9d 9h). I have no pair and no meaningful draw with my 10-high. As the chip leader with only 11 hands remaining in the tournament, I should avoid unnecessary risks. Since the Small Blind checked to me, I will check as well to see a free river card and re-evaluate, or fold if a bet occurs later on this street.

I hold Ah4h on a board of Jc 2d 9d 9h. I have Ace-high with no straight or flush draws (only one heart on board). The action checked to me on the flop and now checked to me again on the turn. My Ace-high has some showdown value against missed draws (diamonds) or worse high cards. Betting turns my hand into a bluff and risks reopening the action or getting called by pairs that beat me. Since the pot is small and I am the shortest stack, checking back to realize my equity and try to win at showdown or hit an Ace on the river is the most prudent play. There is little value in betting as worse hands won't call and better hands (pairs) likely won't fold.

River brought the King, giving me Kings and 9s two pair. This is a strong hand now. Everyone checked flop and turn, indicating weakness. I should value bet to get called by Jx hands, small pairs, or weaker holdings. A ~half pot bet of around 400 seems appropriate to extract value while not scaring off worse hands.

I am the chip leader with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament. On the river (Kd), the board reads Jc 2d 9d 9h Kd. I hold 10-high (Tc 6d), which has no showdown value whatsoever. Claude (Small Blind) has led out for 400 into a 750 pot. Even if Claude is bluffing with a missed diamond draw, I still lose to any pair or even most Ace-high hands. Furthermore, Pro (the original pre-flop raiser) is still to act behind me. There is no strategic reason to risk chips or attempt a bluff in this spot given my dominant stack and the proximity to the end of the tournament. I will fold and maintain my lead.

Pre-flop, I raised with A4s and got called by both blinds. The flop came J-2-9 with two diamonds, and action checked around. The turn brought a second 9, pairing the board, and again it checked around. The river is the King of diamonds, completing the flush draw and putting a second overcard to the Jack on board. Claude (SB) leads out for 400 into a pot of 750. My Ace-high (Ah4h) has very little showdown value here. Claude's range likely connects with the King, the 9, or the flush. While he could be bluffing with a missed straight draw, the board texture heavily favors his range, and he is betting into two opponents (though one folded). Getting odds of nearly 3:1 isn't enough to justify a call with just Ace-high on this wet board. Preserving my stack of ~45bb is more important than a thin hero call.

I am in UTG with a stack of roughly 46 big blinds (4575 chips). There are 4 players left and 10 hands remaining. My hand is A2o (Ace of Diamonds, Deuce of Spades). In a 4-handed game, UTG is equivalent to the Cutoff. While an Ace has some showdown value, A2o is the weakest off-suit Ace. Opening this hand puts me in a difficult spot out of position against the Button and the blinds, often dominated by better Aces. Given my status as the short stack, I want to preserve chips for higher equity spots or positions where I have leverage (like the Button or Blind vs Blind). The risk of being 3-bet or calling and playing a dominated pot out of position outweighs the stealing potential here. Folding is the prudent play.

A6o on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a standard open from the button - the ace provides decent equity and I have position. With 10 hands remaining, I should continue playing my standard game. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB).

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven suited, a strong hand in 4-max play. Claude's button raise is likely quite wide, and as the chip leader, I want to maintain aggression and apply pressure. 3-betting here allows me to take control of the hand, potentially win the pot immediately, or isolate the button while playing a range that has good post-flop equity and playability.

We’re facing a raise (250) and a 3-bet to 850 with Ts3o from the big blind, out of position, and with no relevant blockers (no A/K/Q to reduce premium holdings). Even getting a decent price, this hand realizes equity terribly in a 3-bet pot OOP and is often dominated when we make a pair (T-x). A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive because Ts3o has poor playability and minimal blocker value versus ranges that will continue. Preserve stack and avoid a low-EV defend spot.

Flash 3-bet me from the SB after my button open. A6o is too weak to continue against a 3-bet, even with position. I'd need to call 600 into a 1200 pot, but A6o performs poorly against typical 3-bet ranges. With 10 hands left and a healthy 100BB stack, I should fold and wait for a better spot rather than hemorrhage chips with marginal hands.

J9o is a marginal hand from UTG, but in a 4-handed game it's playable. I'll make a standard open raise to 250. With 100BB stack and only 3 opponents, this is a reasonable spot to open with a suited broadway connector-adjacent hand.

I am currently holding 5-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude has opened the action with a raise from UTG in a 4-handed game. Given that I have a significant chip lead and there are only a few hands left in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to defend with such a weak hand or attempt a bluff against an established raise. Folding is the most prudent play to protect my stack.

Claude is opening from UTG (4-handed) to 2.5bb and we’ll be out of position for the rest of the hand. J4o has poor playability, is frequently dominated when we make a jack, and doesn’t have the blockers/suitedness needed to justify a 3-bet bluff from the small blind versus a reasonably strong opening range. With plenty of chips, there’s no need to defend this marginally negative spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 3s 6h, a very weak hand. Claude raised from UTG to 250. The pot is 400 (250 + 50 SB + 100 BB), and I need to call 150. While the pot odds are roughly 2.66:1, defending 63o out of position against an UTG range is a losing play, especially as the short stack (4475 chips) with only 8 hands remaining in the tournament. I must preserve my stack for better opportunities to shove or defend.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant stack. With only a few hands left in the tournament (Hand 143 of 150), there is no reason to take unnecessary risks. Holding Eight of Spades and Six of Diamonds in the UTG position is extremely weak. Opening with such a hand from early position, even in a four-handed game, is generally unprofitable and invites trouble from the remaining players, especially with a short stack like Pro potentially looking to shove. I will fold and wait for a better spot to maintain my lead.

Four-handed and on the button, picking up the blinds is a big part of stack accumulation. Even with a weak offsuit hand, I can profitably open very wide because both blinds have to defend often enough, and I can simply fold to any 3-bet/shove without putting in much. I’ll use the minimum raise to keep risk low while still applying pressure/leveraging position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind holding J5 offsuit, a very weak hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has min-raised to 200. Although the price to call is cheap (150 more), I will be out of position against an aggressive stack with a hand that has poor playability and is easily dominated. Additionally, the Big Blind is still to act and could squeeze. Given the late stage of the tournament (Hand 143/150) and my stack size (44 BB), preserving chips is better than bleeding them in marginal spots out of position. Folding is the standard play.

AJo is a strong hand in the big blind facing a min-raise from the button. FiveTwo's button opening range is wide, and AJo plays well as a 3-bet for value. I should raise to around 3-3.5x to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop. Going to 700.

We opened ultra-wide on the button to pick up blinds, but once Claude 3-bets from the big blind his range becomes much stronger and more condensed. T2o has very poor raw equity versus any reasonable 3-bet range, is heavily dominated when it makes a pair, and has almost no suited/straight potential to realize equity even with position. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive this deep because it can easily induce a jam from his value range, and we don’t block anything meaningful. Best play is to abandon the steal attempt and preserve stack.

4-handed we can and should open wider, but 96o is still below the cutoff for first-to-act ranges: it’s dominated when it makes a pair, has poor playability out of position, and invites tough spots versus 3-bets/short-stack jams (Pro on the button). With no need to force marginal EV in the last few hands, I preserve stack and wait for better open/defend candidates.

With 4 players remaining, the Button opening range should be quite wide (typically 50%+). A4o falls well within a standard stealing range from the Button in an unopened pot. It holds an Ace blocker and plays reasonably well in position if called. My stack is 44 BBs, allowing for a standard raise without committing too much. I will raise to 2.5x BB to put pressure on the blinds.

J6 suited in the SB facing a button raise. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play well out of position. While suited, J6 lacks connectivity and will be difficult to play profitably post-flop against the raiser when I'm OOP. With Flash still to act behind me who could squeeze, and the hand's poor playability, folding is the disciplined play here.

I am the chip leader with pocket sevens in the Big Blind. The Button (Pro), who is the short stack with about 41BB, has opened for 2.5x. Pocket sevens is a strong hand heads-up against a wide Button opening range. While I will be out of position post-flop, I prefer a 3-bet here to take initiative and put pressure on the short stack. A raise to 750 is a standard 3-bet size that allows me to define the hand. If Pro pushes all-in, I can evaluate based on pot odds, but for now, I want to see if I can win the pot immediately or narrow Pro's range.

I opened with A4o from the Button as a steal attempt, but the Big Blind (chip leader) has 3-bet to 750. Although I am in position, A4o is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by the 3-bettor's calling range (better Aces, pairs). As the short stack with only 4175 chips and 6 hands remaining in the tournament, preserving my stack is prioritized over playing a bloated pot with a weak Ace. The pot odds of ~32% are not compelling enough given the reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and prudent response to the 3-bet.

I am in UTG position (4-handed) with JTs, a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop. With a stack of 41.75 BB, I am the short stack and need to be active to accumulate chips, especially as we approach the end of the 150-hand session. Opening from UTG 4-handed is effectively opening from the Cutoff, and JTs is a standard open in this spot. The Small Blind (Flash) has been 3-betting aggressively recently, so I will use a standard open size of 2.5x (250) to balance my range and allow me to evaluate based on opponent action.

6s7h offsuit on the button facing a UTG raise. This is a weak hand - not even suited. While I have position, 67o is at the bottom of playable hands against an UTG open. With only 6 hands left in the tournament and a decent stack, no need to get involved with marginal holdings. Fold is the disciplined play.

I am the chip leader with a significant margin as we approach the final hands of the tournament. In this hand, Pro (the short stack) has opened for 250. Jd8d is a strong speculative hand that plays well post-flop and is definitely worth defending for a price of 200 more, especially in a 4-handed game. While a 3-bet worked in the previous hand against the same opponent, doing it again with a marginal hand might provoke a shove from Pro's 3.9k stack, which I'd rather not call. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply, maintain my lead, and potentially outplay the opponents post-flop without committing a large portion of my stack unnecessarily.

We’re getting an excellent price in the BB: calling 150 more to play a 3-way pot with 600 already out there (we’ll be in for 250 total). J6s has decent realization via flush/straight potential and can win big vs Pro’s short stack when we make strong hands. A squeeze here would mostly bloat the pot out of position with a dominated, marginal suited hand and invite calls from Flash/Pro. Best is to defend and see a flop.

The flop is Ace-high, which likely connects with the pre-flop raiser's (Pro) range. I have Jack-high with no draws on this board. In a three-way pot, betting into two opponents on an Ace-high board without equity is ill-advised. Checking allows me to see a turn for free if the others also check or to fold easily if a bet is made, preserving my significant stack lead as we approach the end of the tournament.

Multiway and out of position on A♣7♣3♠ with J♣6♣ (non-nut flush draw). Donk-betting risks getting jammed on by Pro’s short stack, which would put us in an awkward spot (our J-high flush draw often lacks the equity to call off comfortably and can be dominated by higher club draws). By checking, we keep the pot manageable, allow Pro to c-bet his range (so we can realize equity via a call with good price), and we also sometimes get a free turn to improve.

I opened pre-flop with JTs and got two callers (SB and BB). The flop comes 3s 7c Ac. While this board favors my range as the pre-flop raiser (containing strong Aces like AK/AQ which the blinds likely 3-bet), playing a multi-way pot with just Jack-high and no immediate draws is precarious. Betting into two opponents, especially with the chip leader in the SB who has been aggressive, risks getting called or raised when I have very little equity to fall back on. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a turn card which might improve my hand (backdoor straight or pair), and controls the size of the pot given my short stack status in the late stages of the tournament.

I currently hold Jack-high with no diamonds or clubs on a board featuring an Ace and three clubs. After the flop checked around, the turn 5 of clubs completes several potential draws. Since I have zero equity and two opponents behind me, including the short-stacked pre-flop raiser, checking is the most prudent move. My objective is to maintain my substantial chip lead as the tournament concludes in five hands, so I will not risk chips on a complete air hand in a multi-way pot.

We just made a flush on the 5♣, but it’s a very weak one (J♣6♣) on a 3-club board where most single-club holdings that continue (K♣/Q♣/T♣/9♣/8♣) have us crushed. Leading builds a pot in a spot where we’re frequently dominated and also invites getting raised (especially by Flash deep) and forced into tough, expensive decisions. By checking, we keep the pot controlled, let Pro (last to act) stab with Ax/air or value-bet his better clubs, and we get to respond with more information (seeing whether Flash calls/raises) before committing chips. If it checks through, we can still value-bet many rivers; if facing big heat multiway, we can make disciplined folds with this non-nut flush.

I am in position on the turn with Jack-Ten offsuit (hearts) on a board of 3s 7c Ac 5c. The pot is 750 and both opponents (SB and BB) have checked to me again. The board is now very coordinated with three clubs and an Ace. I have no pair, no flush draw (I hold hearts), and no straight draw. While a bet might fold out some weak pairs or non-club hands, I have zero equity if called and am drawing dead against any flush. As the short stack with only 5 hands remaining in the tournament session, preserving my 39 BB stack is more important than attempting a low-percentage bluff into two opponents on a dangerous board. Checking back gives me a free look at the river or allows me to get to showdown cheaply if opponents are also weak, though I likely fold to any river aggression.

We have reached the river on a board of 3s-7c-Ac-5c-9h with Jd-8d (Jack-high). The action has checked around on both the flop and the turn. In a three-way pot with an Ace and three clubs on the board, my Jack-high has virtually no showdown value. However, betting into two opponents who have also checked twice is risky; while they have shown weakness, any small pair or a slow-played Ace will call a bluff. Given that I am the massive chip leader and there are only 5 hands remaining in the tournament (hand 145 of 150), my primary goal is to minimize unnecessary risk. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown, but I am prepared to fold to any significant bet from my opponents.

River goes check to us in a 3-way pot. We have a made flush (J♣6♣) but it’s a low, non-nut flush on a 3-club board, so we don’t want to polarize or face a big bet from Pro (short) or a check-raise from Flash (deep) for a large price. Checking back would often miss value because most opponents won’t bluff enough here, but a small block/value bet can get called by worse one-club hands (T♣/8♣/4♣/2♣ type holdings) and occasionally by stubborn Ax, while letting us comfortably fold if we face significant aggression (which will be heavily weighted to higher flushes).

It is Hand 145/150 and I am the short stack with 3925 chips. I hold JhTh on a board of 3s 7c Ac 5c 9h. Action went check-check around on the flop and turn. On the river, the Big Blind (FiveTwo) leads out for 350 into a pot of 750. I have only Jack-high, which loses to any pair, any Ace, and any flush. The small bet size often indicates a thin value bet (like a small pair) or a blocker bet rather than a polarized bluff, making it unlikely I am ahead. With no showdown value and two opponents in the hand (SB checked, but is still present), calling is a losing play. I fold to preserve my stack for the final few hands.

I have Jack-high on a board of 3s-7c-Ac-5c-9h. The action checked around on both the flop and the turn, indicating weakness from all players. On the river, FiveTwo has bet 350 into a 750 pot. While this could be a 'steal' bet designed to take down a small pot that nobody seems to want, my Jack-high has virtually no showdown value; I lose to any pair, any Ace, any club, and even most other non-pairing hands like King-high or Queen-high. Given that I am the tournament leader by a significant margin and there are only five hands remaining, I have no reason to make a speculative call here. I will fold to preserve my stack and protect my lead.

K5o from UTG is a clear fold. This hand is too weak to open from early position - it will often be dominated by better kings. With 5 hands left and a healthy 103BB stack, no need to take marginal spots with weak offsuit holdings.

I am on the button with As Jc, which is a very strong hand for an opening range in a 4-handed game. Claude has already folded from UTG. With only five hands remaining in the tournament and being the chip leader, I want to continue applying pressure while playing fundamentally sound poker. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds, or play a post-flop game with a strong range advantage against the blinds.

With 4 players and button opening wide, AKo is well ahead of Flash’s range and wants to build a pot. Flat-calling from the SB also invites the short-stacked BB to come along cheaply and realizes equity out of position. I’ll 3-bet for value and isolation with a sizing that pressures the BB and keeps Flash’s worse hands in.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 4h, a very weak hand. Facing a button raise to 300 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1400, I am getting poor odds to call with a hand that likely has very low equity against the ranges involved. The cost to call (1300) is a significant portion of my remaining stack (3825), and there is no strategic merit in defending here. I will fold and preserve my chips.

I opened to 300 with AJo on the button, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 1400 from the small blind. FiveTwo has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament and frequently 3-bets my opens. AJ is well ahead of a wide 3-betting range and performs decently even against a tighter one. Given that there are only 5 hands left and I am the chip leader, I want to avoid high-variance pre-flop confrontations like a 4-bet jam unless I am certain I am ahead. Calling the additional 1100 gives me the opportunity to play a post-flop game with position and evaluate the board. The pot odds are favorable, and my hand is too strong to fold to a player this active.

3-bet pot heads-up OOP. This flop (Q-9-7 with a spade draw) connects with some of Flash’s flatting range, but I still retain a meaningful range advantage from the SB 3-bet (overpairs, AQ/KQ, strong Broadway/spade combos). With AKo I have 6 clean outs to top pair and I benefit from fold equity versus hands like small pairs, A-high floats, and some suited connectors that whiff. I’ll c-bet small to deny equity and keep my range uncapped while keeping the pot manageable; if raised, I’ll generally fold, and if called I can continue on A/K turns and selectively barrel good scare cards.

I am holding Ace-high with the Ace of spades, which serves as a crucial blocker on this two-spade board (7s Qd 9s). FiveTwo has led out for 900 into a pot that was 2900, offering me nearly 4.2-to-1 odds to call. Given my position, the strength of my overcards, and the backdoor nut flush potential with the As, folding is too conservative against an aggressive opponent. Calling allows me to see a turn card and potentially realize my equity or outplay the opponent if they check on a later street. I will call the 900 bet.

Turn brings the 8h, which meaningfully improves Flash’s flop-calling range (JT gets there, 98/87 make two pair, T9 picks up more equity) while my exact hand (AcKh) still has only A/K overcard equity and no spade blocker to reduce the likelihood of strong continues. In this dynamic texture, a second barrel will get called too often by Qx, draws, and newly-improved hands, and risks turning my hand into a low-EV bet/fold. Checking keeps the pot controlled, allows me to realize my overcard equity, and lets me respond based on Flash’s sizing (generally folding to meaningful pressure).

The turn 8h is a highly coordinated card that completes several possible straights, specifically any hand containing a Ten (like JT, which is a plausible 3-betting hand for FiveTwo). After FiveTwo checked, I have the option to bet or check behind. Given that I have Ace-high with the nut flush blocker (As) and a straight draw (a Ten completes a straight for me), checking back is the most strategic move. It allows me to realize my equity for free, avoids a potential check-raise on a scary board, and maintains my chip lead as we approach the final hands of the tournament. There is no need to turn my hand into a bluff here when I have decent showdown value and a strong draw.

River is a total brick (2h) after we c-bet flop and checked back turn. With AKo we have pure ace-high: we do beat his missed spade draws and some floated A-highs that gave up on the turn, but we lose to any Qx/9x/pair/two-pair/straight that continues. Bluffing here is unattractive: this runout (Q-9-7-8) hits a lot of his preflop-call/flop-call range (Qx, 9x, JT, 98/87), and without a spade blocker we don’t reduce the likelihood of missed flush draws enough to justify turning our hand into a big river bluff. Checking maximizes our chance to win at showdown versus his give-ups and keeps us from lighting chips on fire into a range that calls too often; if he fires big, we can comfortably fold the ace-high.

The board is highly coordinated (7s Qd 9s 8h 2h), with several straights possible (any Ten makes a straight). FiveTwo 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. While my Ace-high has very little showdown value, it is possible I am ahead of missed spade draws or hands like AK. However, most pairs (even small ones like 55 or 66) beat me. Despite this, betting as a bluff is risky on such a connected board against an aggressive opponent who might have been check-calling with a marginal hand like JJ, TT, or a weak Queen. Given that I am the chip leader with only 4 hands remaining in the tournament, the most prudent strategy is to check back, realize whatever showdown value I have, and preserve my stack to secure the tournament lead.

I am holding J8o UTG in a 4-handed game. With only 4 hands remaining in the tournament and being the chip leader, there is no reason to play a marginal hand like J8o from early position, especially with a short stack (Pro) in the blinds who may be looking for a spot to shove. Folding is the most prudent strategy to preserve my lead.

Four-handed and on the button, we should be opening very wide to pressure the blinds and pick up dead money. Q4o isn’t a strong hand, but it’s good enough as a steal in position, especially with opponents who have shown plenty of folding preflop and can be outplayed postflop. I’ll use a small-but-standard open size to risk little while still generating fold equity; if we face a 3-bet we can comfortably fold.

Current hand is 147 of 150. I am in the Small Blind with 8c 3h, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 250. Even though the raise is small (2.5x), calling would put me out of position with a junk hand against a strong stack. My stack is the shortest at 3775, and I need to preserve chips for better opportunities or shove spots in these final few hands. There is no strategic merit in defending 83o here.

5-3 offsuit is a very weak hand. While I'm getting about 2.7:1 pot odds, this hand plays poorly postflop and I'm out of position against FiveTwo who has been aggressive. With only 4 hands left and a decent stack of 10,250, no need to defend with trash. Fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re first to act 4-handed and 7♢5♥ offsuit is too weak and poorly playable out of position against three players, especially with the big blind (Flash) deep and capable of applying pressure. Limping invites an isolation raise and we don’t realize equity well; opening as a steal is too thin with this exact hand. With only a couple hands left, preserving stack and choosing better spots to pressure the short stack (Pro) is higher EV.

I am holding 2h 4c on the Button, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I am in position, I am the shortest stack with 3,775 chips (approx. 38 BB) and facing two opponents in the blinds (Claude and Flash) who cover me significantly. With only 3 hands left in the tournament, there is no incentive to attempt a risky steal with such poor holdings against large stacks who can easily defend or 3-bet. Preserving my stack for the final two hands is the prudent play.

QTs is a strong hand heads-up against just the BB. With only 2 hands left in the tournament and a solid stack of 102 BBs, I should raise to try to take down the pot or build value. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x).

I have K9o in the Big Blind. Claude raised from the Small Blind to 250. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 350, which provides excellent pot odds of 30% equity required. K9o is a strong hand to defend in a blind-versus-blind scenario, and I will be in position for the remainder of the hand. Since we are near the end of the tournament (hand 148 of 150) and I am currently the chip leader, calling to see a flop is a lower-variance play than 3-betting, which might invite a 4-bet and force me to fold my equity.

Flopped middle pair (tens) with queen kicker on 8d As Tc board. I'm heads-up against Flash who defended BB. This is a good board to c-bet - I have decent equity with second pair plus a gutshot to the straight (need J). Standard c-bet around 50% pot for value and protection.

On this board of 8d-As-Tc, I have King-high with no direct draw. This board texture heavily favors Claude's pre-flop raising range from the Small Blind, as it contains an Ace and two broadway/connected cards. Claude has followed up with a half-pot continuation bet. With very little equity and no clear path to improving to the best hand, I should fold and preserve my chip lead, especially since there are only three hands remaining in the tournament.

Current hand is 149/150. I am UTG (4-handed) with Jd Th (Jack-Ten offsuit) and a stack of 3775 (approx 38 BB). While 4-handed play requires wider ranges, JTo is at the very bottom of the opening range from the Cutoff/UTG. Given the aggressive large stacks in the blinds (Flash and FiveTwo both >20k) and the button (Claude 10.5k), opening this hand invites 3-bets or difficult post-flop situations out of position against deep stacks who can exert maximum pressure. With only 2 hands remaining, preserving the stack to avoid a high-variance marginal spot is preferable to risking chips for a small blind steal with a easily dominated hand.

Hand 149/150, on the button with As6d. With only Pro folding before me, I have a decent suited ace that plays well in position. Standard button raise to steal blinds or build a pot with position. Raising to 2.5x (250) is standard.

Ace-Six offsuit is a strong hand in 4-handed play, especially when facing a Button open. Claude has been active in late positions, and a 3-bet here allows me to take initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. It also discourages the Big Blind from squeezing or seeing a cheap flop. I will raise to 850 to put pressure on the Button's wide opening range.

We’re in the BB facing a BTN open and an SB 3-bet, with Claude still behind us. Js9h (offsuit) performs poorly versus even moderately wide 3-bet ranges because it’s frequently dominated (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, etc.), and we’d be forced to play a bloated pot out of position, often multiway if Claude overcalls. A 4-bet bluff with J9o is a low-quality candidate (poor blockers, bad playability when called). Given the stack depths and positional disadvantage, folding is the highest-EV, lowest-variance choice here.

Flash 3-bet me from the SB. A6o is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range. Even with position, I'm often dominated by better aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) or facing pocket pairs. With 10,250 chips and only 2 hands remaining, no need to take a -EV spot. Folding and preserving stack.

Final hand of the tournament. Q3s from UTG is a weak holding - typically a fold in this position. I'm in 3rd place with 10,250 chips, safely ahead of Pro (3,675). No need to take unnecessary risks with a marginal hand from the worst position. Folding preserves my stack and tournament standing.

It is the final hand of the tournament. I am currently the chip leader with 23,525 chips, followed by FiveTwo at 22,400 and Pro at 3,675. Claude has already folded. Holding Qc4c on the button, I have a standard opening hand. My goal is to maximize my stack and protect my lead. A raise to 300 exerts pressure on the blinds and aims to take down the pot immediately. If FiveTwo or Pro three-bets or jams, I can safely fold and still retain the chip lead, as the loss of 300 chips would not be enough for FiveTwo to overtake me in this final hand (23,225 vs a maximum possible stack for FiveTwo of approximately 22,850 if he takes my 300 and the big blind). Raising also prevents a simple walk for the blinds and keeps me in control of the action.

Flash is opening the button to 300, likely with a wide range. Calling in the small blind with 98o puts us out of position and often multiway (BB is getting a great price), which makes realization tough. I prefer turning this into an aggressive squeeze: a 3-bet leverages position disadvantage by creating fold equity versus Flash’s wide opens and pressures the short-stacked BB from realizing equity cheaply. If we face a 4-bet from Flash or a BB shove, we can comfortably fold this hand.

This is the final hand of the session (150/150). I am in the Big Blind with 7c 2c, a very weak hand. The action involves a Button raise from Flash and a substantial 3-bet to 1250 from FiveTwo in the Small Blind. My hand has very poor equity against a 3-betting range, and I am not getting the right pot odds (need ~41%) to call, nor do I have fold equity to bluff shove given the strength shown. Preserving my remaining 3675 stack is the only logical play.

This is the final hand of the tournament. My primary goal is to secure the chip lead and win. Currently, my stack is 23,225 (after my 300 raise) and FiveTwo's stack is 21,200 (after their 1,250 3-bet). If I fold now, my stack remains 23,225. Even if FiveTwo wins this pot, their stack will only increase to 21,600 (21,200 + my 300 + 100 BB). By folding, I mathematically guarantee finishing with the most chips and winning the tournament. Calling with a weak hand like Qc4c would only introduce unnecessary risk and potential for FiveTwo to overtake me on the flop.
